topics.title: Introduction to Quality Management - Definitions of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Quality Management - Definitions of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Formal Definition of Quality

The most widely accepted and globally standardized definition of **Quality** is provided by the **ISO 9000:2015** standard, which forms the backbone of all modern quality management systems. According to ISO 9000, Quality is formally defined as:

> **"The degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfills requirements."**

Breaking this formal definition into its three operative pillars:

1. **Object** — anything that can be perceived, conceived, or produced (a product, service, process, person, system, or even an idea).
2. **Inherent Characteristics** — the permanent, built-in features or attributes possessed by the object (e.g., tensile strength of steel, response time of a website, taste of a dish).
3. **Requirements** — the documented needs, expectations, or obligations (stated or implied) of the customer, regulator, or standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Quality is **NOT** a single, static number. It is a *relative degree*. A product is never "absolute quality"; it is "quality relative to a defined set of requirements." This nuance is a frequently tested concept in KTU board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making "Quality" Click

Imagine you walk into a restaurant in Kochi and order an **Appam with Stew**.

| Your Expectation (Requirement) | What You Receive (Inherent Characteristic) | Quality Degree |
|-------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|----------------|
| Appam must be soft, fluffy, and white | Appam is rubbery, thick, and slightly burnt | **LOW Quality** |
| Appam must be soft, fluffy, and white | Appam is soft, fluffy, white, and stew is flavorful | **HIGH Quality** |

The dish itself never changed in name — only the **degree** to which it satisfied your requirement changed. That *measurable gap* between expectation and delivery is the heart of the word **Quality**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Quality:**
> $$\text{Quality} = f(\text{Requirements}, \text{Inherent Characteristics}, \text{Degree of Fulfillment})$$
> Quality is therefore a **continuous variable**, not a binary yes/no attribute.

## 1.3 Why "Quality" Has So Many Definitions

Quality is a **multidimensional** and **subjective** concept. Depending on whose perspective you adopt — the customer, the engineer, the manufacturer, or the accountant — the meaning shifts. This is why quality experts across decades have offered their own specialized definitions, each capturing a unique facet.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Exam Answers:** Whenever a question asks "Define Quality," always begin with the **ISO definition** (for technical credibility), then cite **2-3 guru definitions** (for analytical depth). This layering strategy fetches full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Evolution of Quality Thinking

The conceptualization of quality has evolved through **three major historical eras**, each leaving behind a distinct intellectual legacy:

- **Era 1 — Inspection Era (Pre-1920s):** Quality = Conformance to specifications, achieved through end-of-line checking.
- **Era 2 — Quality Control Era (1920s–1950s):** Quality = Statistical control of processes (Shewhart, Deming).
- **Era 3 — Quality Management Era (1950s–Present):** Quality = Strategic, customer-centric, system-wide excellence (Juran, Crosby, Feigenbaum, Taguchi).

## 2.2 Definitions by the Quality Gurus — The KTU Hall of Fame

The following table is a **mandatory revision artifact** for any KTU Quality Engineering paper. These are the definitions most frequently quoted in board questions.

| **Quality Guru** | **Definition of Quality** | **Core Theme** |
|------------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| **Joseph M. Juran** | "Fitness for Use" | Customer satisfaction oriented |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | "Conformance to Requirements" | Zero defects, prevention-based |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | "A predictable degree of uniformity and dependability at low cost" | Statistical, process-focused |
| **Walter A. Shewhart** | Two-sided: Objective (Quality of Design) + Subjective (Quality of Conformance) | Originator of SPC |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | "Loss imparted to society from the time a product is shipped" | Loss-function, societal view |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | "Total Quality Control" — an organization-wide integrated effort | System-wide management |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | "Quality is a continuous process of customer satisfaction through Total Quality Control" | Company-wide, customer-driven |
| **David A. Garvin** | Five distinct approaches: Transcendent, Product-based, User-based, Manufacturing-based, Value-based | Multi-perspective framework |
| **Peter F. Drucker** | "Quality is not what you put in, it is what the customer gets out" | Customer-outcome focused |
| **ISO 9000:2015** | Degree to which inherent characteristics fulfill requirements | Standardized global view |

## 2.3 Garvin's Five Approaches to Quality — Deep Dive

**David Garvin** of Harvard Business School provided a celebrated framework classifying quality into **five mutually exclusive approaches**. This is a **high-weightage KTU topic** and appears almost every academic year.

### 2.3.1 Transcendent Approach

- **Premise:** Quality is an absolute, universally recognizable property — something you feel intuitively but cannot measure precisely.
- **Analogy:** A sunset is "beautiful" — you know it, but cannot quantify it.
- **Limitation:** Non-operational, hard to translate into engineering specifications.

### 2.3.2 Product-Based Approach

- **Premise:** Quality is a precise, measurable variable in the product itself. As the quantity of an ingredient or attribute increases, quality increases.
- **Example:** A silk saree with 200 threads per inch is higher quality than one with 100.
- **Limitation:** Assumes more is always better, which is not universally true (e.g., over-engineering).

### 2.3.3 User-Based Approach

- **Premise:** Quality is **in the eyes of the beholder**. It is the ability to satisfy consumer preferences at an affordable cost.
- **Example:** A luxury car is "high quality" to an affluent buyer but inaccessible to a budget buyer.
- **Limitation:** Highly subjective, varies between individuals and cultures.

### 2.3.4 Manufacturing-Based Approach

- **Premise:** Quality is **conformance to specifications and engineering requirements**. The emphasis is on process discipline and zero deviation.
- **Example:** A bolt must have diameter $d = 10 \text{ mm} \pm 0.05 \text{ mm}$.
- **Limitation:** Ignores whether the specification itself was correctly chosen for the customer.

### 2.3.5 Value-Based Approach

- **Premise:** Quality is the ratio of **performance** (usefulness) to **price** (cost). It is the best combination of quality and affordability.
- **Example:** A high-performance laptop at a justified price point.

Mathematically, the value-based view can be expressed as:

$$V = \frac{P}{C}$$

where $V$ is the perceived value, $P$ is perceived performance, and $C$ is perceived cost.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These definitions are not academic exercises — they directly drive industrial decisions:

- **Juran's "Fitness for Use"** → Drives design FMEA and customer feedback loops.
- **Crosby's "Conformance"** → Drives Six Sigma and Zero-Defect programs.
- **Taguchi's "Loss to Society"** → Drives robust design and tolerance optimization.
- **ISO 9000 Definition** → Drives certification audits and global supply-chain acceptance.
- **Garvin's Framework** → Drives strategy selection for market positioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **Taguchi Loss Function** is particularly critical for engineering streams. It states that any deviation from the target value $T$ causes a loss $L$ to society, even if the product is within specification limits:
> $$L(y) = k(y - T)^2$$
> where $k$ is a constant (loss coefficient) and $y$ is the actual quality characteristic value. This **quadratic loss** principle is a favorite KTU Part B question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Analytical Matrix & Step-by-Step Conceptual Derivations

Since "Definitions of Quality" is a conceptual-management topic, the analytical depth is delivered through **comparative matrices**, **dimensional analysis**, and **logical derivations** rather than algebraic calculus. This is the KTU-approved way to extract maximum marks in a management theory answer.

## 3.1 Comparative Analytical Matrix — The 5-Column Comparison

The following matrix is the single most important analytical artifact for this topic. In a 14-mark KTU answer, presenting this table guarantees substantial credit.

| **Dimension** | **Juran** | **Crosby** | **Deming** | **Taguchi** | **Garvin** |
|---------------|-----------|------------|------------|-------------|------------|
| Core Definition | Fitness for Use | Conformance to Requirements | Uniformity & Dependability | Loss to Society | Five-perspective framework |
| Primary Focus | Customer | Specifications | Process | Society / Variability | Multi-dimensional |
| Measure of Quality | Customer satisfaction index | Number of defects | Statistical deviation | Loss in monetary terms | Depends on approach |
| Unit of Quality | Percentage satisfaction | Defects per million (DPMO) | Standard deviation $\sigma$ | Currency (₹ / $) | Relative rank |
| Best Application | Service industries, customer-facing | Manufacturing, process control | Repetitive production | Engineering design | Strategic positioning |
| Era / Origin | 1950s | 1970s | 1940s | 1980s | 1980s |
| KTU Keyword to Use | "FUS" | "Zero Defects" | "Statistical Control" | "Loss Function" | "Five Approaches" |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: How Definitions Evolved

To answer *"Discuss the evolution of the concept of Quality"* in a KTU exam, use the following **structured 5-step argumentative flow**:

**Step 1 — The Beginning (Pre-Industrial):** Quality was a *craft-based* concept. The artisan personally guaranteed the product. There was no formal definition; quality was synonymous with the maker's reputation.

**Step 2 — Industrial Revolution Shift:** With mass production came standardization. Quality shifted from *artisan reputation* to *specification conformance*. This birthed the **Inspection Era** and the **Manufacturing-Based Approach** of Garvin.

**Step 3 — Statistical Revolution (Shewhart → Deming):** Walter Shewhart introduced **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** in 1924. Deming extended this into a management philosophy. Quality now meant *predictable uniformity*, not just end-of-line checking.

**Step 4 — Customer-Centric Revolution (Juran, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa):** The post-WWII Japanese quality movement redefined quality as *customer satisfaction* and *fitness for use*. Quality became a *strategic management* function, not just a *production* function.

**Step 5 — Modern Holistic Era (Taguchi → ISO 9000 → TQM):** The current view is **holistic and global**. Quality encompasses customer value, societal cost (Taguchi), international standardization (ISO 9000), and total organizational participation (TQM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy:** This 5-step flow can be presented in a KTU exam as a structured narrative paragraph. Linking each step to a **named guru** and a **decade** demonstrates evaluative thinking, which is rewarded at the top of the marking rubric.

## 3.3 Derivation of Garvin's Framework Logic

Garvin argued that since quality means different things to different stakeholders, no single definition suffices. He therefore constructed a **multi-dimensional conceptual model**.

**Logic Step A:** Identify all stakeholders who interpret quality differently.
$\rightarrow$ Customer, Engineer, Producer, Accountant, Society

**Logic Step B:** For each stakeholder, derive their unique quality lens.

$$
\text{Stakeholder} = \begin{cases} \text{Customer} \rightarrow \text{User-Based View} \\ \text{Engineer} \rightarrow \text{Product-Based View} \\ \text{Producer} \rightarrow \text{Manufacturing-Based View} \\ \text{Accountant} \rightarrow \text{Value-Based View} \\ \text{Society} \rightarrow \text{Transcendent View} \end{cases}
$$

**Logic Step C:** Combine all five views into a single integrated framework, which is the **Garvin 5-Approach Wheel** — used even today for strategic quality positioning.

## 3.4 Numerical Illustration of Taguchi Loss Function

To make the concept mathematically tangible, consider a shaft designed to have diameter $T = 50 \text{ mm}$. Suppose a part is produced with $y = 50.2 \text{ mm}$, which is technically within the specification limit of $\pm 0.5 \text{ mm}$ (so it passes inspection). However, Taguchi argued that the customer still suffers a loss.

Let the loss coefficient be $k = 10{,}000$ ₹/$\text{mm}^2$.

$$L = k \cdot (y - T)^2$$

$$L = 10{,}000 \cdot (50.2 - 50.0)^2$$

$$L = 10{,}000 \cdot (0.2)^2$$

$$L = 10{,}000 \cdot 0.04$$

$$L = 400 \text{ ₹ per unit}$$

**Insight:** Even though the product is *within specification*, society still bears a ₹400 loss per unit. This proves Taguchi's central thesis: **specification conformance ≠ zero loss**. This is a powerful KTU illustration worth 2-3 bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evolution of Quality Definitions — Timeline Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram maps the historical evolution of quality thinking across eras, gurus, and the conceptual shift from inspection to management.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    era1["ERA 1 CRAFT ERA PRE 1900"] --> era2["ERA 2 INSPECTION ERA 1900 1920"]
    era2 --> era3["ERA 3 QUALITY CONTROL ERA 1920 1950"]
    era3 --> era4["ERA 4 QUALITY MANAGEMENT ERA 1950 1990"]
    era4 --> era5["ERA 5 TOTAL QUALITY ERA 1990 PRESENT"]

    guru1["SHEWHART SPC 1924"] --> era3
    guru2["DEMING 14 POINTS 1950"] --> era4
    guru3["JURAN FITNESS FOR USE 1954"] --> era4
    guru4["FEIGENBAUM TQC 1956"] --> era4
    guru5["CROSBY QUALITY IS FREE 1979"] --> era4
    guru6["TAGUCHI LOSS FUNCTION 1985"] --> era5
    guru7["GARVIN 5 APPROACHES 1987"] --> era5
    guru8["ISO 9000 STANDARD 1987"] --> era5
    guru9["ISHIKAWA COMPANY WIDE TQC"] --> era4

    iso1["ISO 9000 1987"] --> iso2["ISO 9000 2000"]
    iso2 --> iso3["ISO 9000 2015 CURRENT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each horizontal stratum represents a historical era, and each guru is a vertical "feeder" who contributed a key concept to that era. This visualization is excellent for KTU answer scripts because it shows evaluative depth.

## 4.2 Garvin's Five Approaches — Wheel Architecture

The following block diagram presents Garvin's 5 approaches as a centralized **Quality Interpretation Hub**, where each approach feeds into a common quality outcome.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    transc["TRANSCENDENT ABSOLUTE BEAUTY"] --> hub["QUALITY DECISION HUB"]
    product["PRODUCT BASED MEASURABLE ATTRIBUTES"] --> hub
    user["USER BASED CUSTOMER PERCEPTION"] --> hub
    manu["MANUFACTURING BASED CONFORMANCE TO SPECS"] --> hub
    value["VALUE BASED PERFORMANCE VS PRICE"] --> hub

    hub --> outcome["INTEGRATED QUALITY POSITIONING"]
    outcome --> strat["STRATEGIC MARKET ADVANTAGE"]
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: How Definitions Link to Quality Tools

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    def1["JURAN FITNESS FOR USE"] --> tool1["QFD HOUSE OF QUALITY"]
    def2["CROSBY CONFORMANCE"] --> tool2["SIX SIGMA DMAIC"]
    def3["DEMING UNIFORMITY"] --> tool3["SPC CONTROL CHARTS"]
    def4["TAGUCHI LOSS TO SOCIETY"] --> tool4["ROBUST DESIGN DOE"]
    def5["FEIGENBAUM TQC"] --> tool5["SYSTEMS AUDIT"]
    def6["ISO 9000 DEGREE OF FULFILLMENT"] --> tool6["PROCESS DOCUMENTATION AUDIT"]

    tool1 --> out["QUALITY ENGINEERING DELIVERABLES"]
    tool2 --> out
    tool3 --> out
    tool4 --> out
    tool5 --> out
    tool6 --> out
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Discuss the relationship between quality definitions and quality tools,"* this concept map can be redrawn in your answer script as a hand-sketched block diagram. Examiners reward visual organization with bonus clarity marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**"Define quality as per ISO 9000:2015. Briefly explain the term 'degree of fulfillment' in this context."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

According to **ISO 9000:2015**, quality is defined as *"the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfills requirements."*

The term **"degree of fulfillment"** refers to the extent or level to which the inherent (built-in) characteristics of a product, service, or process successfully meet the stated or implied requirements of the customer or specification. It is a continuous, measurable variable — not a binary "yes/no." For example, a smartphone whose battery lasts 18 hours when the requirement is 20 hours has a 90% degree of fulfillment, even though it still functions.

**[Examiner's note: 1 mark ISO definition + 1 mark explanation of degree + 1 mark example = 3 marks]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**"State and explain Joseph Juran's definition of quality."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Joseph M. Juran, one of the founding fathers of modern quality management, defined quality as **"Fitness for Use."**

This definition emphasizes that quality is not about the product's luxury, prestige, or technical sophistication — it is purely about how well the product serves the customer's intended purpose. A low-cost product can still be "high quality" if it perfectly satisfies the user's needs. Juran's definition has three components:

1. **Quality of Design** — how well the product is engineered to meet customer needs.
2. **Quality of Conformance** — how well the produced item matches the design.
3. **Availability** — the product is functional when needed.
4. **Field Service** — support provided post-sale.

**[Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the 4 components = 3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various definitions of quality given by prominent quality gurus. Highlight how each definition reflects a different perspective on the same concept. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Garvin's five approaches to quality with suitable examples. Which approach do you consider most appropriate for a manufacturing organization and why? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Quality is a multidimensional concept. Since the 1950s, several quality gurus have contributed definitions, each illuminating a different facet of the same construct.

**Body (5 marks):**
- **Joseph Juran (1954) — "Fitness for Use":** Juran's definition is customer-centric. He argued that a product is high quality only when it serves its intended purpose. His framework includes quality of design, quality of conformance, availability, and field service.
- **Philip Crosby (1979) — "Conformance to Requirements":** Crosby reduced quality to a simple binary — either the product meets specifications or it does not. He championed the "Zero Defects" philosophy and argued that quality is free because the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of rework.
- **W. Edwards Deming (1950s) — "Predictable Uniformity and Dependability":** Deming defined quality in statistical terms — the reduction of variation in processes. He introduced the famous 14 Points for Management and the PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle.
- **Genichi Taguchi (1985) — "Loss to Society":** Taguchi revolutionized quality by defining it as the *loss imparted to society* due to product variability, even if within specification. He proposed the **quadratic loss function** $L = k(y - T)^2$.
- **Armand Feigenbaum (1956) — "Total Quality Control":** Feigenbaum argued that quality is not the responsibility of the production department alone; it is a company-wide integrated effort.
- **ISO 9000:2015:** "Degree to which inherent characteristics fulfill requirements" — the globally standardized, process-oriented definition.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Each guru's definition is a lens on the same elephant called "Quality." Together, they form a comprehensive conceptual umbrella covering customer, process, statistical, societal, and systemic dimensions.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Garvin's Five Approaches (5 marks):**

| **Approach** | **Definition** | **Example** |
|--------------|----------------|-------------|
| Transcendent | Quality is absolute, intuitive, non-measurable | A "premium" feel of a Rolex watch |
| Product-Based | Quality is a measurable, internal attribute | Thread count in a bedsheet |
| User-Based | Quality lies in customer satisfaction | Personalized smartphone features |
| Manufacturing-Based | Quality is conformance to specifications | Engine bore diameter $d = 80 \pm 0.01 \text{ mm}$ |
| Value-Based | Quality is performance relative to price | Maruti Alto: high value for budget segment |

**Application Analysis (2 marks):**
For a **manufacturing organization**, the **Manufacturing-Based Approach** is most appropriate because:
1. It translates customer needs into **measurable engineering specifications**.
2. It aligns with **process control tools** like SPC, control charts, and Six Sigma.
3. It supports **regulatory compliance** and **ISO 9000** certification.
4. It minimizes rework cost by catching deviations early.

However, mature organizations today integrate the **User-Based** and **Value-Based** approaches to ensure that the *specifications themselves* are aligned with customer expectations and price points.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** "Quality has evolved from a craft-based concept to a strategic management function." Discuss this statement with reference to the historical evolution of quality thinking. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Taguchi Loss Function. How does it challenge the traditional "conformance to specifications" view of quality? Illustrate with a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Historical Evolution (5 marks):**

1. **Craft Era (Pre-1900):** Quality was synonymous with the artisan's personal reputation. No formal definition existed; quality was implicit in the maker's signature.
2. **Inspection Era (1900–1920):** With mass production, the need for end-of-line checking arose. Quality became **"conformance to specifications"** — this is the **Manufacturing-Based Approach** of Garvin.
3. **Quality Control Era (1920–1950):** Walter Shewhart introduced **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**. Quality became a statistical concept — control of variation. Deming later extended this to a management philosophy.
4. **Quality Management Era (1950–1990):** Juran ("Fitness for Use"), Feigenbaum (TQC), Ishikawa (Company-Wide TQC), and Crosby ("Zero Defects") reframed quality as a **strategic, organization-wide** function driven by **customer satisfaction**.
5. **Total Quality Era (1990–Present):** Taguchi ("Loss to Society"), Garvin (5 approaches), and **ISO 9000:2015** established a **holistic, global, and integrated** view. Quality is now a boardroom concern, not a shop-floor concern.

**Strategic Shift (2 marks):**
The transformation can be summarized as:

$$\text{Craftsmanship} \rightarrow \text{Inspection} \rightarrow \text{Control} \rightarrow \text{Management} \rightarrow \text{Strategy}$$

This shift reflects the increasing recognition that quality is **not a department** but an **organizational culture** and a **competitive weapon**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Taguchi Loss Function — Theory (3 marks):**
Dr. Genichi Taguchi redefined quality as the **loss a product imparts to society** after it is shipped to the customer. He argued that any deviation from the **target value** $T$ of a quality characteristic results in a loss, even if the deviation is within the specification limits.

The mathematical form of the Taguchi Loss Function is:

$$L(y) = k \cdot (y - T)^2$$

where:
- $L(y)$ = Loss incurred (in monetary terms, e.g., ₹)
- $k$ = Loss coefficient (a constant depending on the product)
- $y$ = Actual measured value of the quality characteristic
- $T$ = Target value (the ideal value)

**Challenge to Traditional View (2 marks):**
Traditional quality (Crosby's view) considers any product within specification limits to be "good." Taguchi challenged this by showing that:
- A product **within spec** but **off-target** still causes customer dissatisfaction and societal cost.
- The traditional "Goal-Post Mentality" of specification limits is statistically and economically **flawed**.
- Quality improvement should target the **target value** itself, not just the specification boundaries.

**Numerical Illustration (2 marks):**
Let the target diameter of a shaft be $T = 100 \text{ mm}$ and $k = 5000$ ₹/$\text{mm}^2$.

**Case 1:** Actual diameter $y_1 = 100.0 \text{ mm}$ (exactly on target)
$$L_1 = 5000 \cdot (100.0 - 100.0)^2 = 0 \text{ ₹}$$

**Case 2:** Actual diameter $y_2 = 100.4 \text{ mm}$ (within typical $\pm 0.5$ mm spec)
$$L_2 = 5000 \cdot (100.4 - 100.0)^2 = 5000 \cdot 0.16 = 800 \text{ ₹ per unit}$$

**Conclusion:** Even though Case 2 passes traditional inspection, it imposes an ₹800 loss per unit on society. This is why Taguchi advocated **robust design** and **on-target manufacturing**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> 1. **Defining Quality in a single line without context** — Always pair the ISO definition with at least one guru definition. A bare ISO line = 1 mark; a layered answer = 3-4 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "Quality Control" with "Quality Management"** — QC is statistical/production focused; QM is strategic/organization-wide. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Omitting Taguchi's Loss Function in any 14-mark answer about quality definitions** — This is considered a **mandatory component** in Part B answers. Skipping it loses 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Writing about "Quality Circles" or "Kaizen" when asked about definitions** — These are *tools*, not *definitions*. Stay focused on the conceptual definitions.
> 5. **Forgetting to cite decades/years for each guru** — Examiners reward chronological precision. "Juran (1954)" scores higher than just "Juran."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ISO 9000:2015 Definition:** *"Degree to which inherent characteristics fulfill requirements."* — This is the foundational citation for every answer.
- **Juran's "Fitness for Use"** — The most customer-centric definition. Applicable to products and services alike.
- **Crosby's "Conformance to Requirements"** — Process-disciplined, zero-defects oriented. Quantified using DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities).
- **Deming's "Uniformity and Dependability"** — Statistical, process-variation focused. Underpins SPC.
- **Taguchi's "Loss to Society"** — Captured mathematically by $L = k(y - T)^2$. Challenges the goal-post mentality.
- **Feigenbaum's "Total Quality Control"** — Quality is a company-wide responsibility, not a department.
- **Garvin's 5 Approaches:** Transcendent, Product-based, User-based, Manufacturing-based, Value-based — must be presented in a tabular or wheel format in exam answers.
- **Evolution Flow:** Craft → Inspection → Control → Management → Total Quality.
- **High-Yield Keywords for 2024 Scheme:** *Inherent characteristics, Fitness for use, Zero defects, Loss function, Goal-post mentality, Multi-dimensional quality, Customer-defined quality.*
- **Numerical Anchor:** Always remember the Taguchi loss formula $L = k(y - T)^2$ — at least one numerical example can fetch bonus marks in any Part B answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensions of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dimensions of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **Quality Engineering and Management**, the **Dimensions of Quality** are defined as the *distinct, measurable, and customer-perceivable attributes* that collectively define the totality of "quality" in a product, service, or process. These dimensions provide a structured, multi-dimensional framework to translate the abstract notion of quality into concrete, evaluable characteristics.

The most widely accepted academic framework, originally proposed by **David A. Garvin (1984)** in his seminal Harvard Business Review article *"What Does 'Product Quality' Really Mean?"*, classifies quality into **Eight Dimensions**. Later, quality gurus and ISO standards expanded these into broader managerial, value-based, and product-based perspectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Core Definition**
> Quality is **multidimensional**. A single defect-free product is *not* necessarily a "high-quality" product. Customers evaluate quality across several independent yet interconnected dimensions. Therefore, quality management must address **all dimensions** in a balanced manner to achieve total customer satisfaction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Buying a Smartphone"

Imagine a student purchasing a smartphone. The student does **not** judge quality by a single factor. Instead, they subconsciously evaluate:

| Student's Evaluation | Corresponding Quality Dimension |
|---|---|
| Does the phone run apps smoothly and fast? | **Performance** |
| Does it have fingerprint sensor, dual camera, 5G? | **Features** |
| Will it work without breakdown for 3+ years? | **Reliability** |
| Is every unit identical to the advertised one? | **Conformance** |
| How long before the battery degrades? | **Durability** |
| How easy is it to get a service center? | **Serviceability** |
| Does it look stylish and feel premium? | **Aesthetics** |
| Is the brand trusted and well-reputed? | **Perceived Quality** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Insight:** If a phone has blazing-fast performance but a poor service network, the *overall* quality perception collapses. This is exactly why KTU emphasizes "Dimensions" — quality is a **vector**, not a scalar.

### 1.3 The Three Universal Perspectives of Quality (Foundational Layer)

Before Garvin's eight dimensions, quality must be understood through three competing yet complementary conceptualizations, often called the **Perspectives of Quality** (per Garvin and later refined by Reeves & Bednar):

1. **User-Based View:** Quality lies in the eyes of the customer — fitness for use, meeting expectations.
2. **Manufacturing-Based View:** Quality is conformance to specifications, zero defects, process capability.
3. **Product-Based View:** Quality is a precise and measurable variable — higher quality means higher cost (premium raw materials, tighter tolerances).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question mentions "define quality," examiners expect you to mention *all three* perspectives in the first paragraph itself to secure full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Dimensions of Quality (Garvin's Framework) — Exhaustive Breakdown

Below is the rigorous, KTU-aligned elaboration of every dimension. Each dimension is dissected into its **operational meaning**, **measurable indicators**, and **engineering application**.

#### Dimension 1 — Performance
- **Meaning:** The primary operating characteristics of a product or service. It is the *core function* the product is designed to deliver.
- **Measurement:** Through technical specifications, KPIs (e.g., miles per gallon, processor speed, mean time between failures).
- **Engineering Utility:** Performance forms the baseline of any quality discussion. If performance is poor, no other dimension can compensate.

#### Dimension 2 — Features
- **Meaning:** The "extras" or additional characteristics beyond the primary function. These are the *bells and whistles*.
- **Measurement:** Count-based, checklist-based, often qualitative.
- **Example:** A car with basic AC vs. a car with climate control, sunroof, and ambient lighting — both can "drive," but the second has more features.

#### Dimension 3 — Reliability
- **Meaning:** The probability that a product will perform its intended function **without failure** over a specified period of time under stated conditions.
- **Measurement:** MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures), failure rate λ(t), reliability function R(t).
- **KTU Critical Concept:** Reliability is **time-dependent**, unlike conformance.

#### Dimension 4 — Conformance
- **Meaning:** The degree to which a product's design and operating characteristics match **pre-established standards or specifications**.
- **Measurement:** Number of defects per million opportunities (DPMO), conformance rate, sigma level.
- **Note:** Conformance is **static** and **binary** (pass/fail).

#### Dimension 5 — Durability
- **Meaning:** The measure of product's **useful life** before it deteriorates and requires replacement. It includes both technical and economic life.
- **Measurement:** Service life in hours/years/kilometers, wear curves, accelerated life testing data.
- **Distinction from Reliability:** A product can be reliable (rarely fails) but not durable (wears out quickly through normal use).

#### Dimension 6 — Serviceability
- **Meaning:** The speed, courtesy, competence, and ease with which the product is **repaired and maintained**.
- **Measurement:** Mean Time To Repair (MTTR), service network coverage, customer satisfaction scores.
- **Example:** Apple vs. local brand — Apple's serviceability (even if costly) is perceived as high due to widespread service centers.

#### Dimension 7 — Aesthetics
- **Meaning:** The **subjective, sensory dimension** of quality — how a product looks, feels, sounds, tastes, or smells.
- **Measurement:** Highly subjective; measured through consumer panels, sensory evaluation, hedonic rating scales.
- **Note:** Aesthetics is *judgmental* and personal, making it the most difficult dimension to standardize.

#### Dimension 8 — Perceived Quality
- **Meaning:** The quality **image** or **reputation** of a product in the customer's mind, often based on brand name, advertising, word-of-mouth, and past experience.
- **Measurement:** Brand equity surveys, Net Promoter Score (NPS), market share.
- **Key Insight:** Two technically identical products can have drastically different perceived quality (e.g., branded vs. unbranded medicines).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction to Master:**
> - **Reliability** → failure-free operation over time
> - **Durability** → total useful life (includes wear-out)
> - **Conformance** → meeting predefined specs
> - **Features** → extra characteristics beyond core function
> - **Aesthetics** → sensory, subjective appeal
> - **Perceived Quality** → image, brand-based opinion

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Reliability, Conformance, and Capability Metrics)

The following formulas are critical for numerical questions in KTU ESE and internal assessments. They span the mathematical backbone of quality dimensions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)} &= \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} \\[6pt]
\text{Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)} &= \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}} \\[6pt]
\text{Availability (A)} &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \\[6pt]
\text{Reliability Function } R(t) &= e^{-\lambda t} \quad \text{(for constant failure rate)} \\[6pt]
\text{Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)} &= \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6 \\[6pt]
\text{Yield} &= 1 - \frac{\text{Number of Defective Units}}{\text{Total Number of Units Produced}} \\[6pt]
\text{Cycle Time} &= \text{Process Time} + \text{Queue Time} + \text{Move Time} + \text{Wait Time}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol / Variable | Application in Quality Dimensions | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MTBF = Total Time / Failures | MTBF | Reliability, Durability | Hours |
| 2 | MTTR = Total Downtime / Repairs | MTTR | Serviceability | Hours |
| 3 | Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) | A | Reliability + Serviceability | Percentage (0–1) |
| 4 | R(t) = $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate | Reliability over time | Dimensionless |
| 5 | DPMO formula | D, U, O | Conformance (Six Sigma) | defects/million |
| 6 | First Pass Yield (FPY) | FPY | Conformance, Process Quality | Percentage |
| 7 | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\prod$ of stage yields | Multi-process Conformance | Percentage |
| 8 | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | COPQ | All dimensions (loss function) | Currency |

> [!TIP]
> **Reliability vs. Availability:** MTBF alone is insufficient — a product that fails often but is repaired instantly may have *higher availability* than a product that rarely fails but takes weeks to service. Availability is the **holistic** operational metric.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Performance (horsepower), Reliability (warranty claims), Durability (vehicle life), Serviceability (garage reach) — all must be optimized simultaneously.
- **Software Industry:** Performance (response time), Features (API count), Reliability (uptime %), Conformance (bug-free), Serviceability (support tickets).
- **Healthcare:** Aesthetics is irrelevant; **Conformance** (sterility), **Reliability** (drug potency over shelf life), and **Perceived Quality** (doctor trust) dominate.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Aesthetics and Perceived Quality often drive purchase decisions, even when technical specifications are similar.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability Function R(t) for Constant Failure Rate

The reliability function is the **foundational mathematical model** for the *Reliability* dimension. The derivation from first principles is shown below.

**Step 1 — Start with the empirical observation.**
For many engineering components (especially during their useful life), the failure rate $\lambda$ remains approximately **constant**. This is known as the *useful-life region* of the classic **bathtub curve**.

$$
\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 2 — Recall the definition of hazard rate.**
The hazard (failure) rate is mathematically related to the reliability function as:

$$
\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

This states: the fraction of survivors that fail per unit time equals the negative logarithmic derivative of reliability.

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the derivative term.**

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides from 0 to $t$.**

$$
\int_0^t \frac{dR(u)}{R(u)} = -\int_0^t \lambda \, du
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the initial condition $R(0) = 1$ (at time zero, the product is certain to function).**

$$
\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\lambda t
$$

$$
\ln R(t) - \ln(1) = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate both sides.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

This is the **exponential reliability model**, valid for components with constant failure rate. The Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) is:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** When a question provides a failure rate (e.g., "0.001 failures/hour"), you must use $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ to compute survival probability and MTTF $= 1/\lambda$ as the expected life.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) and Sigma Level

The **Conformance** dimension is quantified in modern quality management using Six Sigma metrics.

**Step 1 — Define DPMO.**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Total Defects Observed (D)}}{\text{Units Produced (U)} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit (O)}} \times 10^6
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Process Sigma (Z) using the inverse standard normal distribution.**

For a defect rate $p$ (proportion defective):

$$
Z = \Phi^{-1}\bigl(1 - p\bigr) + 1.5
$$

The "1.5 sigma shift" is the empirical correction factor for long-term process drift, as defined by Motorola.

**Step 3 — Worked Example.**

Suppose in a printed circuit board assembly:
- Units produced $U = 10{,}000$
- Defects found $D = 250$
- Opportunities per unit $O = 5$ (5 soldering points per board)

Compute DPMO:

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{250}{10{,}000 \times 5} \times 10^6 = \frac{250}{50{,}000} \times 10^6 = 5{,}000 \text{ DPMO}
$$

This corresponds approximately to a **4.0 sigma process** in the Six Sigma yield tables.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Dimensions to Metrics and Tools

The following matrix shows how each of Garvin's eight dimensions is measured in practice and which engineering tools apply.

| Dimension | Quantitative Metric | Qualitative Indicator | Engineering Tool / Methodology |
|---|---|---|---|
| Performance | Technical specifications, KPIs | Customer benchmark | Benchmarking, QFD |
| Features | Feature count, checklist | Customer surveys | House of Quality, Kano Model |
| Reliability | MTBF, R(t), failure rate | Warranty claims | FMEA, Weibull Analysis |
| Conformance | DPMO, sigma level, FPY | Audit pass rate | SPC, Control Charts, Six Sigma |
| Durability | Service life, wear curves | Field failure data | Accelerated Life Testing |
| Serviceability | MTTR, availability | Service satisfaction | TPM, Reliability Engineering |
| Aesthetics | Sensory scores | Panel evaluation | Sensory Engineering, Ergonomics |
| Perceived Quality | NPS, brand equity | Reputation index | Brand Audits, Customer Surveys |

### 3.4 Categorization of Dimensions (KTU Classification)

For exam clarity, the eight dimensions can be grouped into three categories:

1. **Objective / Measurable:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability
2. **Subjective / Perceptual:** Aesthetics, Perceived Quality
3. **Time-Dependent:** Reliability, Durability, Serviceability (require longitudinal data)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Always identify the dimension's *category* (objective/subjective, time-dependent) before proposing a measurement scheme. This demonstrates conceptual depth and is rewarded with extra marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Garvin's Eight Dimensions — Master Overview Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Dimensions of Quality] --> B[Objective Dimensions]
    A --> C[Subjective Dimensions]
    A --> D[Time Dependent Dimensions]

    B --> B1[Performance]
    B --> B2[Features]
    B --> B3[Conformance]

    C --> C1[Aesthetics]
    C --> C2[Perceived Quality]

    D --> D1[Reliability]
    D --> D2[Durability]
    D --> D3[Serviceability]

    B1 --> M1[Measured via KPIs and Benchmarks]
    B2 --> M2[Measured via Checklists]
    B3 --> M3[Measured via DPMO and Sigma Level]
    C1 --> M4[Measured via Sensory Panels]
    C2 --> M5[Measured via Brand Equity Surveys]
    D1 --> M6[Measured via MTBF and R of t]
    D2 --> M7[Measured via Service Life]
    D3 --> M8[Measured via MTTR and Availability]
```

### 4.2 Interrelationship Flow Between Dimensions

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Performance] --> Q[Customer Satisfaction]
    F[Features] --> Q
    R[Reliability] --> Q
    C[Conformance] --> Q
    D[Durability] --> Q
    S[Serviceability] --> Q
    A[Aesthetics] --> Q
    PQ[Perceived Quality] --> Q
    Q --> Out[Total Perceived Value and Brand Loyalty]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Flow:** All eight dimensions act as parallel input streams into the central node "Customer Satisfaction." No single dimension is *optional*; the customer integrates them holistically. This is why the diagram has a converging structure rather than a hierarchical one.

### 4.3 Reliability Dimension — Bathtub Curve Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph BathtubCurve[Bathtub Curve of Reliability]
        direction LR
        A1[Infant Mortality Region] --> A2[Useful Life Region]
        A2 --> A3[Wear Out Region]
    end
    A1 --> N1[High Failure Rate Lambda Decreasing]
    A2 --> N2[Constant Lambda]
    A3 --> N3[Lambda Increasing]
    N2 --> M[Reliability Model R of t equals e to the power minus lambda t]
```

### 4.4 Conformance Dimension — Six Sigma Capability Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    CTQ[Critical to Quality CTQ] --> SPEC[Define Specifications]
    SPEC --> MEAS[Measurement System Analysis]
    MEAS --> CAP[Process Capability Cp and Cpk]
    CAP --> CTRL[SPC Control Charts]
    CTRL --> IMPROVE[Continuous Improvement DMAIC]
    IMPROVE --> CTQ
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Quality Dimension Evaluation System

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Input Product or Service] --> DI[Dimension Identification Module]
    DI --> M1[Performance Evaluator]
    DI --> M2[Features Evaluator]
    DI --> M3[Reliability Evaluator]
    DI --> M4[Conformance Evaluator]
    DI --> M5[Durability Evaluator]
    DI --> M6[Serviceability Evaluator]
    DI --> M7[Aesthetics Evaluator]
    DI --> M8[Perceived Quality Evaluator]
    M1 --> AGG[Aggregation Engine]
    M2 --> AGG
    M3 --> AGG
    M4 --> AGG
    M5 --> AGG
    M6 --> AGG
    M7 --> AGG
    M8 --> AGG
    AGG --> OUT[Total Quality Score and Customer Satisfaction Index]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Block Diagram Usage in KTU Exam:** When a 14-mark question asks you to "explain the dimensions of quality with a suitable diagram," this architecture flow is the gold-standard response. It demonstrates both **conceptual breadth** (all 8 dimensions) and **system thinking** (aggregation into a unified metric).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]
**"List and briefly explain any three dimensions of quality as given by David Garvin."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three dimensions selected are:

1. **Performance (1 mark):** The primary operating characteristics of a product; how well the product executes its core function. Example: The fuel efficiency of a car.

2. **Reliability (1 mark):** The probability that a product will perform its intended function without failure over a specified period under stated conditions. Quantified using MTBF or the reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

3. **Aesthetics (1 mark):** The subjective, sensory appeal of a product — its look, feel, sound, and overall appearance. It is judgmental and personal to the customer.

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between Reliability and Durability as dimensions of quality."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Reliability | Durability |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Probability of failure-free operation over time | Total useful life of a product |
| Focus | Avoidance of breakdowns | Resistance to wear-out |
| Time Dependence | Time-dependent stochastic measure | Cumulative life span |
| Measurement | MTBF, R(t) | Service life (years/hours) |
| Example | A bulb working 1000 hours without burning out | The same bulb lasting exactly 1000 hours before dimming |

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for example)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **eight dimensions of quality** as proposed by David A. Garvin. For each dimension, provide one engineering example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing process produces 5,000 units per day. The process has 4 critical quality characteristics (opportunities per unit). In a one-week production run (5 working days), 350 defects were identified during final inspection. Calculate the **DPMO** and the **process sigma level**. Comment on the process capability. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Eight Dimensions of Quality (7 marks = 1 mark per dimension + 1 mark aggregate example)**

1. **Performance** — primary operating characteristics. *Example:* Engine horsepower in a car.
2. **Features** — additional characteristics beyond the core function. *Example:* Sunroof in a car.
3. **Reliability** — probability of failure-free operation over time. *Example:* MTBF of an aircraft engine.
4. **Conformance** — degree to which product matches specifications. *Example:* Tolerance of a ball bearing.
5. **Durability** — measure of product's useful life. *Example:* Tire life in kilometers.
6. **Serviceability** — speed and ease of repair. *Example:* Service network reach of a laptop brand.
7. **Aesthetics** — subjective sensory appeal. *Example:* Finish and color of a smartphone.
8. **Perceived Quality** — customer's quality image/reputation. *Example:* Brand trust for a pharmaceutical company.

*Aggregate (closing 1 mark):* These eight dimensions together define the total quality perception; quality is multidimensional and not reducible to a single metric.

**(b) DPMO and Sigma Level Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Identify the given values.

$$
U = 5{,}000 \text{ units/day} \times 5 \text{ days} = 25{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

$$
O = 4 \text{ opportunities per unit}
$$

$$
D = 350 \text{ defects}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the DPMO formula.

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{350}{25{,}000 \times 4} \times 10^6
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{350}{100{,}000} \times 10^6 = 3{,}500 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute defect proportion.

$$
p = \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} = 0.0035
$$

Yield $= 1 - 0.0035 = 0.9965$ or **99.65%**.

**Step 4:** Estimate process sigma using standard Six Sigma yield table.

A yield of 99.65% corresponds approximately to a **4.0 sigma process** (using the 1.5 sigma shift convention).

**Step 5:** Comment on capability.

The process operates at a 4.0 sigma level, which means roughly 3,500 defects per million opportunities. While this is acceptable for many industries, it is **significantly below** the world-class Six Sigma benchmark of 3.4 DPMO (6 sigma). The process is capable but has substantial improvement potential. *Conclusion (1 mark for comment):* The organization should adopt **DMAIC** methodology to drive continuous improvement.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[Alternative Internal Choice — KTU Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the three universal **perspectives of quality** (User-based, Manufacturing-based, Product-based). Why is it important for a quality manager to understand all three? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The failure rate of an electronic control unit is observed to be constant at $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures per hour. Compute: (i) the reliability $R(t)$ at $t = 1000$ hours, (ii) the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF), and (iii) the probability that the unit will survive at least 2500 hours. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Perspectives of Quality (7 marks)**

1. **User-Based View (2 marks):** Quality is defined as *fitness for use* and the customer's perception of how well a product meets their needs. Different customers may judge the same product differently. The focus is on **subjective satisfaction**.

2. **Manufacturing-Based View (2 marks):** Quality is defined as *conformance to specifications* and absence of defects. The focus is on **engineering precision, process control, and zero-defect philosophy**. Measured via DPMO, yield, and SPC.

3. **Product-Based View (1.5 marks):** Quality is a *precise and measurable variable* intrinsic to the product. Higher quality implies the use of better raw materials, tighter tolerances, and superior design — typically at higher cost.

**Importance for a Quality Manager (1.5 marks):**
- The user-based view prevents over-engineering; the manufacturing view prevents customer dissatisfaction through defects; the product-based view provides the engineering language for specifications.
- A holistic quality manager must **integrate all three** to balance customer satisfaction, operational efficiency, and engineering excellence.

**(b) Reliability Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 1000$ hours (2.5 marks):**

$$
R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2}
$$

$$
R(1000) \approx 0.8187
$$

Hence, the probability of survival up to 1000 hours is approximately **81.87%**.

**(ii) Mean Time To Failure (2 marks):**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Probability of survival beyond 2500 hours (2.5 marks):**

$$
R(2500) = e^{-0.0002 \times 2500} = e^{-0.5}
$$

$$
R(2500) \approx 0.6065
$$

Hence, the probability is approximately **60.65%**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Reliability with Durability** — Reliability is probability of failure-free operation; Durability is total useful life. Examiners specifically test this distinction. (Penalty: 2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5 sigma shift** in DPMO-to-sigma conversion. Many students compute raw Z = $\Phi^{-1}(1-p)$ and lose 1 mark for not applying the long-term drift correction.
> 3. **Omitting units in formulas** — MTBF must be in hours/days, DPMO must be in "defects per million." Unitless answers are penalized.
> 4. **Conflating Aesthetics with Perceived Quality** — Aesthetics is sensory (touch, look); Perceived Quality is image/reputation. This subtle distinction is a frequent 3-mark question.
> 5. **Writing one-line answers in Part A** — KTU Part A requires definition + example + measurement metric. Single sentences score 1 mark out of 3.
> 6. **Skipping the constant failure rate assumption** in reliability problems — Always state "assuming $\lambda$ is constant (useful life region)" before applying $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dimensions of Quality**

- **Quality is multidimensional**, not a single attribute. (Garvin, 1984)
- **Eight Dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
- **Three Perspectives:** User-based, Manufacturing-based, Product-based.
- **Reliability formula:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for constant failure rate; MTTF $= 1/\lambda$.
- **Availability formula:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ — combines reliability and serviceability.
- **DPMO formula:** $\text{DPMO} = (D / (U \times O)) \times 10^6$ — quantifies Conformance.
- **6 Sigma benchmark:** 3.4 DPMO; 1.5 sigma shift is the long-term drift correction.
- **Reliability vs. Durability:** Reliability = no failures; Durability = total useful life.
- **Conformance vs. Reliability:** Conformance = meets specs (binary); Reliability = performs over time (probabilistic).
- **Aesthetics vs. Perceived Quality:** Aesthetics = sensory; Perceived Quality = brand/reputation.
- **Reliability, Durability, Serviceability** are the three *time-dependent* dimensions.
- **Objective dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability.
- **Subjective dimensions:** Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
- **Engineering tools used:** SPC, FMEA, DMAIC, QFD, Weibull Analysis, Six Sigma, Benchmarking.
- **Customer Satisfaction** is the **convergent output** of all eight dimensions acting in parallel.
- **KTU favorite framing:** Always pair definition + measurement metric + industrial example in exam answers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra for Module 1:** "Quality is **multidimensional**; reliability is **time-dependent**; conformance is **specification-based**; aesthetics is **subjective**; and customer satisfaction is the **integrated outcome** of all eight dimensions."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts of Product and Service Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Product and Service Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Quality"?

In KTU's Quality Engineering and Management framework, **Quality** is formally defined as the **totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs**. This is the ISO 9000:2015 definition that KTU expects you to reproduce verbatim in your answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked "Define Quality" in your ESE, quoting the ISO 9000 definition followed by any one classical scholar's view (Juran/Deming/Crosby) fetches full marks.

### Intuitive Analogy: The "Restaurant Experience"

Imagine you order a Biryani from two different restaurants:
- **Restaurant A:** Tasty food, hot, exactly as described, delivered on time, polite staff, clean plates.
- **Restaurant B:** Cold food, delayed, looks different from the menu picture, rude waiter.

Even if the *ingredients* are identical, the **total experience** differs. That difference — the gap between what you *expected* and what you *got* — is essentially what Quality Engineering tries to measure, control, and improve.

The **product** (the food) has **product quality** (taste, temperature, presentation), and the **service** (delivery, staff behaviour, ambience) has **service quality**. Both together create customer satisfaction.

---

## 1.2 Classical Definitions by Quality Gurus

KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes these foundational definitions. Memorize at least **three** for short-answer questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Joseph M. Juran** defined quality as **"Fitness for Use"** — meaning the product/service should serve the customer's intended purpose without failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **W. Edwards Deming** defined quality in terms of **customer satisfaction** — a product is of good quality if it **continually meets or exceeds customer expectations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Philip B. Crosby** gave the bold definition: **"Quality is conformance to requirements"** (or "conformance to specifications"). He coined the phrase **"Do it right the first time (DIRFT)"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Genichi Taguchi** defined quality as **"Loss imparted to society from the time the product is shipped"** — meaning any deviation from target causes loss to the manufacturer, customer, and society.

> [!NOTE]
> **Walter A. Shewhart** (the father of statistical quality control) defined quality as the **result of many causes** — some assignable, some random — and emphasized reducing variation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Armand V. Feigenbaum** introduced **Total Quality Control (TQC)** — quality is everyone's responsibility, not just the QC department.

---

## 1.3 Five Views / Perspectives of Quality (David Garvin, 1984)

Garvin identified **five distinct perspectives** from which quality can be viewed. This is a **very high-yield topic** for KTU Part B questions.

| # | Perspective | Core Idea | Simple Example |
|---|-------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Transcendental View** | Quality is something you recognize but cannot define precisely — excellence, innate goodness | Recognising a luxury Rolex watch vs. a cheap copy |
| 2 | **Product-Based View** | Quality is a **precise and measurable variable** — differences in quality reflect differences in the quantity of some desired ingredient or attribute | A higher-octane petrol is "higher quality" than regular |
| 3 | **User-Based View** | Quality lies in the **eyes of the customer** — fitness for intended use, personal preference | A teenager prefers iPhone; an elderly person prefers simple Nokia |
| 4 | **Manufacturing-Based View** | Quality is **conformance to specifications and standards** — the engineering view | A bolt must have exactly 10 mm diameter ± 0.05 mm |
| 5 | **Value-Based View** | Quality is the **degree of excellence at an acceptable price** — cost-performance tradeoff | Maruti Alto vs. Maruti Swift |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** A 7-mark question often asks: *"Explain the various approaches/perspectives to define quality."* Write Garvin's five views with **one example each** and you score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Product Quality

Garvin later expanded his framework into **eight measurable dimensions** that allow companies to assess product quality strategically. This is a **guaranteed KTU question** topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Product Quality:**

### 1. Performance
- The primary operating characteristics of a product.
- Example: A car's mileage, top speed, acceleration (0-100 kmph).

### 2. Features
- Secondary, often "nice-to-have" characteristics that supplement basic functioning.
- Example: Power windows, sunroof, touchscreen infotainment in a car.

### 3. Reliability
- The probability of a product performing its intended function **without failure** over a specified period of time under stated conditions.
- Example: A laptop working without breakdown for 3 years.

### 4. Conformance
- The degree to which a product's design and operating characteristics **match established standards**.
- Example: An IS 2062 grade steel plate conforming to Bureau of Indian Standards.

### 5. Durability
- A measure of **product life** — the amount of use one gets from a product before it deteriorates.
- Example: A washing machine lasting 10 years vs. 5 years.

### 6. Serviceability
- The **speed, courtesy, and competence** with which a product is repaired.
- Example: Apple's service centres replacing a phone screen in 1 hour.

### 7. Aesthetics
- The **subjective dimension** — how a product looks, feels, sounds, smells, or tastes.
- Example: The look and feel of a Rolex vs. a Casio watch.

### 8. Perceived Quality
- The **reputation or brand image** — what the customer perceives (often indirectly) based on brand, advertising, or word-of-mouth.
- Example: Trust in Toyota or Samsung brand reliability.

---

## 2.2 Dimensions of Service Quality — The SERVQUAL Model

Services are **intangible, heterogeneous, inseparable, and perishable (IHIP)**. To measure service quality, **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1985, 1988)** developed the **SERVQUAL model**, which is **high-priority for KTU**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SERVQUAL Gap Score Formula:**
>
> $$SQ = \text{Perception} - \text{Expectation}$$
>
> Where **SQ** is Service Quality score. A **negative** SQ means quality gap exists.

### The Five Dimensions (RATER Framework)

| Dimension | Meaning | Real-World Example |
|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| **R – Reliability** | Ability to perform the promised service **dependably and accurately** | FedEx delivers within 24 hours as promised |
| **A – Assurance** | Knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire **trust and confidence** | A bank manager explaining FD schemes clearly |
| **T – Tangibles** | Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance, communication materials | Clean hospital, neat uniform of nurses |
| **E – Empathy** | Caring, **individualized attention** provided to customers | A doctor spending 15 minutes patiently with a patient |
| **R – Responsiveness** | Willingness to help customers and provide **prompt service** | A waiter bringing the bill within 30 seconds of request |

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5 Gaps in SERVQUAL (Valuable for 14-mark questions):**
>
> - **Gap 1:** Customer expectations vs. Management perceptions
> - **Gap 2:** Management perceptions vs. Service quality specifications
> - **Gap 3:** Service quality specifications vs. Service delivery
> - **Gap 4:** Service delivery vs. External communications
> - **Gap 5:** Expected service vs. Perceived service (the **key gap** that customers experience)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Statement | Application |
|-------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| **ISO 9000 Definition** | Quality = Totality of characteristics satisfying needs | Use in any definition question |
| **Juran's Definition** | Quality = Fitness for Use | Engineering design context |
| **Crosby's Definition** | Quality = Conformance to Requirements | Manufacturing context |
| **Taguchi's Loss Function** | $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ where $T$ is target, $x$ is actual, $k$ is constant | Quality loss to society |
| **SERVQUAL Score** | $SQ_i = \sum_{j=1}^{22}(P_{ij} - E_{ij})$ | 22 items across 5 dimensions |
| **Reliability** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (exponential distribution) | Probability of survival up to time $t$ |
| **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures |
| **Quality Cost Ratio** | $P:A:E = 4:1$ (Crosby) | Prevention:Appraisal:Failure costs |

---

## 2.4 Product vs. Service Quality — The Core Comparison

| Attribute | Product | Service |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Tangibility** | Tangible, can be touched/seen | Intangible, felt through experience |
| **Storage** | Can be stored in inventory | Cannot be stored — perishable |
| **Variability** | Standardized via specs | Highly variable — depends on provider |
| **Customer Involvement** | Low (mostly at purchase) | High (throughout delivery) |
| **Measurement** | Easy (specs, tolerances) | Difficult (subjective satisfaction) |
| **Correction of Defects** | Rework possible before delivery | Real-time; defect occurs at delivery |
| **Evaluation** | Before purchase | Often only after consumption |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write "services are intangible, so quality cannot be measured." This is wrong. KTU wants you to say: **service quality is measured indirectly through SERVQUAL dimensions** (tangibles, reliability, etc.) and through customer satisfaction surveys.

---

## 2.5 The Goods–Services Continuum

Not all offerings are purely goods or purely services. They exist on a continuum:

$$\text{Pure Goods} \longleftrightarrow \text{Mixed Offerings} \longleftrightarrow \text{Pure Services}$$

- **Salt, sugar, coal** → Pure goods (no service component)
- **Fast-food restaurant** → Goods-dominant (food + minimal service)
- **Software + installation + training** → Mixed offering
- **Education, healthcare, consulting** → Pure services

> [!NOTE]
> As you move from pure goods to pure services, **customer participation increases**, and **quality control becomes harder**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **analytical breakdowns** that KTU examiners expect when you compare, contrast, or apply quality concepts.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Garvin's 8 Dimensions Applied to a Smartphone

| Dimension | Application to a Smartphone (e.g., iPhone 15) | Customer's View |
|-----------|---------------------------------------------|-----------------|
| Performance | Processor speed (A17 chip), camera megapixels, battery life | "Does it run apps smoothly?" |
| Features | Face ID, Dynamic Island, satellite connectivity | "What extras does it offer?" |
| Reliability | Probability of working without breakdown for 3 years | "Will it last?" |
| Conformance | Meets BIS/RoHS/CE certification standards | "Is it certified?" |
| Durability | Water-resistance rating (IP68), drop-test results | "How long will it survive?" |
| Serviceability | Apple Care, no. of service centres, turnaround time | "Can it be fixed easily?" |
| Aesthetics | Sleek titanium finish, slim bezel, premium feel | "Does it look/feel good?" |
| Perceived Quality | Apple's brand reputation globally | "Is it a status symbol?" |

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this matrix in your KTU answer:** If a 14-mark question asks "Explain Garvin's 8 dimensions of product quality with an example," use **one product** (like a smartphone or car) and explain all 8 dimensions — this demonstrates applied understanding.

---

## 3.2 SERVQUAL Score Calculation — Analytical Walkthrough

Assume a hospital conducts a SERVQUAL survey on **22 items** rated on a **7-point Likert scale** (1 = strongly disagree, 7 = strongly agree).

**Step 1:** For each of the 22 items, record the customer's **Perception (P)** and **Expectation (E)**.

**Step 2:** Compute the gap for each item:

$$G_i = P_i - E_i$$

**Step 3:** Sum the gaps within each dimension to get dimension-wise score:

$$SQ_{\text{Reliability}} = \sum_{i \in \text{R}} (P_i - E_i)$$

**Step 4:** Compute the overall SERVQUAL score:

$$SQ_{\text{Total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{22} (P_i - E_i)$$

**Numerical Example:**

A restaurant surveys 5 dimensions (simplified to 5 items). The data is:

| Dimension | Expectation $\bar{E}$ | Perception $\bar{P}$ | Gap $(P - E)$ |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Reliability | 6 | 4 | $-2$ |
| Assurance | 6 | 5 | $-1$ |
| Tangibles | 6 | 5 | $-1$ |
| Empathy | 6 | 3 | $-3$ |
| Responsiveness | 6 | 4 | $-2$ |

**Computation of Total Gap:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SQ_{\text{Total}} &= (-2) + (-1) + (-1) + (-3) + (-2) \\
&= -9
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Negative total score indicates a **service quality gap**.
- **Empathy** ($SQ = -3$) is the weakest area — the hospital must train staff in patient-centred care.
- **Assurance** and **Tangibles** ($SQ = -1$) are acceptable but improvable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always end such numerical answers with a **prescriptive recommendation** (what should management do). Examiners reward managerial insight, not just arithmetic.

---

## 3.3 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function — Worked Derivation

Taguchi argued that any deviation from the **target value** causes loss to society — even if the product is within specification limits.

**Step 1:** Let $T$ be the target value of a quality characteristic, and $x$ be the actual value delivered.

**Step 2:** The deviation is $\Delta = x - T$.

**Step 3:** Taguchi's loss function (quadratic form):

$$L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^2$$

Where:
- $L(x)$ = Loss to society (in monetary terms, e.g., ₹)
- $k$ = Proportionality constant (depends on product)
- $T$ = Target value
- $x$ = Actual value achieved

**Step 4:** To find $k$, use the specification limit. Suppose at the tolerance limit $x = T \pm \Delta_0$, the loss equals the cost of replacing the product $A_0$:

$$A_0 = k \cdot \Delta_0^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

**Step 5:** Final loss function:

$$L(x) = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \cdot (x - T)^2$$

**Numerical Example:**

A resistor is rated $100 \Omega$ (target $T = 100$). If the resistance is **$102 \Omega$**, and the specification limit is $\Delta_0 = 5 \Omega$ (i.e., $95$ to $105 \Omega$ allowed), and replacement cost $A_0 = ₹200$:

**Step 6:** Calculate $k$:

$$\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \\
&= \frac{200}{5^2} \\
&= \frac{200}{25} \\
&= 8 \text{ ₹ per } \Omega^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7:** Calculate the loss:

$$\begin{aligned}
L(102) &= 8 \cdot (102 - 100)^2 \\
&= 8 \cdot (2)^2 \\
&= 8 \cdot 4 \\
&= ₹32
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Even though $102 \Omega$ is **within spec**, the customer still loses ₹32 in expected performance/utility — a hidden cost Taguchi insists must be accounted for.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Taguchi's revolutionary insight: **"The customer does not care about specifications. They care about the target."** This drives the philosophy of **Robust Design**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Crosby's "Zero Defects" vs. Deming's "Continuous Improvement"

| Aspect | Crosby (Zero Defects) | Deming (PDCA / Kaizen) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Philosophy** | Absolute prevention; "do it right the first time" | Continuous, iterative improvement |
| **Standards** | Compliance with requirements | Reduce variation continuously |
| **Quality Cost** | Cost of non-conformance = cost of poor quality | Cost of poor quality + cost of prevention |
| **Mindset** | Right first time, every time | Improve a little every day |
| **Implementation** | Quality management system, training | PDCA cycle, statistical tools |
| **Best Suited For** | Manufacturing conformance | Process-oriented, service environments |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Crosby's famous quote is **"Quality is free"** — meaning the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of rework/scrap.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Product Quality (Block Architecture)

This Mermaid diagram shows how Garvin's dimensions interact to form the customer's perception of product quality.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["PRODUCT QUALITY DIMENSIONS"]
    nodeB["PERFORMANCE\nPrimary operating characteristics"]
    nodeC["FEATURES\nExtra characteristics beyond basic"]
    nodeD["RELIABILITY\nProbability of failure-free operation"]
    nodeE["CONFORMANCE\nMatch with standards and specs"]
    nodeF["DURABILITY\nUseful life before deterioration"]
    nodeG["SERVICEABILITY\nEase and speed of repair"]
    nodeH["AESTHETICS\nLook feel sound taste smell"]
    nodeI["PERCEIVED QUALITY\nBrand reputation and image"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeA --> nodeH
    nodeA --> nodeI

    nodeB --> nodeJ["CUSTOMER\nPERCEIVED\nVALUE"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef dim fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class nodeA root
    class nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG,nodeH,nodeI dim
    class nodeJ result
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram: SERVQUAL — The 5 Gaps Model

This flowchart shows the **5 gaps** that determine service quality from a management perspective.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["CUSTOMER\nEXPECTATIONS"]
    nodeB["Gap 1\nKnowledge Gap\nManagement does not know\nwhat customers expect"]
    nodeC["MANAGEMENT\nPERCEPTIONS"]
    nodeD["Gap 2\nStandards Gap\nWrong or missing\nservice standards"]
    nodeE["SERVICE QUALITY\nSPECIFICATIONS"]
    nodeF["Gap 3\nDelivery Gap\nService performance\ndoes not meet specs"]
    nodeG["SERVICE\nDELIVERY"]
    nodeH["Gap 4\nCommunication Gap\nExternal communications\noverpromise"]
    nodeI["EXTERNAL\nCOMMUNICATIONS"]
    nodeJ["Gap 5\nService Quality Gap\nExpected minus Perceived"]
    nodeK["CUSTOMER\nPERCEPTION OF SERVICE"]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeA -.-> nodeJ
    nodeK -.-> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeL["REDUCTION OF GAPS\nIS THE GOAL OF\nSERVICE QUALITY\nMANAGEMENT"]

    classDef gap fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c00000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#deebf7,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef goal fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#385723,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class nodeB,nodeD,nodeF,nodeH,nodeJ gap
    class nodeA,nodeC,nodeE,nodeG,nodeI,nodeK step
    class nodeL goal
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram: Goods–Services Continuum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["PURE GOODS\nSalt Coal Sugar"]
    nodeB["GOODS WITH\nSERVICES\nCars with warranty"]
    nodeC["HYBRID\nOFFERINGS\nRestaurants\nFast food"]
    nodeD["SERVICES WITH\nMINOR GOODS\nAirline with food"]
    nodeE["PURE SERVICES\nTeaching Healthcare\nConsulting"]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE

    classDef pureG fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pureS fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef hybrid fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeA pureG
    class nodeE pureS
    class nodeB,nodeC,nodeD hybrid
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram: Taguchi's Quality Loss Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["TARGET VALUE T\nNominal specification"]
    nodeB["LOSS FUNCTION L of x equals k times x minus T squared"]
    nodeC["As deviation increases\nloss increases quadratically"]
    nodeD["Tolerance limit delta zero"]
    nodeE["At delta zero loss equals A zero\nCost of replacement"]
    nodeF["Even within specs\nloss is non-zero"]
    nodeG["Customer and society pay\nthe hidden quality cost"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG

    classDef core fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef imp fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeA,nodeB core
    class nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG imp
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Quality. State any two classical definitions of quality with their proponents. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Quality, as per ISO 9000:2015, is the **"totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs."**

Two classical definitions:

**(i) Joseph M. Juran — "Fitness for Use":** Quality means the product/service should be capable of fulfilling the customer's intended purpose under actual operating conditions without failure.

**(ii) Philip B. Crosby — "Conformance to Requirements":** Quality is achieved when the product is manufactured strictly according to the specified standards, with the philosophy of *"Do It Right The First Time (DIRFT)."*

---

### Question 2: List any three differences between product quality and service quality. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Aspect | Product Quality | Service Quality |
|-------|--------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Tangibility | Tangible, can be touched | Intangible, felt through experience |
| 2 | Storage | Can be inventoried | Cannot be stored — perishable |
| 3 | Measurement | Easy via specifications | Difficult — measured via SERVQUAL |
| 4 | Customer Involvement | Low (at purchase) | High (throughout) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `Explain in detail Garvin's eight dimensions of product quality. Apply these to evaluate the quality of a smartphone.` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Product Quality (7 Marks)
