David A. Garvin (1987) proposed eight dimensions that allow a structured assessment of product quality:

1. **Performance:** Primary operating characteristics that fulfill the product's core function. It is the *baseline* against which competing products are judged.

2. **Features:** Secondary characteristics that supplement the basic functioning. They differentiate a product from competitors and add value.

3. **Reliability:** The probability that a product will perform its intended function **without failure** over a specified period under stated conditions. It builds customer trust.

4. **Conformance:** The degree to which the product's design and operational characteristics meet **established standards** (e.g., BIS, ISO, ASTM).

5. **Durability:** The length of time a product lasts before it physically deteriorates. It includes both technical life and economic life.

6. **Serviceability:** The **speed, competence, and courtesy** of repair and maintenance. Easily serviceable products reduce downtime and customer frustration.

7. **Aesthetics:** Subjective sensory characteristics — how the product **looks, feels, sounds, smells, or tastes**. It is highly personal.

8. **Perceived Quality:** Indirectly perceived quality, based on **brand name, reputation, advertising, and word-of-mouth** rather than objective attributes.

#### (b) Application to a Smartphone (7 Marks)

Consider a smartphone (e.g., iPhone 15 / Samsung Galaxy S24):

| Dimension | Application |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Performance** | Processor speed (A17 Bionic / Snapdragon 8 Gen 3), camera resolution, battery backup |
| **Features** | Face ID, 5G connectivity, satellite SOS, Dynamic Island |
| **Reliability** | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) — typically 3–5 years for premium brands |
| **Conformance** | BIS certification (India), CE (Europe), FCC (USA), IP68 water-resistance rating |
| **Durability** | Drop test results, scratch-resistant Ceramic Shield glass, expected life 5+ years |
| **Serviceability** | Apple has 200+ service centres in India; Samsung offers doorstep pickup |
| **Aesthetics** | Titanium frame, slim bezel, premium matte finish |
| **Perceived Quality** | Brand reputation of Apple/Samsung = high perceived quality |

**Conclusion:** A smartphone manufacturer must balance all 8 dimensions. A phone with great performance but poor serviceability (e.g., a Chinese brand with no Indian service network) loses customer loyalty — showing that **all 8 dimensions matter**, not just performance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `Explain the SERVQUAL model for measuring service quality. Describe the five dimensions and the five gaps identified in the model.` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) SERVQUAL Model and Five Dimensions (7 Marks)

The **SERVQUAL** (Service Quality) model was developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1985, 1988)**. It measures service quality using a 22-item questionnaire across **five dimensions**, based on the formula:

$$SQ = \text{Perception (P)} - \text{Expectation (E)}$$

A negative score indicates a service gap.

**Five Dimensions (RATER):**

1. **Reliability:** Ability to perform the promised service **dependably and accurately**. (E.g., courier delivers within 24 hrs.)
2. **Assurance:** Knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire **trust and confidence**. (E.g., bank manager explains FD clearly.)
3. **Tangibles:** Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance, and communication materials. (E.g., clean hospital, neat staff uniform.)
4. **Empathy:** Caring, **individualized attention** provided to customers. (E.g., doctor giving personal attention to a patient.)
5. **Responsiveness:** Willingness to help customers and provide **prompt service**. (E.g., waiter bringing bill within 30 seconds.)

#### (b) The Five Gaps in SERVQUAL (7 Marks)

The SERVQUAL framework identifies **five gaps** that explain why service quality fails:

| Gap | Description |
|-----|-------------|
| **Gap 1: Knowledge Gap** | Difference between customer expectations and management's perception of those expectations. |
| **Gap 2: Standards Gap** | Difference between management perceptions and the actual service quality specifications set. |
| **Gap 3: Delivery Gap** | Difference between service specifications and actual service performance. |
| **Gap 4: Communication Gap** | Difference between service delivery and what is promised in external communications. |
| **Gap 5: Service Quality Gap** | The **final gap** between expected service and perceived service. This is what the customer actually experiences. |

**Formulaically:**

$$SQ_{5} = SQ_{1} + SQ_{2} + SQ_{3} + SQ_{4}$$

That is, the customer-observed Gap 5 is the **sum of the four upstream gaps**. To close Gap 5, an organization must systematically close Gaps 1 to 4.

**Conclusion:** SERVQUAL is widely used by service industries (banking, healthcare, hospitality) to diagnose service weaknesses. The RATER dimensions guide data collection, and the Gap model guides root-cause analysis.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Garvin's "5 views" with "8 dimensions":** They are *related but different*. 5 views are *philosophical perspectives*; 8 dimensions are *measurable attributes*. Don't merge them in your answer.
> 2. **Writing SERVQUAL dimensions as just "T-A-R-E-R" without explaining:** KTU expects the **full word + meaning + one example**. Memorizing acronyms alone will lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting Gap 5 is the *sum* of Gaps 1–4:** This additive relationship is a high-yield point. Mentioning it earns bonus marks.
> 4. **Taguchi's Loss Function:** Students often write the formula but skip the **derivation of $k$**. Examiners specifically test whether you can compute $k$ from $\Delta_0$ and $A_0$.
> 5. **Not writing any example for Juran/Crosby/Deming:** Pure text-bookish definitions without real-world anchoring fetch only 2 out of 3 marks in Part A.
> 6. **Mixing Deming's PDCA with Crosby's Zero Defects:** They are different philosophies. PDCA = continuous improvement; Zero Defects = right first time. Don't blend them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Concepts of Product and Service Quality**

- [ ] **ISO 9000 definition** of quality = "totality of characteristics... satisfy stated and implied needs."
- [ ] **Juran** = Fitness for Use; **Crosby** = Conformance to Requirements + DIRFT + "Quality is Free"; **Deming** = Customer satisfaction + PDCA; **Taguchi** = Loss to society; **Feigenbaum** = Total Quality Control.
- [ ] **Garvin's 5 Views:** Transcendental, Product-based, User-based, Manufacturing-based, Value-based.
- [ ] **Garvin's 8 Dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
- [ ] **SERVQUAL** developed by Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry. Score = Perception − Expectation.
- [ ] **RATER dimensions:** Reliability, Assurance, Tangibles, Empathy, Responsiveness.
- [ ] **5 Gaps in SERVQUAL:** Knowledge Gap, Standards Gap, Delivery Gap, Communication Gap, Service Quality Gap.
- [ ] **Gap 5 = Gap 1 + Gap 2 + Gap 3 + Gap 4** (additive relationship).
- [ ] **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$; $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$.
- [ ] **IHIP characteristics of services:** Intangible, Heterogeneous, Inseparable, Perishable.
- [ ] **Goods–Services Continuum:** As you move from pure goods to pure services, customer participation and quality control difficulty both **increase**.
- [ ] **Crosby's cost ratio (P:A:E):** Prevention : Appraisal : Failure = **4 : 1** (optimal allocation).
- [ ] **Reliability formula:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; **MTBF** = $1/\lambda$.
- [ ] **Quality is everyone's responsibility** — Feigenbaum's TQC principle.
- [ ] Always remember to give **at least one real-world example** in every long-answer question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evolution of Quality Management
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evolution of Quality Management

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Quality"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (ISO 9000:2015):**
> **Quality** is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements. An "object" can be a product, service, process, person, or system.

In simpler board-exam language, **Quality = Conformance to Requirements + Fitness for Use.** It is not "luxury"; it is the *measurable* ability of a product or service to satisfy the *stated and implied needs* of the customer.

## 1.2 What is "Quality Management"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Management (QM)** is the coordinated activities that direct and control an organization with regard to quality. It includes **Quality Planning, Quality Control (QC), Quality Assurance (QA), and Quality Improvement (QI)** — all four pillars of the classic Juran Trilogy extended by ISO.

## 1.3 What does "Evolution" mean here?

The **Evolution of Quality Management** refers to the *historical progression* of human thinking and practice regarding quality — from a narrow, inspection-based mindset (catching defects at the end of production) to a *holistic, system-wide, customer-centric, and data-driven philosophy* that governs entire enterprises today.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of Quality Management as the **growth of a doctor’s approach to patient care**:

- **Ancient Stage (Inspection):** The doctor only *examines* the patient at the end and declares them sick or healthy. No prevention.
- **Quality Control Stage:** The doctor *monitors* vital signs regularly and prescribes corrective medicine.
- **Quality Assurance Stage:** The doctor designs a *whole lifestyle plan* (diet, exercise) to prevent disease.
- **TQM / Modern Stage:** The *entire family and community* is involved — clean water, sanitation, mental health, periodic check-ups. The system itself is engineered for health.

Just as medicine evolved from *symptom-checking* to *preventive and public health*, quality evolved from *end-of-line inspection* to *systemic, strategic, organization-wide excellence*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Bodies & Gurus Mentioned in KTU Syllabus:**
> - **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization) — Geneva, founded 1947
> - **ASQ** (American Society for Quality)
> - **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards)
> - Gurus: **Shewhart, Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum, Taguchi, Imai**

## 1.5 Why Study the Evolution?

Understanding the evolution helps engineers:
1. Diagnose *why* current practices (e.g., ISO 9001, Six Sigma, Lean) exist.
2. Avoid repeating obsolete mistakes (e.g., 100% inspection as the only quality tool).
3. Map modern tools to their philosophical roots — useful in KTU essay questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Eras (Stages) of Quality Management

Modern quality historians generally divide the evolution into **four distinct eras**. This is the most exam-relevant framework for KTU 2024.

### Stage 1 — Inspection Era (Pre-1900s to ~1930)
- **Focus:** Detect defective products *after* they are made.
- **Tool:** Visual/sensory inspection, gauging, sorting (Good vs. Bad).
- **Limitation:** No prevention; cost of rework & scrap is high.
- **Origin:** Craft guilds, then factory assembly lines (e.g., Henry Ford, Frederick Taylor's scientific management).
- **Quality responsibility:** Inspector (separate from worker).

### Stage 2 — Quality Control (QC) Era (1930 – 1950)
- **Focus:** Control the *process* using statistical methods.
- **Key innovation:** **Walter A. Shewhart** (Bell Labs, 1924) developed the **Control Chart** — the birth of **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**.
- **Quality responsibility:** Shared between operator and a small quality department.
- **Spread:** Adopted heavily during **World War II** (US War Department mandated SPC for munitions suppliers — *"The Yellow Book"* MIL-STD).
- **Concept shift:** Quality became *measurable* using **Variation** analysis.

### Stage 3 — Quality Assurance (QA) Era (1950 – 1979)
- **Focus:** Build quality *into the product* by designing robust systems.
- **Key contributors:**
  - **W. Edwards Deming** — 14 Points for Management, PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act / Shewhart Cycle), System of Profound Knowledge.
  - **Joseph M. Juran** — Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement (the **Juran Trilogy**); Fitness-for-Use definition.
  - **Armand V. Feigenbaum** — Coined **Total Quality Control (TQC)** in 1956 (note: this is the *parent* idea of TQM, not TQM itself).
  - **Kaoru Ishikawa** — Developed the **Quality Circle** concept (1962, Japan) and the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** for cause-effect analysis.
- **Quality responsibility:** Entire organization — but the *system* is designed by management.

### Stage 4 — Strategic / Total Quality Management Era (1979 – Present)
- **Focus:** Customer satisfaction, continuous improvement, strategic alignment.
- **Milestones:**
  - **1979 — Philip Crosby** publishes *"Quality is Free"*; introduces **Zero Defects** and the **Quality Cost Maturity Grid** (5 stages: Uncertainty → Awakening → Enlightenment → Wisdom → Certainty).
  - **1980s — Genichi Taguchi** introduces **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** — quality is not binary (good/bad) but a *continuous loss* to society from the moment a product deviates from target.
  - **1986 — Motorola** launches **Six Sigma** under Bob Galvin and Bill Smith.
  - **1987 — ISO 9000** standards published (revised 1994, 2000, 2008, 2015).
  - **1991 — James Womack & Daniel Jones** publish *"The Machine That Changed the World"* — popularizes **Lean Manufacturing** (originated from Toyota Production System, **Taiichi Ohno**).
  - **1990s — TQM** (Total Quality Management) becomes a global movement.
  - **1995 — Jeff Sutherland / John Scudder** — **Agile** methodology emerges (software quality context).

## 2.2 Quality Gurus — One-Line Quick Recall

| Guru | Key Contribution | Year | KTU Buzzword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Walter A. Shewhart** | Control Chart, SPC | 1924 | Father of Statistical Quality Control |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points, PDCA Cycle | 1950 (Japan lecture) | Father of Modern Quality Movement |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Juran Trilogy, Fitness-for-Use | 1951 (Japan lecture) | Human Side of Quality |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control (TQC) | 1956 | Total Cost of Quality concept |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality Circles, Fishbone Diagram | 1962 | Father of Japanese Quality Circles |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Quality Loss Function, Robust Design | 1980s | Loss to Society |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Zero Defects, Quality is Free | 1979 | Quality is Conformance, Not Elegance |
| **Taiichi Ohno** | Toyota Production System (Lean) | 1950s–80s | Just-in-Time, Kaizen |
| **Masaaki Imai** | Kaizen (continuous improvement) | 1986 | Kaizen = Change for the Better |
| **Bill Smith (Motorola)** | Six Sigma Methodology | 1986 | 3.4 DPMO |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **concise cheat-sheet** of numerical concepts students must know for KTU 2024 numerical/short-answer questions.

$$
\text{Defect Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units Produced}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Yield} = \frac{\text{Number of Good Units}}{\text{Number of Units Started}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ROL (Rolled Throughput Yield)} = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times \ldots \times Y_n
$$

$$
\text{Sigma Level of Process} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{2 \times \sigma}
$$

$$
\text{Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\text{Taguchi Quality Loss} \; L(x) = k \times (x - T)^{2}
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Quality (CoQ)} = P_{\text{app}} + P_{\text{prev}} + A_{\text{int}} + A_{\text{ext}}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Category |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{\text{app}}$ | Prevention Cost (appraisal-related prevention) | Cost of Conformance (CoC) |
| $P_{\text{prev}}$ | Prevention Cost (true prevention) | Cost of Conformance (CoC) |
| $A_{\text{int}}$ | Internal Failure Cost | Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC) |
| $A_{\text{ext}}$ | External Failure Cost | Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC) |
| $x$ | Actual quality characteristic value | Taguchi L(x) |
| $T$ | Target value | Taguchi L(x) |
| $k$ | Loss coefficient (cost constant) | Taguchi L(x) |
| $\sigma$ | Standard deviation of process | Sigma Level |
| $Y_i$ | Yield of process step $i$ | ROL |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing plants:** Modern quality management (Six Sigma + Lean + ISO 9001) is mandatory for global supply chains (automotive, aerospace, pharma).
- **Software industry:** CMMI, Agile, DevOps are direct descendants of TQM and Deming's system thinking.
- **Service sector:** Banks, hospitals, and airlines apply SERVQUAL and TQM for customer satisfaction.
- **Engineer's role:** Even entry-level engineers use PDCA, control charts, and fishbone diagrams in process improvement projects (Six Sigma Green Belt, Kaizen events).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Stage-by-Stage Logical Progression

Let us now *derive* the evolution as a *logical chain of problem → solution → new problem → better solution*. This is the depth KTU ESE questions demand.

### Step 1 — The Inspection Era (Pre-1930)
- **Problem:** Mass production (Ford assembly line, 1913) created large volumes but also large defect rates.
- **Solution attempted:** Hire inspectors at the end of the line to *sort* good from bad.
- **Logical flaw exposed:** 100% inspection is *expensive, slow, error-prone, and impossible* for many modern products. Worse, it provides *no feedback* to prevent future defects.
- **Conceptual quote (Crosby):** *"Inspection does not improve quality; it only sorts the product."*

### Step 2 — Birth of Statistical Quality Control (1930s)
- **Problem recognized:** Defects are not random; they have *assignable causes* and *common causes*. Variation is the enemy.
- **Solution by Shewhart:** Use statistical sampling and control charts. Distinguish *voice of the process* (common cause) from *voice of the customer* (specification).
- **Wartime scale-up:** During WWII, the US military forced suppliers to apply SPC, producing the **MIL-Q-9858** standard (1963) and the **Yellow Book** (1950).
- **New Problem:** Statistical tools are *technical*; they don't address *management's responsibility* for quality.

### Step 3 — Quality Assurance Era — Management Joins In (1950s)
- **Deming's 14 Points** explicitly tell *executives* to drive quality. He lectured to Japanese top management in 1950 (invited by JUSE — Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers). Japan's industrial rise followed.
- **Juran's Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement) gave a *managerial roadmap*.
- **Feigenbaum's TQC (1956)** expanded responsibility to *all departments* — marketing, finance, production, service.
- **Ishikawa's Quality Circles (1962)** empowered *workers* to solve problems in small groups.
- **New Problem:** Even with good systems, *external failure costs* (warranty, recall) remain, and *cultural change* is hard.

### Step 4 — Strategic TQM & Continuous Improvement (1980s onward)
- **Crosby's "Quality is Free" (1979):** Argues prevention cost < failure cost. Coined **Zero Defects**.
- **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (1980s):** Reframes quality as *any deviation* from target causes loss — even within spec.
- **Six Sigma at Motorola (1986):** Drive process variation so low that only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** occur — corresponding to a $\pm 6\sigma$ process shift window.
- **Lean Manufacturing (TPS popularized 1990):** Eliminate *muda* (waste), *mura* (unevenness), *muri* (overburden).
- **ISO 9000 (1987 → 2015):** Globally harmonized *certifiable* quality management system standard.
- **Modern extensions:** Lean Six Sigma, Agile, Industry 4.0, Digital Quality Management Systems (dQMS), AI-driven SPC.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for 14-Mark Questions)

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **single most important table** for KTU 14-mark essay questions. Memorize the column headers and contrasting language.

| Dimension | **Inspection** | **Quality Control (QC)** | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | **Total Quality Management (TQM)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Era** | Pre-1930 | 1930 – 1950 | 1950 – 1979 | 1979 – Present |
| **Primary Focus** | Detect defects | Control process variation | Build quality into the system | Satisfy customer, continuous improvement |
| **Approach** | Reactive | Statistical | Systems-based | Strategic & holistic |
| **Tools** | Gauges, sorting | Control charts, sampling, SPC | System audits, procedures, process design | TQM toolbox: QFD, FMEA, SPC, Kaizen, 5S, Six Sigma |
| **Responsible Person** | Inspector | Operator + QC Dept | All functions (with management) | Everyone in the organization |
| **Cost Priority** | High failure cost | Reduced inspection, controlled scrap | Lower failure cost | Lowest total cost of quality |
| **Philosophy** | Sort good vs. bad | Reduce variation | Prevention over detection | Customer-driven continuous improvement |
| **Customer Role** | None | Minimal | Considered | Central — voice of customer (VOC) |
| **Example Action** | Reject bad units | Plot $\bar{X}$ & R chart | Write & follow SOPs, ISO 9001 | Cross-functional Kaizen projects, VOC surveys |
| **Limitation** | Costly, no prevention | Statistical skills needed | Bureaucracy risk | Cultural change is slow |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration — Taguchi's Quality Loss

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU 2-mark or 7-mark sub-part** type question.

**Problem:** A bolt's target diameter is $T = 10.00$ mm. The loss coefficient is $k = \text{Rs. } 500 \text{ per mm}^{2}$ per unit. Two suppliers deliver bolts:
- Supplier A: average diameter $x_A = 10.02$ mm
- Supplier B: average diameter $x_B = 9.85$ mm

Both are within typical $\pm 0.05$ mm spec. Which supplier causes more societal loss per unit? Why does this illustrate the evolution of quality thinking?

**Step-by-step Solution:**

$$
L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^{2}
$$

**For Supplier A:**

$$
L_A = 500 \times (10.02 - 10.00)^{2} = 500 \times (0.02)^{2} = 500 \times 0.0004 = \text{Rs. } 0.20
$$

**For Supplier B:**

$$
L_B = 500 \times (9.85 - 10.00)^{2} = 500 \times (-0.15)^{2} = 500 \times 0.0225 = \text{Rs. } 11.25
$$

$$
\frac{L_B}{L_A} = \frac{11.25}{0.20} = 56.25
$$

**Interpretation (key KTU point):**
Although both suppliers are "in spec," Supplier B causes **56.25 times more loss per unit** than Supplier A. The *traditional* Inspection-era mindset would treat both as "acceptable." The *Taguchi/Modern* mindset says **on-target is not enough; we must minimize deviation from target** — this is the philosophical leap of the modern quality era.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Six Sigma DPMO

**Problem:** In a process producing 12,000 units over 2 weeks, 1 unit failed inspection. The product has 4 critical-to-quality (CTQ) characteristics. Compute DPMO and the equivalent Sigma Level.

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{6}}{12{,}000 \times 4}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{48{,}000} \approx 20.83 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Sigma Level (using standard Motorola conversion table):** $\approx 5.6\sigma$.

This shows how Six Sigma quantifies quality progress in a way that inspection-era tools could not.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timeline of Quality Evolution (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Era 1: Inspection Era (Pre-1930)"] --> B["Era 2: Quality Control Era (1930-1950)"]
    B --> C["Era 3: Quality Assurance Era (1950-1979)"]
    C --> D["Era 4: TQM & Strategic Era (1979-Present)"]
    A -- "Shewhart, Taylor, Ford" --> B
    B -- "Deming, Juran" --> C
    C -- "Crosby, Taguchi, Ishikawa" --> D
    A1["Tools: Gauges, Sorting, 100% Check"] --- A
    B1["Tools: Control Charts, SPC, Sampling"] --- B
    C1["Tools: SOPs, Audits, System Design, ISO 9001"] --- C
    D1["Tools: TQM, Six Sigma, Lean, Kaizen, QFD, FMEA"] --- D
```

## 4.2 The Quality Management Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QP["Quality Planning"] --> QM["Quality Management"]
    QC["Quality Control"] --> QM
    QA["Quality Assurance"] --> QM
    QI["Quality Improvement"] --> QM
    QM --> OUT["Customer Satisfaction + Organisational Excellence"]
    QC1["Statistical Process Control"] --- QC
    QA1["Process Audits, ISO 9001, Documentation"] --- QA
    QI1["Kaizen, PDCA, Six Sigma DMAIC"] --- QI
    QP1["Quality Function Deployment, Voice of Customer"] --- QP
```

## 4.3 The Deming / Shewhart Cycle — PDCA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Plan: Identify problem and plan change"] --> D1
    D1["Do: Implement the plan on a small scale"] --> C1
    C1["Check: Measure and analyze results"] --> A1
    A1["Act: Standardize or revise the plan"] --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle, also called the **Deming Wheel** or **Shewhart Cycle**, is the engine of continuous improvement. It is *the* most tested concept in KTU module-1 questions.

## 4.4 Taguchi Quality Loss Function Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T["Target Value T (Loss = 0)"] --> L
    L["L x equals k times x minus T squared"] --> C
    C["Loss increases quadratically as x deviates from T"]
    S1["Specification Limit LSL"] --- L
    S2["Specification Limit USL"] --- L
    C1["Even within spec, loss exists"] --- C
```

## 4.5 Crosby's Five Stages of Quality Maturity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1A["Stage 1: Uncertainty - 'What is quality?'"] --> S2A
    S2A["Stage 2: Awakening - 'Quality costs!'] --> S3A
    S3A["Stage 3: Enlightenment - 'Prevention saves money'"] --> S4A
    S4A["Stage 4: Wisdom - 'Zero Defects as culture'"] --> S5A
    S5A["Stage 5: Certainty - 'Quality is a habit'"]
```

## 4.6 Mapping of Quality Gurus to Their Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1["Shewhart 1924: Control Chart + SPC"] --> M1["QC Era"]
    G2["Deming 1950: 14 Points + PDCA"] --> M2["QA Era"]
    G3["Juran 1951: Juran Trilogy + Fitness for Use"] --> M2
    G4["Feigenbaum 1956: Total Quality Control TQC"] --> M2
    G5["Ishikawa 1962: Quality Circles + Fishbone"] --> M2
    G6["Crosby 1979: Zero Defects + 5 Stages"] --> M3["TQM Era"]
    G7["Taguchi 1980s: Quality Loss Function + Robust Design"] --> M3
    G8["Bill Smith 1986: Six Sigma DMAIC"] --> M3
    G9["Ohno Toyota: Lean TPS Just in Time"] --> M3
    G10["Imai 1986: Kaizen Continuous Improvement"] --> M3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 OEMET615):**
> - Part A (2 marks each, ~5 Qs) — Short definitions/recall.
> - Part B (14 marks, Module Internal Choice) — Two full 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.
> - **Bloom's levels covered here:** Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2–3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"Quality is conformance to requirements." — Justify this statement by Philip Crosby and contrast it with the older "Inspection" approach. (2 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- Crosby (1979) defined quality as **conformance to requirements**, not elegance or luxury.
- The Inspection era relied on detecting non-conformance *after* production. It sorted good from bad but did not prevent defects.
- Crosby's approach shifts the focus to **prevention, doing it right the first time**, and **Zero Defects** as a performance standard.
- Contrast: Inspection = reactive detection; Crosby = proactive conformance-based management responsibility.
- (Conclude: Crosby bridged Inspection → QA era and laid the foundation for TQM.)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**State the Quality Loss Function as proposed by Taguchi. What does it convey about "in-spec but off-target" products? (2 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- Taguchi's Quality Loss Function: $L(x) = k (x - T)^{2}$
- $x$ = actual value, $T$ = target value, $k$ = loss coefficient.
- It conveys that **any deviation from target causes loss to society**, even if the product is *within* specification limits.
- Hence, modern quality aims to **minimize variation around the target**, not just stay within spec.

---

## Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

> **"Quality Management has evolved through four distinct eras." Discuss in detail, highlighting the limitations of each era that necessitated the move to the next. (14 Marks)**

**Model Solution Framework:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Trace the four eras with their drivers and limitations.**

- **Era 1: Inspection Era (Pre-1930).** Focus on detection; tools = gauges, sorting. Driver: mass production. Limitation: 100% inspection is impossible for complex products, costly, and detects defects only after they occur.
- **Era 2: QC Era (1930–1950).** Shewhart's Control Chart; statistical thinking; SPC. Limitation: Technical focus; management not involved; cannot prevent system-level design defects.
- **Era 3: QA Era (1950–1979).** Deming's 14 Points, Juran's Trilogy, Feigenbaum's TQC, Ishikawa's Quality Circles. System-based prevention. Limitation: External failure cost still high, customer not central, ISO 9001 can become bureaucratic.
- **Era 4: TQM / Strategic Era (1979–Present).** Customer focus, continuous improvement; Crosby, Taguchi, Six Sigma, Lean, ISO 9000:2015. Limitation (still evolving): Cultural change, integration with digital/Industry 4.0.

**(b) [7 Marks] — Comparative analytical matrix + contribution of gurus + present status.**

- Provide a clear comparison (refer to **Section 3.2 Matrix** above).
- Summarize gurus' contributions in a paragraph.
- Mention current trends: Lean Six Sigma integration, AI-driven quality, digital QMS, Industry 4.0.
- Conclude: The evolution is **not just history but a continuous loop** of problem → improvement → new problem.

**Examiner's expected key phrases:** "voice of the customer," "prevention over detection," "statistical thinking," "system of profound knowledge," "continuous improvement (Kaizen)."

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the contributions of any **three Quality Gurus** (Shewhart, Deming, Juran, Crosby, Taguchi, Ishikawa) and their impact on the evolution of quality.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A shaft has target diameter $T = 25.00$ mm. The loss coefficient is $k = \text{Rs. } 800 \text{ per mm}^{2}$. Supplier X delivers shafts with mean diameter $25.04$ mm; Supplier Y delivers shafts with mean diameter $24.88$ mm. Both are within $\pm 0.10$ mm specification. **(i)** Compute the per-unit Taguchi quality loss for each supplier. **(ii)** Which supplier is more harmful to society and by what factor? **(iii)** Explain how this illustrates the modern (TQM-era) view of quality versus the Inspection-era view.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Contributions of three gurus (select any 3):**

- **Walter A. Shewhart — Father of Statistical Quality Control.** Developed the **Control Chart** in 1924 at Bell Labs. Introduced the concept that *variation* has two components — *common cause* (system) and *special cause* (assignable). His PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle became the engine of continuous improvement.

- **W. Edwards Deming — Father of the Modern Quality Movement.** Delivered the famous 1950 lecture in Japan. Proposed **14 Points for Management**, the **System of Profound Knowledge** (Appreciation for a System, Knowledge of Variation, Theory of Knowledge, Psychology). Showed that **94% of quality problems are management's responsibility, not workers'**.

- **Joseph M. Juran — Father of Quality Planning.** Defined quality as **fitness for use**, not conformance. Introduced the **Juran Trilogy**: Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement. Emphasized the *human* side of quality and the need for *quality planning* before production.

- **Philip B. Crosby** — *"Quality is Free"* (1979), **Zero Defects**, 5-stage quality maturity grid. Quality = **conformance to requirements, not elegance**. Prevention cost is always cheaper than failure cost.

- **Genichi Taguchi** — **Quality Loss Function** $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$; **Robust Design** (parameter design, tolerance design). Quality is a continuous loss, not binary.

- **Kaoru Ishikawa** — **Quality Circles** (1962, Japan), **Fishbone Diagram** (cause-and-effect). Empowered shop-floor workers to participate in problem solving.

**(b) [7 Marks] Taguchi Loss numerical:**

**(i) Loss for Supplier X:**

$$
L_X = k \times (x_X - T)^2 = 800 \times (25.04 - 25.00)^2
$$

$$
L_X = 800 \times (0.04)^2 = 800 \times 0.0016 = \text{Rs. } 1.28 \text{ per unit}
$$

**Loss for Supplier Y:**

$$
L_Y = 800 \times (24.88 - 25.00)^2 = 800 \times (-0.12)^2
$$

$$
L_Y = 800 \times 0.0144 = \text{Rs. } 11.52 \text{ per unit}
$$

**(ii) Harmful supplier and factor:**

$$
\frac{L_Y}{L_X} = \frac{11.52}{1.28} = 9
$$

**Supplier Y is 9 times more harmful to society per unit** than Supplier X.

**(iii) Modern TQM view vs. Inspection-era view:**

- **Inspection-era view:** Both suppliers are "within spec" → both are *acceptable*. Quality is binary (pass/fail).
- **Modern TQM view:** Quality is **continuous**. Supplier Y is **off-target by 0.12 mm**, causing quadratic loss to the customer, downstream processes, and society (e.g., wear, vibration, failure). The TQM-era manager will **work with Supplier Y to bring the mean back to 25.00 mm**, not just declare them "in spec."
- This is the **philosophical leap from Inspection → QC → QA → TQM** in one numerical example.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TQC (Feigenbaum, 1956) with TQM.** TQC = Total Quality Control; TQM = Total Quality Management. TQC is the *parent*; TQM (popularized 1985 by US Navy/Dept. of Defense) is the *child* with customer-focus and strategic alignment.
> 2. **Attributing PDCA to Deming only.** PDCA was *originally* Shewhart's idea (1939); Deming *popularized* it in Japan. KTU expects "Shewhart Cycle / Deming Wheel."
> 3. **Wrong sigma-to-DPMO conversions.** Six-Sigma = 3.4 DPMO, not 0 DPMO (the 3.4 accounts for 1.5-sigma long-term drift).
> 4. **Taguchi Loss Function — forgetting to square the deviation.** $L = k(x-T)^2$, not $k(x-T)$.
> 5. **Writing "Six Sigma is 6 standard deviations" loosely.** It is a **performance target = 3.4 DPMO**, achieved by a $\pm 6\sigma$ process with 1.5-sigma shift.
> 6. **Skipping the "Why" — examiners reward evolution logic**, not just a list of gurus.
> 7. **Forgetting units in Taguchi loss problems** (Rs./unit).
> 8. **Mixing Crosby's "Quality is Free" with Deming's PDCA** — they are different gurus, do not cross-credit.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision bullet list — read this aloud before entering the exam hall.**

- **Quality (ISO 9000:2015):** Degree to which inherent characteristics fulfil requirements.
- **Quality Management =** Planning + Control + Assurance + Improvement.
- **Four Eras:** **Inspection (pre-1930) → QC (1930-50) → QA (1950-79) → TQM/Strategic (1979-now).**
- **Shewhart (1924):** Control Chart, SPC, PDCA. *Father of Statistical QC.*
- **Deming (1950):** 14 Points, System of Profound Knowledge, 94% responsibility on management. *Father of Modern Quality Movement.*
- **Juran (1951):** Juran Trilogy (Plan, Control, Improve), Fitness-for-Use. *Father of Quality Planning.*
- **Feigenbaum (1956):** Coined **Total Quality Control (TQC)**, *not* TQM.
- **Ishikawa (1962):** Quality Circles, Fishbone Diagram.
- **Crosby (1979):** "Quality is Free", Zero Defects, 5-stage quality maturity grid, Quality = Conformance to Requirements.
- **Taguchi (1980s):** Quality Loss Function $L = k(x-T)^2$, Robust Design.
- **Six Sigma (Motorola, 1986):** 3.4 DPMO, DMAIC (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control).
- **Lean / TPS (Ohno, Toyota):** Eliminate muda (waste), mura (variation), muri (overburden); Just-in-Time.
- **Kaizen (Imai, 1986):** Continuous, incremental improvement; everyone, every day.
- **ISO 9000:** 1987 → 1994 → 2000 → 2008 → **2015** (current). Risk-based thinking since 2015.
- **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act (Shewhart/Deming).
- **Taguchi Loss:** $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$ — quadratic, never zero unless $x=T$.
- **DPMO Formula:** $\dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}}$.
- **Sigma Level Formula:** $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{2\sigma}$.
- **Cost of Quality:** Prevention + Appraisal (CoC) **vs.** Internal + External Failure (CoNC). Goal: shift the balance toward CoC.
- **Mindset evolution:** Catch defects → Control process → Build system → Satisfy customer → Continuously improve.
- **Modern trends (for 2-mark updates):** Lean Six Sigma, Industry 4.0, Digital QMS, AI-driven SPC, Agile.

> **End of Notes — Evolution of Quality Management | Module 1 | OEMET615 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Quality Control

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Control (QC)** is the operational arm of quality management that focuses on fulfilling quality requirements by performing **operational techniques and activities** that monitor a process and eliminate causes of unsatisfactory performance at every stage of the production cycle.

According to the universally accepted ISO 9000:2015 / KSQI (Kerala State Quality Initiative) framework adopted by KTU, Quality Control is formally defined as:

> *"Part of quality management focused on fulfilling quality requirements."*

While **Quality Assurance (QA)** is *process-oriented* and *preventive*, **Quality Control (QC)** is *product-oriented* and *detective/corrective*. The two are inseparable pillars of Total Quality Management (TQM).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a restaurant kitchen.**
> 
> - **Quality Assurance (QA)** = The head chef writing the recipe book (SOPs) — *prevention*.
> - **Quality Control (QC)** = The food inspector tasting every dish before it leaves the kitchen — *detection*.
> - **Quality Management (QM)** = The entire restaurant brand management ensuring customer satisfaction — *strategy*.

In an engineering factory:
- **QA** builds the manufacturing process correctly (right machines, right training, right SOPs).
- **QC** checks whether the *output* (the product) meets specifications through **inspection**, **testing**, and **measurement**.
- **QM** ties both together with continuous improvement (Kaizen).

### 1.3 Objectives of Quality Control

| S.No | Objective | Practical Meaning |
|------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Conformance to Standards** | Ensure every unit meets design specifications |
| 2 | **Defect Detection** | Identify non-conforming products early |
| 3 | **Cost Reduction** | Minimize scrap, rework, and warranty costs |
| 4 | **Process Stability** | Maintain consistency over time |
| 5 | **Customer Satisfaction** | Deliver reliable, defect-free goods |

### 1.4 Scope and Boundaries of Quality Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In Module 1, students must clearly distinguish between:
> - **Quality Control (QC)** → Reactive, inspection-based, product-focused
> - **Quality Assurance (QA)** → Proactive, process-based, prevention-focused
> - **Total Quality Management (TQM)** → Strategic, organization-wide, philosophy

QC operates primarily on **three pillars**:

1. **Inspection** — Visual or dimensional examination of products.
2. **Testing** — Functional/performance verification.
3. **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — Data-driven monitoring using control charts.

The standard metric used to quantify QC effectiveness is the **Parts Per Million (PPM)** defect rate, expressed as **1.0 PPM = 0.0001% defectives** in modern Six Sigma literature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Operational Layers of QC

Quality Control functions across a strict hierarchy:

**Layer 1 — Off-line QC (Pre-production)**
- Design reviews, FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis), prototype validation.
- Decides *what* to inspect and *how*.

**Layer 2 — In-process / On-line QC (During production)**
- Real-time monitoring using **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts.
- Detects process drift *before* defects pile up.

**Layer 3 — Final / Incoming QC (Post-production)**
- 100% inspection, sampling inspection, or skip-lot inspection.
- Decides whether to *accept*, *reject*, or *rectify* a batch.

### 2.2 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) — The Engine of Modern QC

SQC, popularized by **Walter A. Shewhart (1924)** at Bell Laboratories, transforms QC from intuition-based inspection to mathematics-based monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Philosophy:** *Variation is inherent in every process. QC's job is to distinguish between COMMON CAUSE variation (random, inherent) and SPECIAL CAUSE variation (assignable, actionable).*

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the **control limit multipliers** ($\mathbf{A_2, D_3, D_4, d_2, d_3}$) and their relation to sample size $\mathbf{n}$. A single slip here costs 2 marks.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **X̄-chart UCL** | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ = grand mean; $\bar{R}$ = avg range |
| 2 | **X̄-chart CL** | $CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}$ | Centre line |
| 3 | **X̄-chart LCL** | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Lower limit |
| 4 | **R-chart UCL** | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_4$ constant |
| 5 | **R-chart LCL** | $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | $D_3$ constant |
| 6 | **p-chart UCL** | $UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | Fraction defective |
| 7 | **c-chart UCL** | $UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Defects per unit |
| 8 | **Process Capability (Cp)** | $C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability |
| 9 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Actual capability |
| 10 | **Standard Deviation (via Range)** | $\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | $d_2$ depends on $n$ |
| 11 | **Six Sigma Defect Rate** | $\text{Yield} = \Phi(C_{pk})$ | $\Phi$ = standard normal CDF |

#### Control Chart Constants (Critical for KTU Problems)

| $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Bosch, Tata Motors):** SPC charts on engine block machining — Cp ≥ 1.33 mandatory.
- **Pharmaceuticals (USFDA regulated):** c-charts monitor tablet coating defects.
- **Semiconductor Industry:** Cpk ≥ 1.5 is the industry benchmark for high-yield fabs.
- **Aerospace (AS9100 standard):** Six Sigma QC protocols are non-negotiable.
- **Civil Engineering (KTU bridge projects):** Concrete cube strength monitored via X̄-R charts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Shewhart 3-Sigma Control Limits

The foundation of every SQC control chart rests on the empirical rule that for any normal distribution, **99.73% of observations lie within $\pm 3\sigma$** of the mean. Shewhart chose the 3-sigma limits as a balance between:
- **False alarms** (Type I error) at 0.27%
- **Missed shifts** (Type II error) detection capability

**Derivation for the X̄-chart:**

Consider $m$ subgroups, each of size $n$, drawn from a process with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Standard Error of } \bar{X} &= \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\
\text{Estimate of } \sigma_{\bar{X}} \text{ via Average Range} &= \hat{\sigma}_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} = \frac{A_2 \bar{R}}{3} \\
\text{where } A_2 &= \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} \\
\text{Centre Line (CL)} &= \bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} \bar{X}_i}{m} \\
\text{Upper Control Limit (UCL)} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + 3\hat{\sigma}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\
\text{Lower Control Limit (LCL)} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - 3\hat{\sigma}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation:**
1. The standard error shrinks as $\sqrt{n}$ grows — larger samples give tighter control bands.
2. Since $\sigma$ is rarely known, $\bar{R}$ (average range) is used as a robust estimator.
3. The factor $A_2$ is tabled against $n$ so that practitioners don't recompute $d_2$ each time.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the p-chart Control Limits (Variable Sample Size)

When the quality characteristic is **attribute-based** (pass/fail), we track the fraction defective $p_i = D_i / n_i$ for the $i$-th subgroup.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean fraction defective} &= \bar{p} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} D_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} n_i} \\
\text{Standard deviation of } p_i &= \sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} \\
\text{Upper Control Limit} &= UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} \\
\text{Lower Control Limit} &= LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} \\
\text{If } LCL_p < 0, &\text{ set } LCL_p = 0 \quad \text{(physical constraint)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation:**
- $p_i$ follows approximately a binomial distribution; its variance is $p(1-p)/n$.
- The square-root term is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of a proportion.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Process Capability Index (Cp and Cpk)

**Cp (Potential Capability)** measures the spread of the process relative to the specification width:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specification Width} &= USL - LSL \\
\text{Process Spread (6\sigma)} &= 6\sigma \\
C_p &= \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Spread}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $C_p = 1.00$ → Process just meets spec (0.27% out, ~2700 PPM).
- $C_p = 1.33$ → Industry minimum for existing processes.
- $C_p = 1.50$ → Six Sigma target (~3.4 PPM).
- $C_p = 2.00$ → World-class (zero PPM theoretically).

**Cpk (Actual Capability)** accounts for *off-center* processes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) \\
\text{where } C_{pu} &= \frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} \\
\text{and } C_{pl} &= \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

A process can have $C_p = 1.5$ but $C_{pk} = 0.9$ if the mean is shifted — showing that **Cp alone is insufficient**.

---

### 3.4 Worked-Out Analytical Matrix (KTU-Style Solved Problem)

> **Problem:** Twenty samples of size 5 are drawn from a manufacturing process. The grand mean is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 100$ mm and average range is $\bar{R} = 4.2$ mm. Determine the control limits for the X̄ and R charts. (Given $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$ for $n=5$.)

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 100 + (0.577)(4.2) = 100 + 2.4234 = 102.42 \text{ mm} \\
CL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} = 100 \text{ mm} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 100 - 2.4234 = 97.58 \text{ mm} \\
UCL_R &= D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(4.2) = 8.88 \text{ mm} \\
CL_R &= \bar{R} = 4.2 \text{ mm} \\
LCL_R &= D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(4.2) = 0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

If a subsequent sample mean $\bar{X}_{21} = 103.0$ mm is observed, it lies **above UCL = 102.42 mm**, indicating an **assignable cause** (special cause variation) — the process is **out of statistical control**, and investigation must begin immediately.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Quality Control Operational Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Input] --> B[In-Process QC Layer]
    B --> C{SPC Monitoring via Control Charts}
    C -->|Within Limits| D[Continue Production]
    C -->|Outside Limits| E[Stop and Investigate]
    E --> F[Root Cause Analysis - 5 Whys and Ishikawa]
    F --> G[Corrective Action]
    G --> B
    D --> H[Final Inspection Layer]
    H --> I[Acceptance Sampling - Single, Double, Multiple]
    I -->|Accept| J[Customer Delivery]
    I -->|Reject| K[Rework or Scrap Disposal]
    H --> L[100 Percent Inspection - Critical Characteristics]
    L --> J
    K --> M[Cost of Poor Quality COPQ Ledger]
    J --> N[Feedback Loop to TQM System]
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 The Seven QC Tools Framework (Brought by Ishikawa & Imai)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Cause and Effect Diagram - Fishbone Ishikawa] --> TOOLS[7 QC Tools]
    Q2[Check Sheet] --> TOOLS
    Q3[Control Chart - Shewhart] --> TOOLS
    Q4[Histogram] --> TOOLS
    Q5[Pareto Chart - 80 by 20 Rule] --> TOOLS
    Q6[Scatter Diagram] --> TOOLS
    Q7[Stratification - Data Layering] --> TOOLS
    TOOLS --> OUT[Quality Problem Solving and Continuous Improvement]
```

### 4.3 Statistical Quality Control Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Collect Subgroup Data] --> T{Data Type}
    T -->|Variable: length, weight, time| V[Use X-bar and R Charts]
    T -->|Attribute: pass or fail| A{Subgroup Size}
    A -->|Fixed n| P[p-chart or np-chart]
    A -->|Variable n| PP[p-chart with varying limits]
    P --> C[Plot and Monitor]
    V --> C
    PP --> C
    C --> D{Points Outside 3 Sigma}
    D -->|Yes| X[Out of Control - Take Action]
    D -->|No| Y{Western Electric Rules Triggered}
    Y -->|Yes| X
    Y -->|No| Z[Process In Control - Continue]
```

### 4.4 Position of QC in the Quality Management Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QM[Quality Management - Strategic Level] --> QA[Quality Assurance - Process Level]
    QM --> QC[Quality Control - Product Level]
    QA --> SOP[Standard Operating Procedures SOPs]
    QA --> AUDIT[Audits and Reviews]
    QC --> INS[Inspection and Testing]
    QC --> SPC[Statistical Process Control]
    SPC --> CC[Control Charts]
    SPC --> AS[Acceptance Sampling]
    CC --> XBAR[X-bar and R]
    CC --> ATTR[p, np, c, u charts]
    AS --> OC[OC Curve Analysis]
    OC --> AOQ[Average Outgoing Quality]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**
Quality Assurance (QA) is a **proactive, process-oriented** set of activities designed to ensure that quality requirements are built *into* the system. Example: writing a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) for welding before production begins.

Quality Control (QC) is a **reactive, product-oriented** set of activities that detects defects *after* or *during* production. Example: performing a 100% dimensional check of finished shafts using a vernier caliper.

QA is **prevention-driven** (build quality in), while QC is **detection-driven** (find the defects). Both together form the Quality Management System.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the seven tools of Statistical Quality Control and state the purpose of a Pareto chart.*

**Model Answer:**
The seven QC tools are: (1) Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa/Fishbone), (2) Check Sheet, (3) Control Chart, (4) Histogram, (5) Pareto Chart, (6) Scatter Diagram, and (7) Stratification.

A **Pareto chart** is a bar graph arranged in descending order of frequency, used to identify the **vital few** causes of problems from the **trivial many** — based on the **80/20 rule** (Pareto Principle) which states that roughly 80% of defects arise from 20% of causes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Statistical Process Control (SPC). Explain the structure and principle of an X̄-R control chart with a neat sketch of control limits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A process produces bolts with a target diameter of 20 mm. Twenty samples of size 5 yielded a grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}} = 20.05$ mm and average range $\bar{R} = 0.42$ mm. Given $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$. (i) Calculate control limits for the X̄ and R charts. (ii) Comment if a new sample mean of 20.50 mm is in control.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** SPC is the application of statistical techniques — chiefly control charts — to monitor, control, and improve a manufacturing process over time by distinguishing random (common cause) variation from assignable (special cause) variation.

The **X̄-R chart** is a two-chart system used together:
- The **X̄-chart** monitors the process *location* (mean).
- The **R-chart** monitors the process *spread* (variability).

If the R-chart first goes out of control, the X̄-chart interpretation is invalid. Control limits are placed at $\pm 3\sigma$ around the centre line, providing a 99.73% confidence band for in-control variation.

The chart structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\
CL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} \\
UCL_R &= D_4 \bar{R} \\
CL_R &= \bar{R} \\
LCL_R &= D_3 \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\bar{X}} &= 20.05 \text{ mm}, \quad \bar{R} = 0.42 \text{ mm}, \quad A_2 = 0.577, \quad D_4 = 2.114 \\
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= 20.05 + (0.577)(0.42) = 20.05 + 0.2423 = 20.292 \text{ mm} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= 20.05 - 0.2423 = 19.808 \text{ mm} \\
UCL_R &= (2.114)(0.42) = 0.888 \text{ mm} \\
LCL_R &= 0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

New sample mean $= 20.50$ mm. Since $20.50 > UCL_{\bar{X}} = 20.292$ mm, the process is **out of statistical control**. The point lies above the upper control limit, signaling an **assignable cause** that requires immediate investigation (machine wear, tool offset, raw material change).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define process capability. Derive the expressions for $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Why is $C_{pk}$ preferred over $C_p$ in modern QC?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CNC lathe produces shafts with $USL = 25.10$ mm, $LSL = 24.90$ mm. The process mean is $\bar{X} = 24.97$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.03$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pu}$, $C_{pl}$, and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the process capability.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Process capability** is the ability of a process to produce output within specification limits, measured under stable conditions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\
C_{pk} &= \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$C_p$ only measures the *spread* of the process; it ignores whether the mean is centered. $C_{pk}$ accounts for both spread *and* centering, hence it reflects the **actual** capability. A process can have $C_p = 2.0$ but $C_{pk} = 0.8$ if the mean is shifted — making $C_p$ dangerously misleading. Hence, $C_{pk}$ is preferred.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.20}{0.18} = 1.111 \\
C_{pu} &= \frac{25.10 - 24.97}{3 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.13}{0.09} = 1.444 \\
C_{pl} &= \frac{24.97 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.07}{0.09} = 0.778 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(1.444, 0.778) = 0.778
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Although $C_p = 1.111$ suggests marginal capability, $C_{pk} = 0.778$ reveals that the process is **not capable** (industry minimum $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$). The process mean (24.97 mm) is shifted closer to the LSL, producing a one-sided risk of undersized shafts. **Corrective action**: recenter the process to $\bar{X} = 25.00$ mm, which would raise $C_{pk}$ to approximately $1.111$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing QA with QC** — Examiner specifically tests this in 2-mark questions. State which is *proactive* (QA) and which is *reactive* (QC).
> 2. **Forgetting to set $LCL = 0$** when the formula gives a negative value (physically, range or fraction defective cannot be negative). This loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong constant selection** — students often use $A_2$ for $n = 3$ when the problem states $n = 5$. Always re-verify $n$ from the question.
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation step** — after computing control limits, you MUST comment on whether a new data point is in/out of control. Just calculating without interpretation costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Reporting $C_p$ alone** — KTU examiners penalize answers that omit $C_{pk}$ analysis in capability problems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **QC** is *detection-oriented*; **QA** is *prevention-oriented*; **QM** is *strategy-oriented*.
- Shewhart's **3-sigma** control limits form the mathematical basis of all control charts.
- The **X̄-R chart** is used for *variable* data; the **p, np, c, u charts** are used for *attribute* data.
- $A_2, D_3, D_4, d_2$ values depend on sample size $n$ — memorize the table for $n = 2, 3, 4, 5$.
- **Cp** measures potential capability (spread only); **Cpk** measures actual capability (spread + centering).
- Industry standard for capable processes: **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** (existing process); **$C_{pk} \geq 1.67$** (new process); **$C_{pk} \geq 2.00$** (Six Sigma / world-class).
- **Pareto principle** (80/20 rule) directs QC focus to the *vital few* defect causes.
- Always interpret a control chart by first checking the **R-chart**, then the **X̄-chart** (logical precedence).
- **Six Sigma** philosophy: 3.4 defects per million opportunities, equivalent to $C_{pk} = 1.5$ with 1.5-sigma shift.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Assurance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Assurance

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Assurance (QA)** is a systematic, planned set of activities implemented within the **Quality Management System (QMS)** that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy **quality requirements**. It is a *proactive*, *process-oriented*, and *prevention-driven* managerial philosophy.

According to **ISO 9000:2015**, QA is defined as *"part of quality management focused on providing confidence that quality requirements will be fulfilled."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Quality Assurance is studied as the **upstream, system-wide discipline** that establishes the policies, standards, audits, and procedures necessary to prevent defects — in stark contrast to Quality Control (QC), which is the downstream detection-based activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Quality Assurance**
> QA is **not** a single activity; it is a **management philosophy** that encompasses the entire organizational structure, documented procedures, training, audits, and corrective/preventive actions (CAPA) aimed at *building quality in* rather than *inspecting quality in*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe vs. Taste-Test" Metaphor

Imagine you are running a **college canteen**:

- **Quality Assurance** is like writing down a **detailed recipe book** — specifying the exact quantity of salt, cooking temperature, ingredient sourcing, staff hygiene rules, and kitchen cleanliness protocols. You follow the *system* so that every plate of biryani tastes great **before** it reaches a customer.
- **Quality Control**, on the other hand, is when the **manager tastes each plate before serving** and rejects the bad ones. If many plates fail, you realize your *recipe book* is flawed.

**The Insight:** QA builds the *system* (recipe) that makes defects unlikely. QC *detects* the defects that escape the system. A smart manager fixes the recipe (QA) rather than forever tasting plates (QC).

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Standards

| Term | Meaning | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| **QMS** | Quality Management System | The "nervous system" of the organization |
| **SOP** | Standard Operating Procedure | The "muscle memory" of every worker |
| **CAPA** | Corrective and Preventive Action | The "immune system" of the process |
| **PDCA** | Plan–Do–Check–Act (Deming Cycle) | The "heartbeat" of continuous improvement |
| **TQM** | Total Quality Management | The "company-wide culture" of quality |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality vs. Reliability vs. Grade**
> - *Quality* = Degree to which a product meets requirements (conformance).
> - *Reliability* = Probability of performing without failure over time.
> - *Grade* = A category based on features (a luxury car vs. an economy car — both can be high quality).
>
> Confusing these is a common **2-mark trap** in KTU exams.

---

## 1.4 Quality Assurance vs. Quality Control — The Foundational Distinction

This is the single most frequently asked distinction in KTU Module 1 examinations. Memorize it.

| Dimension | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | **Quality Control (QC)** |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | **Process** (how work is done) | **Product** (the output) |
| Orientation | **Prevention** of defects | **Detection** of defects |
| Approach | **System-wide, managerial** | **Operational, inspection-based** |
| Timing | **Before & during** production | **After** production |
| Tools | Audits, SOPs, Training, FMEA | Gauges, gauges, sampling, testing |
| Responsibility | **Everyone** (especially management) | **QC department / inspectors** |
| Cost Impact | **Lower long-term cost** | **Higher long-term cost** |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of a Robust Quality Assurance System

A high-scoring KTU answer should structure QA around these five pillars:

1. **Pillar 1 — Top Management Commitment & Quality Policy**
   - Without leadership buy-in, QA collapses. The quality policy is the documented *intent* of the organization.
   - Example: *"We will deliver zero-defect software within 48 hours."*

2. **Pillar 2 — Documented Quality Standards & SOPs**
   - Every repeatable task must have a written procedure. SOPs eliminate variance due to human discretion.

3. **Pillar 3 — Training & Competency Development**
   - A procedure is only as good as the operator. QA mandates continuous skill upgrading (e.g., Six Sigma Green Belt certification).

4. **Pillar 4 — Process Audits & Reviews**
   - Internal audits verify that the *system* is being followed. External audits (by certification bodies like TÜV, BV) provide third-party assurance.

5. **Pillar 5 — Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA) Loop**
   - **Corrective Action:** Eliminates the *cause* of a detected non-conformity (so it does not recur).
   - **Preventive Action:** Eliminates the *cause* of a *potential* non-conformity (so it never occurs).
   - This is the engine of **Kaizen** (continuous improvement).

---

## 2.2 The Deming Cycle (PDCA) — The Operating System of QA

Every QA program runs on the **PDCA wheel**:

- **Plan:** Identify the goal, define processes, and determine resources.
- **Do:** Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot).
- **Check:** Measure actual performance against expected; analyze gaps.
- **Act:** Standardize the improvement or revise the plan; loop again.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PDCA is iterative, not linear.** A single rotation yields one improvement; thousands of rotations yield *kaizen*. In KTU 14-mark answers, always draw the PDCA wheel as a **circular diagram**, not a straight line.

---

## 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The Financial Language of QA

Quality has a *price tag*, and QA aims to **minimize total COQ**. The **PAF Model** (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) divides quality costs into four buckets:

- **Prevention Costs (P):** Training, quality planning, SOP development, supplier capability building.
- **Appraisal Costs (A):** Inspection, testing, calibration of gauges, audits.
- **Internal Failure Costs (IF):** Rework, scrap, re-inspection, defect analysis.
- **External Failure Costs (EF):** Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost goodwill.

$$\text{Total COQ} \;=\; C_P \;+\; C_A \;+\; C_{IF} \;+\; C_{EF}$$

> **Strategic Insight:** Spending **1 unit on Prevention** typically saves **10 to 100 units** in failure costs. Thus, optimal QA strategy **increases P and A** while **dramatically decreasing IF and EF**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quality Assurance & Related Metrics

The following table consolidates the high-yield equations a KTU student must master for Module 1 problems:

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Cost of Quality | $C_{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | $C_P$ = Prevention; $C_A$ = Appraisal; $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure; $C_{EF}$ = External Failure | Strategic cost optimization |
| 2 | Conformance Cost | $C_{Conf} = C_P + C_A$ | Cost of doing things right | Quality budgeting |
| 3 | Non-Conformance Cost | $C_{NonConf} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Cost of doing things wrong | Loss function analysis |
| 4 | Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $USL$ = Upper Spec Limit; $LSL$ = Lower Spec Limit; $\sigma$ = Std. Dev. | Process evaluation |
| 5 | Process Capability (shifted) | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | $\mu$ = process mean | Real-world capability |
| 6 | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \times O}$ | $D$ = Defects; $N$ = Units; $O$ = Opportunities per unit | Six Sigma metrics |
| 7 | Rolled Throughput Yield | $Y_{RT} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | $Y_i$ = Yield of stage $i$ | Multi-stage process yield |
| 8 | First-Time Yield | $Y_{FT} = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{entered}}$ | Good units / Entered units | Single-stage yield |
| 9 | Process Sigma Level | $\sigma_{level} = 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(DPMO)}$ | Empirical approximation | Six Sigma conversion |
| 10 | Quality Loss Function (Taguchi) | $L(x) = k \cdot (x - T)^2$ | $k$ = constant; $T$ = target | Robust design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Markdown Rule Applied:** All absolute-value / division expressions above use `\dfrac{}{}` or `\min[...]` instead of vertical pipes, ensuring KTU-rendered Markdown tables do not break visually.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (Mechanical):** ISO 9001:2015 certified plants use QA to reduce weld defects in automotive frames from **3.4 DPMO** (5-Sigma) to **3.4 DPMO** (6-Sigma).
- **Software Engineering:** CMMI Level 5 organizations apply QA through formal peer reviews, configuration management, and statistical defect tracking.
- **Civil Engineering:** QA covers material traceability, mix-design verification, and slump-cone testing protocols before concrete placement.
- **Healthcare:** NABH accreditation enforces QA on sterilization cycles, surgical checklists, and patient-handover protocols.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From DPMO to Process Sigma Level

This derivation is **frequently expected** in KTU 14-mark answers under topics like Six Sigma / Quality Metrics. We derive the conversion from DPMO to the underlying process sigma level.

### Step 1 — Establish the Yield Statistic

Let $Y$ be the first-pass yield expressed as a decimal. Then the **defect rate** is $1 - Y$.

We define the **DPMO** as the number of defects per million opportunities:

$$DPMO \;=\; 1{,}000{,}000 \;\times\; (1 - Y)$$

### Step 2 — Express Yield as a Tail-Area of the Normal Distribution

For a centered, stable process, the yield represents the area under the standard normal curve within specification limits. If the spec limits are $\pm z \sigma$ from the mean:

$$Y \;=\; \Phi(z) \;-\; \Phi(-z) \;=\; 2 \Phi(z) \;-\; 1$$

where $\Phi(z)$ is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the standard normal.

### Step 3 — Solve for the Z-Score (the "Sigma Level")

Rearranging for $z$:

$$\Phi(z) \;=\; \dfrac{Y + 1}{2} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{2 \times 1{,}000{,}000}$$

The process sigma level is therefore:

$$\sigma_{level} \;=\; z \;=\; \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{2 \times 1{,}000{,}000}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Empirical Closed-Form Approximation (Used in Practice)

For industrial hand-calculations, the inverse normal is approximated by a fitted quadratic in $\ln(DPMO)$:

$$\sigma_{level} \;\approx\; 0.8406 \;+\; \sqrt{29.37 \;-\; 2.221 \cdot \ln(DPMO)}$$

> **Why the constant 0.8406?** It is a *bias correction* that accounts for the **1.5-sigma shift** convention used by Motorola/Bill Smith — industry treats a "true" 6-sigma process as having a 4.5-sigma short-term capability with a 1.5-sigma long-term drift.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

A manufacturing line produces **2,300 defects** in **15,000 units**, with **3 opportunities per unit**. Compute the sigma level.

**Step 5a — Compute DPMO:**

$$DPMO \;=\; \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \times O} \;=\; \dfrac{2{,}300 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{15{,}000 \times 3}$$

$$DPMO \;=\; \dfrac{2.3 \times 10^{9}}{45{,}000} \;=\; 51{,}111.11$$

**Step 5b — Compute Natural Log:**

$$\ln(51{,}111.11) \;\approx\; 10.842$$

**Step 5c — Apply the Approximation:**

$$\sigma_{level} \;\approx\; 0.8406 \;+\; \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \times 10.842}$$

$$2.221 \times 10.842 \;\approx\; 24.080$$

$$29.37 - 24.080 \;=\; 5.290$$

$$\sqrt{5.290} \;\approx\; 2.300$$

$$\sigma_{level} \;\approx\; 0.8406 + 2.300 \;\approx\; 3.14 \; \sigma$$

**Conclusion:** The process is operating at approximately **3.14 sigma**, well below the Six Sigma benchmark of 6.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Quality Philosophies

The KTU 2024 syllabus demands that students be able to *contrast* quality philosophies. The following matrix is your high-yield reference:

| Philosophy | Father / Origin | Core Idea | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inspection** | Ancient (Pharaohs) | Detect bad units | Simple | No prevention |
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Walter Shewhart (1924) | Statistical control of product | Data-driven | Reactive |
| **Quality Assurance (QA)** | Joseph Juran / Feigenbaum | System-wide prevention | Proactive | Requires culture shift |
| **TQM** | Deming / Juran / Ishikawa | Customer-focused total involvement | Holistic | Slow to implement |
| **Six Sigma** | Motorola (1986) | 3.4 DPMO via DMAIC | Highly quantitative | Narrow scope |
| **Lean** | Toyota (TPS) | Eliminate waste (muda) | Speed & cost | May overlook variation |
| **Lean Six Sigma** | Combined | Eliminate waste + variation | Best of both worlds | Complex tooling |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Marker Framework:** When asked *"Discuss the evolution of quality management"* — answer in **chronological order** with **one-line contribution** of each pioneer. Deming (statistical), Juran (human side), Crosby (zero defects), Feigenbaum (TQM), Ishikawa (fishbone), Taguchi (loss function), Ohno (lean).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Cost of Quality Optimization

A factory reports the following annual quality costs (in ₹ Lakhs):

| Category | Value |
|---|---|
| Prevention ($C_P$) | 8 |
| Appraisal ($C_A$) | 12 |
| Internal Failure ($C_{IF}$) | 35 |
| External Failure ($C_{EF}$) | 25 |

**Part (a):** Compute Total COQ.

$$C_{COQ} = 8 + 12 + 35 + 25 = 80 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Part (b):** Compute Conformance and Non-Conformance costs.

$$C_{Conf} = C_P + C_A = 8 + 12 = 20 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$C_{NonConf} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 35 + 25 = 60 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Part (c):** Strategic recommendation.

Non-conformance costs are **3×** the conformance costs. A 50% increase in prevention budget (₹ 8L → ₹ 12L) typically cuts internal failure by 40% (₹ 35L → ₹ 21L). New COQ estimate:

$$C_{COQ}^{new} \approx 12 + 12 + 21 + 18 = 63 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Savings:** ₹ **17 Lakhs per year**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Quality Assurance System — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a Quality Assurance system, showing how policy flows down to operational feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1["STRATEGIC LAYER"]
        nodePolicy["QUALITY POLICY AND OBJECTIVES"]
        nodeMgmt["TOP MANAGEMENT COMMITMENT"]
    end

    subgraph A2["TACTICAL LAYER"]
        nodePlan["QUALITY PLANNING AND SOP DESIGN"]
        nodeTrain["TRAINING AND COMPETENCY"]
        nodeAudit["INTERNAL AND EXTERNAL AUDITS"]
    end

    subgraph A3["OPERATIONAL LAYER"]
        nodeDo["PROCESS EXECUTION DO"]
        nodeCheck["MONITORING AND MEASUREMENT CHECK"]
        nodeAct["CORRECTIVE AND PREVENTIVE ACTION ACT"]
    end

    subgraph A4["FEEDBACK LAYER"]
        nodeCustomer["CUSTOMER SATISFACTION METRICS"]
        nodeData["QUALITY DATA WAREHOUSE"]
    end

    nodeMgmt --> nodePolicy
    nodePolicy --> nodePlan
    nodePolicy --> nodeTrain
    nodePlan --> nodeAudit
    nodeTrain --> nodeAudit
    nodeAudit --> nodeDo
    nodeDo --> nodeCheck
    nodeCheck --> nodeAct
    nodeAct --> nodePlan
    nodeCustomer --> nodeData
    nodeData --> nodeCheck
    nodeData --> nodeAudit
    nodeData --> nodePolicy
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **Strategic Layer** sets intent (policy + leadership).
> - The **Tactical Layer** translates intent into standards and skills.
> - The **Operational Layer** is the *PDCA engine* running on the shop floor.
> - The **Feedback Layer** closes the loop by feeding customer voice and quality data back to every other layer.

---

## 4.2 PDCA Cycle — Continuous Improvement Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["PLAN\nDefine goal and method"]
    nodeD["DO\nExecute pilot"]
    nodeC["CHECK\nMeasure results"]
    nodeA["ACT\nStandardize or revise"]
    nodeP --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeP
```

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always indicate the **circular** nature with a feedback arrow. A linear arrow sequence gets **partial credit only**.

---

## 4.3 Cost of Quality — Optimization Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeQ["TOTAL COST OF QUALITY C COQ"]
    nodeP["PREVENTION COSTS C P\nIncreases with QA maturity"]
    nodeA["APPRAISAL COSTS C A\nPlateaus after stabilization"]
    nodeIF["INTERNAL FAILURE C IF\nDrops sharply with QA"]
    nodeEF["EXTERNAL FAILURE C EF\nDrops sharply with QA"]
    nodeQ --> nodeP
    nodeQ --> nodeA
    nodeQ --> nodeIF
    nodeQ --> nodeEF
    nodeIF -. "INVEST IN P HERE" .-> nodeP
    nodeEF -. "INVEST IN P HERE" .-> nodeP
```

> **Strategic Insight:** This diagram illustrates the **optimal QA spend** — increase P and A **only until** the drop in IF + EF equals the marginal increase in P + A. Beyond that point, the law of diminishing returns applies.

---

## 4.4 QA vs QC — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeQ["DEFECT OBSERVED"]
    nodeQC["QUALITY CONTROL QC\nDetect and reject defect"]
    nodeQA["QUALITY ASSURANCE QA\nFix the process that caused defect"]
    nodeSys["CORRECTED PROCESS\nNo recurrence"]
    nodeQ --> nodeQC
    nodeQ --> nodeQA
    nodeQA --> nodeSys
    nodeQC --> nodeQ
    nodeSys --> nodeQA
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Quality Assurance. How is it different from Quality Control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Quality Assurance (QA):** QA is a planned and systematic set of activities implemented within the quality system that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. It is a **proactive, process-oriented, prevention-based** management philosophy.

**Difference from QC:**

| Aspect | QA | QC |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Process | Product |
| Goal | Prevent defects | Detect defects |
| Stage | Before/during production | After production |

QA is **system-wide** while QC is **inspection-based**. QA builds quality in; QC inspects quality in.

*(Full marks for tabular comparison with at least 3 points.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain the four categories of Cost of Quality.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Cost of Quality (COQ) consists of four categories:

1. **Prevention Costs** – Expenditure to *prevent* defects (e.g., training, quality planning, SOPs).
2. **Appraisal Costs** – Expenditure to *detect* defects (e.g., inspection, testing, audits).
3. **Internal Failure Costs** – Costs of defects found *before* delivery (e.g., rework, scrap).
4. **External Failure Costs** – Costs of defects found *after* delivery (e.g., warranty, returns, lost goodwill).

$$\text{Total COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

The first two are **conformance costs**; the last two are **non-conformance costs**. Optimal QA shifts cost from non-conformance to prevention.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

### ✏️ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Define Quality Assurance in detail. Explain the **PDCA cycle** with a suitable engineering example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **five pillars of a Quality Assurance system**. How does CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) ensure continuous improvement? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — QA Definition + PDCA Cycle (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Quality Assurance is a systematic, planned set of activities within the QMS that provides confidence to customers and management that quality requirements will be met. It is **prevention-oriented** and **process-focused**, established by Joseph Juran and Armand Feigenbaum in the 1950s.

**PDCA Cycle (5 marks):**

The PDCA cycle, also known as the **Deming Wheel**, is a four-step iterative model for continuous improvement:

- **P (Plan):** Identify a problem or goal. Establish objectives, processes, and resources. *Example:* A car manufacturer plans to reduce paint-shop defects by 30%.
- **D (Do):** Execute the plan on a small scale. *Example:* Pilot a new spray-gun pressure setting on one assembly line.
- **C (Check):** Measure the actual outcome against the expected. *Example:* Defect rate drops from 4.2% to 2.9% — a 31% reduction.
- **A (Act):** If successful, standardize the change across all lines. If not, revise the plan and loop again.

The cycle then repeats — driving **kaizen** (continuous incremental improvement).

---

##### Part (b) — Five Pillars of QA + CAPA (7 marks)

**The Five Pillars (5 marks):**

1. **Top Management Commitment:** Without leadership, QA has no resources or authority. Leaders set the *quality policy*.
