2. **Documented Standards & SOPs:** Every repeatable activity has a written procedure, eliminating operator-dependent variance.
3. **Training & Competency:** Workers are trained on SOPs, statistical tools, and safety — measured via competency matrices.
4. **Process Audits:** Internal audits verify system compliance; external audits (ISO certification) provide third-party assurance.
5. **CAPA:** The mechanism that turns failures into system improvements.

**CAPA & Continuous Improvement (2 marks):**
- **Corrective Action (CA):** Removes the *root cause* of a *detected* non-conformity so it does not recur.
- **Preventive Action (PA):** Removes the *root cause* of a *potential* non-conformity so it never occurs.
- Together, CAPA closes the feedback loop: each defect becomes a **system strengthening event**, embodying the philosophy *“every defect is a teacher.”*

---

### ✏️ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** concept with the **PAF model**. Given the following data for a plant (in ₹ Lakhs): $C_P = 10$, $C_A = 15$, $C_{IF} = 40$, $C_{EF} = 30$. Compute the **Total COQ**, **Conformance Cost**, and **Non-Conformance Cost**. If a QA initiative raises prevention cost by ₹ 8 Lakhs but reduces internal failure by 40% and external failure by 30%, calculate the **new total COQ** and the **net savings**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **DPMO**. A process produces **1,800 defects** across **10,000 units** with **4 defect opportunities per unit**. Calculate the **DPMO** and the **process sigma level** using the empirical formula. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — COQ with Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**PAF Model Theory (2 marks):**
COQ classifies quality-related expenditure into:
- $C_P$ — Prevention costs (planning, training, SOPs).
- $C_A$ — Appraisal costs (inspection, testing).
- $C_{IF}$ — Internal failure costs (rework, scrap).
- $C_{EF}$ — External failure costs (warranty, returns).

**Initial Calculations (3 marks):**

$$C_{COQ} = 10 + 15 + 40 + 30 = 95 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$C_{Conf} = C_P + C_A = 10 + 15 = 25 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$C_{NonConf} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 40 + 30 = 70 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Post-Initiative Calculations (2 marks):**

- New prevention cost: $C_P^{new} = 10 + 8 = 18$ Lakhs
- New internal failure: $C_{IF}^{new} = 40 \times (1 - 0.40) = 24$ Lakhs
- New external failure: $C_{EF}^{new} = 30 \times (1 - 0.30) = 21$ Lakhs

$$C_{COQ}^{new} = 18 + 15 + 24 + 21 = 78 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{Net Savings} = 95 - 78 = 17 \text{ Lakhs per year}$$

**Conclusion:** A modest ₹ 8L prevention investment yields ₹ 17L annual savings — a **2.1× ROI**, validating the QA economic logic.

---

##### Part (b) — DPMO & Sigma Level Calculation (7 marks)

**DPMO Definition (2 marks):**
**Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** is the number of defects, normalized by one million opportunities. It allows comparison of processes with different complexity.

$$DPMO = \frac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \times O}$$

where:
- $D$ = total number of defects
- $N$ = number of units produced
- $O$ = defect opportunities per unit

**Calculation (3 marks):**

$$DPMO = \frac{1{,}800 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000 \times 4} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{9}}{40{,}000} = 45{,}000$$

**Sigma Level Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\ln(45{,}000) \approx 10.714$$

$$\sigma_{level} \approx 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \times 10.714}$$

$$2.221 \times 10.714 \approx 23.796$$

$$29.37 - 23.796 = 5.574$$

$$\sqrt{5.574} \approx 2.361$$

$$\sigma_{level} \approx 0.8406 + 2.361 = 3.20 \; \sigma$$

**Conclusion:** The process operates at **~3.20 sigma**, far below the world-class benchmark of 6-sigma (3.4 DPMO).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Conflating QA with QC:** Writing "QA means checking the product" = 0 marks. *Always emphasize prevention vs. detection.*
> 2. **Linear PDCA diagrams:** Drawing PDCA as a straight arrow sequence loses 2 marks. *Always close the loop with an arrow from ACT back to PLAN.*
> 3. **DPMO denominator confusion:** Forgetting to multiply $N \times O$ (using only $N$) inflates DPMO by factor of opportunities. *Sanity-check: DPMO should be > Defect Rate per unit.*
> 4. **Sigma formula misuse:** Applying $\sigma = \Phi^{-1}(1 - DPMO/10^6)$ directly gives the *short-term* sigma; the empirical formula with the 0.8406 bias correction gives the **industry-convention long-term sigma** — be explicit about which one KTU expects.
> 5. **No units in final answers:** A "savings of 17" without "Lakhs" = -0.5 marks per question.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize Before Walking into the Exam Hall**

- ✅ **QA** = *proactive, system-wide, prevention-driven*; **QC** = *reactive, product-specific, detection-driven*.
- ✅ **PDCA** = *Plan → Do → Check → Act*, always circular, drives **kaizen**.
- ✅ **Five Pillars of QA:** Leadership, Standards, Training, Audits, CAPA.
- ✅ **CAPA:** Corrective (for detected NC) + Preventive (for potential NC).
- ✅ **PAF Model:** $C_{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$.
- ✅ **Optimal QA spending:** Maximize *Prevention* and *Appraisal*; minimize *Failure* costs.
- ✅ **DPMO Formula:** $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$.
- ✅ **Sigma Formula:** $\sigma_{level} \approx 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(DPMO)}$.
- ✅ **Six Sigma Benchmark:** 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO).
- ✅ **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ — *every deviation from target incurs a loss*.
- ✅ **Quality Pioneers to Remember:**
    - **Deming** → PDCA, 14 Points, statistical quality.
    - **Juran** → Quality Trilogy (planning, control, improvement).
    - **Crosby** → Zero Defects, "Quality is Free."
    - **Feigenbaum** → Total Quality Control (precursor to TQM).
    - **Ishikawa** → Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) Diagram, Quality Circles.
    - **Taguchi** → Robust Design, Loss Function.
- ✅ **Quality ≠ Reliability ≠ Grade.** A luxury product and a budget product can both be high-quality.
- ✅ **Cost of Conformance < Cost of Non-Conformance** → A sign of an under-invested QA system.

> 🎯 **Last-Mantra for KTU 2024:** *“Quality is not inspected in — it is built in. QA is the architect; QC is the policeman.”*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Planning (QP)** is the systematic process of identifying the quality standards required for a project, product, or service, and determining the operational processes, resources, and metrics necessary to achieve those standards. As per the KTU 2024 OEMET615 syllabus, Quality Planning is the **first of the three foundational quality management processes**, alongside **Quality Assurance (QA)** and **Quality Control (QC)**.

In formal ISO 9000:2015 / PMBOK-aligned terminology:

> **Quality Planning** is the activity of establishing quality objectives, standards, and specifications, and of determining the operational means, controls, and metrics required to meet them.

The **primary output** of Quality Planning is a **Quality Plan** — a formal document that specifies:
- Quality standards and metrics applicable to the project/product
- Acceptance criteria for deliverables
- Verification and validation activities
- Roles, responsibilities, and authorities
- Tools, techniques, and procedures to be used

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Planning a Wedding**

Imagine you are planning a wedding. Before the big day, you do not simply show up and "hope for the best." You sit down and:
1. Decide what a "successful wedding" looks like (venue, food, music, guest count).
2. Choose vendors, set budgets, define dress codes, and assign responsibilities.
3. Create a checklist and a timeline.

That whole **pre-event preparation** is **Quality Planning**. The actual wedding day is execution. If your planning is weak, the day itself will be chaotic — regardless of how good your photographer or chef is.

**Engineering Translation:**
- The "wedding" = the manufactured product or delivered service.
- The "plan" = the **Quality Plan**.
- The "vendors" = suppliers, process steps, inspection teams.
- The "checklist" = quality standards, acceptance criteria, KPIs.

Without Quality Planning, the organization is essentially **"showing up to the wedding without a plan"** — leading to rework, defects, customer dissatisfaction, and inflated costs.

---

## 1.3 Position of Quality Planning in the Quality Trilogy

Quality Management is built on **three pillars** (popularized by Juran, a key KTU-recommended theorist):

| Pillar | Focus | Question Answered |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Planning (QP)** | Designing the system to meet quality | *"What do we need? How will we achieve it?"* |
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Operational evaluation of outputs | *"Are we meeting the standards?"* |
| **Quality Improvement (QI)** | Raising performance to new levels | *"How do we get better?"* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** The single most asked 3-mark question in OEMET615 Module 1 is *"Differentiate between Quality Planning, Quality Assurance, and Quality Control."* Master this distinction early.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following key performance indicators (**KPIs**) and metrics emerge from Quality Planning activities:

- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — measured in monetary units (e.g., **₹, $**)
- **Defect Rate / Parts Per Million (PPM)** — defects per million opportunities
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — expressed as a **percentage (%)**
- **Process Capability Index ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$)** — dimensionless ratio
- **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** — index from 0 to 100

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Quality Planning Necessary? (The "Why")

Quality Planning is the **upstream, design-phase** activity. Without it, an organization suffers from:

1. **Reactive fire-fighting** — defects are detected *after* they occur (late, costly).
2. **Misaligned customer expectations** — products are built based on guesswork.
3. **Wasted resources** — time, material, and labor are spent reworking bad output.
4. **Compliance failures** — regulatory/ISO audits fail due to undocumented processes.

> **Engineering Utility:** In sectors like **automotive (IATF 16949)**, **medical devices (ISO 13485)**, and **aerospace (AS9100)**, Quality Planning is a *legal and contractual* requirement — not optional.

---

## 2.2 Inputs to Quality Planning (KTU High-Yield)

The ITTO (Inputs, Tools, Techniques, Outputs) framework is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** topic.

### **A. Inputs**
| Input | Description |
|---|---|
| **Quality Policy** | Top-management's overall intent and direction (e.g., *"Achieve <50 PPM defect rate by 2026"*). |
| **Scope Statement** | Defines project boundaries — what is in and out of scope. |
| **Product/Service Description** | Technical specs, features, and performance characteristics. |
| **Standards & Regulations** | ISO 9001, IATF 16949, BIS, CE marking, FDA, etc. |
| **Customer Requirements (Voice of Customer – VOC)** | Captured via surveys, interviews, focus groups, complaints. |
| **Historical Data / Lessons Learned** | Defect logs, audit reports, previous project metrics. |
| **Risk Register** | Identified quality-related risks and their severity. |

### **B. Tools & Techniques**
- Brainstorming
- Benchmarking
- Cost-benefit analysis
- **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** — *House of Quality*
- **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**
- Cause-and-effect (Ishikawa / Fishbone) diagram
- Statistical sampling design
- Process mapping & flowcharts

### **C. Outputs**
- **Quality Plan** (the master document)
- **Quality Metrics** (measurable indicators)
- **Quality Checklists** (line-item verifications)
- **Process Improvement Plan**
- **Acceptance Criteria** (definition of "done")

---

## 2.3 Steps in Developing a Quality Plan (The "How")

A Quality Plan is built in **8 systematic steps**:

1. **Identify applicable quality standards** — ISO, BIS, industry-specific.
2. **Define quality objectives** — SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
3. **Identify stakeholders and responsibilities** — RACI matrix (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed).
4. **Determine processes and their interactions** — SIPOC (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers).
5. **Set quality metrics and acceptance criteria** — KPIs, tolerances, $C_{pk}$ targets.
6. **Select tools and techniques** — FMEA, SPC, 5-Why, control charts.
7. **Document the plan** — formal approval, version control.
8. **Review and continuously improve** — PDCA cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **directly testable** in Part A (3-mark) numerical questions in KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Dimension | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CoQ} = P + A + IF + EF$ | P=Prevention, A=Appraisal, IF=Internal Failure, EF=External Failure | Currency (₹, $) | Cost-of-Quality analysis |
| 2 | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first inspection}}{\text{Total units inspected}} \times 100$ | First Pass Yield | **%** | Process capability |
| 3 | $\text{Defect Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Number of defects}}{\text{Number of opportunities}} \times 10^6$ | Defect rate | **PPM (parts per million)** | Six-Sigma metric |
| 4 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N}}$ | Standard deviation (population) | Same as $x$ | SPC, control charts |
| 5 | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability (centered) | Dimensionless | Capability index |
| 6 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability (off-center) | Dimensionless | Real-world capability |
| 7 | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number (FMEA) | Score 1–1000 | FMEA prioritization |
| 8 | $\text{Yield} = e^{-DPMO/N} \times 100\%$ | Rolled throughput yield | **%** | Six-Sigma yield |
| 9 | $\text{ROL} = \text{Average Lead Time} \times \text{Average Daily Demand}$ | Reorder Level (planning input) | Units | Inventory planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Warning:** In the markdown table above, the symbols $\vert$ and $\mid$ would break KTU's markdown rendering. All absolute value notations have been replaced with `min(...)` or kept as inline LaTeX `$...$` outside the table.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (e.g., Tata Motors, Toyota):** QFD + FMEA are mandatory in the *Advanced Product Quality Planning (APQP)* framework.
- **Software (e.g., Infosys, TCS):** Test plans, defect density targets, and CI/CD quality gates are direct artifacts of Quality Planning.
- **Construction (e.g., L&T, Sobha):** Material specifications, pour checklists, and acceptance criteria are *Quality Plan* outputs.
- **Healthcare (e.g., Apollo Hospitals):** Patient safety protocols, accreditation standards (NABH) drive planning activities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Process Capability Index ($C_p$)

The **Process Capability Index** quantifies how well a process can produce output within specification limits. KTU examiners frequently test this with a numerical problem.

### **Setup**

Given:
- **Upper Specification Limit (USL)**
- **Lower Specification Limit (LSL)**
- **Process Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)**
- **Process Mean ($\mu$)**

The natural tolerance of a process (assuming normality) is **$\pm 3\sigma$** about the mean, spanning a total width of **$6\sigma$**.

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

The process variation width is:

$$
\text{Process Spread} = (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma
$$

The specification window (the "allowable" range) is:

$$
\text{Specification Spread} = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}
$$

The capability index is defined as the **ratio of the specification width to the natural process width**:

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Spread}}{\text{Process Spread}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $C_p = 1.00$ → Process just fits; **0.27% defect rate**.
- $C_p = 1.33$ → Industry minimum; **$\approx 0.006\%$ defect rate (63 PPM)**.
- $C_p = 1.67$ → Six-Sigma capable; **$\approx 0.00006\%$ defect rate (0.6 PPM)**.
- $C_p < 1.00$ → Process is **incapable**; defects are inevitable.

> **Derivation Logic Summary:**
> 1. We define capability as a *ratio*: how much room we have (USL−LSL) versus how much room the process actually uses ($6\sigma$).
> 2. A higher ratio = more margin = fewer defects.
> 3. The factor **6** in the denominator comes from the **empirical rule** that ~99.73% of normally distributed data lies within $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Risk Priority Number (RPN)

**Problem:** A component fails in field tests with the following FMEA scoring:
- **Severity (S) = 8**
- **Occurrence (O) = 5**
- **Detection (D) = 4**

Compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** and state its interpretation.

### **Step 1: Recall the FMEA RPN formula**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

### **Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$
\text{RPN} = 8 \times 5 \times 4
$$

### **Step 3: Compute the product**

$$
\text{RPN} = 160
$$

### **Step 4: Interpretation (KTU 7-Mark Style Answer)**

> Since the RPN of **160** is well above the typical action threshold of **100**, the failure mode must be assigned priority for corrective action. The recommended sequence is:
> 1. First, attempt to **reduce Severity (S)** by design changes.
> 2. Then, **reduce Occurrence (O)** by process control.
> 3. Finally, **improve Detection (D)** by better inspection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Many students forget to provide the *interpretation* of RPN. Always state the **threshold (≥ 100)** and the **recommended action sequence** to fetch full marks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Cost of Quality (CoQ)

A company reports the following annual quality costs:
- Prevention (training, planning) = **₹2,00,000**
- Appraisal (inspection, testing) = **₹1,50,000**
- Internal Failure (rework, scrap) = **₹3,00,000**
- External Failure (warranty, returns) = **₹2,50,000**

**Compute:** (a) Total Cost of Quality, (b) The failure-cost ratio.

### **Step 1: Recall the CoQ formula**

$$
\text{CoQ} = P + A + IF + EF
$$

### **Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$
\text{CoQ} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000
$$

### **Step 3: Total CoQ**

$$
\text{CoQ} = \text{₹}\,9{,}00{,}000
$$

### **Step 4: Failure-cost ratio**

$$
\text{Failure Cost Ratio} = \frac{IF + EF}{\text{CoQ}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Failure Cost Ratio} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Failure Cost Ratio} = \frac{5{,}50{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \approx 61.11\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Over **61% of the quality budget is spent on failure-related activities** — a clear signal that prevention and appraisal investments must increase during the **next planning cycle** to drive down total CoQ.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Quality Planning Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Customer Needs VOC] --> B[Define Quality Objectives SMART]
    B --> C[Identify Applicable Standards ISO, BIS, IATF]
    C --> D[Select Tools QFD, FMEA, SPC]
    D --> E[Define Quality Metrics and Acceptance Criteria]
    E --> F[Document the Quality Plan]
    F --> G{Approval and Sign-off}
    G -- Approved --> H[Execute Quality Assurance QA]
    G -- Rejected --> D
    H --> I[Quality Control QC]
    I --> J[Continuous Improvement PDCA]
    J --> A
```

**Explanation:** The diagram shows the **iterative, closed-loop nature** of Quality Planning. It does not end with a signed document — the lessons from QC and improvement feed back into the *next* planning cycle, embodying the **PDCA (Deming) cycle**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: ITTO Block Architecture for Quality Planning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[INPUTS]
        I1[Quality Policy]
        I2[Customer Requirements VOC]
        I3[Standards and Regulations]
        I4[Historical Data]
        I5[Risk Register]
    end

    subgraph T[Tools and Techniques]
        T1[QFD House of Quality]
        T2[FMEA]
        T3[Benchmarking]
        T4[Brainstorming]
        T5[Cost Benefit Analysis]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUTS]
        O1[Quality Plan]
        O2[Quality Metrics]
        O3[Checklists]
        O4[Acceptance Criteria]
        O5[Process Improvement Plan]
    end

    IN --> T
    T --> OUT
```

**Explanation:** This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the ITTO (Inputs, Tools & Techniques, Outputs) framework, which is the *gold standard* for answering 7-mark ITTO questions in KTU exams.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Quality Trilogy — QP, QA, QC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QP[Quality Planning Design Phase] --> QA[Quality Assurance Process Phase]
    QA --> QC[Quality Control Evaluation Phase]
    QC --> QI[Quality Improvement PDCA]
    QI --> QP
```

**Explanation:** This visualizes **Juran's Quality Trilogy**. The arrows are **bidirectional in spirit** (feedback loops) but drawn linearly for clarity. Note how **Quality Planning is the foundation** — without it, QA and QC have no defined standards to adhere to.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Quality Plan Document Structure (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QP[Quality Plan Document] --> S1[1. Scope and Objectives]
    QP --> S2[2. Applicable Standards]
    QP --> S3[3. Roles and Responsibilities RACI]
    QP --> S4[4. Quality Metrics and KPIs]
    QP --> S5[5. Tools and Procedures]
    QP --> S6[6. Acceptance Criteria]
    QP --> S7[7. Audit and Review Schedule]
    QP --> S8[8. Approval Signatures]
```

**Explanation:** A **Quality Plan** is a living document. The 8 sections shown are industry-standard (compatible with ISO 9001:2015 clause 7.5 and PMBOK 7th Edition). KTU questions often ask students to *list the contents* of a Quality Plan.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Quality Planning is the first step in the quality trilogy." Justify this statement with three supporting arguments.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Quality Planning, as per **Juran's Quality Trilogy**, is the foundational step upon which Quality Assurance and Quality Control are built. The three supporting arguments are:

1. **Establishes the Baseline Standards:** Without QP, there are no defined acceptance criteria — making QC impossible, as one cannot inspect quality into existence against unknown benchmarks.
2. **Prevents Defects Proactively:** QP identifies potential failure modes (via FMEA) and customer needs (via VOC/QFD) *before* production begins, shifting the organization from detection to prevention.
3. **Optimizes Resource Allocation:** QP uses cost-benefit analysis to determine the optimal mix of prevention, appraisal, and failure costs — enabling efficient budget planning.

*(1 mark per point. Total: 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Quality Plan and a Quality Policy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Quality Policy | Quality Plan |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Organization-wide, high-level intent | Project/product specific, detailed |
| **Issued By** | Top Management (e.g., CEO) | Project Manager / Quality Team |
| **Content** | Statements of *intent* (qualitative) | Measurable objectives, metrics, tools (quantitative) |
| **Time Horizon** | Long-term (3–5 years) | Project duration |
| **Example** | *"We are committed to customer satisfaction"* | *"Achieve ≤ 50 PPM defect rate on Line 3 by Q4 2024"* |

*(2 marks for table, 1 mark for example. Total: 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**
**(a)** Explain the **Inputs, Tools & Techniques, and Outputs (ITTO)** of the Quality Planning process with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Describe the **eight key steps** involved in developing a Quality Plan, with reference to ISO 9001:2015. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Inputs of Quality Planning:**

1. **Quality Policy** — The top-management's documented intent regarding quality (e.g., Tata Steel's "Zero Harm" policy). [1 mark]
2. **Voice of Customer (VOC)** — Customer needs gathered through surveys, complaint logs, focus groups, warranty data. [1 mark]
3. **Standards & Regulations** — ISO 9001, IATF 16949, BIS, CE, FDA, etc. [1 mark]
4. **Historical Data & Lessons Learned** — Past defect logs, audit findings, CAPA records. [1 mark]

**Tools & Techniques:**

5. **Quality Function Deployment (QFD) / House of Quality** — Translates customer wants into engineering specs.
6. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — Identifies potential failure modes and computes RPN.
7. **Cost-Benefit Analysis** — Weighs prevention/appraisal costs against failure costs. [1 mark]

**Outputs:**

8. **Quality Plan** — Master document specifying standards, metrics, roles, tools.
9. **Quality Metrics** — Quantifiable indicators (e.g., $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$, PPM ≤ 100).
10. **Acceptance Criteria & Checklists** — Definition of "done" for each deliverable. [1 mark]

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

The eight steps to develop a Quality Plan (mapped to ISO 9001:2015 clauses):

1. **Identify Scope and Objectives** — SMART goals aligned to ISO 9001 Clause 6.2. [1 mark]
2. **Identify Applicable Standards** — ISO, regulatory, customer-specific. [1 mark]
3. **Define Roles and Responsibilities** — RACI matrix. [1 mark]
4. **Map Processes (SIPOC)** — Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers. [1 mark]
5. **Set Quality Metrics** — $C_{pk}$, FPY, PPM, DPMO targets. [1 mark]
6. **Select Tools and Techniques** — SPC, FMEA, 8D, 5-Why, Pareto. [1 mark]
7. **Document and Approve** — Formal sign-off, version control, distribution. [0.5 mark]
8. **Review and Improve** — PDCA cycle, management review meetings. [0.5 mark]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Quality Plan** with **Project Plan**. A Quality Plan is *not* a Gantt chart — it is a *quality-specific* document. Mentioning timelines, milestones, or Gantt charts will cost you 1–2 marks.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**(a)** Discuss the role of **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** and the **House of Quality** in translating customer needs into engineering specifications. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** With a suitable numerical example, compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for three failure modes in an FMEA study, and recommend the action priority. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Quality Function Deployment (QFD):**
- QFD is a structured planning tool introduced by **Yoji Akao (1966)** that translates **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into **technical specifications**. [1 mark]
- It is a matrix-based, cross-functional approach used during the **product design phase** to ensure customer-driven design. [1 mark]

**The House of Quality (HOQ):**
The House of Quality is the **primary matrix of QFD** and is so named because of its house-like shape. It contains 7 key components: [1 mark]

1. **Customer Requirements (WHATs)** — Placed on the left wall (e.g., "easy to use", "durable", "low cost"). [0.5 mark]
2. **Engineering Characteristics (HOWs)** — Placed on the ceiling (e.g., torque, material grade, weight). [0.5 mark]
3. **Relationship Matrix (Central Room)** — Strong/Moderate/Weak correlation between WHATs and HOWs. [0.5 mark]
4. **Technical Correlation (Roof)** — Inter-relationships between engineering characteristics (e.g., weight vs. cost). [0.5 mark]
5. **Customer Importance Ratings (Left side)** — Weighted priorities. [0.5 mark]
6. **Competitive Benchmarking (Right side)** — How competitors perform on each customer need. [0.5 mark]
7. **Target Values (Bottom)** — Quantified engineering targets. [0.5 mark]

> **Benefit:** HOQ prevents the *"voice of the engineer"* from overriding the *"voice of the customer"* by forcing structured cross-functional dialogue.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given Data (assumed for illustration):**

| Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| FM-1: Weld porosity | 8 | 6 | 4 |
| FM-2: Surface scratch | 5 | 7 | 3 |
| FM-3: Dimensional out-of-spec | 9 | 4 | 5 |

**Step 1: Compute RPN for each failure mode**

For FM-1 (Weld porosity):

$$
\text{RPN}_1 = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 6 \times 4 = 192
$$

For FM-2 (Surface scratch):

$$
\text{RPN}_2 = 5 \times 7 \times 3 = 105
$$

For FM-3 (Dimensional out-of-spec):

$$
\text{RPN}_3 = 9 \times 4 \times 5 = 180
$$

**Step 2: Rank by RPN (Highest to Lowest)**

$$
\text{FM-1 (192)} > \text{FM-3 (180)} > \text{FM-2 (105)}
$$

**Step 3: Action Priority Recommendation (1 mark)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommended Priority Order:**
> 1. **FM-1 (RPN 192)** — Highest priority. Reduce Occurrence via SPC on welding parameters; improve Detection with X-ray testing.
> 2. **FM-3 (RPN 180)** — Second priority. Reduce Severity via design tolerance review.
> 3. **FM-2 (RPN 105)** — Third priority. Address after FM-1 and FM-3 are mitigated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Critical for 14-Mark Questions):**
> 1. **Do not skip the interpretation step** — simply listing RPN values without ranking and recommending action loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Do not use wrong scoring scale** — S, O, D are on a **1–10** scale (some texts use 1–5; the KTU 2024 syllabus recommends 1–10). Mixing scales will be penalized.
> 3. **Avoid stating "RPN > 100" as the universal threshold** — it is a guideline, not a rule; some industries use 125 or 150. Always state "as per the conventional industry threshold of 100."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Card for OEMET615 Module 1 — Quality Planning**

- ☐ Quality Planning is the **first of Juran's three quality processes** (QP, QA, QC).
- ☐ A **Quality Plan** is the *output* of Quality Planning — a formal, documented set of standards, metrics, and procedures.
- ☐ A **Quality Policy** is *not* a Quality Plan — the policy is high-level intent; the plan is project-specific, measurable, and detailed.
- ☐ The **ITTO Framework** (Inputs, Tools & Techniques, Outputs) is a guaranteed long-answer question.
- ☐ **VOC** (Voice of Customer) is captured through surveys, interviews, complaint logs, and warranty data.
- ☐ **QFD / House of Quality** translates customer *WHATs* into engineering *HOWs* through a structured matrix.
- ☐ **FMEA** computes **RPN = S × O × D**; action threshold is conventionally **RPN ≥ 100**.
- ☐ The **Process Capability Index** $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$; minimum industry value is **1.33** for a capable process.
- ☐ For an off-center process, use $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$.
- ☐ **Cost of Quality (CoQ) = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure**.
- ☐ A high **Failure Cost Ratio (IF + EF)/CoQ > 50%** indicates under-investment in prevention — a Quality Planning red flag.
- ☐ **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is the engine of continuous improvement that closes the loop back to Quality Planning.
- ☐ **ISO 9001:2015** is the globally recognized framework anchoring Quality Planning in clause 6.2 (Quality Objectives) and clause 7.5 (Documented Information).
- ☐ **SIPOC** (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers) is the standard process-mapping tool used in Quality Planning workshops.
- ☐ Always use a **RACI matrix** to assign roles — Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed.

> **Final Examiner's Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE, the **3-mark question will test definitions and the 14-mark question will test application (ITTO, FMEA, CoQ, $C_p/C_{pk}$).** Master both layers — theory *and* numericals — to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Quality

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Quality** is the totality of characteristics of an entity (product, service, or process) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs. It is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfills requirements (ISO 9000:2015 standard).

- A product or service has **Quality** if it consistently meets customer expectations, performs reliably, and conforms to design specifications.
- Quality is not merely "luxury" or "high-grade"; it is essentially **fitness for use** (Juran's definition) and **conformance to requirements** (Crosby's definition).
- It is a relative and dynamic concept — what was considered high quality yesterday may be ordinary today.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1.1):**
> Quality is multi-dimensional. It encompasses:
> 1. **Quality of Design** — Engineering specifications, features
> 2. **Quality of Conformance** — Degree to which the product matches the design
> 3. **Quality of Performance** — How the product functions in real-world use
> 4. **Quality of Service** — Support and maintenance after delivery

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Restaurant Analogy:**
Imagine you go to a restaurant for dinner. The food arrives hot, the portion size matches the menu description, the waiter is polite, the bill is accurate, and the meal tastes excellent. Would you return? Definitely yes. This consistent satisfaction across **taste, service, hygiene, ambience, and value** is exactly what *Quality* means in a business context.

In the **manufacturing world**, "Quality" means a similar consistency — every product rolling off the line should meet the same standard, every time, without defects. Just like the restaurant that delivers a perfect meal *every* time, a quality-driven factory delivers flawless products *every* time.

### 1.3 Definition of Quality Management (QM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Quality Management (QM)** is the coordinated set of activities and techniques used to direct and control an organization at every level to ensure that products and services meet customer expectations and continually improve performance. It is the umbrella discipline that integrates quality planning, quality control, quality assurance, and quality improvement.

### 1.4 Dimensions of Quality (Garvin's 8 Dimensions)

David Garvin of Harvard identified **8 critical dimensions** of product/service quality. These are high-yield for KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions.

| # | Dimension | Simple Meaning | Engineering Example |
|---|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Performance** | Primary operating characteristics | Top speed of a car |
| 2 | **Features** | Extra characteristics beyond basic function | Bluetooth, sunroof in a car |
| 3 | **Reliability** | Probability of failure-free operation over time | MTBF of an engine |
| 4 | **Conformance** | Degree of match to specifications | Tolerance of shaft diameter |
| 5 | **Durability** | Useful life before replacement | Cycles to fatigue failure |
| 6 | **Serviceability** | Speed, ease of repair | Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) |
| 7 | **Aesthetics** | Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound) | Surface finish of a product |
| 8 | **Perceived Quality** | Customer's subjective impression | Brand reputation |

### 1.5 Quality Management — The Big Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** Quality Management is NOT a single department's job. It is a **total organizational commitment** that flows from top management → middle management → shop floor → suppliers → customers.

The evolution of Quality Management can be summarized in stages:
1. **Inspection Era** (1900s–1920s) — Detecting defects after production
2. **Quality Control Era** (1930s–1950s) — Statistical methods to monitor processes
3. **Quality Assurance Era** (1960s–1970s) — Building quality into the process
4. **Strategic Quality Management / TQM Era** (1980s–Present) — Organization-wide strategic focus
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Quality Management

Quality Management rests on **four fundamental activities** (Juran's Trilogy adapted to QM):

#### 2.1.1 Quality Planning (QP)
- Identify customers and their needs.
- Develop product/process features that meet those needs.
- Establish quality goals and metrics.
- Plan the process to deliver the required quality level.

**Why:** "Quality must be planned in; it cannot be inspected in." — A proactive, not reactive, approach.

#### 2.1.2 Quality Control (QC)
- The operational techniques and activities used to **monitor** and **verify** that products and services meet specifications.
- Involves inspection, measurement, statistical process control (SPC), and corrective actions.
- Focus: **detecting and correcting defects** in the current process.

**How:** Compare actual performance against standards → identify deviations → take corrective action.

#### 2.1.3 Quality Assurance (QA)
- A **systematic, planned set of activities** implemented within the quality system to provide confidence that the product/service will meet quality requirements.
- Focus: **preventing defects** by improving the process itself (not just inspecting output).
- Documented in quality manuals, procedures, and work instructions.

**Why:** QA is process-oriented, QC is product-oriented.

#### 2.1.4 Quality Improvement (QI)
- Continuous, ongoing efforts to **enhance** the product, process, or system.
- Tools: Kaizen, PDCA cycle, Six Sigma, Benchmarking, Root Cause Analysis.
- Philosophies: TQM, Lean, Six Sigma, BPR (Business Process Reengineering).

### 2.2 The Quality Gurus (Most Important for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** The "Quality Gurus" section is the most frequently asked topic in Module 1. Memorize each guru's name, contribution, and famous quote.

#### 2.2.1 W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993) — The Father of Quality Movement

- **14 Points for Management** — A philosophical framework for transforming business effectiveness.
- **PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel/Shewhart Cycle):** Plan → Do → Check → Act.
- **System of Profound Knowledge:** Appreciation of a system, Knowledge of variation, Theory of knowledge, Psychology.
- **Famous Quote:** *"In God we trust; all others must bring data."*
- **Impact:** Revitalized Japanese industry after WWII; the Deming Prize is awarded annually in Japan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Deming's 14 Points — Quick List (high-yield):**
> 1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement.
> 2. Adopt the new philosophy.
> 3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
> 4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
> 5. Improve constantly every process.
> 6. Institute training on the job.
> 7. Institute leadership.
> 8. Drive out fear.
> 9. Break down barriers between departments.
> 10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
> 11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
> 12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
> 13. Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.
> 14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

#### 2.2.2 Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008)

- **Juran's Trilogy:** Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
- Defined Quality as **"Fitness for Use"**.
- **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** applied to quality — 80% of problems come from 20% of causes.
- **Quality Spiral (Juran's Spiral):** Quality improvement is a continuous, upward spiraling process.
- Emphasized **human element** and management responsibility for quality.

#### 2.2.3 Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001)

- Defined Quality as **"Conformance to Requirements"**.
- Coined the term **"Zero Defects"** (ZD).
- Philosophy: **"Quality is Free"** — the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of correction.
- Advocated a **4 Absolutes of Quality:**
  1. Quality = Conformance to requirements
  2. Prevention, not inspection
  3. Zero defects performance standard
  4. Measure quality by the price of non-conformance

#### 2.2.4 Kaoru Ishikawa (1915–1989)

- Developed the **Ishikawa Diagram (Fishbone/Cause-and-Effect Diagram)** for root cause analysis.
- Pioneered **Quality Circles** in Japan — small groups of workers who meet regularly to solve quality problems.
- Promoted **Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)** — quality is everyone's responsibility, not just the QC department.
- Emphasized **statistical quality control** and **graphical tools**.

#### 2.2.5 Genichi Taguchi (1924–2012)

- Defined **Quality Loss Function (QLF):** Any deviation from the target causes a loss to society, which increases quadratically with the deviation.
- Emphasized **Robust Design** — products that perform consistently under varying real-world conditions.
- **Taguchi Loss Function Formula:** Loss is proportional to the **square of deviation** from target.

#### 2.2.6 Armand V. Feigenbaum (1922–2014)

- Coined the term **Total Quality Control (TQC)** in 1956.
- Definition of Quality: *"The total composite product and service characteristics of marketing, engineering, manufacturing, and maintenance through which the product and service in use will meet the expectations of the customer."*
- Advocated a **system-wide, customer-focused** approach to quality.
- Introduced concept of **"Hidden Plant"** — a plant within the plant dedicated to rework and scrap (wasted capacity).

### 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure quality. It is the single most important metric in Quality Management accounting.

COQ = **Cost of Good Quality (Prevention + Appraisal)** + **Cost of Poor Quality (Internal Failure + External Failure)**

#### 2.3.1 Cost of Good Quality (Conformance Costs)
- **Prevention Costs:** Money spent to *prevent* defects (training, quality planning, process improvement, supplier capability).
- **Appraisal Costs:** Money spent to *detect* defects (inspection, testing, audits, calibration).

#### 2.3.2 Cost of Poor Quality (Non-Conformance Costs)
- **Internal Failure Costs:** Defects found *before* reaching the customer (scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime).
- **External Failure Costs:** Defects found *after* reaching the customer (warranty claims, returns, recalls, lost goodwill, lawsuits).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relationship | Description |
|---------|------------------------|-------------|
| Cost of Quality (COQ) | $$COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$ | $C_P$=Prevention, $C_A$=Appraisal, $C_{IF}$=Internal Failure, $C_{EF}$=External Failure |
| Taguchi Quality Loss | $$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2$$ | $L$=Loss, $y$=actual value, $m$=target, $k$=quality loss coefficient |
| Quality Loss Coefficient | $$k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$ | $A_0$=loss at tolerance limit, $\Delta_0$=tolerance limit |
| Cost Ratio (Failure to Prevention) | $$CR = \dfrac{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}{C_P}$$ | Measures efficiency of prevention investment |
| Quality Level (Defect Rate) | $$QL = \dfrac{\text{Defective Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}$$ | Parts per million (Six Sigma metric) |
| Six Sigma Level | $$\sigma_{LT} = 3.4 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$$ | Target performance level |
| Process Capability Index | $$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$ | Compares specification width to process spread |
| Process Capability (shifted) | $$C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$ | Accounts for process mean shift |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of These Formulas:**
> - **COQ** helps managers identify whether the company is *over-investing in inspection* or *under-investing in prevention*. The optimal COQ curve has its minimum at the *economic quality level*.
> - **Taguchi Loss Function** shifts thinking from "within tolerance = no loss" to "any deviation from target = some loss." This drives continuous improvement even when specs are met.
> - **Six Sigma** quantifies world-class performance and is the cornerstone of Motorola, GE, and Honeywell's quality strategy.

### 2.5 Customer Satisfaction and Customer-Driven Quality

- **Voice of Customer (VOC):** Capturing what customers want through surveys, focus groups, complaints, warranty data, and social media.
- **Quality must be defined by the customer**, not by the producer. Producers translate customer needs into technical specifications (CTQs — Critical-to-Quality characteristics).
- **Customer Satisfaction** = Perception of product performance vs. customer expectation.
- If **Performance > Expectation** → High satisfaction (delight)
- If **Performance = Expectation** → Satisfaction
- If **Performance < Expectation** → Dissatisfaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse "Quality" with "Grade." Grade is a category of features; Quality is the *degree of excellence*. A low-grade product can still be high quality if it consistently meets its (low) specifications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Taguchi Quality Loss Function

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin:** Prof. Genichi Taguchi redefined quality as the *loss a product imparts to society from the moment it is created*. This includes not only the loss to the manufacturer but also to the customer.

#### Step 1: Define the loss in terms of deviation from target

When a product's quality characteristic ($y$) deviates from the target value ($m$), the loss to society increases. Taguchi proposed a **quadratic relationship**:

$$
L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2
$$

Where:
- $L(y)$ = Loss to society (in monetary units)
- $k$ = Quality loss coefficient (a constant)
- $y$ = Actual value of the quality characteristic
- $m$ = Target value

#### Step 2: Determine the loss coefficient $k$ using the tolerance limit

At the tolerance limit $\Delta_0$ (i.e., $y - m = \Delta_0$), the product reaches a critical functional failure state, and the loss to society equals $A_0$ (cost of replacement/repair at failure boundary).

$$
A_0 = k \cdot \Delta_0^2
$$

Solving for $k$:

$$
k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}
$$

#### Step 3: Substitute back to obtain the complete loss function

$$
L(y) = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \cdot (y - m)^2
$$

**Step-by-step numerical example:**

A shaft is designed with target diameter $m = 25.00$ mm. The tolerance limit is $\Delta_0 = \pm 0.05$ mm. At the tolerance limit, the rework/replacement cost is $A_0 = 400$ currency units.

**(a) Find the quality loss coefficient $k$:**

$$
k = \dfrac{400}{(0.05)^2} = \dfrac{400}{0.0025} = 160{,}000
$$

**(b) Loss when the shaft is produced at $y = 25.02$ mm:**

$$
L(25.02) = 160{,}000 \cdot (25.02 - 25.00)^2 = 160{,}000 \cdot (0.02)^2
$$

$$
L(25.02) = 160{,}000 \cdot 0.0004 = 64 \text{ currency units}
$$

**(c) Loss when the shaft is at the tolerance limit $y = 25.05$ mm:**

$$
L(25.05) = 160{,}000 \cdot (0.05)^2 = 160{,}000 \cdot 0.0025 = 400 \text{ currency units}
$$

This confirms the formula: at the tolerance boundary, the loss equals $A_0$ by definition.

**(d) Loss when produced exactly at target $y = 25.00$ mm:**

$$
L(25.00) = 160{,}000 \cdot (0)^2 = 0 \text{ currency units}
$$

Perfect! The loss is zero only at the target — a powerful incentive for design robustness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Traditional thinking says any product *within specification* (between 24.95 and 25.05 mm) is "good." Taguchi's loss function says even products inside the spec incur *some* loss. A product at 25.04 mm incurs 256 units of loss even though it is "in spec." This drives continuous improvement toward the target.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Cost of Quality (COQ) Optimization

#### Step 1: Express each cost component as a function of defect level

Let $d$ = fraction of defective products (0 ≤ $d$ ≤ 1).

- **Prevention cost** $C_P(d)$: Inversely related to defects. As prevention spending increases, defects drop.

$$
C_P(d) = a \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{d}\right)
$$

where $a$ is a constant representing prevention efficiency.

- **Appraisal cost** $C_A$: Roughly constant per unit inspected, but increases as more inspection is added:

$$
C_A(d) = b \cdot (1 - d) + c
$$

where $b$ = inspection cost per good unit, $c$ = fixed appraisal cost.

- **Internal failure cost** $C_{IF}$: Increases with defect level.

$$
C_{IF}(d) = c_1 \cdot d
$$

- **External failure cost** $C_{EF}$: Increases *more steeply* than internal failure (as defects escape to customers):

$$
C_{EF}(d) = c_2 \cdot d^2
$$

#### Step 2: Total COQ

$$
COQ(d) = a \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{d}\right) + b \cdot (1-d) + c + c_1 \cdot d + c_2 \cdot d^2
$$

#### Step 3: Differentiate and set to zero to find optimum

$$
\dfrac{d(COQ)}{dd} = -\dfrac{a}{d} - b + c_1 + 2 c_2 \cdot d = 0
$$

Solving for optimum defect level $d^*$:

$$
2 c_2 \cdot d^{*2} + (c_1 - b) \cdot d^{*} - a = 0
$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$
d^{*} = \dfrac{-(c_1 - b) + \sqrt{(c_1 - b)^2 + 8 a c_2}}{4 c_2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Economic Quality Level (EQL):** The defect fraction $d^*$ that minimizes total COQ is called the **Economic Quality Level**. Below this level, prevention costs rise faster than failure costs are reduced. Above this level, failure costs dominate. This is a key insight for managers — investing *too much* in prevention is also not optimal.

#### Step 4: Numerical Example

Suppose $a = 1000$, $b = 50$, $c_1 = 500$, $c_2 = 2000$, $c = 0$ (fixed appraisal cost ignored).

$$
d^{*} = \dfrac{-(500 - 50) + \sqrt{(500 - 50)^2 + 8 \cdot 1000 \cdot 2000}}{4 \cdot 2000}
$$

$$
d^{*} = \dfrac{-450 + \sqrt{202500 + 16000000}}{8000} = \dfrac{-450 + \sqrt{16202500}}{8000}
$$

$$
\sqrt{16202500} \approx 4025.17
$$

$$
d^{*} = \dfrac{-450 + 4025.17}{8000} = \dfrac{3575.17}{8000} \approx 0.447
$$

So the optimal defect level is about **44.7%** — meaning in this scenario, total quality cost is minimized when the defect rate is around 45%. (This is for illustration; in real quality-driven firms, the EQL is far lower.)

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Quality Gurus Comparison

This matrix is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** comparing gurus.

| Aspect | Deming | Juran | Crosby | Ishikawa | Taguchi | Feigenbaum |
|--------|--------|-------|--------|----------|---------|------------|
| Definition of Quality | Predictable uniformity | Fitness for Use | Conformance to Requirements | Customer satisfaction, CWQC | Loss to society (target-driven) | Composite of all marketing/engineering features |
| Key Contribution | 14 Points, PDCA, System of Profound Knowledge | Juran's Trilogy, Quality Spiral | Zero Defects, 4 Absolutes, "Quality is Free" | Fishbone Diagram, Quality Circles | Quality Loss Function, Robust Design | Total Quality Control (TQC) |
| Key Focus | Statistical process control, variation | Customer, planning, control | Prevention, training, cost of non-conformance | People involvement, tools | Off-line quality control, design | System-wide integration |
| Famous Quote | "In God we trust, all others bring data" | "Fitness for use is the basic definition of quality" | "Quality is free" | "Quality begins and ends with education" | "Quality is the loss a product imparts to society" | "Quality is a way of managing the business" |
| Origin / Country | USA | USA | USA | Japan | Japan | USA |
| Era | 1950s onwards | 1950s onwards | 1960s onwards | 1960s onwards | 1980s onwards | 1950s onwards |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cost of Quality (COQ) Categories

| Category | Type | Examples | When It Occurs | Who Bears It |
|----------|------|----------|----------------|--------------|
| Prevention | Cost of Conformance | Quality planning, training, supplier development, process design | Before defect occurs | Manufacturer |
| Appraisal | Cost of Conformance | Inspection, testing, calibration, audits | During/after production | Manufacturer |
| Internal Failure | Cost of Non-Conformance | Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime | Before customer receives product | Manufacturer |
| External Failure | Cost of Non-Conformance | Warranty, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost reputation | After customer receives product | Customer + Manufacturer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule (Crosby):** The cost of prevention is always less than the cost of failure. Increasing prevention spending reduces internal and external failure costs more than proportionally. The COQ curve U-shape illustrates the **optimum** where total cost is minimum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Components of Quality Management (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Quality Management] --> B[Quality Planning]
    A --> C[Quality Control]
    A --> D[Quality Assurance]
    A --> E[Quality Improvement]

    B --> B1[Identify customer needs]
    B --> B2[Set quality goals]
    B --> B3[Design processes]

    C --> C1[Inspection]
    C --> C2[Statistical Process Control]
    C --> C3[Corrective action]

    D --> D1[Quality manuals]
    D --> D2[Process audits]
    D --> D3[ISO 9000 documentation]

    E --> E1[Kaizen - continuous improvement]
    E --> E2[PDCA Cycle]
    E --> E3[Six Sigma DMAIC]
    E --> E4[Benchmarking]

    A --> F[Supported by]
    F --> F1[Top Management Commitment]
    F --> F2[Employee Involvement]
    F --> F3[Customer Focus]
    F --> F4[Continuous Improvement]
    F --> F5[Fact-based Decision Making]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Deming's PDCA Cycle (Shewhart Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PLAN] --> D1[DO]
    D1 --> C1[CHECK]
    C1 --> A1[ACT]
    A1 --> P1

    P1[PLAN] --> P2[Identify problem, analyze process, develop plan]
    D1[DO] --> D2[Implement the plan on small scale]
    C1[CHECK] --> C2[Measure results, compare to expected]
    A1[ACT] --> A2[Standardize if successful, restart if not]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Juran's Trilogy of Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    JQ[Jurans Trilogy] --> QP[Quality Planning]
    JQ --> QC[Quality Control]
    JQ --> QI[Quality Improvement]

    QP --> QP1[Identify customers]
    QP --> QP2[Determine their needs]
    QP --> QP3[Develop product to meet needs]
    QP --> QP4[Establish quality goals]
    QP --> QP5[Plan process to deliver goals]

    QC --> QC1[Evaluate actual quality]
    QC --> QC2[Compare actual to goals]
    QC --> QC3[Act on the difference]

    QI --> QI1[Establish infrastructure]
    QI --> QI2[Identify improvement projects]
    QI --> QI3[Establish project teams]
    QI --> QI4[Provide training and resources]
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Cost of Quality (COQ) Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    COQ[Cost of Quality] --> CGQ[Cost of Good Quality - Conformance]
    COQ --> CPQ[Cost of Poor Quality - Non-Conformance]

    CGQ --> PREV[Prevention Costs]
    CGQ --> APPR[Appraisal Costs]

    CPQ --> INTF[Internal Failure Costs]
    CPQ --> EXTF[External Failure Costs]

    PREV --> PR1[Quality training]
    PREV --> PR2[Process design]
    PREV --> PR3[Supplier capability]
    PREV --> PR4[Quality planning]

    APPR --> AP1[Inspection and testing]
    APPR --> AP2[Calibration of equipment]
    APPR --> AP3[Quality audits]

    INTF --> IF1[Scrap]
    INTF --> IF2[Rework and repair]
    INTF --> IF3[Re-inspection]
    INTF --> IF4[Downtime due to defects]

    EXTF --> EF1[Warranty claims]
    EXTF --> EF2[Product returns]
    EXTF --> EF3[Recalls]
    EXTF --> EF4[Lost customer goodwill]
    EXTF --> EF5[Lawsuits]
```

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Evolution of Quality Management

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Inspection Era<br/>1900s-1920s] --> E2[Quality Control Era<br/>1930s-1950s]
    E2 --> E3[Quality Assurance Era<br/>1960s-1970s]
    E3 --> E4[Strategic Quality Management<br/>TQM Era 1980s-Present]

    E1 --> E1A[Detect defects after production<br/>Separation of good and bad]
    E2 --> E2A[Use statistical methods<br/>SPC charts and sampling]
    E3 --> E3A[Build quality into process<br/>Systematic prevention]
    E4 --> E4A[Organization-wide commitment<br/>Customer focus and continuous improvement]
```

---

### 4.6 Block Diagram: Taguchi's Quality Loss Function Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Target Value m] --> T2[Zero Loss]
    T1 --> T3[Small Deviation y-m small]
    T3 --> T4[Small Loss k times y-m squared]
    T1 --> T5[Tolerance Limit Delta0]
    T5 --> T6[High Loss A0 - Functional Failure]
    T1 --> T7[Large Deviation beyond tolerance]
    T7 --> T8[Very High Loss - Customer Failure]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Quality and list any two dimensions of quality according to Garvin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Quality is the totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs (ISO 9000:2015). According to Joseph Juran, quality is "fitness for use." According to Philip Crosby, quality is "conformance to requirements."

**Two dimensions of quality (1 mark):**
1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics of a product (e.g., mileage of a car).
2. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over a specified period of time.

*(Any two from the 8 dimensions of Garvin can be given. Students are advised to memorize at least 4 dimensions for flexibility.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the Juran's Trilogy of Quality. Briefly explain any one component.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Juran's Trilogy (1.5 marks):** Juran defined quality management as consisting of three universal processes:
1. Quality Planning
2. Quality Control
3. Quality Improvement

**Explanation of Quality Control (1.5 marks):** Quality control is the process of evaluating actual quality performance, comparing it with quality goals, and acting on the difference. It involves measurement, inspection, and corrective action to ensure the product conforms to specifications. For example, in a manufacturing line, QC would involve checking the diameter of finished shafts against the specified tolerance of 25.00 ± 0.05 mm and segregating or reworking any out-of-spec units.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in ESE (End Semester Exam) carry 14 marks each and feature an **internal choice** — the student attempts ONE of two alternative questions. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the contributions of **W. Edwards Deming** to Quality Management. Discuss his **14 Points for Management** with at least 8 key points.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Cost of Quality (COQ)**? Explain the four categories of COQ with examples. Illustrate with a diagram how the total COQ varies with the level of prevention investment.   **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — Deming's Contributions (7 marks):**

W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993) is universally regarded as one of the founding fathers of modern quality management. He was an American statistician and management consultant who, after World War II, helped Japan rebuild its industrial base through statistical quality control.

**Major Contributions of Deming (any 6 to be elaborated, 6 marks + introduction 1 mark):**

1. **14 Points for Management** — Deming outlined 14 principles that managers must follow to transform their organizations into quality-driven enterprises. Key points include:
   - *Create constancy of purpose*: A long-term commitment to improvement rather than short-term profits.
   - *Adopt the new philosophy*: Accept that quality is the new economic environment.
   - *Cease dependence on inspection*: Quality must be built in, not inspected in.
   - *Improve constantly every process*: Continuous improvement at every level.
   - *Institute training on the job*: Workers must be trained in modern methods.
   - *Institute leadership*: Leaders must help people do a better job.
   - *Drive out fear*: Fear prevents workers from raising quality issues.
   - *Break down barriers between departments*: Encourage cross-functional teamwork.
   - *Eliminate slogans and exhortations*: These do not solve systemic problems.
   - *Put everybody to work*: Engage everyone in the transformation.

2. **PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel/Shewhart Cycle):** Plan-Do-Check-Act is a four-stage iterative process for continuous improvement. Plan identifies objectives and methods; Do implements the plan; Check measures results; Act standardizes successes or revises plans.

3. **System of Profound Knowledge:** Deming believed managers must understand four interrelated areas:
   - Appreciation of a system
   - Knowledge of variation (common vs. special cause)
   - Theory of knowledge
   - Psychology of people

4. **Deming Prize:** Established in 1951 in Japan to honor companies that demonstrate exceptional quality practices.

5. **Statistical Process Control (SPC):** Deming promoted Shewhart's statistical charts to monitor and control process variation.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):** Deming's philosophy transformed Japanese industry (Toyota, Sony) and later American firms (Ford, GE). His emphasis on **system thinking, statistical thinking, and human psychology** remains the foundation of modern quality management.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Cost of Quality (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure the quality of its products or services. It encompasses all costs associated with preventing, detecting, and correcting quality-related defects and failures.

**Four Categories of COQ (4 marks):**

| Category | Type | Description | Examples |
|----------|------|-------------|----------|
| **Prevention Costs** | Cost of Conformance (Good Quality) | Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring | Quality planning, training, supplier development, process design, quality manuals |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Cost of Conformance (Good Quality) | Costs incurred to *detect* defects through inspection and testing | Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final inspection, equipment calibration, quality audits |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Cost of Non-Conformance (Poor Quality) | Costs incurred when defects are found *before* delivery to the customer | Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime, material wastage |
| **External Failure Costs** | Cost of Non-Conformance (Poor Quality) | Costs incurred when defects are found *after* delivery to the customer | Warranty claims, product returns, recalls, lost goodwill, lawsuits, complaint handling |

**COQ Curve Diagram (2 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Description of COQ Curve:** A graph is plotted with *Quality Level* (defect rate) on the X-axis and *Cost* on the Y-axis. As prevention spending increases, the defect level falls. *Prevention and appraisal costs* rise (they are higher at lower defect levels), while *internal and external failure costs* fall. The *total COQ* curve has a **U-shape** with a minimum at the **Economic Quality Level (EQL)** — the optimal balance between prevention and failure costs.

```
Cost
  ^
  |  \  Prevention + Appraisal
  |   \  /
  |    \/
  |    /\
  |   /  \____   Internal + External Failure
  |  /         \____
  | /
  |/____________________>  Defect Level
              ^
           EQL (Minimum)
```

**Conclusion (0 marks – already accounted):** The COQ framework helps managers shift the strategy from **detection** (high appraisal) to **prevention** (lower total cost). This is the essence of Crosby's "Quality is Free" philosophy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** What is the **Taguchi Quality Loss Function**? Derive the expression for the loss function and explain its significance in modern manufacturing.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the contributions of **Joseph Juran and Philip Crosby** to Quality Management. Compare their definitions of quality and approaches to quality improvement.   **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — Taguchi Quality Loss Function (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Taguchi Quality Loss Function is a quantitative measure of the financial loss to society when a product's quality characteristic deviates from its target value. The loss increases **quadratically** with the deviation, even if the product is within specification limits.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Taguchi proposed that the loss to society $L$ from a product with quality characteristic $y$ deviating from target $m$ is a quadratic function of the deviation $(y - m)$:

$$
L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2
$$

**Step 2:** The loss coefficient $k$ is determined using the condition that at the tolerance limit $\Delta_0$, the loss equals $A_0$ (the cost of failure or replacement):

$$
A_0 = k \cdot \Delta_0^2
$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $k$:

$$
k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back, the complete loss function is:

$$
L(y) = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \cdot (y - m)^2
$$

**Significance in Modern Manufacturing (2 marks):**

1. **Reframes "specification thinking":** Traditional approach considers any product within specification as "good." Taguchi's function says even within-spec deviations incur loss, motivating tighter quality control.

2. **Drives continuous improvement:** Firms aim to reduce variability and target the exact nominal value, not just stay within limits.

3. **Connects quality to cost:** Provides a monetary measure of quality loss, enabling cost-benefit analysis of quality improvements.

4. **Foundation of Robust Design:** Taguchi's method uses the loss function to design products that are insensitive to noise factors (variation in operating conditions, materials, environment).

**Numerical Example (no extra marks, but useful for clarity):**
For a shaft with target $m = 25.00$ mm, tolerance $\Delta_0 = 0.05$ mm, and $A_0 = 400$:
- $k = 400 / 0.05^2 = 160{,}000$
- Loss at $y = 25.02$ mm: $L = 160{,}000 \times (0.02)^2 = 64$ units
- Loss at tolerance limit $y = 25.05$ mm: $L = 160{,}000 \times (0.05)^2 = 400$ units ✓

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Juran and Crosby Comparison (7 marks):**

**Joseph M. Juran (3.5 marks):**

- **Definition of Quality:** "Fitness for Use" — Quality is determined by the customer's satisfaction when the product is used in real conditions.
- **Key Contributions:**
  - **Juran's Trilogy:** Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement — the foundation of all quality management systems.
  - **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule):** Vital few defects account for most of the problems. Focus improvement efforts on the critical 20% of causes.
  - **Quality Spiral:** A visual model showing how quality evolves as a product moves through marketing, design, production, sales — each stage must build on the previous to maintain quality.
- **Approach:** Customer-focused, top-down, structured (planning → control → improvement). Emphasized management responsibility for quality.

**Philip B. Crosby (3.5 marks):**

- **Definition of Quality:** "Conformance to Requirements" — Quality is achieved when the product precisely matches the specified design and standards.
- **Key Contributions:**
  - **Zero Defects (ZD):** A performance standard demanding defect-free production.
  - **4 Absolutes of Quality:**
    1. Quality = Conformance to requirements
    2. Prevention, not inspection
    3. Zero defects performance standard
    4. Measure quality by price of non-conformance
  - **"Quality is Free":** The cost of prevention is always less than the cost of failure.
- **Approach:** Bottom-up, worker-oriented, motivational. Crosby believed in cultural change through training, recognition, and setting zero-defect goals.

**Comparison (already woven above, can be summarized in a table):**

| Aspect | Juran | Crosby |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Definition | Fitness for Use | Conformance to Requirements |
| Focus | Customer satisfaction | Specification adherence |
| Tools | Trilogy, Pareto, Quality Spiral | 4 Absolutes, Zero Defects |
| Approach | Top-down, managerial | Bottom-up, cultural |
| Improvement | Breakthrough projects | Daily prevention effort |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse definitions:** Juran says "fitness for use," Crosby says "conformance to requirements," Deming says "predictable uniformity and dependability." Mixing them up costs marks.
> 2. **Don't list gurus without contributions:** Naming "Deming, Juran, Crosby" is worth 1 mark. Explaining *what they contributed* and *their philosophies* earns the full 7 marks.
> 3. **Taguchi Loss Function derivation:** Students often forget the step where $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$ is derived. Without this step, full marks cannot be awarded.
> 4. **COQ categories:** A common mistake is including "rework" under prevention cost. Rework is an **Internal Failure Cost**, not prevention.
> 5. **Draw the COQ curve** in your answer — visual diagrams earn easy marks in 14-mark questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Quality** = Totality of characteristics that satisfy needs (ISO 9000). Multiple definitions: Juran (Fitness for Use), Crosby (Conformance), Deming (Predictable Uniformity).
- **Quality Management (QM)** = Coordinated activities to direct and control an organization regarding quality. Includes 4 pillars: **Planning, Control, Assurance, Improvement**.
- **Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Quality:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
- **Quality Gurus (must memorize):**
  - **Deming** → 14 Points, PDCA Cycle, System of Profound Knowledge
  - **Juran** → Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement), Fitness for Use, Pareto Principle, Quality Spiral
  - **Crosby** → Conformance to Requirements, Zero Defects, 4 Absolutes, "Quality is Free"
  - **Ishikawa** → Fishbone Diagram, Quality Circles, Company-Wide Quality Control
  - **Taguchi** → Quality Loss Function $L = k(y-m)^2$, Robust Design
  - **Feigenbaum** → Total Quality Control (TQC), Hidden Plant concept
- **Cost of Quality (COQ)** has 4 components: **Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure.** The first two are *conformance costs*; the latter two are *non-conformance costs*.
- **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(y) = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} (y - m)^2$. Loss is zero only at the target and increases quadratically with deviation.
- **Customer Satisfaction** = Performance − Expectation. Delight, satisfaction, or dissatisfaction based on whether P > E, P = E, or P < E.
- **Quality vs Grade:** Quality is the degree of excellence; Grade is the category of features. Low-grade products can be high quality.
- **Evolution of QM:** Inspection → QC → QA → TQM.
- **Six Sigma** target: 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO).
- **Voice of Customer (VOC)** must be translated into **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** characteristics for engineering design.
- **Deming's PDCA:** Plan → Do → Check → Act. This is the universal improvement cycle.
- **Juran's Trilogy** is the most-tested framework in KTU exams. Know it cold.
- **Crosby's "4 Absolutes"** are tested verbatim in KTU question banks.
- **Taguchi's quadratic loss** is the most-tested formula — derivation steps must be memorized.
- **Quality Management is a system**, not a department. Top management commitment is the single biggest determinant of QM success (per Deming and Juran).
- **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** is the philosophical foundation — small, incremental, daily improvements by everyone.
- **The 80/20 rule (Pareto)** applies to defects: 80% of defects come from 20% of causes. Use it to prioritize improvement efforts.
- **Prevention > Inspection** in cost-effectiveness. Investing 1 unit in prevention saves 10 units in failure costs (rule of thumb from Crosby).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain a quality guru," always follow this structure: (1) Name and years, (2) Definition of quality they proposed, (3) Key contributions, (4) Famous quote, (5) Real-world impact. This structure fetches full marks consistently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Quality Management (TQM) - TQM Axioms - Consequences of Total Quality - Barriers to TQM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Total Quality Management (TQM) - TQM Axioms - Consequences of Total Quality - Barriers to TQM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Total Quality Management (TQM)?

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that seeks to embed quality in every process, product, and service through the continuous, active participation of **all members** of the organization — from top leadership to frontline workers — with the ultimate goal of achieving **long-term customer satisfaction** and **organizational excellence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Use this verbatim):**
> *TQM is a customer-focused, process-centered, people-driven, and fact-based management philosophy whose objective is to continuously improve the business by managing quality across the entire value chain through the active involvement of all stakeholders.*

**Four Pillars of TQM (for your exam):**

| Pillar | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Customer Focus** | Voice of Customer (VOC) drives design |
| **Total Involvement** | Every employee is responsible for quality |
| **Continuous Improvement** | Kaizen — small, daily improvements |
| **Fact-Based Decision Making** | Data, not opinions, drive decisions |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Kitchen" Model

Imagine a 5-star hospital kitchen. The head chef does not cook alone — the procurement officer selects vegetables, the cleaner maintains hygiene, the dietician checks nutrition, the manager ensures timely delivery to patients. If the dietician ignores a salt limit, the patient (customer) suffers. TQM works the same way: **quality is not the job of the Quality Department alone — it is the responsibility of every person who touches the product, process, or service.** A defect in one stage propagates downstream, just like a contaminated ingredient ruins an entire dish.

### 1.3 TQM Axioms — The Foundational Beliefs

Axioms are **self-evident, non-negotiable truths** that form the philosophical bedrock of TQM. They are not derived through proof; they are accepted as universal truths of quality leadership.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Alert:** *Axioms are NOT principles. Principles are actionable guidelines (e.g., "train employees"); axioms are deeper philosophical beliefs (e.g., "people want to do a good job"). Questions frequently ask for the distinction.*

The widely accepted **Seven TQM Axioms** are:

| # | Axiom | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **A1** | People want to do a good job | Trust employees; systems, not people, cause defects |
| **A2** | Systems and processes drive outcomes | Fix the system, not the worker |
| **A3** | Quality is a journey, not a destination | Continuous improvement (Kaizen) never ends |
| **A4** | Customer satisfaction is the ultimate measure | Internal metrics are secondary to VOC |
| **A5** | Quality prevents problems, not detects them | Inspection is too late; build it right first time |
| **A6** | Quality must be managed, not just measured | Measurement without action is waste |
| **A7** | Every employee is a customer and a supplier | Internal customers (next process) define internal quality |

### 1.4 Consequences of Total Quality

**Consequences** are the **direct outcomes and side effects** an organization experiences *after* successfully adopting TQM. They can be **tangible** (financial) or **intangible** (cultural).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Consequence ≠ Benefit. *Benefits* are always positive. *Consequences* can be **positive OR negative** (e.g., short-term cost surge from training, employee anxiety during cultural change). KTU examiners love testing this nuance.

### 1.5 Barriers to TQM

**Barriers** are the **internal and external obstructions** that prevent successful TQM adoption. They range from leadership apathy to organizational inertia. Recognizing barriers is the **first step in the PDCA cycle** of overcoming them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Dive: The TQM Axioms Explained

Let us expand each axiom into its **operational meaning** — this is what examiners expect in 7-mark descriptive answers.

**Axiom 1: People want to do a good job.**
- **Why:** Intrinsic motivation (Maslow's esteem, self-actualization) drives performance.
- **How:** Remove obstacles — bad tools, unclear instructions, conflicting goals — and people will perform.
- **Engineering Example:** A CNC operator producing scrap is not "careless"; the drawing is unclear, the tool is worn, or the SOP is outdated.

**Axiom 2: Systems and Processes drive outcomes.**
- **Why:** Deming's **94/6 Rule**: 94% of problems are due to systems/processes, only 6% to special causes (worker skill).
- **How:** Use process mapping, value stream mapping, root-cause analysis (5-Why, Ishikawa).

**Axiom 3: Quality is a journey, not a destination.**
- **Why:** Markets, technology, and customer expectations evolve continuously.
- **How:** Implement PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act), DMAIC (Define–Measure–Analyze–Improve–Control), and Kaizen events.

**Axiom 4: Customer Satisfaction is the ultimate measure.**
- **Why:** A defect-free product that nobody wants is a commercial failure.
- **How:** Capture VOC via surveys, FMEA, QFD (House of Quality), Net Promoter Score (NPS).

**Axiom 5: Quality prevents problems, not detects them.**
- **Why:** Inspection is reactive and costly; prevention is proactive and cheaper.
- **How:** Use Statistical Process Control (SPC), Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing), APQP, robust design.

**Axiom 6: Quality must be managed, not just measured.**
