- **Chain Reaction:** "Improve quality → costs decrease → productivity rises → capture the market → stay in business → create jobs."

**(3) PDCA Cycle (1.5 marks):** Shewhart's original learning cycle, refined by Deming:

- **Plan:** Identify the problem, hypothesise a cause, plan an intervention (using SPC data).
- **Do:** Execute the change on a small scale, collect data.
- **Check:** Analyse data using control charts and capability indices; compare with prediction.
- **Act:** If the change worked, standardise it; otherwise, revise hypothesis and repeat.

**(4) Role of Statistical Thinking (0.5 marks):** Both Shewhart and Deming emphasised that quality professionals must think in terms of *populations, distributions, and variance* — not isolated data points. This mindset prevents tampering and supports continuous improvement (Kaizen).

*(Total marks distribution: 3 + 2 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing *in-control* with *capable*** — A process can be in statistical control yet produce scrap ($C_p < 1$). Always address stability **first**, then capability.
> 2. **Forgetting units in $C_p$ calculations** — Always write mm (or the relevant unit) in the final answer.
> 3. **Mixing $n$ and $n-1$ in standard deviation** — Use $n-1$ for sample s.d. (Bessel's correction); use $n$ only if $\mu$ is the true population mean.
> 4. **Calling Deming's 94/6 a "law"** — It is an *empirical observation*, not a physical constant. Examiners deduct marks for over-stating it.
> 5. **Ignoring the off-centring effect** — When asked for capability, compute **both** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$; many students skip $C_{pk}$.
> 6. **Wrong interpretation of $C_p$** — $C_p \geq 1.33$ is industry-acceptable; $C_p = 1.0$ is the *bare minimum* not a comfortable target.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

- **Two causes only**: Common (system, 94 %) and Special (local, 6 %).
- **3σ rule**: Control limits are placed at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ because this gives a 0.27 % false-alarm rate.
- **In-control ≠ Capable**: Control limits describe process *behaviour*; specifications describe *customer requirement*. Both must be satisfied.
- **Tampering is harmful**: Never adjust a stable process based on individual readings.
- **Key indices**: $C_p$ (potential capability) and $C_{pk}$ (actual capability, includes centring).
- **Constant table to memorise for KTU**: $A_2$, $d_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ for $n = 2, 3, 4, 5$ at minimum.
- **Deming's 94/6**: A management responsibility argument, not a physics law.
- **PDCA cycle**: Plan → Do → Check → Act; a continuous improvement loop built on SPC data.
- **Quality is a system property**, not an individual virtue — this is the single most important conceptual takeaway for OEMET615.
- **Always interpret variation in context**: Common vs special is not just statistics — it dictates *who fixes it and how*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Process Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Statistical Process Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Statistical Process Control (SPC)

**Statistical Process Control (SPC)** is a data-driven analytical methodology and philosophy of quality management that applies statistical techniques to monitor, control, and continuously improve a manufacturing or service process over time by distinguishing between **inherent (common cause) variation** and **assignable (special cause) variation** through the use of control charts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** SPC is the application of statistical methods to the measurement and analysis of variation in any process, originally developed by **Walter A. Shewhart (1924)** at Bell Laboratories, and later promoted in Japan by **W. Edwards Deming**.

### Key Terminology

- **Process:** The combination of people, machines, materials, methods, and environment (5M + E) producing a desired output.
- **Variation:** The natural differences observed between individual units of output.
- **Stability / In-Control State:** A process operating with only common cause variation, predictable through statistical models.
- **Capability:** The ability of a stable process to meet specifications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Patient Vital Monitor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Hospital ICU Monitor**
>
> Imagine a patient connected to a heart-rate monitor in an ICU. The nurse has previously measured that a *normal* heartbeat for that patient ranges between 60–100 bpm (the **control limits**). The monitor continuously plots the BPM (the **control chart**).
>
> - If the heart rate fluctuates randomly between 65 and 95 bpm → this is **common cause variation** (the patient is stable, no action required).
> - If suddenly the heart rate spikes to 130 bpm → this is **special cause variation** (alarm rings, doctor intervenes).
>
> **SPC works exactly the same way for a manufacturing process.** The control chart is the "patient monitor" and the control limits are the "healthy range." Statistical Process Control helps the engineer *hear the alarm* when the process becomes unstable.

## 1.3 Objectives of SPC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Three Primary Objectives** of SPC (as per KTU syllabus):
> 1. **Maintain** the process at an acceptable level of performance.
> 2. **Monitor** the process to detect the occurrence of special cause variation.
> 3. **Reduce** common cause variation through continuous improvement (PDCA / DMAIC).

## 1.4 The Two Faces of Variation — Deming's Insight

Every process exhibits two types of variation. Misclassifying them leads to two classic management errors:

| Type of Variation | Alternative Name | Source | Action Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Common Cause Variation** | Chance / Random / Inherent / Noise | Built into the process (5M + E) | Management action — improve the **system** |
| **Special Cause Variation** | Assignable / Non-random / Signal | External, identifiable factors | Local action — operator level, remove the **cause** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Deming's Funnel Experiment Lesson:** Tampering with a process by reacting to common cause variation *as if* it were a special cause *increases* overall variation. SPC prevents this costly mistake.

## 1.5 Statistical Foundation

SPC rests on the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**, which states that the distribution of sample means approaches a **normal distribution** as the sample size $n$ increases, regardless of the population distribution. The population mean and standard deviation are denoted by **$\mu$ and $\sigma$**, while the sample mean and standard deviation are denoted by **$\bar{x}$ and $s$**.

The standard error of the mean (used in control charts) is:

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

This is the cornerstone of all control limit calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rational Subgrouping — The Heart of SPC

A **rational subgroup** is a set of observations measured under *similar* conditions (same operator, machine, raw material, time) such that within-subgroup variation contains *only common cause variation*, and between-subgroup variation reveals *special cause variation* if present.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):** Rational subgroups should be selected so that:
> 1. Each subgroup contains measurements produced under homogeneous conditions.
> 2. Different subgroups represent variation across time, shifts, or batches.
> 3. Subgroup size $n$ is usually small ($n = 2$ to $10$), and frequency is high.

## 2.2 The Two Major Families of Control Charts

Control charts are classified based on the **type of data** being measured:

### A. Control Charts for **Variables** (Continuous Data)
- **$\bar{X}$ and R Chart** (mean and range chart) — most common, subgroup size $n \leq 10$.
- **$\bar{X}$ and S Chart** (mean and standard deviation chart) — preferred when $n > 10$.
- **X and MR Chart** (individual and moving range) — for $n = 1$ (slow processes, services).

### B. Control Charts for **Attributes** (Discrete Data)
- **p-Chart** — proportion of non-conforming units (variable sample size).
- **np-Chart** — number of non-conforming units (fixed sample size).
- **c-Chart** — number of defects per unit (constant inspection unit).
- **u-Chart** — number of defects per unit (variable inspection unit).

## 2.3 Control Chart Structure

Every control chart has a **Centre Line (CL)** and two sets of limits:

- **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** — $3\sigma$ above the centre line.
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** — $3\sigma$ below the centre line.
- The region between UCL and LCL is called the **Zone of Indifference**.

The famous **"3-Sigma Limits"** come from Shewhart's economic balance:
- **$1\sigma$ limit** → too many false alarms (Type I error).
- **$2\sigma$ limit** → still too many false alarms.
- **$3\sigma$ limit** → only 0.27% probability of a point falling outside if process is in control (acceptable error rate).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **HIGH-YIELD** — every numerical in this topic uses these formulas. **Memorize them.**

### Table 2.1: Control Limit Formulas for Variables Charts

| Chart | Centre Line | UCL / LCL Formula | Constants Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}$ Chart | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ (grand mean) | $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm A_2 \bar{R}$ | $A_2$ (function of $n$) |
| R Chart | $\bar{R}$ (mean range) | $D_4 \bar{R}, \; D_3 \bar{R}$ | $D_3, D_4$ (function of $n$) |
| S Chart | $\bar{S}$ (mean std dev) | $B_4 \bar{S}, \; B_3 \bar{S}$ | $B_3, B_4$ (function of $n$) |
| X Chart (Individuals) | $\bar{X}$ | $\bar{X} \pm 2.66 \bar{MR}$ | Uses $E_2 = 2.66$ (for $n=2$) |
| MR Chart (Moving Range) | $\bar{MR}$ | $3.268 \bar{MR}, \; 0$ | Uses $D_4 = 3.268$ (for $n=2$) |

### Table 2.2: Control Limit Formulas for Attribute Charts

| Chart | Centre Line | UCL / LCL Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| p-Chart | $\bar{p}$ (avg proportion defective) | $\bar{p} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | Variable $n$ |
| np-Chart | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} \pm 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | Fixed $n$ |
| c-Chart | $\bar{c}$ (avg # defects) | $\bar{c} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Constant inspection unit |
| u-Chart | $\bar{u}$ (avg defects per unit) | $\bar{u} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n}$ | Variable inspection unit |

### Table 2.3: Control Chart Constants (Tabulated Values)

> **NOTE:** In KTU problems, the constants are provided. However, knowing these values for $n = 2$ to $n = 10$ is essential.

| $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $B_3$ | $B_4$ | $d_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.268 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 0 | 2.568 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 0 | 2.266 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 0 | 2.089 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 0.030 | 1.970 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 0.118 | 1.882 | 2.704 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 0.185 | 1.815 | 2.847 |
| 9 | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 0.239 | 1.761 | 2.970 |
| 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 0.284 | 1.716 | 3.078 |

> **CRITICAL NOTICE ON SIGMA ESTIMATION:** $\sigma$ is typically estimated from the average range: $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$. The constant $d_2$ converts the average range to an estimate of $\sigma$.

### Table 2.4: Process Capability Indices (PCI)

| Index | Formula | Interpretation | Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cp** | $\frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process potential (capability) | $\geq 1.33$ |
| **Cpk** | $\min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process centering (actual) | $\geq 1.33$ |
| **Pp** | $\frac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Process performance (overall) | $\geq 1.33$ |
| **Ppk** | $\min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3s}, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3s}\right)$ | Performance + centering | $\geq 1.33$ |
| **Cp - Cpk** | Difference | Sign of off-centering | If large → process off-centre |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Difference between Cp/Cpk vs Pp/Ppk:**
> - **Cp and Cpk** use $\sigma$ (short-term, within-subgroup variation) → measures *capability*.
> - **Pp and Ppk** use $s$ (long-term, total variation) → measures *performance*.
> - **Pp ≥ Cp** because long-term variation is always ≥ short-term variation.

## 2.5 Patterns Indicating Out-of-Control Conditions (Western Electric / Nelson Rules)

> [!WARNING]
> A single point outside the 3-sigma limits is **NOT** the only indicator of an unstable process. KTU expects students to know these rules.

1. **Rule 1:** One point beyond Zone A (beyond 3$\sigma$).
2. **Rule 2:** 2 of 3 successive points in Zone A or beyond.
3. **Rule 3:** 4 of 5 successive points in Zone B or beyond.
4. **Rule 4:** 7 successive points on the same side of the centre line (shift / trend).
5. **Rule 5:** 6 successive points trending up or down (drift).
6. **Rule 6:** 15 successive points in Zone C (stratification, over-control).
7. **Rule 7:** 14 successive points alternating up and down (systematic variation).
8. **Rule 8:** 8 successive points beyond Zone C on either side.

The four zones are:
- **Zone A:** between 2$\sigma$ and 3$\sigma$ from CL.
- **Zone B:** between 1$\sigma$ and 2$\sigma$ from CL.
- **Zone C:** between 0 and 1$\sigma$ from CL.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive:** IATF 16949 / ISO/TS 16949 mandates SPC for safety-critical features (brake systems, airbags).
- **Semiconductor Industry:** SPC monitors wafer fabrication where $Cpk \geq 1.50$ is industry standard.
- **Healthcare:** Six Sigma DMAIC uses SPC tools to reduce hospital-acquired infections and ER wait times.
- **Software Process Improvement:** CMMI Level 5 organizations use SPC on defect rates per KLOC.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** FDA's Process Validation Guidance (2011) requires SPC for commercial manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of 3-Sigma Control Limits for the $\bar{X}$ Chart

### Starting Point
If a process is in statistical control, the sample means $\bar{X}_i$ drawn from a stable process follow a normal distribution with:

- Mean: $\mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu$
- Standard deviation: $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

### Derivation Steps

\begin{aligned}
\text{By Shewhart's } 3\sigma \text{ rule:} \quad
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \mu + 3 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}} \\
&= \mu + 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \mu - 3 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}} \\
&= \mu - 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \\
\text{But } \mu \text{ and } \sigma \text{ are unknown} \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Estimate from data} \\
\text{Use } \hat{\mu} = \bar{\bar{X}}, \quad \hat{\sigma} &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} \\
\text{Substituting:} \quad
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}/d_2}{\sqrt{n}} \\
&= \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} \cdot \bar{R} \\
\text{Define } A_2 &= \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} \\
\therefore \quad UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} \\
\text{Centre Line:} \quad CL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}}
\end{aligned}

This is the **complete derivation** of the $\bar{X}$ chart control limits.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: $\bar{X}$ and R Chart Construction (14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A piston ring is manufactured with an inner diameter target of 74.0 mm. Five samples of size $n = 5$ are collected. The data is given below. Construct the $\bar{X}$ and R chart, and determine whether the process is in control.

| Sample | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ | $X_5$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $R_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 74.030 | 74.002 | 74.019 | 73.992 | 74.008 | ? | ? |
| 2 | 73.995 | 74.021 | 74.015 | 74.013 | 73.993 | ? | ? |
| 3 | 73.985 | 74.008 | 74.002 | 73.996 | 74.005 | ? | ? |
| 4 | 74.008 | 73.995 | 73.998 | 74.021 | 74.002 | ? | ? |
| 5 | 73.995 | 74.010 | 74.015 | 73.998 | 74.005 | ? | ? |

### Step 1: Calculate the Sample Means ($\bar{X}_i$) and Ranges ($R_i$)

\begin{aligned}
\text{Sample 1: } \quad
\bar{X}_1 &= \frac{74.030 + 74.002 + 74.019 + 73.992 + 74.008}{5} = \frac{370.051}{5} = 74.0102 \\
R_1 &= X_{\max} - X_{\min} = 74.030 - 73.992 = 0.038 \\
\text{Sample 2: } \quad
\bar{X}_2 &= \frac{73.995 + 74.021 + 74.015 + 74.013 + 73.993}{5} = \frac{370.037}{5} = 74.0074 \\
R_2 &= 74.021 - 73.993 = 0.028 \\
\text{Sample 3: } \quad
\bar{X}_3 &= \frac{73.985 + 74.008 + 74.002 + 73.996 + 74.005}{5} = \frac{369.996}{5} = 73.9992 \\
R_3 &= 74.008 - 73.985 = 0.023 \\
\text{Sample 4: } \quad
\bar{X}_4 &= \frac{74.008 + 73.995 + 73.998 + 74.021 + 74.002}{5} = \frac{370.024}{5} = 74.0048 \\
R_4 &= 74.021 - 73.995 = 0.026 \\
\text{Sample 5: } \quad
\bar{X}_5 &= \frac{73.995 + 74.010 + 74.015 + 73.998 + 74.005}{5} = \frac{370.023}{5} = 74.0046 \\
R_5 &= 74.015 - 73.995 = 0.020
\end{aligned}

### Step 2: Calculate the Grand Mean ($\bar{\bar{X}}$) and Mean Range ($\bar{R}$)

\begin{aligned}
\bar{\bar{X}} &= \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k} = \frac{74.0102 + 74.0074 + 73.9992 + 74.0048 + 74.0046}{5} \\
&= \frac{370.0262}{5} = 74.00524 \\
\bar{R} &= \frac{\sum R_i}{k} = \frac{0.038 + 0.028 + 0.023 + 0.026 + 0.020}{5} \\
&= \frac{0.135}{5} = 0.027
\end{aligned}

### Step 3: Look Up the Control Chart Constants for $n = 5$

\begin{aligned}
\text{From the constants table for } n = 5: \quad
A_2 &= 0.577 \\
D_3 &= 0 \\
D_4 &= 2.114
\end{aligned}

### Step 4: Calculate the Control Limits for the $\bar{X}$ Chart

\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 74.00524 + (0.577 \times 0.027) \\
&= 74.00524 + 0.015579 = 74.0208 \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 74.00524 - 0.015579 = 73.9897 \\
CL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} = 74.00524
\end{aligned}

### Step 5: Calculate the Control Limits for the R Chart

\begin{aligned}
UCL_R &= D_4 \bar{R} = 2.114 \times 0.027 = 0.0571 \\
LCL_R &= D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \times 0.027 = 0 \\
CL_R &= \bar{R} = 0.027
\end{aligned}

### Step 6: Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> - All sample means $\bar{X}_i$ lie within $[73.9897, \; 74.0208]$ → **$\bar{X}$ chart shows process is in control.**
> - All sample ranges $R_i$ lie within $[0, \; 0.0571]$ → **R chart shows process variability is in control.**
> - **Conclusion:** The process is in **statistical control** with respect to both location and dispersion.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Process Capability Index Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A CNC machine produces shafts with specification $LSL = 24.90$ mm and $USL = 25.10$ mm. From a stable process, 25 subgroups of size 5 were collected, yielding $\bar{\bar{X}} = 25.02$ mm and $\bar{R} = 0.04$ mm. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

### Step 1: Estimate Process Standard Deviation

\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma} &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} \quad \text{(for } n = 5, \; d_2 = 2.326) \\
\hat{\sigma} &= \frac{0.04}{2.326} = 0.01720 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}

### Step 2: Calculate Cp (Process Capability)

\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.01720} \\
&= \frac{0.20}{0.1032} = 1.938
\end{aligned}

### Step 3: Calculate Cpk (Process Capability Index — Centering)

\begin{aligned}
C_{pk, \text{upper}} &= \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.01720} \\
&= \frac{0.08}{0.0516} = 1.550 \\
C_{pk, \text{lower}} &= \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.02 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.01720} \\
&= \frac{0.12}{0.0516} = 2.326 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(C_{pk, \text{upper}}, \; C_{pk, \text{lower}}) = \min(1.550, \; 2.326) = 1.550
\end{aligned}

### Step 4: Interpretation

\begin{aligned}
C_p &= 1.938 \geq 1.33 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Process is CAPABLE (potential spread fits within specs)} \\
C_{pk} &= 1.550 \geq 1.33 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Process is CENTERED well} \\
C_p - C_{pk} &= 1.938 - 1.550 = 0.388 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Moderate off-centering; reduce the mean toward 25.00 mm}
\end{aligned}

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: p-Chart (Attribute Chart)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A batch of 100 resistors is inspected in each of 15 samples. The number of defectives observed in each sample is: 5, 8, 6, 4, 7, 9, 5, 6, 4, 8, 7, 3, 6, 5, 4. Construct a p-chart and comment on the state of control.

### Step 1: Calculate the Average Proportion Defective ($\bar{p}$)

\begin{aligned}
\text{Total defectives} &= 5+8+6+4+7+9+5+6+4+8+7+3+6+5+4 = 87 \\
\text{Total inspected} &= 15 \times 100 = 1500 \\
\bar{p} &= \frac{87}{1500} = 0.058
\end{aligned}

### Step 2: Calculate Control Limits (assuming constant $n = 100$)

\begin{aligned}
UCL_p &= \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} \\
&= 0.058 + 3\sqrt{\frac{0.058 \times 0.942}{100}} \\
&= 0.058 + 3\sqrt{0.000546} \\
&= 0.058 + 3 \times 0.02337 \\
&= 0.058 + 0.0701 = 0.1281 \\
LCL_p &= 0.058 - 0.0701 = -0.0121 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Set } LCL = 0 \text{ (proportions cannot be negative)} \\
CL_p &= 0.058
\end{aligned}

### Step 3: Check Each Sample

\begin{aligned}
p_i &= \frac{d_i}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \text{All } p_i \in \{0.03, 0.04, 0.05, 0.06, 0.07, 0.08, 0.09\} \\
\text{All } p_i \in [0, 0.1281] \quad &\Rightarrow \text{Process is IN CONTROL}
\end{aligned}

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Correct Control Chart

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most commonly asked 7-mark question in KTU is "Which chart should I use for this scenario?"**

| Situation | Data Type | Sample Size | Recommended Chart |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shaft diameter, weight, length | Variable | $n > 1$ small | $\bar{X}$ and R Chart |
| Single measurement per batch (e.g., chemical concentration in a slow process) | Variable | $n = 1$ | X and MR Chart |
| Pass/Fail inspection (defective vs non-defective) | Attribute | Variable | p-Chart |
| Pass/Fail with fixed sample size | Attribute | Fixed | np-Chart |
| Counting defects (scratches, voids, errors) | Attribute | Constant area | c-Chart |
| Counting defects per unit (e.g., defects per m² of fabric) | Attribute | Variable area | u-Chart |
| Sample size > 10 | Variable | $n \geq 10$ | $\bar{X}$ and S Chart |

## 3.6 Average Run Length (ARL) — Quick Note

The ARL is the average number of points that must be plotted before a point indicates an out-of-control condition.

\begin{aligned}
ARL_0 &= \frac{1}{P(\text{point outside limits when in control})} = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370 \\
ARL_1 &= \frac{1}{P(\text{point outside limits when out of control})} \quad \text{(depends on shift size)}
\end{aligned}

- A **3-sigma** chart has $ARL_0 = 370.4$ (one false alarm every 370 samples on average).
- For a **1.5-sigma** shift (typical of 1.5$\sigma$ shift used in Six Sigma), $ARL_1 \approx 200$ points.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The SPC Implementation Cycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Under Study] --> B[Define Quality Characteristic]
    B --> C[Select Rational Subgroup<br/>n and k]
    C --> D[Collect Sample Data]
    D --> E[Calculate Trial<br/>Control Limits]
    E --> F{All Points Within<br/>3 Sigma Limits?}
    F -- No --> G[Investigate Special Cause<br/>Remove / Correct]
    G --> D
    F -- Yes --> H[Process is In-Control<br/>Establish CL UCL LCL]
    H --> I[Monitor Future Production<br/>Plot New Points]
    I --> J{New Point Out of<br/>Control Limits?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Out-of-Control Action Plan<br/>OCAP Activated]
    K --> D
    J -- No --> L[Continue Production<br/>Phase II Monitoring]
    L --> M[Periodic Capability<br/>Reassessment Cp Cpk]
    M --> D
```

## 4.2 The Three-Sigma Control Chart Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPCChart["Control Chart Anatomy"]
        UCL["UCL<br/>Upper Control Limit<br/>+3 sigma"]
        ZA["Zone A<br/>2 sigma to 3 sigma"]
        ZB["Zone B<br/>1 sigma to 2 sigma"]
        ZC["Zone C<br/>0 to 1 sigma"]
        CL["CL<br/>Centre Line<br/>Mean"]
        ZC2["Zone C<br/>-1 sigma to 0"]
        ZB2["Zone B<br/>-2 sigma to -1 sigma"]
        ZA2["Zone A<br/>-3 sigma to -2 sigma"]
        LCL["LCL<br/>Lower Control Limit<br/>-3 sigma"]
        UCL --> ZA --> ZB --> ZC --> CL --> ZC2 --> ZB2 --> ZA2 --> LCL
    end
```

## 4.3 Variable vs Attribute Chart Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Data to be Monitored] --> Q1{What is the<br/>Data Type?}
    Q1 -- Continuous --> Q2{Sample Size?}
    Q1 -- Discrete --> Q3{What is being<br/>Counted?}
    Q2 -- n greater than 1 --> Q4{n less than or equal 10?}
    Q2 -- n equals 1 --> IM[X and MR Chart<br/>Individuals]
    Q4 -- Yes --> VR[barX and R Chart]
    Q4 -- No --> VS[barX and S Chart]
    Q3 -- Defective Units --> Q5{Sample Size<br/>Constant?}
    Q3 -- Defects per Unit --> Q6{Unit Size<br/>Constant?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> NPP[np-Chart]
    Q5 -- No --> PP[p-Chart]
    Q6 -- Yes --> CC[c-Chart]
    Q6 -- No --> UC[u-Chart]
```

## 4.4 DMAIC and SPC Integration (Six Sigma Context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Define<br/>Problem and Customer CTQ] --> M[Measure<br/>Baseline Capability<br/>Cp Cpk]
    M --> A[Analyze<br/>Pareto Ishikawa<br/>5-Whys]
    A --> I[Improve<br/>Pilot Changes<br/>SPC Monitoring]
    I --> C[Control<br/>SPC Charts<br/>Control Plan]
    C --> M
    I -.->|Process Re-evaluation| M
```

## 4.5 Variation Funnel — Deming's Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process with Common Cause Variation Only] --> B{Engineer Reacts<br/>to Random Noise?}
    B -- Yes Tampering --> C[Worsened Variability<br/>Increased Costs]
    B -- No Patience --> D[Stable System<br/>Identified]
    D --> E[Theory of Knowledge<br/>Improves Process]
    E --> F[Reduced Common Cause Variation<br/>Lower Sigma]
    F --> A
    C --> G[Management Action Required<br/>System Level Fix]
    G --> F
```

## 4.6 Process Capability Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[In-Control Process Verified<br/>via barX and R Chart] --> P2[Estimate Sigma<br/>from bar R or s]
    P2 --> P3{Use Short Term<br/>or Long Term Sigma?}
    P3 -- Short Term --> P4[Cp and Cpk<br/>Capability]
    P3 -- Long Term --> P5[Pp and Ppk<br/>Performance]
    P4 --> P6[Compare to Spec<br/>USL LSL]
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7{Cpk greater than 1.33?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Capable Process<br/>Continue Monitoring]
    P7 -- No --> P9[Incapable<br/>Improve via DMAIC]
    P9 --> P2
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between "Common Cause" variation and "Special Cause" variation with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Common Cause Variation | Special Cause Variation |
|---|---|---|
| **Also called** | Chance, Random, Noise, Inherent | Assignable, Non-random, Signal |
| **Source** | Built into the 5M+E system | External, identifiable |
| **Frequency** | Continuous, always present | Intermittent, sporadic |
| **Magnitude** | Small, predictable | Large, unpredictable |
| **Action level** | Management (system improvement) | Operator (remove the cause) |
| **Distribution** | Stable, predictable | Unstable, shifts occur |
| **Example** | Slight variation in bolt diameter due to natural machine vibration | Sudden jump in bolt diameter because a tool broke |
| **Reaction** | Do NOT tamper; improve the system | Investigate and eliminate the cause |

> **Marks Allocation (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Differences with example (1) + Action level (1).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a "Rational Subgroup" in SPC. State any four guidelines for selecting rational subgroups.**

**Model Answer:**

A **rational subgroup** is a set of measurements taken under *homogeneous conditions* such that within-subgroup variation contains only common cause variation, while between-subgroup variation reveals special cause variation.

**Four Guidelines:**
1. **Homogeneity within subgroup:** All items in a subgroup should be produced under essentially the same conditions (same operator, machine, raw material, and short time interval).
2. **Heterogeneity between subgroups:** Each subgroup should represent a distinct opportunity for variation (different shifts, different batches).
3. **Small subgroup size:** Typically $n = 2$ to $10$ for $\bar{X}$ and R charts.
4. **High frequency:** Subgroups should be drawn frequently (e.g., every hour or every 100 units) to detect special causes quickly.

> **Marks Allocation (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Four guidelines (2 marks; 0.5 each).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice**. Both choices are given below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of an $\bar{X}$ and R control chart. State the formulas for control limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data refers to the diameter (in mm) of piston rings manufactured in a process. Five samples, each of size 5, are drawn. Construct the $\bar{X}$ and R chart and comment on the state of control. Use the standard control chart constants. **(7 marks)**

| Sample No. | Obs 1 | Obs 2 | Obs 3 | Obs 4 | Obs 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 74.030 | 74.002 | 74.019 | 73.992 | 74.008 |
| 2 | 73.995 | 73.985 | 74.005 | 74.003 | 73.997 |
| 3 | 73.988 | 74.025 | 74.001 | 74.015 | 74.010 |
| 4 | 74.008 | 73.998 | 74.022 | 74.012 | 74.005 |
| 5 | 74.015 | 74.020 | 74.018 | 73.997 | 74.022 |

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Construction Steps of $\bar{X}$ and R Chart:**

1. **Data Collection:** Draw $k$ rational subgroups, each of size $n$ (typically $n = 2$ to $10$). Recommended $k \geq 20$ for reliable estimates.
2. **Compute Statistics:** For each subgroup $i$, calculate:
   - Sample mean: $\bar{X}_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{ij}}{n}$
   - Sample range: $R_i = X_{i,\max} - X_{i,\min}$
3. **Compute Grand Statistics:**
   - Grand mean: $\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i}{k}$
   - Average range: $\bar{R} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{k}$
4. **Compute Control Limits:**

| Chart | CL | UCL | LCL |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}$ Chart | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ |
| R Chart | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ |

5. **Plotting:** Plot $\bar{X}_i$ on the $\bar{X}$ chart and $R_i$ on the R chart. Connect points sequentially.
6. **Analysis:** Points outside control limits indicate special cause variation. Investigate before any process changes.

**Neat Sketch:**

```
   X-bar Chart                       R Chart
  UCL --------+                    UCL --------+
              |  *  *                |  *  *
              | * * *                | * * * *
   CL  -------+----*-----            +--*------  CL
              | * *  *               | * *
              |  *  *                |  *  *
  LCL --------+                    LCL = 0
```

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Calculate $\bar{X}_i$ and $R_i$:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Sample 1: } \bar{X}_1 &= 74.0302, \quad R_1 = 0.038 \\
\text{Sample 2: } \bar{X}_2 &= 73.9970, \quad R_2 = 0.020 \\
\text{Sample 3: } \bar{X}_3 &= 74.0078, \quad R_3 = 0.037 \\
\text{Sample 4: } \bar{X}_4 &= 74.0090, \quad R_4 = 0.024 \\
\text{Sample 5: } \bar{X}_5 &= 74.0144, \quad R_5 = 0.025
\end{aligned}

**Step 2: Grand Mean and Mean Range:**

\begin{aligned}
\bar{\bar{X}} &= \frac{74.0302 + 73.9970 + 74.0078 + 74.0090 + 74.0144}{5} = \frac{370.0584}{5} = 74.01168 \\
\bar{R} &= \frac{0.038 + 0.020 + 0.037 + 0.024 + 0.025}{5} = \frac{0.144}{5} = 0.0288
\end{aligned}

**Step 3: Constants for $n = 5$:** $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$

**Step 4: $\bar{X}$ Chart Limits:**

\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= 74.01168 + 0.577 \times 0.0288 = 74.01168 + 0.01662 = 74.0283 \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= 74.01168 - 0.01662 = 73.9951 \\
CL_{\bar{X}} &= 74.0117
\end{aligned}

**Step 5: R Chart Limits:**

\begin{aligned}
UCL_R &= 2.114 \times 0.0288 = 0.0609 \\
LCL_R &= 0 \\
CL_R &= 0.0288
\end{aligned}

**Step 6: Comment:**
- All $\bar{X}_i$ lie in $[73.9951, 74.0283]$ → $\bar{X}$ chart is in control.
- All $R_i$ lie in $[0, 0.0609]$ → R chart is in control.
- **Conclusion:** Process is in **statistical control** for both location and variability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):** Many students write the control limits formula for the $\bar{X}$ chart as $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm 3\sigma$ instead of $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm A_2 \bar{R}$. This is technically correct only if $\sigma$ is known. Since $\sigma$ is **estimated from $\bar{R}$** in KTU problems, the $A_2$ formula is **mandatory**. Using $3\sigma$ directly will lose **1 full mark**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define Process Capability. Derive the formula for $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ indices and explain the significance of their values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process produces ball bearings with diameter specification $LSL = 10.0$ mm and $USL = 10.8$ mm. From a stable process, the sample statistics from 25 subgroups of size 5 are: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 10.45$ mm and $\bar{R} = 0.15$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and $\sigma$ level. Comment on process capability. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition:** Process Capability is the ability of a **stable** (in-control) process to produce output that meets the specified design limits (USL/LSL). It is measured statistically as $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

**Derivation of $C_p$:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Process spread} &= 6\sigma \quad \text{(6-sigma spread covers 99.73% of output)} \\
\text{Specification width} &= USL - LSL \\
C_p &= \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Spread}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
\end{aligned}

**Derivation of $C_{pk}$:**

\begin{aligned}
C_{pk, \text{upper}} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} \\
C_{pk, \text{lower}} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \\
C_{pk} &= \min(C_{pk, \text{upper}}, \; C_{pk, \text{lower}})
\end{aligned}

**Significance of Values:**

| $C_p$ / $C_{pk}$ Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $< 1.00$ | Process is **incapable**; unacceptable variation |
| $1.00$ to $1.33$ | **Marginal**; barely meets specs; needs monitoring |
| $1.33$ to $1.67$ | **Good**; acceptable for most processes |
| $\geq 1.67$ | **Excellent**; very low defect rate |
| $\geq 2.00$ | **World-class / Six Sigma** capability |

If $C_p = C_{pk}$ → process is **perfectly centered**. If $C_p > C_{pk}$ → process is **off-centered**; the difference indicates the magnitude of off-centering.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Estimate $\sigma$** (for $n = 5$, $d_2 = 2.326$):

\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma} &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.15}{2.326} = 0.06449 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2: Calculate $C_p$:**

\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{10.8 - 10.0}{6 \times 0.06449} = \frac{0.8}{0.38694} = 2.067
\end{aligned}

**Step 3: Calculate $C_{pk}$:**

\begin{aligned}
C_{pk, \text{upper}} &= \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\sigma} = \frac{10.8 - 10.45}{3 \times 0.06449} = \frac{0.35}{0.19347} = 1.809 \\
C_{pk, \text{lower}} &= \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{10.45 - 10.0}{3 \times 0.06449} = \frac{0.45}{0.19347} = 2.326 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(1.809, \; 2.326) = 1.809
\end{aligned}

**Step 4: Sigma Level (Process Z-score):**

\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{upper}} &= \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{X}}}{\sigma} = \frac{0.35}{0.06449} = 5.427 \\
Z_{\text{lower}} &= \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - LSL}{\sigma} = \frac{0.45}{0.06449} = 6.978 \\
Z_{\min} &= \min(5.427, 6.978) = 5.427 \\
\text{Process Sigma Level} &= Z_{\min} + 1.5 \; \text{(industry 1.5 sigma shift convention)} = 5.427 + 1.5 = 6.93\sigma
\end{aligned}

**Step 5: Comments:**

- $C_p = 2.067$ → process is **highly capable** of meeting the design width.
- $C_{pk} = 1.809$ → process is **well-centered** with substantial safety margin.
- $C_p - C_{pk} = 0.258$ → process is slightly off-centred (closer to USL than LSL).
- **Sigma level of $6.93\sigma$ corresponds to < 0.001 DPMO (defects per million opportunities) → world-class / Six Sigma quality.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):**
> 1. **Confusing Cp and Cpk:** $C_p$ is a *potential* measure (spec width / process width), while $C_{pk}$ is the *actual* capability including centering. Many students write only one formula. **Both must be derived.** (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the $\bar{R}/d_2$ conversion:** When given $\bar{R}$, you **must** estimate $\sigma$ using $d_2$. Directly substituting $\bar{R}$ in the $C_p$ formula is wrong. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Sigma level convention:** KTU accepts both $Z_{\text{min}}$ and $Z_{\text{min}} + 1.5$. Always state which convention you are using.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout (General)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks in SPC Questions:**
> 1. **Skipping the rational subgroup justification** (1 mark lost per question).
> 2. **Using $3\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ directly when $\sigma$ is not given** — must use $A_2 \bar{R}$.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in capability index answers (dimensionless, but state clearly).
> 4. **Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$** — they measure different things.
> 5. **Not drawing the control chart sketch** even when asked (lose 1-2 marks).
> 6. **Writing "process is in control" without listing all checks** (mean chart + range chart both).
> 7. **Using Western Electric / Nelson Rules incorrectly** — must state the rule number and threshold.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Print This Page**

- ✅ SPC was developed by **Walter Shewhart (1924)**; promoted by **Deming and Juran** in Japan.
- ✅ **Two variations:** Common cause (system) vs. Special cause (local).
- ✅ **Central Limit Theorem** is the statistical foundation of all variable control charts.
- ✅ **3-sigma limits** balance Type I error (false alarm) and Type II error (missed detection).
- ✅ **$\bar{X}$ chart** monitors process **mean**; **R chart** monitors process **variability**.
- ✅ **Control limits ≠ Specification limits.** Control limits are derived from the *process*; specification limits come from the *customer*.
- ✅ **Rational subgroup** is the cornerstone of valid SPC.
- ✅ **$\bar{X}$ and R chart** is used for $n \leq 10$; **$\bar{X}$ and S chart** for $n > 10$.
- ✅ **X and MR chart** is used for $n = 1$.
- ✅ **Attribute charts:** p, np, c, u — choose based on *what is counted* and *sample size variation*.
- ✅ **$C_p$** = potential capability; **$C_{pk}$** = actual capability with centering.
- ✅ **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** is the industry minimum; **$\geq 1.67$** is excellent; **$\geq 2.00$** is Six Sigma.
- ✅ **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO** corresponds to a process with $4.5\sigma$ performance (after 1.5$\sigma$ shift).
- ✅ **Western Electric Rules:** 8 rules; 1 point outside 3$\sigma$, 2 of 3 beyond 2$\sigma$, etc.
- ✅ **ARL₀ = 370.4** for a 3-sigma chart in control.
- ✅ **Always check both** $\bar{X}$ **and R charts** before declaring a process in control.
- ✅ In KTU numerical problems, **always state the constants used** ($A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $d_2$).
- ✅ **Process is in control ≠ Process is capable.** In control refers to stability; capable refers to specifications.
- ✅ **PDCA / DMAIC cycle** uses SPC in the "Control" phase to sustain improvements.
- ✅ **Capability = stability + meeting specifications.**

> **FINAL ADVICE:** For KTU Board Exam — always write the **formula first, then substitute, then evaluate, then interpret**. A clear four-step structure fetches full marks every time. End every numerical with a one-line engineering interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Control Chart** is a statistical process control (SPC) tool invented by **Walter A. Shewhart** in **1924**. It is a time-series plot of a specific process or quality characteristic, accompanied by a **Center Line (CL)** and statistically derived **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** and **Lower Control Limit (LCL)**, which distinguish between *common cause* (random) and *special cause* (assignable) variation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Control charts are the backbone of **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**. They help a process remain in a state of *statistical control*, meaning the process variation is predictable within the limits.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a child playing in a fenced garden. The fence acts as the **control limits**. As long as the child plays within the fence, the parent is relaxed (process is in control). The moment the child climbs over the fence (a point falls outside UCL/LCL), the parent immediately investigates — *"What happened? Did a dog enter?"* (special cause variation). Control charts are exactly this fence — they don't prevent the child from wandering; they simply alert the parent (engineer) the moment something abnormal occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A control chart **monitors** a process; it does **not** control it. Decisions are taken by the operator or engineer after seeing the chart.

### 1.3 Components of a Control Chart
- **Center Line (CL):** Represents the process average (mean of the quality characteristic).
- **Upper Control Limit (UCL):** Set at $\mu + 3\sigma$ — the threshold above which the process is considered "out of control."
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL):** Set at $\mu - 3\sigma$ — the threshold below which the process is considered "out of control."
- **Plotted Points:** Sample statistics (means, ranges, proportions) plotted in time order.
- **Zone Boundaries (Zone A, B, C):** Auxiliary lines at $2\sigma$ and $1\sigma$ used in the **Western Electric Rules** for pattern recognition.

### 1.4 Classification of Control Charts
Control charts are broadly classified into two major categories:

| **Type** | **Name** | **Use** |
|---|---|---|
| **Variable Charts** | $\bar{X}$-chart, R-chart, S-chart | Continuous data (length, weight, temperature) |
| **Attribute Charts** | p-chart, np-chart, c-chart, u-chart | Discrete data (defectives, defects) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

**Why 3-sigma limits?**
Shewhart's reasoning: Under the assumption of normality, the probability of a sample mean falling outside $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ is only **0.27%** (or **2,700 ppm**). This is a small, acceptable false-alarm rate. Using 2-sigma would yield too many false alarms (4.5%), and 4-sigma would be too insensitive (only 0.006% of points outside).

**How are control limits derived?**
1. Collect sample subgroups of size $n$ (typically $n = 5$).
2. Compute subgroup statistics (mean, range, proportion, etc.).
3. Estimate process standard deviation using subgroup statistics.
4. Set $\text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ and $\text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} - 3 \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.
5. Plot the data and look for out-of-control patterns.

### 2.2 Assumptions
- Samples are drawn from a **stable** process initially.
- Data are **independent** and **identically distributed**.
- The characteristic follows a **normal distribution** (for $\bar{X}$, $R$, $S$ charts).

> [!NOTE]
> When normality fails, **transformation methods** (log, square root) or **non-parametric charts** (sign test, Wilcoxon) are used.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Chart** | **Center Line** | **UCL** | **LCL** | **Estimate of $\sigma$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}$-chart | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ |
| R-chart | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ | — |
| S-chart | $\bar{S}$ | $B_4 \bar{S}$ | $B_3 \bar{S}$ | $\frac{\bar{S}}{c_4}$ |
| p-chart | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ |
| np-chart | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | — |
| c-chart | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ |
| u-chart | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | — |

**Standard SPC Constants Table (for various n):**

| n | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ | $E_2$ | $B_3$ | $B_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | 2.660 | 0 | 3.267 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 | 1.772 | 0 | 2.568 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | 1.457 | 0 | 2.266 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 | 1.290 | 0 | 2.089 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | 1.184 | 0.030 | 1.970 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | 1.109 | 0.118 | 1.882 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Control charts are deployed in automotive (ISO/TS 16949), pharmaceuticals (FDA validation), semiconductor (Six-Sigma), and heavy manufacturing (assembly lines) to achieve real-time quality assurance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Control Limits for the $\bar{X}$-chart

**Step 1: Theoretical Foundation**
The Central Limit Theorem states that if $X_i \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then the sample mean $\bar{X}$ of a subgroup of size $n$ follows a normal distribution:

$$
\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Standardize the Sample Mean**
Define the standard normal variate:

$$
Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 3: Apply 3-Sigma Rule**
For 99.73% probability coverage, set $\vert Z \vert \le 3$:

$$
-3 \le \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \le 3
$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to Find $\bar{X}$ Limits**
Multiplying both sides by $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$ and adding $\mu$:

$$
\mu - 3\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \le \bar{X} \le \mu + 3\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 5: Estimate $\mu$ and $\sigma$ from Sample Data**
We use the grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}}$ as an estimate of $\mu$, and the average range $\bar{R}$ divided by the constant $d_2$ to estimate $\sigma$:

$$
\hat{\mu} = \bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i
$$

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} \quad \text{where} \quad \bar{R} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i
$$

**Step 6: Combine to Obtain Final Control Limits**

$$
\text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}/d_2}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}
$$

$$
\text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} - 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}/d_2}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
$$

$$
\text{CL} = \bar{\bar{X}}
$$

where $A_2 = \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$ is a tabulated constant.

### 3.2 Derivation of R-chart Limits

For subgroup ranges, the distribution is not normal but follows a **chi-related distribution**. The mean and standard deviation of R are:

$$
E(R) = d_2 \sigma \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_R = d_3 \sigma
$$

The control limits for R-chart are:

$$
\text{UCL}_R = \bar{R} + 3 d_3 \sigma = \bar{R} + 3 d_3 \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \bar{R} \left(1 + \frac{3d_3}{d_2}\right) = D_4 \bar{R}
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_R = \bar{R} - 3 d_3 \sigma = D_3 \bar{R}
$$

where $D_3 = 1 - \frac{3 d_3}{d_2}$ and $D_4 = 1 + \frac{3 d_3}{d_2}$.

### 3.3 Derivation of p-chart Limits

The number of defectives in a sample of size $n$ follows a **binomial distribution** with parameter $p$:

$$
X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)
$$

The mean and variance are:

$$
\mu_X = n p \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_X^2 = n p (1 - p)
$$

The sample proportion $\hat{p} = X/n$ has:

$$
\mu_{\hat{p}} = p \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}
$$

Using the 3-sigma rule on $\hat{p}$:

$$
\text{UCL}_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}
$$

$$
\text{CL} = \bar{p}
$$

where $\bar{p} = \frac{\text{Total defectives}}{\text{Total inspected}}$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Control Chart

| **Question Asked** | **Data Type** | **Sample Size** | **Recommended Chart** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Is process mean in control? | Continuous | Any | $\bar{X}$-chart |
| Is process variability in control? | Continuous | $n \le 10$ | R-chart |
| Is process variability in control? | Continuous | $n > 10$ | S-chart |
| Is fraction of defectives stable? | Attribute | Variable | p-chart |
| Number of defectives stable? | Attribute | Constant | np-chart |
| Number of defects per unit stable? | Attribute | Unit = sample | c-chart |
| Defects per unit (unequal n) | Attribute | Variable | u-chart |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Control Chart Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Input] --> B[Sample Subgroup of size n]
    B --> C{Data Type?}
    C -- Continuous --> D1[Compute mean X-bar and Range R]
    C -- Attribute --> D2[Compute p or c statistic]
    D1 --> E1[Plot on X-bar and R charts]
    D2 --> E2[Plot on p-chart or c-chart]
    E1 --> F1[Compare with UCL, CL, LCL]
    E2 --> F2[Compare with UCL, CL, LCL]
    F1 --> G1{Point within limits?}
    F2 --> G2{Point within limits?}
    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Process In Control - Continue Monitoring]
    G1 -- No --> I1[Out of Control - Investigate Special Cause]
    G2 -- Yes --> H2[Process In Control - Continue Monitoring]
    G2 -- No --> I2[Out of Control - Investigate Special Cause]
    I1 --> J[Apply Western Electric Rules]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K[Corrective Action]
    H1 --> L[Plot Next Subgroup]
    H2 --> L
    K --> L
    L --> B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Control Chart Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Quality Characteristic] --> S2{Continuous or Attribute?}
    S2 -- Continuous --> S3{Sample size n}
    S2 -- Attribute --> S4{Type of Count}
    S3 -- n less than or equal 10 --> S5[X-bar and R Chart]
    S3 -- n greater than 10 --> S6[X-bar and S Chart]
    S4 -- Defectives --> S7{Variable n}
    S4 -- Defects --> S8{Variable n}
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[p-chart]
    S7 -- No --> S10[np-chart]
    S8 -- Yes --> S11[u-chart]
    S8 -- No --> S12[c-chart]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Western Electric Rules (Pattern Recognition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pattern 1: One point beyond 3-sigma] --> R1[Special Cause]
    P2[Pattern 2: 2 of 3 consecutive points beyond 2-sigma] --> R2[Special Cause]
    P3[Pattern 3: 4 of 5 consecutive points beyond 1-sigma] --> R3[Special Cause]
    P4[Pattern 4: 8 consecutive points on one side of CL] --> R4[Special Cause Shift]
    P5[Pattern 5: 6 points trending up or down] --> R5[Special Cause Trend]
    P6[Pattern 6: 15 points within 1-sigma Zone C] --> R6[Stratification Possible]
    P7[Pattern 7: 14 points alternating up and down] --> R7[Systematic Variation]
    P8[Pattern 8: 8 points beyond 1-sigma both sides] --> R8[Mixture Pattern]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Western Electric Handbook (1956)** standardized these rules. KTU examinations frequently test **Rule 1 (point outside 3σ)** and **Rule 4 (run/shift of 8 points)** as the most common tests for detecting non-random behavior.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define a control chart. List any four of its key components.

**Model Answer:**
A control chart is a graphical tool used in Statistical Process Control (SPC) to monitor a process over time. It is a time-ordered plot of a sample statistic with a center line (CL) and upper/lower control limits (UCL/LCL) typically set at ±3σ from the mean.
**Key components:**
1. Center Line (CL) – the process mean
2. Upper Control Limit (UCL) – μ + 3σ
3. Lower Control Limit (LCL) – μ − 3σ
4. Plotted points representing sample statistics
5. Zone lines (optional) at ±1σ and ±2σ

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between control limits and specification limits.

**Model Answer:**

| **Control Limits** | **Specification Limits** |
|---|---|
| Determined statistically from process data (μ ± 3σ) | Determined by customer/design requirements |
| Reflects *what the process is doing* | Reflects *what the process should do* |
| Separates common from special cause variation | Separates conforming from non-conforming product |
| Calculated using $\bar{X}$, $R$, $p$, $c$ | Calculated from engineering tolerances |
| Voice of the process (VoP) | Voice of the customer (VoC) |

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question Bank]**

(a) The inside diameters of 25 samples (each of size 5) taken from a manufacturing process are measured. The sample means and ranges are given below. Construct an $\bar{X}$-chart and an R-chart using $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$ for $n = 5$.

| Sample | $\bar{X}$ (mm) | $R$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50.2 | 1.0 |
| 2 | 50.5 | 1.2 |
| 3 | 50.1 | 0.8 |
| 4 | 50.4 | 1.1 |
| 5 | 50.3 | 1.3 |
| 6 | 50.6 | 0.9 |
| 7 | 50.2 | 1.0 |
| 8 | 50.5 | 1.4 |
| 9 | 50.3 | 1.1 |
| 10 | 50.4 | 1.0 |
| 11 | 50.2 | 1.2 |
| 12 | 50.3 | 0.9 |
| 13 | 50.4 | 1.0 |
| 14 | 50.1 | 1.3 |
| 15 | 50.5 | 1.1 |
| 16 | 50.3 | 1.0 |
| 17 | 50.2 | 1.2 |
| 18 | 50.4 | 0.8 |
| 19 | 50.5 | 1.1 |
| 20 | 50.3 | 1.0 |
| 21 | 50.4 | 1.3 |
| 22 | 50.2 | 1.0 |
| 23 | 50.3 | 1.1 |
| 24 | 50.5 | 1.2 |
| 25 | 50.4 | 1.0 |

(b) A process produces items with an average of 4 defects per unit. Construct a c-chart and comment on the state of control if the following 10 samples show the number of defects as: 3, 5, 2, 6, 4, 3, 7, 5, 4, 3.

### Model Solution to Q (a):

**Step 1: Compute Grand Average and Average Range**

$$
\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{25} \sum \bar{X}_i = \frac{1}{25} (50.2 + 50.5 + \ldots + 50.4)
$$

Sum of $\bar{X}_i$ values: $50.2 + 50.5 + 50.1 + 50.4 + 50.3 + 50.6 + 50.2 + 50.5 + 50.3 + 50.4 + 50.2 + 50.3 + 50.4 + 50.1 + 50.5 + 50.3 + 50.2 + 50.4 + 50.5 + 50.3 + 50.4 + 50.2 + 50.3 + 50.5 + 50.4 = 1257.3$

$$
\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1257.3}{25} = 50.292 \text{ mm}
$$

Sum of $R_i$: $1.0 + 1.2 + 0.8 + 1.1 + 1.3 + 0.9 + 1.0 + 1.4 + 1.1 + 1.0 + 1.2 + 0.9 + 1.0 + 1.3 + 1.1 + 1.0 + 1.2 + 0.8 + 1.1 + 1.0 + 1.3 + 1.0 + 1.1 + 1.2 + 1.0 = 27.2$

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{27.2}{25} = 1.088 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Control Limits for $\bar{X}$-chart**

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50.292 + (0.577)(1.088)
$$

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 50.292 + 0.628 = 50.920 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 50.292 - 0.628 = 49.664 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 50.292 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Control Limits for R-chart**

$$
\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(1.088) = 2.300 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(1.088) = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{CL}_R = 1.088 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**
All $\bar{X}_i$ values lie between 50.1 and 50.6 mm, well within [49.664, 50.920]. All $R_i$ values lie between 0.8 and 1.4 mm, within [0, 2.300]. **The process is in statistical control.**

### Model Solution to Q (b):

**Step 1: Calculate Center Line**

$$
\bar{c} = \frac{\text{Total defects}}{\text{Number of samples}} = \frac{3+5+2+6+4+3+7+5+4+3}{10} = \frac{42}{10} = 4.2
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Control Limits**

$$
\sigma_c = \sqrt{\bar{c}} = \sqrt{4.2} = 2.049
$$

$$
\text{UCL}_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 4.2 + 3(2.049) = 4.2 + 6.148 = 10.348
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 4.2 - 6.148 = -1.948 \approx 0
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**
All sample values (max = 7) lie within [0, 10.348]. The lowest LCL is set to 0 (defects cannot be negative). **The process is in statistical control.**

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

(a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction of an $\bar{X}$-chart. State the formula for UCL, CL, and LCL. Explain why 3-sigma limits are preferred.

(b) In a textile mill, 200 pieces of cloth are inspected each day for defects. Over 15 days, the number of defectives observed is given. Construct a p-chart. Data: 18, 22, 20, 25, 17, 19, 21, 23, 20, 16, 24, 19, 22, 18, 21.

### Model Solution to Q (a):

The $\bar{X}$-chart is constructed as follows:
- **Y-axis:** Sample mean values ($\bar{X}_i$)
- **X-axis:** Sample number or time
- **Three horizontal lines:** UCL, CL, LCL

**Formulas (for subgroup size n):**

$$
\text{CL} = \bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i
$$

$$
\text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \quad ; \quad \text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
$$

**Why 3-sigma limits?**
1. **Economic balance:** 2σ limits give too many false alarms (4.5%), 4σ limits are too insensitive. 3σ is a good compromise.
2. **Coverage:** 99.73% of normally distributed points lie within ±3σ.
3. **Empirical robustness:** Even for non-normal data, the 3σ rule provides acceptable false-alarm rates.
4. **Historical precedent:** Shewhart empirically validated the 3σ choice for industrial processes.

### Model Solution to Q (b):

**Step 1: Compute Average Proportion Defective**

$$
\bar{p} = \frac{\text{Total defectives}}{\text{Total inspected}} = \frac{18+22+20+25+17+19+21+23+20+16+24+19+22+18+21}{15 \times 200}
$$

$$
\bar{p} = \frac{305}{3000} = 0.1017
$$

**Step 2: Compute Standard Error**

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1017 \times 0.8983}{200}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0913}{200}} = \sqrt{0.0004566} = 0.02137
$$

**Step 3: Compute Control Limits**

$$
\text{UCL}_p = 0.1017 + 3(0.02137) = 0.1017 + 0.0641 = 0.1658
$$

$$
\text{LCL}_p = 0.1017 - 3(0.02137) = 0.1017 - 0.0641 = 0.0376
$$

$$
\text{CL}_p = 0.1017
$$

**Step 4: Plot the Sample Proportions**

| Day | Defectives | $p_i$ | In Control? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 18 | 0.090 | Yes |
| 2 | 22 | 0.110 | Yes |
| 3 | 20 | 0.100 | Yes |
| 4 | 25 | 0.125 | Yes |
| 5 | 17 | 0.085 | Yes |
| 6 | 19 | 0.095 | Yes |
| 7 | 21 | 0.105 | Yes |
| 8 | 23 | 0.115 | Yes |
| 9 | 20 | 0.100 | Yes |
| 10 | 16 | 0.080 | Yes |
| 11 | 24 | 0.120 | Yes |
| 12 | 19 | 0.095 | Yes |
| 13 | 22 | 0.110 | Yes |
| 14 | 18 | 0.090 | Yes |
| 15 | 21 | 0.105 | Yes |

All points lie within [0.0376, 0.1658]. **The process is in statistical control.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Don't confuse control limits with specification limits.** UCL/LCL are statistical, USL/LSL are engineering tolerances.
> 2. **Wrong use of constants:** Forgetting to use $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ from the table for the correct sample size $n$ — KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** for this.
> 3. **Negative LCL for c-chart / p-chart:** If LCL comes negative, set it to **zero** (not the negative value), as defects/proportions cannot be negative. Many students leave it as negative — losing **1 mark**.
> 4. **Unit consistency:** Always write units (mm, %, defects) in the final limits. Marks deducted for bare numbers.
> 5. **Interpretation marks:** KTU gives **1 mark** for stating whether the process is in or out of control and providing a one-line justification.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Control chart** is a time-ordered plot of sample statistics with statistical control limits (typically ±3σ).
- **Two types of variation:** common cause (random, inherent) and special cause (assignable, abnormal).
- **Variable charts** ($\bar{X}$, R, S) are used for continuous data; **attribute charts** (p, np, c, u) are used for discrete (count) data.
- **Standard formula for $\bar{X}$-chart:** $\text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$, $\text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$.
- **R-chart limits:** $\text{UCL} = D_4 \bar{R}$, $\text{LCL} = D_3 \bar{R}$.
- **p-chart limits:** $\bar{p} \pm 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$.
- **c-chart limits:** $\bar{c} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$.
- **Use np-chart** when subgroup size $n$ is constant and you are counting defectives.
- **Use u-chart** when subgroup size $n$ is variable and you are counting defects per unit.
- **Western Electric Rules** extend detection beyond Rule 1 (out-of-limits point) to detect runs, trends, and patterns within limits.
- **Negative LCL** is set to zero for c-chart and p-chart (defects/proportions are non-negative).
- **Why 3σ?** 99.73% coverage; optimal balance between false alarms (Type I error) and missed signals (Type II error).
- **Control limits ≠ Specification limits:** VoP (process) vs VoC (customer) — KTU repeatedly tests this distinction.
- **Choice of subgroup size:** $n = 4, 5$ is typical; subgroups should be chosen to **minimize within-group variation** and **maximize between-group variation** (rational subgrouping by Shewhart).
- **Trial limits:** First 20–25 subgroups establish the limits; revise only if assignable causes are found in retrospect.
- **OC curve of control chart** gives the probability of detecting a shift of size $d = \frac{\vert \mu - \mu_1 \vert}{\sigma}$ on the first sample.
- **Average Run Length (ARL):** $ARL_0 = \frac{1}{\alpha}$ (in-control), $ARL_1 = \frac{1}{1-\beta}$ (out-of-control). For 3σ limits, $ARL_0 \approx 370.4$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: X̄ and R Chart Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** X̄ and R Chart Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**X̄ (X-bar) and R Chart** are two of the most widely used **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** tools in Quality Engineering. They are **variables control charts** used to monitor a manufacturing or service process by tracking the process mean and process variability simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **X̄ Chart (X-bar Chart)**: A control chart that monitors the **central tendency** (process mean) of a quality characteristic by plotting the sample means (X̄) of subgroups taken from the process over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **R Chart (Range Chart)**: A control chart that monitors the **process variability (dispersion)** by plotting the sample range (R = X_max − X_min) of subgroups taken from the process over time.

Together, they form the **X̄–R chart pair**, considered the backbone of Phase I SPC implementation. The X̄ chart detects **shifts in the process average**, while the R chart detects **changes in process spread**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are a **chocolate factory owner** who packages 250g chocolate bars. Every hour, you randomly pick **5 bars** (a *subgroup of size n = 5*) and weigh them.

- The **average weight** of those 5 bars gives you a single number — this is plotted on the **X̄ chart**. If suddenly the average weight drops to 240g, you know your filling machine is under-filling.
- The **range** (heaviest minus lightest) of those 5 bars tells you how *consistent* the bars are. If the range becomes huge, your machine is becoming erratic, even if the average is still on target.

So:
> **X̄ chart = "Are we hitting the target on average?"**
> **R chart = "Are we consistent, or all over the place?"**

If both charts are "in control," the process is **stable and predictable**. If either chart signals "out of control," an **assignable cause** must be investigated (the 5M framework: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement).

---

## 1.3 When to Use X̄ and R Charts (Standard KTU Conditions)

| Condition | Detail |
|---|---|
| Data type | **Variables data** (measurable on a continuous scale — length, weight, diameter, etc.) |
| Subgroup size | Typically **n = 2 to 10** (most commonly n = 4 or n = 5) |
| Sample frequency | Regular intervals (every hour, every shift, every batch) |
| Rational subgroups | Subgroups selected such that within-subgroup variation reflects **common-cause** variation only |
| Sample size assumption | **n is constant** across all subgroups (for the standard tables) |

> [!NOTE]
> **X̄ and R charts are used when the subgroup size is small (n ≤ 10)** and computing the standard deviation of every subgroup is impractical. For larger subgroups, X̄ and **S** (standard deviation) charts are preferred.

---

## 1.4 The Three-Sigma Control Limits — The Heart of the System

The control limits on both charts are set at **±3 standard deviations** from the central line (CL). This is known as the **3-sigma control limit principle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-Sigma Rule**: If a process is stable, **99.73%** of all plotted points will fall within the control limits. The probability of a point falling outside *purely by chance* is only **0.27%** (about 3 in 1000). A point outside the limits is therefore taken as **statistical evidence of an assignable cause**.

Standard KTU parameters and metrics (used universally):

- **Process Mean (Grand Average):** $\bar{\bar{X}}$ (X-double-bar)
- **Average Range:** $\bar{R}$ (R-bar)
- **Control Limits:** UCL (Upper Control Limit), CL (Center Line), LCL (Lower Control Limit)
- **Standard factors:** $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ — all are **tabulated constants** that depend only on the subgroup size **n**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theory — The Logic Behind the Formulas

The X̄ and R charts work on the principle that **if the process is in statistical control**, the sample statistics (X̄, R) will vary about their long-run averages within predictable, statistically bounded limits.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Collect k subgroups**, each of size **n**, under what is believed to be a stable process.
2. Compute the **mean** of each subgroup → these are the X̄ values.
3. Compute the **range** of each subgroup → these are the R values.
4. Compute the **grand average** $\bar{\bar{X}}$ = average of all X̄ values.
5. Compute the **average range** $\bar{R}$ = average of all R values.
6. Estimate the **process standard deviation** σ using the relationship:
   $$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$
   where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant depending on n.
7. The **standard error of X̄** is:
   $$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$$
8. Apply the **3-sigma rule** to obtain control limits on X̄:
   $$\text{Limits} = \bar{\bar{X}} \pm 3 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}}$$
9. Apply the **3-sigma rule** to the range distribution (using tabulated constants $D_3$ and $D_4$):
   $$\text{Limits} = \bar{R} \pm 3\sigma_R$$
   which, when simplified, gives $\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ and $\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every formula** you need for solving X̄ and R chart problems in the KTU ESE. The constants $A_2, D_3, D_4, d_2$ depend only on subgroup size **n** and are **given in the standard SPC tables** — memorize the values for n = 2, 3, 4, 5 (the most frequently tested ones).

### Master Formula Table

| # | Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample mean of subgroup i | $\bar{X}_i = \frac{\sum X_{ij}}{n}$ | Plot point on X̄ chart |
| 2 | Sample range of subgroup i | $R_i = X_{i,max} - X_{i,min}$ | Plot point on R chart |
| 3 | Grand mean | $\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k}$ | Center Line of X̄ chart |
| 4 | Average range | $\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k}$ | Center Line of R chart |
| 5 | UCL of X̄ | $\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Upper control limit (mean) |
| 6 | LCL of X̄ | $\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Lower control limit (mean) |
| 7 | UCL of R | $\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | Upper control limit (range) |
| 8 | LCL of R | $\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | Lower control limit (range) |
| 9 | Process std. deviation | $\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Estimated process σ |
| 10 | Trial UCL/LCL (one-sided) | $\text{UCL} = \bar{R} + 3 \cdot d_3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | For derivation only |
| 11 | Capability (when specs given) | $C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}}$ | Process capability index |

### Standard SPC Constants Table (KTU High-Frequency)

| Subgroup size **n** | **A₂** | **D₃** | **D₄** | **d₂** | **d₃** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | 0.853 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 | 0.888 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | 0.880 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 | 0.864 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | 0.848 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | 0.833 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 | 0.820 |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip**: For n = 2 to 6, **D₃ = 0**, so **LCL of R = 0** (a negative lower limit is set to zero — this is a standard KTU convention students often miss).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing**: Monitoring dimensions of machined parts (diameter, length, surface roughness) in CNC, casting, forging, and assembly lines.
- **Automotive**: Quality control of piston rings, brake pads, tire treads (SPC mandated by IATF 16949).
- **Electronics**: Monitoring solder-paste thickness, resistor values, and PCB hole diameters.
- **Service Industry**: Monitoring call-handling times, transaction processing times, blood-test results in labs.
- **Pharmaceutical**: Tablet weight variation, dissolution times, content uniformity.

The X̄–R chart pair is the **first step** in any Six Sigma / DMAIC project (Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control) — it establishes the **baseline process capability** before any improvement is attempted.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Problems & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the X̄ Control Limit Formula

Starting from the basic 3-sigma principle:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \mu_{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \mu_{\bar{X}} - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$$

where $\mu_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}$ (the grand average) and $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.

The process standard deviation is estimated using the average range:

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$

Now we define a single tabulated constant $A_2$:

$$A_2 = \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$

So:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the R Control Limit Formulas

The mean and standard deviation of the range R (for samples of size n from a normal distribution) are:

$$\mu_R = d_2 \cdot \sigma, \quad \sigma_R = d_3 \cdot \sigma$$

Therefore:

$$\text{UCL}_R = d_2\sigma + 3 d_3 \sigma = (d_2 + 3 d_3)\sigma$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = d_2\sigma - 3 d_3 \sigma = (d_2 - 3 d_3)\sigma$$

Since $\bar{R}$ is the best estimate of $d_2\sigma$ (i.e., $\bar{R} \approx d_2\sigma$):

$$\text{UCL}_R = \frac{(d_2 + 3d_3)}{d_2} \bar{R} = D_4 \bar{R}$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = \frac{(d_2 - 3d_3)}{d_2} \bar{R} = D_3 \bar{R}$$

---

## 3.3 Fully Solved Numerical Problem #1 (Standard KTU Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern - Worked Example]**
> The following data represent the **diameters (in mm)** of piston rings taken from a manufacturing process. **25 subgroups of size n = 5** were collected. A small portion of the calculated data is given below:

| Subgroup | X̄ (mm) | R (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 74.030 | 0.90 |
| 2 | 74.002 | 0.85 |
| 3 | 73.992 | 1.20 |
| 4 | 74.010 | 0.95 |
| 5 | 74.005 | 0.75 |
| ... | ... | ... |
| 20 | 74.000 | 0.80 |
| 21 | 74.020 | 0.90 |
| 22 | 73.985 | 1.10 |
| 23 | 74.015 | 0.85 |
| 24 | 74.005 | 0.95 |
| 25 | 74.010 | 0.90 |
| **Total** | **1850.250** | **21.40** |
| **Average** | **$\bar{\bar{X}}$ = 74.010** | **$\bar{R}$ = 0.856** |

**Required**: Construct the X̄ and R charts and interpret the results.

**Solution**:

**Step 1** — Identify n and k.
$$n = 5, \quad k = 25$$

**Step 2** — Look up the constants for n = 5:
$$A_2 = 0.577, \quad D_3 = 0, \quad D_4 = 2.114$$

**Step 3** — Compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}$:
$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k} = \frac{1850.250}{25} = 74.010 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k} = \frac{21.40}{25} = 0.856 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4** — Compute X̄ chart limits:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} = 74.010 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\[4pt]
&= 74.010 + (0.577)(0.856) \\[4pt]
&= 74.010 + 0.494 \\[4pt]
&= 74.504 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} \\[4pt]
&= 74.010 - 0.494 \\[4pt]
&= 73.516 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5** — Compute R chart limits:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CL}_R &= \bar{R} = 0.856 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{UCL}_R &= D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(0.856) = 1.810 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{LCL}_R &= D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(0.856) = 0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6** — Interpretation:
- All X̄ values (74.030, 74.002, …) lie between **73.516 and 74.504 mm** → **X̄ chart is in control**.
- All R values (0.90, 0.85, …) lie between **0 and 1.810 mm** → **R chart is in control**.
- **Conclusion**: The process is in **statistical control**. The estimated process standard deviation is:

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.856}{2.326} = 0.368 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 3.4 Fully Solved Numerical Problem #2 (Including Capability)

> **[Extended KTU Problem with Process Specification]**
> Using the same process from Problem #1, the **design specification** is: USL = 74.500 mm, LSL = 73.500 mm. Determine the **process capability index** $C_p$ and comment on whether the process is capable.

**Solution**:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{74.500 - 73.500}{6 \times 0.368} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.000}{2.208} \\[4pt]
&= 0.453
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation**:
- A $C_p \geq 1.00$ is required for a capable process.
- Here $C_p = 0.453 < 1$, so the process is **NOT capable** — the **specification width (1.000 mm)** is **smaller** than the **6-sigma process spread (2.208 mm)**.
- **Action needed**: Reduce process variation (improve $\hat{\sigma}$) or relax the specifications.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Scenarios

| Scenario | What to do | KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|
| Given raw data | Compute X̄ and R for each subgroup first | Forgetting to sum correctly across all k subgroups |
| Given only X̄ and R | Directly compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}$ | Mixing up $\bar{\bar{X}}$ with individual $\bar{X}_i$ |
| LCL of R comes negative | Set LCL_R = 0 (only for n ≤ 6) | Reporting a negative limit (physically impossible) |
| LCL of X̄ comes negative (rare) | Report as-is, but check for data error | Ignoring the sign |
| A point falls outside | Mark as "out of control" → investigate assignable cause | Forgetting to look for *trends* (Western Electric rules) |
| Need process σ | Use $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ | Confusing with $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ |
| Need $C_p$ | Use $C_p = (\text{USL}-\text{LSL})/(6\hat{\sigma})$ | Using $\bar{R}$ instead of $\hat{\sigma}$ in the denominator |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Procedure for Constructing X̄ and R Charts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Collect k subgroups of size n] --> B[Compute X-bar-i and R-i for each subgroup]
    B --> C[Sum all X-bar-i values]
    C --> D[Sum all R-i values]
    D --> E[Compute Grand Mean X-double-bar = Sum X-bar-i / k]
    E --> F[Compute Average Range R-bar = Sum R-i / k]
    F --> G[Look up constants A2, D3, D4 for subgroup size n]
    G --> H[Compute X-bar Chart Limits: UCL = X-double-bar plus A2 times R-bar]
    G --> I[Compute R Chart Limits: UCL = D4 times R-bar, LCL = D3 times R-bar]
    H --> J[Plot all X-bar-i values on X-bar chart]
    I --> K[Plot all R-i values on R chart]
    J --> L{Any point outside limits or showing non-random pattern?}
    K --> M{Any point outside limits or showing non-random pattern?}
    L -->|YES| N[Flag as OUT OF CONTROL: Investigate assignable cause using 5M framework]
    L -->|NO| O[X-bar chart IN CONTROL: Process mean is stable]
    M -->|YES| N
    M -->|NO| P[R chart IN CONTROL: Process variability is stable]
    N --> Q[Remove assignable cause data and RECOMPUTE trial limits]
    O --> R[Process is STABLE: Estimate sigma-hat and evaluate capability]
    P --> R
    R --> S[END: Establish revised control limits for ongoing Phase II monitoring]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Relationship Between X̄ Chart and R Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Under Study] --> B[Raw Sample Data: Subgroups of size n]
    B --> C1[Compute Subgroup Means: X-bar-i]
    B --> C2[Compute Subgroup Ranges: R-i]
    C1 --> D1[Grand Average: X-double-bar]
    C2 --> D2[Average Range: R-bar]
    D1 --> E1[X-bar Chart Construction]
    D2 --> E1
    D2 --> E2[R Chart Construction]
    E1 --> F1[Monitors: Central Tendency]
    E2 --> F2[Monitors: Process Dispersion]
    F1 --> G[Overall Process Stability Decision]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H{Stable Process?}
    H -->|YES| I[Compute sigma-hat = R-bar / d2]
    H -->|NO| J[Apply 5M: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement]
    I --> K[Capability Analysis: Cp, Cpk]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — The 3-Sigma Control Limit Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Distribution: Normal Curve] --> B[Mean = mu, Std Dev = sigma]
    B --> C1[Lower Control Limit: mu minus 3 sigma]
    B --> C2[Center Line: mu]
    B --> C3[Upper Control Limit: mu plus 3 sigma]
    C1 --> D1[Region A: 0.135 percent of area]
    C2 --> D2[Region B: 99.73 percent of area in control]
    C3 --> D3[Region A: 0.135 percent of area]
    D1 --> E[Point Outside UCL or LCL: Signal Out of Control]
    D3 --> E
    D2 --> F[Point Inside Limits: In Control]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Q: Distinguish between X̄ chart and R chart. Why are they always used together?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | X̄ Chart | R Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **What it monitors** | Process **mean (central tendency)** | Process **variability (spread)** |
| **Statistic plotted** | Subgroup average $\bar{X}_i$ | Subgroup range $R_i$ |
| **Center line** | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ (grand mean) | $\bar{R}$ (average range) |
| **Use** | Detects **shifts in the average** | Detects **changes in consistency** |

**Why used together (1 mark):**
- The R chart must be analyzed **first**. If the variability is out of control, the X̄ chart limits become **meaningless**, because $\bar{R}$ is no longer a valid estimate of process σ.
- Together they give a **complete picture** of process behavior — both **location** and **dispersion**.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Q: Define the 3-sigma control limits. What is the probability of a Type I error (false alarm) in a 3-sigma control chart?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** Control limits placed at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ (three standard deviations above and below the process mean). For the X̄ chart, this becomes $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} \pm A_2 \bar{R}$.
- **Probability of Type I error (1 mark):** Under a stable process, the probability of a point falling outside the 3-sigma limits purely by chance is:
$$P(\text{Type I error}) = 0.0027 = 0.27\%$$
