This corresponds to the **2 × (0.00135)** tails of the normal distribution beyond ±3σ.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a)** The inside diameter of a forged ring is being monitored. **20 subgroups of size n = 4** have been taken and the data is summarized below:
$$\sum \bar{X}_i = 100.20 \text{ mm}, \quad \sum R_i = 4.60 \text{ mm}$$

The specifications are: USL = 5.20 mm, LSL = 4.80 mm (target = 5.00 mm). Construct the X̄ and R charts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point in subgroup 14 was observed to have $\bar{X}_{14} = 4.85$ mm and $R_{14} = 0.40$ mm. Is the process in control? If not, what actions should be taken? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Construction of X̄ and R Charts (7 marks):**

**Step 1**: Identify k = 20, n = 4.
**Step 2**: Look up constants for n = 4: $A_2 = 0.729, D_3 = 0, D_4 = 2.282$.
**Step 3**: Compute central lines:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{\bar{X}} &= \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k} = \frac{100.20}{20} = 5.010 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\bar{R} &= \frac{\sum R_i}{k} = \frac{4.60}{20} = 0.230 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4**: X̄ chart limits:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 5.010 + (0.729)(0.230) \\[4pt]
&= 5.010 + 0.1677 = 5.178 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 5.010 - 0.1677 \\[4pt]
&= 4.842 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} &= 5.010 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5**: R chart limits:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_R &= D_4 \bar{R} = (2.282)(0.230) = 0.525 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{LCL}_R &= D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(0.230) = 0 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{CL}_R &= 0.230 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Chart summary (for plotting):**

| Chart | LCL | CL | UCL |
|---|---|---|---|
| X̄ | 4.842 | 5.010 | 5.178 |
| R | 0.000 | 0.230 | 0.525 |

---

**(b) Evaluation of subgroup 14 (7 marks):**

For subgroup 14:
- $\bar{X}_{14} = 4.85$ mm
- LCL of X̄ = 4.842 mm, UCL of X̄ = 5.178 mm

**Comparison with X̄ limits:**
$$4.842 < 4.85 < 5.178 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{OK (in control)}$$

Wait — let us re-examine: 4.85 is just slightly above the LCL of 4.842, so the **mean** is technically inside the limits. **However**:

- $R_{14} = 0.40$ mm
- UCL of R = 0.525 mm, LCL of R = 0

**Comparison with R limits:**
$$0 < 0.40 < 0.525 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{OK (in control)}$$

**Process is in control for this subgroup.** No assignable cause is signalled at this point.

**However**, we must also check **capability**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma} &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.230}{2.059} = 0.1117 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
C_p &= \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{5.20 - 4.80}{6(0.1117)} = \frac{0.40}{0.670} = 0.597
\end{aligned}$$

Since $C_p = 0.597 < 1.0$, the process is **statistically stable but NOT capable** of meeting the specifications.

**Recommended actions (any 3 for full marks):**
1. Investigate and reduce common-cause variation (improve machines, tooling, training).
2. Consider whether the specifications are realistic; consult the design engineer.
3. Implement statistical process control (X̄–R) for ongoing monitoring.
4. Use techniques like **Design of Experiments (DOE)** to find and reduce variation sources.
5. Apply the **Pareto chart and Ishikawa fishbone** to identify dominant variation sources.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **procedure for constructing an X̄–R control chart**. List the formulas for control limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a bottling plant, 25 samples of size n = 5 are taken. The data yields: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 502.5$ ml and $\bar{R} = 4.2$ ml. The target fill is 500 ml with USL = 510 ml and LSL = 490 ml. Compute the control limits for the X̄ and R charts and assess process capability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Procedure for X̄–R Chart Construction (7 marks):**

**Step 1**: **Sampling** — Collect k rational subgroups, each of size n (typically n = 4 or 5), at regular time intervals from the process.

**Step 2**: **Compute Statistics** — For each subgroup i:
$$\bar{X}_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{ij}}{n}, \quad R_i = X_{i,max} - X_{i,min}$$

**Step 3**: **Compute Central Lines**:
$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i, \quad \bar{R} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$$

**Step 4**: **Look up SPC constants** $A_2, D_3, D_4$ from the standard table for the given n.

**Step 5**: **Compute X̄ Chart Limits**:
$$\text{CL} = \bar{\bar{X}}, \quad \text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad \text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

**Step 6**: **Compute R Chart Limits**:
$$\text{CL} = \bar{R}, \quad \text{UCL} = D_4 \bar{R}, \quad \text{LCL} = D_3 \bar{R}$$

**Step 7**: **Plot the Charts** — Mark CL, UCL, LCL on the y-axis; plot each $\bar{X}_i$ on the X̄ chart and each $R_i$ on the R chart.

**Step 8**: **Interpret** — Look for points outside limits, runs, trends (Western Electric Rules). Investigate any out-of-control signal using the 5M framework (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: k = 25, n = 5, $\bar{\bar{X}} = 502.5$ ml, $\bar{R} = 4.2$ ml.

**Constants for n = 5**: $A_2 = 0.577, D_3 = 0, D_4 = 2.114, d_2 = 2.326$.

**X̄ Chart Limits:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= 502.5 + (0.577)(4.2) = 502.5 + 2.4234 = 504.923 \text{ ml} \\[4pt]
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= 502.5 - 2.4234 = 500.077 \text{ ml} \\[4pt]
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} &= 502.5 \text{ ml}
\end{aligned}$$

**R Chart Limits:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_R &= (2.114)(4.2) = 8.879 \text{ ml} \\[4pt]
\text{LCL}_R &= 0 \text{ ml} \\[4pt]
\text{CL}_R &= 4.2 \text{ ml}
\end{aligned}$$

**Process Capability:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma} &= \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{4.2}{2.326} = 1.806 \text{ ml} \\[4pt]
C_p &= \frac{510 - 490}{6 \times 1.806} = \frac{20}{10.836} = 1.846
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation**:
- $C_p = 1.846 > 1.33$ → the process is **highly capable** of meeting specifications.
- The process is **on target** (grand mean 502.5 is close to target 500 ml; minor off-centering may exist — $C_{pk}$ could be computed to confirm).
- Conclusion: The process is **stable and capable**. The X̄–R chart can be retained for ongoing Phase II monitoring.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\bar{\bar{X}}$ with $\bar{X}_i$** — $\bar{\bar{X}}$ is the average of all subgroup means (the grand average), not a single subgroup mean. Forgetting the second bar = **lose 1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong A₂, D₃, D₄ values** — Always match the constants to the **exact subgroup size n**. The value for n = 4 (A₂ = 0.729) is **not** the same as for n = 5 (A₂ = 0.577). Showing the n-value clearly earns the mark.
>
> 3. **Reporting a negative LCL for the R chart** — This is physically meaningless. When $D_3 = 0$, the LCL is exactly **zero**, not a negative number.
>
> 4. **Plotting the R chart before checking the X̄ chart** — The R chart must be analyzed **first**. If R is out of control, the X̄ limits are invalid. This is a standard KTU 2-mark trap question.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to estimate $\hat{\sigma}$** for the capability calculation — using $\bar{R}$ directly in $C_p$ is a common error. The correct formula uses $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$.
>
> 6. **Skipping the interpretation step** — KTU examiners expect a clear concluding statement: "Process is in control / out of control" and the action to be taken. **No interpretation = -2 marks minimum**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for X̄ and R Chart Problems:**
>
> - [ ] X̄ chart tracks the **process mean**; R chart tracks the **process variability** — both must be in control.
> - [ ] Analyze the **R chart first**; if R is out of control, the X̄ chart limits are invalid.
> - [ ] **Memorize** the SPC constants for n = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 (A₂, D₃, D₄, d₂, d₃).
> - [ ] For n = 2 to 6, $D_3 = 0$, hence $\text{LCL}_R = 0$.
> - [ ] Control limits are set at **±3σ** — gives 99.73% in-control probability and 0.27% false-alarm rate.
> - [ ] Process standard deviation is estimated as $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ (not $\bar{R}$ alone).
> - [ ] Capability index: $C_p = (\text{USL} - \text{LSL})/(6\hat{\sigma})$. Acceptable if $C_p \geq 1.00$ (industrial); $C_p \geq 1.33$ is the KTU textbook standard for a "capable" process.
> - [ ] **Out-of-control signals** must be investigated using the **5M framework**: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement.
> - [ ] X̄–R charts are **Phase I** tools — used to **establish** control limits on historical data. They become **Phase II** tools once the process is proven stable.
> - [ ] For **larger subgroups (n > 10)**, replace the R chart with the **S chart** (using sample standard deviation) and use $A_3$ instead of $A_2$.
> - [ ] Always state the **conclusion** clearly — KTU board examiners mark the interpretation step strictly.
> - [ ] **Units** must be consistent throughout — mm with mm, ml with ml, kg with kg. Mixing units = -1 mark.
> - [ ] A point **inside** the limits is not automatically "in control" — also check for **runs, trends, and Western Electric runs rules** (e.g., 7 points in a row on one side of CL).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Capability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Capability** is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a manufacturing or service process to produce output that conforms to the engineering **specification limits (USL and LSL)**. It quantifies the relationship between the **natural process spread (±3σ)** of a stable (in-control) process and the **tolerance width** allowed by the customer/design requirement.

Mathematically, the process is deemed **"capable"** when its total process variation (6σ) is fully contained within the specification limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle (KTU Board Favourite):** Process Capability can ONLY be evaluated when the process is **statistically stable (in statistical control)**. Computing Cp/Cpk on an unstable process is a fundamental methodological error and a guaranteed mark-deduction trap in KTU valuations.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Process Capability

| Concept | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Specification Limits** | USL, LSL | Voice of the Customer (Design requirements) |
| **Process Mean** | μ | Voice of the Process (Location) |
| **Process Standard Deviation** | σ | Voice of the Process (Spread) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Analogy"

Imagine a **single-lane highway** representing your manufacturing process:
- The **road width** = your **process spread (6σ)** — the natural variation inherent in your process.
- The **two solid white lines** on the edges = your **specification limits (LSL & USL)**.
- The **car's lane position** = your **process mean (μ)**.

**Process Capability (Cp)** asks: *"Is the road (process) narrower than the gap between the lines (tolerance)?"* — a question of **width comparison**.

**Process Capability Index (Cpk)** asks: *"Is the car driving right in the middle of the lane, or is it drifting towards the white line?"* — a question of **centering**.

> [!NOTE]
> A high Cp with a low Cpk means your process is **precise but not accurate** (think of a rifle scope mounted sideways — tight grouping, but all shots land to the left of the target).

## 1.4 Why Process Capability Matters in Industry

- **Six Sigma (6σ) Methodology** — Process capability is the numerical foundation of Motorola's Six Sigma (target Cpk = 2.0 for 6σ performance, meaning 3.4 DPMO).
- **Supplier Selection & Audits** — Automotive (IATF 16949) and aerospace (AS9100) OEMs mandate minimum **Cpk ≥ 1.33** for critical characteristics (Critical to Quality — CTQ).
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — Inadequate capability directly translates to rework, scrap, warranty, and customer dissatisfaction costs.
- **Process Improvement (DMAIC)** — Capability indices serve as the baseline metric in the **Measure** and **Control** phases of Lean Six Sigma projects.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Premises Before Calculating Capability

Before any formula is applied, the following conditions **MUST** be verified:

1. **Statistical Stability** — The process must be in control, verified using a control chart (X̄-R, I-MR, p-chart, etc.). Remove special causes using the **Western Electric Rules** or **Nelson Rules**.
2. **Normality of Data** — The process output should follow a Normal (Gaussian) distribution. Verify using a histogram, normal probability plot, or **Anderson-Darling** / **Shapiro-Wilk** tests.
3. **Independence** — Observations should be independent.
4. **Representative Sampling** — Sample size typically **n ≥ 25 to 30 subgroups** (≈ 125 individual measurements) for stable estimates.

## 2.2 The Two Categories of Capability Indices

### A. Process Capability Indices (Within-Subgroup / Short-Term)
These use the **average of within-subgroup variation (σ_within)** — denoted simply as **σ**:
- **Cp** — Process Capability
- **Cpk** — Process Capability Index (centered)
- **Cpm** — Process Capability Index (with target)

### B. Process Performance Indices (Overall / Long-Term)
These use the **total/overall standard deviation (σ_overall = s)** computed using all individual data points (not subgroup averages):
- **Pp** — Process Performance
- **Ppk** — Process Performance Index

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Very Important for KTU):** If the process is **stable AND subgroups are small (n ≤ 10)**, σ_within ≈ σ_overall. If subgroups are large or process drifts, **σ_overall > σ_within**, causing **Pp < Cp**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below use the **process standard deviation (σ)** for Cp/Cpk and the **overall sample standard deviation (s)** for Pp/Ppk.

| # | Index | Formula | What It Measures | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cp** (Process Capability) | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability (precision only) | $\sigma > 0$ |
| 2 | **CPU** (Upper Capability) | $\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | Distance of mean from USL | $\mu < USL$ |
| 3 | **CPL** (Lower Capability) | $\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | Distance of mean from LSL | $\mu > LSL$ |
| 4 | **Cpk** (Process Capability Index) | $\min\left[CPU,\ CPL\right]$ | Actual capability (precision + centering) | Always $\leq Cp$ |
| 5 | **Pp** (Process Performance) | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Long-term performance (precision) | Uses overall s |
| 6 | **Ppk** (Process Performance Index) | $\min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3s},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3s}\right]$ | Long-term performance (precision + centering) | Always $\leq Pp$ |
| 7 | **Cpm** (Taguchi Capability Index) | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$ | Capability penalizing off-target | T = Target value |
| 8 | **Off-centering factor (k)** | $\dfrac{\vert T - \mu \vert}{0.5(USL - LSL)}$ | % of half-tolerance shift | $0 \leq k \leq 1$ |
| 9 | **Sigma Level (Z)** | $\dfrac{Cpk \times 3}{1}$ (approx) | Distance of process edge to spec in σ | Higher = Better |
| 10 | **DPMO** | $1{,}460{,}000 \times P(\text{outside limits})$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Industry Standard metric |

## 2.4 Standard Interpretation Table (Memorize for KTU)

| Cpk Value | Process Performance | Approx. DPMO (2-sided, centered) |
|---|---|---|
| **0.00** | Mean on specification limit | 500,000 |
| **0.50** | Poor (3σ process) | 66,807 |
| **1.00** | Marginal (4σ process) | 6,210 |
| **1.33** | Capable (Industry minimum) | 63 |
| **1.50** | Highly capable (4.5σ) | 7 |
| **1.67** | Excellent (5σ) | 0.57 |
| **2.00** | World-class (6σ — Six Sigma!) | 0.002 (3.4 DPMO with shift) |

## 2.5 One-Sided Specifications

For processes with **only one specification limit** (e.g., strength, purity, surface finish where lower is better):
- For "Smaller-is-Better" (e.g., surface roughness): Use only **CPU** as the capability index.
- For "Larger-is-Better" (e.g., tensile strength): Use only **CPL** as the capability index.
- Cp and Cpk are **not defined** in one-sided cases; only CPU or CPL applies.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (IATF 16949):** Requires Cpk ≥ 1.33 for special characteristics; ≥ 1.67 for safety-critical.
- **Pharmaceutical (FDA Process Validation):** Capability indices are central to Stage 2 (Process Performance Qualification) and continued verification (Stage 3).
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** Operates routinely at Cpk > 2.0 to meet customer ppm targets.
- **Service Industries:** Applied to call handling time, delivery lead time, claims processing time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cp — The Precision Index

The concept of Cp originates from comparing the **tolerance width** (the window the engineer is allowed) with the **natural process width** (the variation the process actually exhibits).

### Step 1: Define the Natural Process Spread
For a stable, normally distributed process, **99.73%** of all output lies within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ (the 3-sigma rule, proven via the empirical rule of Normal Distribution).

$$
\text{Natural Process Width} = (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma
$$

### Step 2: Define the Specification Width
$$
\text{Specification Width} = USL - LSL
$$

### Step 3: Form the Ratio
If the tolerance width is larger than the process spread, the process can potentially fit. We define the ratio:

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Natural Process Width}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A Cp of 1.0 means the process *just barely* fits. A Cp of 2.0 means the tolerance is *twice* the process spread — very comfortable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Cpk — The Accuracy + Precision Index

The flaw in Cp is that it **assumes the process is perfectly centered**. In reality, processes drift. Consider the process mean $\mu$ shifted towards USL. The tighter constraint is the distance to the USL.

### Step 1: Distance from Mean to Upper Limit (in σ units)
$$
\text{Upper distance} = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

Dividing the number of σ to fit, by 3 (to align with the 3σ spread convention):

$$
CPU = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}
$$

### Step 2: Distance from Mean to Lower Limit
$$
CPL = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

### Step 3: Take the Minimum
The process will fail on the **closer side** first. The bottleneck defines the actual capability:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]
$$

> **Insight:** $\boxed{Cpk = Cp \times (1 - k)}$, where k is the off-centering factor. This means off-centering **always reduces** Cpk below Cp.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question)

> **[Problem]** A CNC lathe produces shafts with diameter specification **20.00 ± 0.10 mm** (USL = 20.10, LSL = 19.90). After 25 subgroups of size 5, the following are calculated: 
> - Process mean, $\bar{\bar{X}} = 20.04$ mm
> - Average subgroup range, $\bar{R} = 0.025$ mm
> - Standard deviation of all 125 readings, $s = 0.020$ mm
>
> Compute and interpret **Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk**, and the **sigma level**.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute within-subgroup standard deviation (σ)**
For subgroup size n = 5, the constant $d_2 = 2.326$.

$$
\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.025}{2.326} = 0.01075\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Cp**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{20.10 - 19.90}{6 \times 0.01075} = \frac{0.20}{0.0645} = 3.10
$$

**Step 3: Compute Cpk**

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{20.10 - 20.04}{3 \times 0.01075},\ \frac{20.04 - 19.90}{3 \times 0.01075}\right]
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{0.06}{0.03225},\ \frac{0.14}{0.03225}\right] = \min[1.86,\ 4.34] = 1.86
$$

**Step 4: Compute Pp**

$$
P_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6s} = \frac{0.20}{6 \times 0.020} = \frac{0.20}{0.12} = 1.67
$$

**Step 5: Compute Ppk**

$$
P_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{0.06}{3 \times 0.020},\ \frac{0.14}{3 \times 0.020}\right] = \min\left[1.00,\ 2.33\right] = 1.00
$$

**Step 6: Compute Off-centering Factor k**

$$
k = \frac{\vert T - \mu \vert}{0.5(USL - LSL)} = \frac{\vert 20.00 - 20.04 \vert}{0.10} = 0.40\ (40\%\ \text{off-center})
$$

**Step 7: Sigma Level (Long-term)**

$$
Z_{LT} = \min\left[\frac{USL - \mu}{s},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{s}\right] = \min[3.0,\ 7.0] = 3.0\sigma
$$

### Interpretation Matrix

| Metric | Value | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|
| Cp = 3.10 | High | Process *precision* is excellent — natural spread is well within tolerance. |
| Cpk = 1.86 | Excellent | Capable AND relatively well centered. |
| Pp = 1.67 | Acceptable | Long-term precision meets industry minimum. |
| Ppk = 1.00 | Marginal! | **Long-term centering is critical — process drifts by 40%!** |
| k = 0.40 | Significant | Re-center the machine mean to T = 20.00 mm. |

> [!NOTE]
> The mismatch between Cpk (1.86) and Ppk (1.00) is a strong indicator that special causes of variation exist between subgroups, OR the overall variation is inflated by long-term drift (tool wear, temperature). This is a frequent **KTU 14-mark question format**.

---

## 3.4 Defect Rate (ppm) from Cpk — Useful Conversion

For a centered process (μ = T), the defect rate is given by:

$$
\text{ppm} = 2 \times \left[1 - \Phi(3C_p)\right] \times 10^6
$$

where $\Phi$ is the Cumulative Standard Normal Distribution function.

| Cpk | ppm Defective (2-sided, centered) |
|---|---|
| 1.00 | 2,700 |
| 1.33 | 63 |
| 1.50 | 7 |
| 1.67 | 0.57 |
| 2.00 | 0.002 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map — Indices Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Data Collection] --> B{Process Stable?}
    B -- NO --> C[STOP - Remove Special Causes using Control Charts]
    B -- YES --> D{Normality Assumed}
    D -- YES --> E[Compute sigma within using R-bar / d2]
    D -- NO --> F[Box-Cox Transform OR Use Non-Parametric]
    E --> G[Compute Cp and Cpk]
    F --> G
    E --> H[Compute Overall s]
    H --> I[Compute Pp and Ppk]
    G --> J{Compare}
    I --> J
    J --> K[Cp equals Cpk]
    J --> L[Cp greater than Cpk]
    K --> M[Process is Centered]
    L --> N[Process is Off-Center]
    M --> O[Check Ppk versus Cpk for Stability]
    N --> P[Re-center Process to Target T]
    O --> Q[Decision Accept / Improve]
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Process Capability Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Quality Data X1, X2, Xn] --> B[Subgroup Sampling n equals 4 or 5]
    B --> C[X-bar and R Control Chart]
    C --> D{In Control?}
    D -- NO --> E[Investigate Assignable Cause]
    D -- YES --> F[Capability Computation Block]
    F --> G[Within sigma equals R-bar / d2]
    F --> H[Overall s equals SD of All Data]
    G --> I[Cp equals USL minus LSL over 6 sigma]
    G --> J[Cpk equals min CPU, CPL]
    H --> K[Pp equals USL minus LSL over 6s]
    H --> L[Ppk equals min upper, lower with s]
    I --> M[Output Capability Report]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Cp vs Cpk Visual Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph C1 [Cp equals 1.33 - Centered Process]
        P1[Process Spread 6 sigma] -.->|Fits with margin| S1[USL]
        M1[Mean at Center] --> P1
        P1 --> S2[LSL]
    end
    subgraph C2 [Cpk equals 0.67 - Off-Center Process]
        P2[Process Spread 6 sigma] -.->|Breaches LSL| S3[USL]
        M2[Mean shifted] --> P2
        P2 --> S4[LSL]
    end
    subgraph C3 [Cp equals 2.0 - Highly Capable]
        P3[Process Spread 6 sigma] -.->|Lots of margin| S5[USL]
        M3[Mean at Center] --> P3
        P3 --> S6[LSL]
    end
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Index

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need a Capability Index] --> Q1{One-Sided or Two-Sided Spec}
    Q1 -- One Sided --> Q2{Which Side is Critical}
    Q2 -- Smaller Better --> One1[Use CPU only]
    Q2 -- Larger Better --> One2[Use CPL only]
    Q1 -- Two Sided --> Q3{Process Centered to Target T}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Two1[Use Cp equals Cpk]
    Q3 -- No --> Two2[Use Cpk accounts for shift]
    Q3 -- Penalty for Deviation --> Two3[Use Cpm Taguchi Index]
    Q3 -- Long Term Performance --> Two4[Use Pp and Ppk]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Process Capability (Cp) and Process Capability Index (Cpk). Why is Cpk always less than or equal to Cp?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cp | Cpk |
|---|---|---|
| Accounts for | Process spread (precision) | Process spread + mean shift (accuracy) |
| Formula | $(USL - LSL) / 6\sigma$ | $\min[(USL-\mu)/3\sigma,\ (\mu-LSL)/3\sigma]$ |
| Centering Assumption | Assumes μ = T | No assumption |
| Value when centered | $Cpk = Cp$ | $Cpk = Cp$ |

**Why Cpk ≤ Cp:** Since $Cpk = \min(CPU, CPL)$ and the arithmetic mean of CPU and CPL equals Cp, the minimum can never exceed the average. So **Cpk ≤ Cp always**. Equality holds when CPU = CPL, i.e., when the process is exactly centered.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**A process has USL = 25 mm, LSL = 20 mm, μ = 22.5 mm, and σ = 0.4 mm. Calculate Cpk and comment on process capability.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{25 - 22.5}{3 \times 0.4},\ \frac{22.5 - 20}{3 \times 0.4}\right]
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{2.5}{1.2},\ \frac{2.5}{1.2}\right] = 2.08
$$

**Comment:** Since Cpk = 2.08 > 1.67, the process is **excellent / world-class** (exceeds Six Sigma level). The process is also perfectly centered (μ = T = 22.5).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Descriptive Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Process Capability. With a neat sketch, explain the concept of Cp and Cpk. How does off-centering affect Cpk?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bearing race has a diameter specification of **50.000 ± 0.050 mm**. A sample of 30 subgroups of size 5 yields:
- Overall mean $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.015$ mm
- Average range $\bar{R} = 0.020$ mm
- Standard deviation of all 150 values, $s = 0.022$ mm

Calculate **Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk** and state whether the process is acceptable for a critical automotive characteristic (Cpk ≥ 1.33 required).

---

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Definition & Concept [7 Marks]

**Definition:** Process Capability is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a stable process to produce output within specified tolerance limits, defined as the ratio of the **tolerance width (USL – LSL)** to the **process spread (6σ)**.

**Sketch Description (to be drawn in exam):**

A horizontal number line showing:
- LSL on the left, USL on the right.
- Inside, a Normal (bell) curve centered between LSL and USL representing the process distribution.
- The 6σ spread extending from μ – 3σ to μ + 3σ.
- Two arrows: a long arrow for "Tolerance Width" and a shorter arrow for "Process Width."

**Cp** measures the **ratio of tolerance width to process width** — it tells us if the process *can* fit within the specification assuming perfect centering.

**Cpk** measures **how well the process actually fits**, accounting for off-centering. It is the minimum of the upper and lower one-sided capabilities.

**Effect of Off-centering:**
- $Cpk = Cp \times (1 - k)$, where $k$ is the off-centering factor.
- As μ drifts from target T, **Cpk decreases linearly**.
- At maximum off-centering (μ on a specification limit), **Cpk = 0**.
- A high Cp with low Cpk is a precision-without-accuracy problem — fixable only by **re-centering**, not by reducing variation.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Within-subgroup σ (use $\bar{R}$ with $d_2 = 2.326$ for n = 5)**

$$
\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.020}{2.326} = 0.0086\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Cp Calculation**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{50.050 - 49.950}{6 \times 0.0086} = \frac{0.100}{0.0516} = 1.94
$$

**Step 3: Cpk Calculation**

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{50.050 - 50.015}{3 \times 0.0086},\ \frac{50.015 - 49.950}{3 \times 0.0086}\right]
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{0.035}{0.0258},\ \frac{0.065}{0.0258}\right] = \min[1.36,\ 2.52] = 1.36
$$

**Step 4: Pp Calculation**

$$
P_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6s} = \frac{0.100}{6 \times 0.022} = \frac{0.100}{0.132} = 0.76
$$

**Step 5: Ppk Calculation**

$$
P_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{0.035}{3 \times 0.022},\ \frac{0.065}{3 \times 0.022}\right] = \min[0.53,\ 0.98] = 0.53
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**

| Metric | Value | Inference |
|---|---|---|
| Cp = 1.94 | Excellent | Short-term precision is world-class. |
| Cpk = 1.36 | Just Acceptable | Meets automotive critical spec of 1.33 — borderline. |
| Pp = 0.76 | Inadequate | Long-term performance is poor — process is not stable. |
| Ppk = 0.53 | Inadequate | Long-term performance is below industry minimum. |

**Conclusion:** The **short-term capability (Cpk = 1.36) is acceptable**, but the **large gap between Cpk and Ppk** signals significant **long-term drift / special causes** (likely tool wear, temperature, material lot variation). The process is **NOT acceptable for a critical characteristic** until the long-term stability is restored.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Six Sigma capability**. How is process capability related to Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)? Show with a suitable numerical illustration how a 4σ process performs versus a 6σ process.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pharmaceutical company fills vials with a target volume of **10.00 mL** (LSL = 9.80, USL = 10.20). The process mean is 10.05 mL and σ = 0.04 mL. Compute:
- (i) Cp and Cpk [3 Marks]
- (ii) The off-centering factor k [2 Marks]
- (iii) Expected ppm defective and DPMO [2 Marks]

---

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Six Sigma & DPMO [7 Marks]

**Six Sigma Concept:** A Six Sigma process is one in which the specification limits are **6 standard deviations away from the process mean** (i.e., Cpk = 2.0). This provides a maximum of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** when accounting for a 1.5σ long-term mean shift.

**Relationship between Cpk and DPMO:**

$$
\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times P(\text{outside spec})
$$

For a centered process:

$$
P(\text{outside spec}) = 2 \times \left[1 - \Phi(3 C_p)\right]
$$

| Process | Cpk | DPMO (centered) | Real-World Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3σ | 0.5 | 66,807 | Fail most audits |
| 4σ | 1.0 | 6,210 | Average industry |
| 5σ | 1.5 | 7 | Best-in-class |
| 6σ | 2.0 | 0.002 | World-class (Motorola, GE) |

**Cost-of-Quality Insight:** Moving from 4σ to 6σ reduces defects by a factor of ~3 million, dramatically reducing warranty, rework, and inspection costs.

---

#### Part (b) — Pharma Vial Problem [7 Marks]

**Given:** Target T = 10.00, USL = 10.20, LSL = 9.80, μ = 10.05, σ = 0.04 mL.

**(i) Cp and Cpk [3 Marks]**

$$
C_p = \frac{10.20 - 9.80}{6 \times 0.04} = \frac{0.40}{0.24} = 1.667
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{10.20 - 10.05}{3 \times 0.04},\ \frac{10.05 - 9.80}{3 \times 0.04}\right] = \min\left[\frac{0.15}{0.12},\ \frac{0.25}{0.12}\right] = \min[1.25,\ 2.08] = 1.25
$$

**(ii) Off-centering Factor k [2 Marks]**

$$
k = \frac{\vert T - \mu \vert}{0.5 \times (USL - LSL)} = \frac{\vert 10.00 - 10.05 \vert}{0.5 \times 0.40} = \frac{0.05}{0.20} = 0.25
$$

The process is **25% off-center** towards the USL.

**(iii) Expected ppm Defective and DPMO [2 Marks]**

The relevant distance is the smaller of:
- To USL: $(10.20 - 10.05) / 0.04 = 3.75\sigma$
- To LSL: $(10.05 - 9.80) / 0.04 = 6.25\sigma$

For a one-tailed normal at z = 3.75: tail probability ≈ 0.0000884 (using standard normal tables)
For z = 6.25: tail probability ≈ 0 (negligible)

$$
P(\text{outside}) \approx 0.0000884 \implies \text{ppm} \approx 88.4
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} \approx 88.4\ \text{defects per million}
$$

**Conclusion:** The process is capable for pharmaceutical use (Cpk = 1.25 marginally acceptable; FDA typically requires Cpk ≥ 1.33 for validated processes), but re-centering to T = 10.00 would push Cpk up to 1.667.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Based on Actual KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $d_2$ constant:** Students often compute σ as the simple SD of the subgroup ranges, forgetting that $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$. This is a **2-mark killer** in numerical questions.
> 
> 2. **Mixing σ and s:** Using σ in Pp/Ppk formulas or s in Cp/Cpk formulas. **Remember:** Cp/Cpk use **σ_within**, Pp/Ppk use **s_overall**.
> 
> 3. **Not verifying stability first:** Computing Cpk without checking the control chart is a methodological error worth **1–2 marks** in the definition/concept sub-parts.
> 
> 4. **Off-by-one in one-sided specs:** For "smaller is better," only **CPU** applies — do not compute Cpk.
> 
> 5. **Misinterpreting Cpk = 0 vs Cpk < 0:** Cpk = 0 means μ is *on* a specification limit. Cpk < 0 means μ is *outside* the specifications (impossible in a stable process). Many students write Cpk = 0 when mean is outside.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process Capability is a ratio of tolerance to process variation** — Cp ignores centering, Cpk includes it.
- **Cpk ≤ Cp always** — equality holds for a perfectly centered process.
- **Cp/Cpk use $\sigma_{within} = \bar{R}/d_2$** (short-term); **Pp/Ppk use $s_{overall}$** (long-term).
- **If Ppk << Cpk** → the process has long-term drift / special causes → build stability first.
- **Industry minimums:** Cpk ≥ 1.00 (general), 1.33 (automotive), 1.67 (safety-critical), 2.00 (Six Sigma).
- **Cpm** is preferred when **target ≠ midpoint** of specification — penalizes off-target deviation.
- **One-sided specs:** Use CPU (smaller-is-better) or CPL (larger-is-better) — do not compute Cp or Cpk.
- **Pre-requisites:** Statistical control + normality + independence + sufficient sample size (≥ 100–125 data points or 25 subgroups).
- **DPMO and Cpk** are inversely related — a Cpk increase of 0.33 typically reduces DPMO by 1–2 orders of magnitude.
- **Tool wear, temperature, and material variation** are the most common real-world causes of Pp < Cp.
- **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function** philosophy is built into Cpm — deviation from target always incurs loss, not just breach of specification.
- **Six Sigma's 3.4 DPMO** assumes a **1.5σ long-term mean shift** — this is why industry uses Z_shift = 1.5 in conversions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceptance Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Acceptance Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Sampling** is a statistical quality control procedure in which a random sample is drawn from a production lot, and based on the inspection results of that sample, a decision is made to either **accept** the entire lot or **reject** (return) it. It is a *lot-by-lot* decision-making tool, NOT a process control tool.

In KTU parlance, acceptance sampling is the **middle path** between two extremes:
- **100% Inspection** (costly, fatiguing, often impossible for destructive testing).
- **No Inspection** (blind acceptance, zero quality assurance).

It uses **probability theory** to balance the risks of the Producer (seller) and the Consumer (buyer).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Mango Vendor at the Train Station**
>
> Imagine you stop a street vendor selling mangoes from a large basket. You cannot taste-test every mango (destructive testing in a sense, plus time-consuming). You randomly pick 5 mangoes. If 4 out of 5 are sweet and ripe, you accept the whole basket. If 2 or more are rotten, you reject it.
>
> The vendor *knows* you may reject — so he ensures most mangoes are good. The risks are:
> - **Producer's Risk (α):** You rejected a basket that actually had only 1% bad mangoes (Type I error — false alarm).
> - **Consumer's Risk (β):** You accepted a basket with 8% bad mangoes because your 5-mango sample happened to miss them (Type II error — missed detection).

This is exactly what happens in industrial lot acceptance — except the sample size **n** and acceptance number **c** are scientifically derived from tables like **MIL-STD-105E** (now **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4**) and **Dodge-Romig**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Symbol | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Lot / Batch** | N | A group of items produced under similar conditions, offered for inspection together. |
| **Sample Size** | n | The number of items randomly drawn from the lot. |
| **Acceptance Number** | c | Maximum number of defectives allowed in the sample to accept the lot. |
| **Reject Number** | Re | Number of defectives that triggers rejection (typically c+1). |
| **Acceptable Quality Level** | AQL | The **worst tolerable** process quality — usually expressed as **% defective** or **defects per hundred units (DPMU)**. |
| **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective** | LTPD | The poorest quality the consumer is willing to accept — used with β (Consumer's Risk). |
| **Producer's Risk** | α | Probability of rejecting a *good* lot (quality = AQL). |
| **Consumer's Risk** | β | Probability of accepting a *bad* lot (quality = LTPD). |
| **Operating Characteristic Curve** | OC Curve | Graph of Pa (Probability of Acceptance) vs. incoming lot quality p. |
| **Average Outgoing Quality** | AOQ | Expected quality of product after inspection + rectification of rejected lots. |
| **Average Outgoing Quality Limit** | AOQL | The **maximum** value of AOQ over all possible incoming qualities — a single number summarizing the plan's discrimination. |

### 1.4 Why Use Acceptance Sampling? (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Reasons in **bullet form** for a 3-mark answer:
> 1. Inspection is **destructive** (bulbs, fuses, explosives, tensile specimens).
> 2. **100% inspection is uneconomical** or impractical for very large lots.
> 3. **Fatigue / monotony** makes 100% inspection unreliable for subjective defects.
> 4. The cost of **sampling is lower** than the cost of 100% inspection.
> 5. It provides a **psychological incentive** to the producer to maintain quality.
> 6. Lot-to-lot **disposition decision** is required (reject lot, accept lot, or screen).

### 1.5 Limitations of Acceptance Sampling

- Involves **sampling risk** — some good lots rejected, some bad lots accepted.
- Provides **less information** about the process than a control chart.
- Requires **planning and documentation** of the sampling plan.
- Does not improve quality — it only **disposes** of the lot. Quality must be built in.

### 1.6 Types of Acceptance Sampling Plans (Classification Tree)

> [!NOTE]
> **Major classification for the KTU exam:**

1. **By Quality Characteristics Inspected**
   - **Attribute Sampling Plan** — counts defective items (pass/fail). Examples: MIL-STD-105E, Dodge-Romig CSP-1, CSP-2.
   - **Variable Sampling Plan** — measures a continuous characteristic (length, weight, tensile strength). Uses x̄ and σ. Examples: MIL-STD-414.

2. **By Number of Samples Drawn**
   - **Single Sampling Plan** — one sample of size *n*; accept if d ≤ c.
   - **Double Sampling Plan** — two samples (n₁, n₂); decisions on first sample or combined.
   - **Multiple Sampling Plan** — sequence of samples (rare in industry, common in exams).
   - **Sequential Sampling Plan** — item-by-item, take sample until decision is reached.

3. **By Use of Rectification**
   - **Rectifying Plan** — all rejected lots are 100% screened, defectives replaced. Used to compute AOQ and AOQL.
   - **Non-Rectifying Plan** — rejected lots are returned or scrapped without screening.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — The Heart of Sampling

The **OC curve** is a plot of **P_a (Probability of Acceptance)** on the Y-axis versus the **incoming lot quality p (fraction defective)** on the X-axis. It is the single most important concept in acceptance sampling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A *good* sampling plan has a steeply descending OC curve. A *poor* plan has a flat curve (it accepts bad lots and rejects good lots with nearly equal probability).

**The mathematics of OC curve for a Single Sampling Plan:**

For a lot of size N with fraction defective p, sampling n items randomly and accepting the lot if the number of defectives d ≤ c:

The number of defectives in the sample follows a **Hypergeometric Distribution** (when n/N > 0.1) or is **approximated by Binomial / Poisson** for large lots.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the table below. Most numerical questions in KTU reduce to plugging into one of these formulas.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Binomial OC | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | n ≥ 30, p < 0.1, finite lot OK |
| 2 | Poisson OC | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | n large, p very small, np = constant |
| 3 | Hypergeometric OC | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{Np}{d}\binom{N-Np}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | n/N > 0.1, small N |
| 4 | Producer's Point | $P_a = 1 - \alpha$ at $p = \text{AQL}$ | p₁ point on OC |
| 5 | Consumer's Point | $P_a = \beta$ at $p = \text{LTPD}$ | p₂ point on OC |
| 6 | Average Sample Number (ASN) — Double | $ASN = n_1 + (1-P_{a1}) \cdot n_2$ | Double sampling |
| 7 | Average Sample Number — Multiple | $ASN = \sum_{i} n_i \cdot P(\text{reaching stage } i)$ | Multiple sampling |
| 8 | Average Outgoing Quality | $AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Rectifying, attribute |
| 9 | AOQ (approx, n ≪ N) | $AOQ \approx P_a \cdot p$ | When n/N is small |
| 10 | Average Outgoing Quality Limit | $AOQL = \max_p \{AOQ\}$ | Found by calculus / numerical |
| 11 | Average Total Inspection | $ATI = n + (1-P_a)(N-n)$ | Rectifying, includes screening cost |
| 12 | Producer's Risk from AQL | $1 - P_a(p=AQL) = \alpha$ | Standard form |
| 13 | Consumer's Risk from LTPD | $P_a(p=LTPD) = \beta$ | Standard form |

### 2.3 The Binomial vs. Poisson Approximation — Critical Decision Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When np is small AND p < 0.1, use **Poisson**. Otherwise use **Binomial**. Never mix the two in the same calculation.

**Why Poisson works:** The Poisson distribution has the property that if n → ∞ and p → 0 such that np = λ (constant), then Binomial(n, p) → Poisson(λ). For acceptance sampling, when fraction defective is small (e.g., < 0.05), this approximation gives accurate results without computing huge binomial coefficients.

### 2.4 Design of a Single Sampling Plan Using AQL and LTPD

This is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**. The procedure:

1. **Given:** p₁ = AQL, p₂ = LTPD, α, β (typically α = 0.05, β = 0.10).
2. **Convert to Poisson parameters:** np₁ and np₂.
3. **Look up in standard Poisson tables** to find (n, c) such that:
   - $P_a(np_1) \geq 1 - \alpha$ (i.e., accept with high probability at AQL)
   - $P_a(np_2) \leq \beta$ (i.e., accept with low probability at LTPD)
4. **Compute** the Operating Ratio: $OR = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = \dfrac{\text{LTPD}}{\text{AQL}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Ratio (OR)** indicates the **discrimination** of the plan. A small OR (< 4) is a sharp plan; a large OR (> 10) is a lenient plan.

### 2.5 Dodge-Romig Sampling Plans (Single Sampling Lot-by-Lot)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dodge-Romig Tables** (1944) are designed to **minimize average total inspection (ATI)** for given AQL and LTPD. There are two types:
> - **CSP-1 (Consumer's Risk Plan):** LTPD and β (β = 0.10) are specified; AQL is variable.
> - **CSP-2 (Average Quality Protection / AOQL Plan):** AOQL is specified; the plan minimizes ATI.

**Inputs to Dodge-Romig Tables:**
- Lot size N
- Process average p̄ (estimated historical % defective)
- LTPD or AOQL (depending on type)

**Outputs from the Tables:** Single sample size *n* and acceptance number *c*.

### 2.6 MIL-STD-105E (Now ANSI/ASQ Z1.4) — Attribute Sampling Standard

**Key features:**
- Designed for **AQL-based** lot-by-lot inspection.
- Uses **three levels of inspection severity:** Normal, Reduced, Tightened.
- Switches between severities based on lot history (switching rules).
- **Inspection Levels:** I, II, III (II is default), plus four special levels S-1 to S-4 for small samples.
- AQL ranges from 0.065% to 6.5% (with special "10 or better" codes).

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU exam usually does NOT require table-lookup from MIL-STD-105E. The expected answer is the **theory** and **switching rules**.

**Switching Rules Summary:**
- **Normal → Tightened:** 2 out of 5 consecutive lots rejected.
- **Tightened → Normal:** 5 consecutive lots accepted.
- **Normal → Reduced:** 10 consecutive lots accepted (and supplier's process stable).
- **Reduced → Normal:** 1 lot rejected.

### 2.7 Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) and AOQL — Derivation

**Setup:** A rectifying inspection plan. All rejected lots are 100% screened and defectives replaced.

For a single sampling plan with sample size n and acceptance number c:
- If the lot is **accepted** (probability = P_a), the outgoing quality equals p (the fraction defective in incoming lot, since we don't screen).
- If the lot is **rejected** (probability = 1 - P_a), 100% inspection removes all defectives → outgoing quality = 0.
- The effective number of "good items out of the lot" considering sampling:

$$ AOQ = \frac{\text{Expected number of defectives shipped}}{\text{Lot size}} = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N} $$

The numerator is: P_a × (defectives in unsampled portion) = P_a × p × (N - n).

Dividing by N gives fraction defective in outgoing material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **AOQL** is found by setting $\dfrac{d(AOQ)}{dp} = 0$ and solving for p*. Then $AOQL = P_a(p^*) \cdot p^* \cdot (1 - n/N)$.

### 2.8 Variable Sampling Plans — The x̄ Method

> [!NOTE]
> Used when the quality characteristic is **measurable** and the lot is judged based on the **mean** of the sample.

For a one-sided specification "less than" U (upper limit), the plan:
- Sample size n, sample mean x̄, known process standard deviation σ.
- Accept if $\bar{x} \leq U - k\sigma$, where k is the **acceptability constant** (tabulated).

**Required formulas:**
- n is determined from $\dfrac{U - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} = k_{\text{table}}$
- Where μ₀ is the AQL-based process mean.

**KTU relevance:** Theory-level question; full numerical problems are rare.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Binomial OC Formula from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question context:** A lot of N = 1000 items has fraction defective p = 0.05. A single sample of n = 50 is drawn, acceptance number c = 2. Find the probability of acceptance.

**Step 1: Identify the distribution.**
Since the lot is large (N = 1000) and the sample is small relative to the lot (n/N = 0.05 < 0.1), we use the **Binomial distribution** with parameters n and p.

**Step 2: Write down the binomial PMF.**

$$ P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}, \quad d = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n $$

**Step 3: State the acceptance condition.**
The lot is accepted if and only if the number of defectives in the sample d ≤ c. So:

$$ P_a = P(d \leq c) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d} $$

**Step 4: Plug in numerical values** (n = 50, c = 2, p = 0.05).

$$ P_a = \binom{50}{0}(0.05)^0(0.95)^{50} + \binom{50}{1}(0.05)^1(0.95)^{49} + \binom{50}{2}(0.05)^2(0.95)^{48} $$

**Step 5: Compute each term.**

$$ T_0 = (1)(1)(0.95)^{50} = 0.95^{50} $$

Take the natural log: $\ln(0.95^{50}) = 50 \cdot \ln(0.95) = 50 \cdot (-0.05129) = -2.5646$.

$$ T_0 = e^{-2.5646} = 0.0769 $$

$$ T_1 = 50 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (0.95)^{49} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{0.0769}{0.95} = 2.5 \cdot 0.0809 = 0.2024 $$

$$ T_2 = \binom{50}{2} \cdot (0.05)^2 \cdot (0.95)^{48} = 1225 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot \frac{0.0769}{0.9025} $$

$$ T_2 = 1225 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot 0.0852 = 0.2609 $$

**Step 6: Sum the three terms.**

$$ P_a = T_0 + T_1 + T_2 = 0.0769 + 0.2024 + 0.2609 = 0.5402 $$

**Step 7: Interpret the result.**
There is a **54.02% probability** that the lot will be accepted. This means there is a **45.98% risk of rejection** for a lot that is actually 5% defective. Such a plan is **inadequate** for protecting a buyer who requires LTPD ≤ 5%.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Poisson OC Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question context:** A bolt manufacturer uses a sampling plan with n = 75, c = 2. A lot has fraction defective p = 0.02. Find the probability of acceptance using the Poisson approximation.

**Step 1: Check the applicability of Poisson approximation.**

$$ np = 75 \times 0.02 = 1.5 $$

Since p = 0.02 < 0.1 and n = 75 is large, the Poisson approximation is valid. The Poisson parameter:

$$ \lambda = np = 1.5 $$

**Step 2: Write the Poisson PMF.**

$$ P(d) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^d}{d!} $$

**Step 3: Compute the acceptance probability.**

$$ P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c=2} \frac{e^{-1.5} (1.5)^d}{d!} $$

**Step 4: Evaluate each term.**

$$ e^{-1.5} = 0.22313 $$

$$ T_0 = \frac{0.22313 \cdot (1.5)^0}{0!} = 0.22313 \cdot 1 = 0.22313 $$

$$ T_1 = \frac{0.22313 \cdot 1.5}{1} = 0.33470 $$

$$ T_2 = \frac{0.22313 \cdot 2.25}{2} = \frac{0.50204}{2} = 0.25102 $$

**Step 5: Sum the three terms.**

$$ P_a = 0.22313 + 0.33470 + 0.25102 = 0.80885 $$

**Step 6: Interpretation.**
With this plan, the probability of accepting a 2% defective lot is **80.9%**. The producer's risk is 1 - 0.8089 = 0.1911, or about **19%** for this particular p.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) for a Single Rectifying Plan

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question context:** A rectifying single sampling plan has N = 2000, n = 50, c = 1. If a lot is rejected, it is 100% inspected and defectives are replaced. Find the AOQ for an incoming lot with p = 0.04.

**Step 1: Compute P_a using Binomial.**

$$ P_a = P(d \leq 1) = (0.96)^{50} + 50 \cdot 0.04 \cdot (0.96)^{49} $$

**Step 2: Evaluate.**

$$ \ln(0.96^{50}) = 50 \cdot \ln(0.96) = 50 \cdot (-0.04082) = -2.0410 $$
$$ 0.96^{50} = e^{-2.0410} = 0.1299 $$

$$ T_1 = 50 \cdot 0.04 \cdot (0.96)^{49} = 2.0 \cdot \frac{0.1299}{0.96} = 2.0 \cdot 0.1353 = 0.2706 $$

$$ P_a = 0.1299 + 0.2706 = 0.4005 $$

**Step 3: Apply the AOQ formula.**

$$ AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} $$

**Step 4: Substitute values.**

$$ AOQ = \frac{0.4005 \cdot 0.04 \cdot (2000 - 50)}{2000} = \frac{0.4005 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 1950}{2000} $$

**Step 5: Simplify step by step.**

$$ 0.4005 \cdot 0.04 = 0.01602 $$

$$ 0.01602 \cdot 1950 = 31.239 $$

$$ AOQ = \frac{31.239}{2000} = 0.01562 $$

**Step 6: Express in % defective.**

$$ AOQ = 0.01562 = 1.562\% $$

**Step 7: Interpretation.**
After inspection, the **expected** fraction defective shipped to the customer is **1.56%** — much lower than the incoming 4% because rejected lots are 100% screened.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Finding the AOQL Analytically

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question context:** Use the previous plan (N = 2000, n = 50, c = 1) and find the AOQL by maximizing AOQ with respect to p.

**Step 1: Write the AOQ function explicitly** (using Binomial for c = 1):

$$ AOQ(p) = \frac{p(N-n)}{N} \left[ (1-p)^n + n p (1-p)^{n-1} \right] $$

**Step 2: Simplify the bracket.**

$$ (1-p)^n + np(1-p)^{n-1} = (1-p)^{n-1} [ (1-p) + np ] = (1-p)^{n-1} [1 - p + np] = (1-p)^{n-1} [1 + (n-1)p] $$

So:

$$ AOQ(p) = \frac{p(N-n)}{N} (1-p)^{n-1} [1 + (n-1)p] $$

**Step 3: Take the derivative and set to zero.**

$$ \frac{d(AOQ)}{dp} = 0 $$

For the typical case where n ≪ N, an approximate form is used:

$$ AOQ(p) \approx p \cdot P_a(p) = p \cdot (1-p)^n + n p^2 (1-p)^{n-1} \text{ (for } c=1) $$

**Step 4: Use numerical methods to find p\* (max AOQ point).**

For n = 50, c = 1, the maximum AOQ occurs near p\* = 0.0297.

**Step 5: Compute AOQ at p\*.**

$$ P_a(0.0297) = (0.9703)^{50} + 50 \cdot 0.0297 \cdot (0.9703)^{49} $$

$$ (0.9703)^{50}: \ln = 50 \cdot \ln(0.9703) = 50 \cdot (-0.03019) = -1.5096 \Rightarrow e^{-1.5096} = 0.2209 $$

$$ T_1 = 50 \cdot 0.0297 \cdot \frac{0.2209}{0.9703} = 1.485 \cdot 0.2277 = 0.3381 $$

$$ P_a = 0.2209 + 0.3381 = 0.5590 $$

$$ AOQL = 0.5590 \cdot 0.0297 \cdot \frac{1950}{2000} = 0.5590 \cdot 0.0297 \cdot 0.975 $$

$$ AOQL = 0.01619 \approx 1.62\% $$

> [!NOTE]
> **The AOQL (1.62%) is the maximum outgoing quality for this plan, regardless of the incoming p.** This single number characterizes the plan's long-term performance.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Average Sample Number (ASN) for a Double Sampling Plan

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question context:** A double sampling plan has n₁ = 50, c₁ = 1, n₂ = 50, c₂ = 4. Find the ASN.

**Step 1: Recall the ASN formula.**

$$ ASN = n_1 + n_2 (1 - P_{a1}) $$

where $P_{a1}$ is the probability of acceptance on the **first** sample.

**Step 2: Compute P_{a1} for p = 0.02 (example).**

$$ P_{a1} = (0.98)^{50} + 50 \cdot 0.02 \cdot (0.98)^{49} $$

$$ \ln(0.98^{50}) = 50 \cdot \ln(0.98) = 50 \cdot (-0.02020) = -1.0101 \Rightarrow e^{-1.0101} = 0.3642 $$

$$ T_1 = 50 \cdot 0.02 \cdot \frac{0.3642}{0.98} = 1.0 \cdot 0.3716 = 0.3716 $$

$$ P_{a1} = 0.3642 + 0.3716 = 0.7358 $$

**Step 3: Compute ASN.**

$$ ASN = 50 + 50 \cdot (1 - 0.7358) = 50 + 50 \cdot 0.2642 = 50 + 13.21 = 63.21 $$

**Step 4: Interpret.**
On average, this double plan inspects **63.21 items per lot**, which is **less than the 75 or 100** items that an equivalent single plan would require. This is the chief **advantage of double sampling**.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Sampling Plans

| Plan Type | Sample Sizes | Total Inspection | Information | Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single** | One (n) | Highest (always n) | Least info per item | Simple | High-volume, low-cost inspection |
| **Double** | Two (n₁, n₂) | Lower (often 60–80% of single) | More info per item | Moderate | Moderate-volume, need for cost saving |
| **Multiple** | Many (4–7 stages) | Lowest | Most info per item | Complex paperwork | Costly inspection, destructive tests |
| **Sequential** | Item-by-item | Lowest in expectation | Maximum info per item | Time-consuming | Very expensive items (missile parts) |
| **Rectifying** | Any of above + 100% on reject | Lot-dependent | Includes 100% on rejects | Highest absolute | Buyer-seller contracts with rework |

---

### 3.7 KTU-Standard Table of Poisson Cumulative Probabilities (Memorize Critical Values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this cheat table for the KTU exam — values are widely reproduced in standard texts.**

| c \\ np | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 4.0 | 5.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.607 | 0.368 | 0.223 | 0.135 | 0.082 | 0.050 | 0.018 | 0.007 |
| 1 | 0.910 | 0.736 | 0.558 | 0.406 | 0.287 | 0.199 | 0.092 | 0.040 |
| 2 | 0.986 | 0.920 | 0.809 | 0.677 | 0.544 | 0.423 | 0.238 | 0.125 |
| 3 | 0.998 | 0.981 | 0.934 | 0.857 | 0.758 | 0.647 | 0.433 | 0.265 |
| 4 | 1.000 | 0.996 | 0.981 | 0.947 | 0.891 | 0.815 | 0.629 | 0.440 |

| 6 | 1.000 | 1.000 | 0.999 | 0.995 | 0.986 | 0.966 | 0.889 | 0.762 |

> **Reading the table:** Row = c, Column = np, Value = P(d ≤ c).

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Acceptance Sampling Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lotA[Incoming Lot of N items] --> s1[Randomly Select Sample of size n]
    s1 --> insp[Inspect Sample Items]
    insp --> d{Count Defectives d}
    d -- d less than or equal to c --> acceptA[LOT ACCEPTED]
    d -- d greater than c --> rejectA[LOT REJECTED]
    acceptA --> shipA[Ship to Customer]
    rejectA --> rect{Rectifying Plan?}
    rect -- YES --> screen[100% Screen and Replace Defectives]
    rect -- NO --> ret[Return Lot to Producer or Scrap]
    screen --> shipA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: OC Curve Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AQLpt[Point p1 AQL] -->|Pa equals 1 minus alpha| OC1[High Pa region]
    OC1 --> BendOC[OC Curve Bend Region]
    BendOC -->|Steep Descent| OC2[Low Pa region]
    OC2 --> LTPDpt[Point p2 LTPD with Pa equals beta]
    LTPDpt --> PoorP[Pa approaches 0 as p approaches 1]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sampling Plan Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ASP[Acceptance Sampling Plan] --> ATT[Attribute]
    ASP --> VAR[Variable]
    ATT --> SGL[Single]
    ATT --> DBL[Double]
    ATT --> MUL[Multiple]
    ATT --> SEQ[Sequential]
    SGL --> MIL[MIL-STD-105E]
    SGL --> DODGE[Dodge Romig]
    VAR --> XBAR[x-bar Method]
    VAR --> SIGMA[Known Sigma Method]
    DODGE --> CSP1[CSP-1 LTPD Plan]
    DODGE --> CSP2[CSP-2 AOQL Plan]
    MIL --> NORM[Normal Inspection]
    MIL --> TIGHT[Tightened Inspection]
    MIL --> RED[Reduced Inspection]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart: Switching Rules in MIL-STD-105E

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start at Normal Inspection]) --> check1{2 of last 5 lots rejected?}
    check1 -- YES --> tight[Switch to TIGHTENED]
    check1 -- NO --> stayN[Continue NORMAL]
    stayN --> check2{10 consecutive lots accepted?}
    check2 -- YES --> red[Switch to REDUCED]
    check2 -- NO --> stayN
    tight --> check3{5 consecutive lots accepted?}
    check3 -- YES --> backN[Back to NORMAL]
    check3 -- NO --> stayT[Stay TIGHTENED]
    red --> check4{1 lot rejected?}
    check4 -- YES --> backN2[Back to NORMAL]
    check4 -- NO --> stayR[Stay REDUCED]
    backN --> start
    backN2 --> start
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Concept Map: Producer Risk vs Consumer Risk

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    producer[PRODUCER SELLER] -->|Wants to avoid| alpha[Producer Risk alpha]
    alpha --> alphaDef[Rejecting GOOD lot at AQL]
    consumer[CONSUMER BUYER] -->|Wants to avoid| beta[Consumer Risk beta]
    beta --> betaDef[Accepting BAD lot at LTPD]
    alpha --> OC1[OC Curve at p1]
    beta --> OC2[OC Curve at p2]
    OC1 --> trade[TRADE OFF]
    OC2 --> trade
    trade --> design[Choose n and c]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define acceptance sampling. State any two situations where acceptance sampling is preferred over 100% inspection.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Acceptance Sampling** is a statistical quality control technique in which a random sample is drawn from a production lot, and based on the inspection results, a decision is made to **accept** or **reject** the entire lot.

**Two situations where acceptance sampling is preferred:**
1. **Destructive testing:** When inspection destroys the item (e.g., testing tensile strength of steel bars, fuse testing, life testing of bulbs). 100% inspection is impossible.
2. **High cost of 100% inspection:** When lot sizes are very large (say, 100,000 items) and inspection time is expensive, sampling is more economical even though it carries some risk.
3. *(Optional third)* Fatigue and monotony in 100% inspection make human inspectors miss defects; sampling reduces this risk.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Distinguish between AQL and LTPD. Why are both required in designing a sampling plan?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AQL (Acceptable Quality Level) | LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) |
|---|---|---|
| **Also called** | Acceptable Quality Limit | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective / RQL |
| **Risk Associated** | Producer's Risk (α) | Consumer's Risk (β) |
| **Pa at this point** | High (typically 0.90 – 0.95) | Low (typically 0.05 – 0.10) |
| **Perspective** | Producer / Seller | Consumer / Buyer |
| **Standard value** | Quality considered tolerable | Quality considered intolerable |

**Why both are required:**
A sampling plan must protect **both parties**. AQL protects the producer from having good lots rejected (low α). LTPD protects the consumer from accepting bad lots (low β). Without both, the plan is biased toward one party. The OC curve must be drawn to pass through both points (p₁ = AQL, P_a = 1 - α) and (p₂ = LTPD, P_a = β).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q (a)** A single sampling plan for attributes has n = 100, c = 2. Compute the probability of acceptance for a lot with 2% defectives using (i) Binomial, (ii) Poisson distribution. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** n = 100, c = 2, p = 0.02.

**(i) Binomial Distribution:**

$$ P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \binom{100}{d} (0.02)^d (0.98)^{100-d} $$

Compute each term:

$$ T_0 = (0.98)^{100} $$

$$\ln(0.98^{100}) = 100 \cdot \ln(0.98) = 100 \cdot (-0.02020) = -2.020$$

$$ T_0 = e^{-2.020} = 0.1329 $$

$$ T_1 = 100 \cdot 0.02 \cdot (0.98)^{99} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.1329}{0.98} = 2 \cdot 0.1356 = 0.2712 $$

$$ T_2 = \binom{100}{2} \cdot (0.02)^2 \cdot (0.98)^{98} = 4950 \cdot 0.0004 \cdot \frac{0.1329}{0.9604} $$

$$ T_2 = 4950 \cdot 0.0004 \cdot 0.1384 = 0.2740 $$

$$ P_a = 0.1329 + 0.2712 + 0.2740 = \boxed{0.6781} $$

**(ii) Poisson Distribution:**
λ = np = 100 × 0.02 = 2.0

$$ P_a = e^{-2.0} \left[ 1 + 2.0 + \frac{(2.0)^2}{2} \right] = 0.1353 \cdot [1 + 2.0 + 2.0] = 0.1353 \cdot 5.0 $$

$$ P_a = \boxed{0.6765} $$

**Comparison:** Binomial = 0.6781, Poisson = 0.6765. The Poisson approximation is excellent (error < 0.25%).

---

**Q (b)** Explain the concept of Operating Characteristic (OC) curve. Sketch a typical OC curve and label AQL, LTPD, Producer's Risk (α), and Consumer's Risk (β). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** is a graphical representation of a sampling plan's ability to discriminate between good and bad lots. It plots the **probability of acceptance (P_a)** on the Y-axis against the **incoming lot quality p (% defective)** on the X-axis.

**Sketch and Labels:**
- The curve starts at **P_a = 1** (when p = 0) and decreases monotonically to **P_a = 0** (when p = 1).
- **Point 1:** (AQL, 1 - α) — high acceptance probability for acceptable quality.
- **Point 2:** (LTPD, β) — low acceptance probability for unacceptable quality.
- The **region above** the curve (between curve and P_a = 1 line) represents the probability of **rejection** (1 - P_a).
- **α** = Probability of rejection when p = AQL (a Type I error).
- **β** = Probability of acceptance when p = LTPD (a Type II error).

**Ideal OC curve:** A vertical step at p = LTPD, indicating perfect discrimination. **Actual OC curve:** Smooth S-shape; steeper curves indicate better discrimination.

**Interpretation:** The OC curve is the **"fingerprint"** of a sampling plan. Two plans with the same AQL/LTPD can have different OC shapes depending on (n, c).

---

#### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q (a)** A rectifying single sampling plan has N = 1000, n = 80, c = 1. The incoming lots have p = 0.03. Compute the **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** and the **Average Total Inspection (ATI)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute P_a using Binomial (n = 80, c = 1, p = 0.03).**

$$ P_a = (0.97)^{80} + 80 \cdot 0.03 \cdot (0.97)^{79} $$

$$ \ln(0.97^{80}) = 80 \cdot \ln(0.97) = 80 \cdot (-0.03046) = -2.4368 $$

$$ T_0 = e^{-2.4368} = 0.0874 $$

$$ T_1 = 80 \cdot 0.03 \cdot (0.97)^{79} = 2.4 \cdot \frac{0.0874}{0.97} = 2.4 \cdot 0.0901 = 0.2162 $$

$$ P_a = 0.0874 + 0.2162 = 0.3036 $$

**Step 2: Compute AOQ.**

$$ AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} = \frac{0.3036 \cdot 0.03 \cdot (1000 - 80)}{1000} $$

$$ AOQ = \frac{0.3036 \cdot 0.03 \cdot 920}{1000} = \frac{0.3036 \cdot 27.6}{1000} = \frac{8.379}{1000} $$

$$ AOQ = 0.008379 \approx \boxed{0.84\%} $$

**Step 3: Compute ATI.**

$$ ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n) = 80 + (1 - 0.3036)(920) $$

$$ ATI = 80 + 0.6964 \cdot 920 = 80 + 640.7 = \boxed{720.7 \text{ items}} $$

**Interpretation:** On average, **720.7 items per lot** are inspected, and the outgoing quality is reduced from 3% (incoming) to 0.84% (outgoing) due to rectifying inspection of rejected lots.

---

**Q (b)** Explain the **Dodge-Romig sampling plans**. Differentiate between **CSP-1** and **CSP-2** plans. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Dodge-Romig Sampling Plans** are a family of single sampling plans for attributes developed by H.F. Dodge and H.G. Romig (1944) for **rectifying inspection**. They are designed to **minimize the Average Total Inspection (ATI)** — i.e., the average number of items inspected per lot — for a given level of consumer protection or outgoing quality.

**CSP-1 (Consumer's Risk Plan):**
- **Inputs:** Lot size N, process average p̄, and **LTPD** with consumer's risk β = 0.10.
- **Design philosophy:** Protects the consumer at LTPD level.
- **Output from tables:** Sample size n and acceptance number c.
- **AQL is determined** by the plan, not specified in advance.
- **Use when:** Consumer's protection is the primary requirement.

**CSP-2 (Average Outgoing Quality Limit Plan):**
- **Inputs:** Lot size N, process average p̄, and **AOQL**.
- **Design philosophy:** Limits the maximum outgoing fraction defective to a specified value.
- **Output from tables:** Sample size n and acceptance number c.
- **LTPD is determined** by the plan, not specified in advance.
- **Use when:** Long-run outgoing quality is the primary concern (e.g., finished goods warehouse).

**Common features of both:**
- Both assume **rectifying inspection** (100% screening of rejected lots).
- Both require an estimate of the **process average p̄** (from historical data).
- Both are based on the **Poisson approximation** for small p̄.
- Both produce **single sampling plans** only (n, c).

**Dodge-Romig vs. MIL-STD-105E:**

| Feature | Dodge-Romig | MIL-STD-105E (Z1.4) |
|---|---|---|
| Inspection type | Rectifying | Non-rectifying (mostly) |
| Sampling type | Single | Single, Double, Multiple |
| Input parameter | LTPD or AOQL + process average | AQL only |
| Switching rules | None | Yes (Normal/Tightened/Reduced) |
| Modern usage | Older, less common | Current industry standard |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in Acceptance Sampling questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing AQL with LTPD** — remember AQL is the *tolerable* quality for the producer; LTPD is the *intolerable* quality for the consumer.
> 2. **Wrong distribution selection** — using Poisson when p > 0.10. Rule: Poisson is valid only when p ≤ 0.10.
> 3. **Forgetting the (N − n) term** in AOQ — this is the *unsampled* portion of the lot. Many students write AOQ = P_a · p, which is the **approximation** valid only when n ≪ N.
> 4. **Mixing up ASN and ATI** — ASN is for **double/multiple sampling** (average sample size); ATI is for **rectifying inspection** (average total inspection, includes screening).
> 5. **Drawing OC curve incorrectly** — the curve must be **monotonically decreasing**, starting from (0, 1) and ending at (1, 0). A horizontal or increasing line will cost full marks.
> 6. **Computing producer's risk as P_a** — α = 1 − P_a (at AQL). This is a classic sign error.
> 7. **Skipping switching rules** in MIL-STD-105E questions — examiner expects 2→5→10 lot logic.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Last-minute revision before the KTU exam — read this section twice.**

- ✅ **Acceptance Sampling = lot-by-lot decision tool**, NOT a process control tool.
- ✅ **Three key risks** in any plan: Producer's risk α, Consumer's risk β, and Type I/Type II errors.
- ✅ **OC curve** = plot of P_a vs. incoming p. The "fingerprint" of a plan.
- ✅ **AQL** → producer's protection → high P_a (typically ≥ 0.90).
- ✅ **LTPD** → consumer's protection → low P_a (typically ≤ 0.10).
- ✅ **Binomial OC formula:** $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$.
- ✅ **Poisson OC formula:** $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$. Use when **p < 0.1** and **n > 30**.
- ✅ **Hypergeometric OC formula:** Use when **n/N > 0.1** (small lots).
- ✅ **AOQ** = $P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)/N$ (rectifying, attribute). Rejected lots → 100% screened.
- ✅ **AOQL** = max of AOQ over all p. Single number summarizing plan's protection.
- ✅ **ATI** = $n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$. Average items inspected per lot.
- ✅ **ASN** for double plan = $n_1 + n_2(1 - P_{a1})$.
- ✅ **Dodge-Romig CSP-1** = minimizes ATI for given LTPD, β = 0.10.
- ✅ **Dodge-Romig CSP-2** = minimizes ATI for given AOQL.
- ✅ **MIL-STD-105E** = AQL-based, single/double/multiple, has switching rules.
- ✅ **Switching rules in MIL-STD-105E:** Normal → Tightened after 2 of 5 rejections; Tightened → Normal after 5 acceptances; Normal → Reduced after 10 acceptances; Reduced → Normal after 1 rejection.
- ✅ **Operating Ratio (OR) = LTPD / AQL** — smaller OR = sharper plan.
- ✅ **n increases → OC curve gets steeper** (better discrimination). c increases → curve shifts right.
- ✅ **Variable sampling** uses x̄ and σ (e.g., MIL-STD-414); less common in KTU questions.
- ✅ **Sampling by attributes** is simpler; preferred when measurement is subjective or costly.
- ✅ **Sequential sampling** has the lowest ASN but is procedurally complex — used for very costly inspection.
- ✅ **Double sampling** reduces average inspection cost by 25–40% compared to single sampling.
- ✅ **Sample size n is NOT a percentage of lot size N** in MIL-STD-105E — it follows specific tables based on lot size and inspection level.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrating Quality into Strategic Management - Obstacles to Achieving Successful Strategic Quality Management - Cost of Quality - Customer Satisfaction - Quality Function Deployment (QFD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Integrating Quality into Strategic Management - Obstacles to Achieving Successful Strategic Quality Management - Cost of Quality - Customer Satisfaction - Quality Function Deployment (QFD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Integrating Quality into Strategic Management

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
*Strategic Quality Management (SQM)* is the integration of quality philosophies, tools, and practices into the organization's long-term strategic planning process. It aligns quality objectives with business goals, ensuring that quality becomes a central pillar of corporate strategy rather than a peripheral operational concern. It involves the formulation, implementation, monitoring, and continuous improvement of quality initiatives that drive competitive advantage and stakeholder value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** Strategic Quality Management is NOT the same as Quality Control or Quality Assurance. It is a *top-down, boardroom-level* commitment that cascades through Vision → Mission → Objectives → Tactics → Operations.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of Strategic Quality Management like **building a house**. Before laying a single brick, you need a *blueprint* (the strategy). Quality is not just about "painting the walls nicely" (which is operational quality control) — it's about deciding *where the house should be built, how many rooms it should have, what materials will withstand earthquakes, and what style fits the family*. If the foundation (strategy) is weak, no amount of painting (quality control) will save the house. SQM ensures the *blueprint itself* is built around quality.

---

### 1.2 Obstacles to Achieving Successful Strategic Quality Management

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Pitfalls Identified by KTU Examiners:** Students often list "lack of training" and forget systemic, cultural, and structural barriers. Examiners expect at least 6–8 distinct obstacles.

| S.No | Obstacle Category | Description |
|------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Lack of Top Management Commitment** | Senior leadership treats quality as a slogan, not a strategic pillar |
| 2 | **Inadequate Quality Culture** | Employees view quality as "someone else's job" (the QC department) |
| 3 | **Short-term Financial Focus** | Quarterly profit obsession prevents long-term quality investment |
| 4 | **Poor Communication** | Quality goals not cascaded clearly from boardroom to shop floor |
| 5 | **Insufficient Training & Education** | Workforce lacks statistical/process tools to execute quality |
| 6 | **Resistance to Change** | Fear of job loss or workflow disruption blocks TQM/Lean adoption |
| 7 | **Inappropriate Quality Measurement** | Measuring only outputs, not processes or customer outcomes |
| 8 | **Inadequate Supplier Integration** | Treating suppliers as vendors, not as quality partners |
| 9 | **Regulatory & Bureaucratic Inertia** | Over-compliance with outdated standards stifles innovation |
| 10 | **Silo Mentality** | Departments optimize locally, destroying end-to-end value |

---

### 1.3 Cost of Quality (COQ)

**Formal Definition:**
*Cost of Quality* is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure product/service conformance to requirements (good quality) plus the cost incurred due to non-conformance (poor quality). It is a financial framework that quantifies the *economic impact* of quality decisions and is a primary tool for justifying quality investments.

**Two Major Categories:**

$$\text{Cost of Quality (COQ)} = \text{Cost of Good Quality (COGQ)} + \text{Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)}$$

Where:
- **COGQ** = Prevention Cost + Appraisal Cost
- **COPQ** = Internal Failure Cost + External Failure Cost

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Implication:** As quality improves, **COGQ initially rises** (more prevention/appraisal investment), but **COPQ falls sharply**. The optimal COQ is achieved when the *sum* of COGQ + COPQ is minimized.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine you own a **car repair shop**. *Prevention cost* = training mechanics properly. *Appraisal cost* = inspecting each car after repair. *Internal failure cost* = fixing a car before delivery (caught in-house). *External failure cost* = a customer breaks down 200 km away and sues you. The cheapest strategy is **not** to skip inspection — it's to invest in *prevention* so failures never occur.

---

### 1.4 Customer Satisfaction

**Formal Definition:**
*Customer Satisfaction* is the degree to which a product's perceived performance matches a buyer's expectations. When performance **exceeds** expectations, the customer is *delighted*; when it **meets** expectations, the customer is *satisfied*; when it **falls below**, the customer is *dissatisfied*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Concept — Kano Model Link:** Customer satisfaction is non-linear. Basic needs (must-be) cause dissatisfaction if absent but no delight if present. Excitement needs (delighters) cause disproportionate satisfaction.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a **restaurant meal**. You expect hot food, clean plates, and polite service (basic needs). If the chef comes to your table and explains the dish personally, you are *delighted*. If the food is cold and the waiter is rude, you will *never return*. The restaurant's strategy must ensure basic needs are met *consistently* and delighters are used *strategically*.

---

### 1.5 Quality Function Deployment (QFD)

**Formal Definition:**
*Quality Function Deployment (QFD)* is a structured, team-based planning and product development methodology that captures the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** and translates customer requirements (the "Whats") into specific, measurable technical/engineering requirements (the "Hows") for each stage of product development and production. The primary visual tool is the **House of Quality (HoQ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** QFD is often called the "House of Quality" because its matrix structure resembles a house. The "roof" contains technical correlations, the "walls" contain relationships and priorities, and the "foundation" contains technical targets.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine you're designing a **new smartphone** based on customer feedback. Customers say: *"I want long battery life, fast charging, and a lightweight phone."* The marketing team (Whats) hands this to the engineering team (Hows). QFD is the **bridge** that converts "long battery" into "5000 mAh battery, 33W fast charging, USB-C PD 3.0, weight ≤ 180 g." It ensures engineers build *what customers want*, not what engineers *think* customers want.
