## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Strategic Quality Management — The Strategic Cascade

Strategic Quality Management follows a **top-down cascading framework** rooted in the Hoshin Kanri and Balanced Scorecard philosophies.

**The Six-Step Strategic Quality Cycle:**

1. **Define Vision & Mission** — Articulate the organization's purpose and quality aspirations.
2. **Conduct Environmental Scan** — SWOT, PESTLE, and Porter's Five Forces analysis with quality lens.
3. **Set Strategic Quality Objectives (SQOs)** — SMART goals aligned with customer and financial outcomes.
4. **Develop Action Plans & Resource Allocation** — Budget, training, tools, and infrastructure.
5. **Deploy Across the Organization (Hoshin Kanri)** — Catchball process to align all departments.
6. **Review, Audit, and Re-strategize** — PDCA cycle at the strategic level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Most organizations fail at Step 5 (Deployment). Research by *Hoshin Planning* studies show that fewer than **10%** of strategic plans are successfully executed. KTU examiners love this statistic.

---

### 2.2 Cost of Quality — The Four Sub-Categories Explained

#### 2.2.1 Prevention Costs (PC)
Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring in the first place.
- Quality planning, training, process design, supplier capability audits, FMEA, design reviews.

#### 2.2.2 Appraisal Costs (AC)
Costs incurred to *detect* defects before they reach the customer.
- Incoming inspection, in-process testing, final QC, calibration of measurement equipment.

#### 2.2.3 Internal Failure Costs (IFC)
Costs incurred when defects are found *before* delivery to the customer.
- Rework, scrap, re-inspection, failure mode analysis, production line stoppages.

#### 2.2.4 External Failure Costs (EFC)
Costs incurred when defects are found *after* delivery to the customer.
- Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits, reputation damage, lost future sales.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of COQ Strategy:** *Prevention is always cheaper than detection, and detection is always cheaper than failure.* A common industry benchmark: every \$1 spent on prevention saves **\$10 in appraisal** and **\$100 in failure costs** (Juran's 1-10-100 rule).

---

### 2.3 Customer Satisfaction — Theoretical Models

#### 2.3.1 Expectancy-Disconfirmation Theory (Oliver, 1980)
Customer satisfaction is a function of the *gap* between expectation (E) and perceived performance (P).

$$\text{Satisfaction} = f(P - E)$$

- If $P > E$ → **Positive Disconfirmation** (Delighted)
- If $P = E$ → **Simple Confirmation** (Satisfied)
- If $P < E$ → **Negative Disconfirmation** (Dissatisfied)

#### 2.3.2 SERVQUAL Model (Parasuraman et al., 1988)
Five dimensions of service quality:
1. **Tangibles** — physical facilities & equipment
2. **Reliability** — ability to perform dependably
3. **Responsiveness** — willingness to help
4. **Assurance** — knowledge & courtesy
5. **Empathy** — caring, individualized attention

$$\text{Service Quality (Q)} = \text{Perception (P)} - \text{Expectation (E)}$$

#### 2.3.3 Net Promoter Score (NPS)
Industry-standard loyalty metric:

$$\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$$

Where Promoters rate 9–10 and Detractors rate 0–6 on a 0–10 scale.

---

### 2.4 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — House of Quality Architecture

The **House of Quality (HoQ)** is the central matrix of QFD. It integrates customer requirements with engineering characteristics.

**Components of the House of Quality:**

| Component | Location in House | Purpose |
|-----------|-------------------|---------|
| **Customer Requirements (Whats)** | Left wall | Voice of Customer, ranked by importance |
| **Technical Requirements (Hows)** | Ceiling/Roof beam | Measurable engineering characteristics |
| **Relationship Matrix** | Central room | Strength of link between Whats and Hows (Strong=9, Medium=3, Weak=1) |
| **Roof (Technical Correlations)** | Triangular roof | How Hows interact with each other (Strong Positive, Positive, Negative, Strong Negative) |
| **Customer Competitive Assessment** | Right wall | How competitors perform on customer requirements |
| **Technical Competitive Assessment** | Floor | How competitors perform on technical characteristics |
| **Technical Targets / Specs** | Basement | Target values, units, tolerances for each How |
| **Priority Weights** | Right of matrix | Ranked Hows for engineering focus |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Memorize the *six input matrices* of the HoQ and the *seven output matrices*. A 14-mark question can ask you to construct or interpret any one of them.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Total Cost of Quality** | $\text{COQ} = \text{PC} + \text{AC} + \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$ | Currency (₹, \$, €) |
| 2 | **Cost of Good Quality** | $\text{COGQ} = \text{PC} + \text{AC}$ | Currency |
| 3 | **Cost of Poor Quality** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$ | Currency |
| 4 | **Quality Cost Ratio** | $\text{QCR} = \dfrac{\text{COGQ}}{\text{COPQ}}$ | Dimensionless; target $\geq 1$ |
| 5 | **Failure Cost as % of Sales** | $\text{FC}\% = \dfrac{\text{IFC} + \text{EFC}}{\text{Total Sales}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 6 | **Cost of Conformance** | $\text{CoC} = \text{PC} + \text{AC}$ | Same as COGQ |
| 7 | **Cost of Non-Conformance** | $\text{CNC} = \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$ | Same as COPQ |
| 8 | **Customer Satisfaction Score** | $\text{CS} = \overline{P_i} - \overline{E_i}$ | Average perception − average expectation |
| 9 | **Net Promoter Score** | $\text{NPS} = \%\text{P} - \%\text{D}$ | Range: −100 to +100 |
| 10 | **QFD Absolute Weight (How j)** | $W_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \times S_{ij}$ | $R_i$ = importance of customer req. $i$; $S_{ij}$ = relationship score |
| 11 | **QFD Relative Weight (How j)** | $RW_j = \dfrac{W_j}{\sum_{k=1}^{m} W_k} \times 100$ | Percentage, sum to 100% |
| 12 | **Quality Loss Function (Taguchi)** | $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ | $k$ = quality loss coefficient; $m$ = target; $y$ = actual |
| 13 | **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Used to measure quality of process |
| 14 | **Customer Requirement Weight** | $w_i = \dfrac{\text{Customer Importance}_i}{\sum \text{Importance}}$ | Normalized weight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for Examiners:** In COQ, ensure you account for *all four* components. Many students miss **External Failure Costs** and lose 2–3 marks.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example: Cost of Quality Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing company incurred the following costs in a financial year:

- Quality training for employees: ₹2,00,000
- Inspection of finished goods: ₹1,50,000
- Rework on defective products: ₹1,80,000
- Warranty claims from customers: ₹3,20,000
- Design review meetings: ₹90,000
- Scrap due to defects: ₹1,20,000
- Product recalls: ₹2,50,000
- Calibration of testing equipment: ₹70,000

**Step 1 — Classify each cost into the four COQ categories.**

We need to map every cost to Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, or External Failure.

| Cost Item | Amount (₹) | Category |
|-----------|------------|----------|
| Quality training for employees | 2,00,000 | Prevention |
| Inspection of finished goods | 1,50,000 | Appraisal |
| Rework on defective products | 1,80,000 | Internal Failure |
| Warranty claims from customers | 3,20,000 | External Failure |
| Design review meetings | 90,000 | Prevention |
| Scrap due to defects | 1,20,000 | Internal Failure |
| Product recalls | 2,50,000 | External Failure |
| Calibration of testing equipment | 70,000 | Appraisal |

**Step 2 — Sum each category.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Prevention Cost (PC)} &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}000 = 2{,}90{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{Appraisal Cost (AC)} &= 1{,}50{,}000 + 70{,}000 = 2{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{Internal Failure Cost (IFC)} &= 1{,}80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{External Failure Cost (EFC)} &= 3{,}20{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 5{,}70{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Cost of Quality.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ} &= \text{PC} + \text{AC} + \text{IFC} + \text{EFC} \\
\text{COQ} &= 2{,}90{,}000 + 2{,}20{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}70{,}000 \\
\text{COQ} &= 13{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute COGQ and COPQ.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COGQ} &= \text{PC} + \text{AC} = 2{,}90{,}000 + 2{,}20{,}000 = 5{,}10{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{COPQ} &= \text{IFC} + \text{EFC} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}70{,}000 = 8{,}70{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Quality Cost Ratio (QCR).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{QCR} &= \dfrac{\text{COGQ}}{\text{COPQ}} = \dfrac{5{,}10{,}000}{8{,}70{,}000} \approx 0.586
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Strategic Interpretation.**

> Since **QCR = 0.586 < 1**, the company is spending *less on prevention/appraisal* than on *failure costs*. This is a classic "**Reactive Quality Cost**" profile (per Juran's COQ model). The company must **shift left** — invest in Prevention (training, robust design) to reduce the dominant External Failure Costs.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: QFD — Computing Absolute & Relative Weights

**Problem Statement:**
A smartphone manufacturer identifies **3 customer requirements (Whats)** and **3 technical characteristics (Hows)**. The relationship matrix uses the standard QFD scoring: Strong = 9, Moderate = 3, Weak = 1, None = 0.

**Given Data:**

| | How 1: Battery Capacity (mAh) | How 2: Processor Speed (GHz) | How 3: Weight (grams, less is better) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Customer Importance (out of 5)** | | | |
| CR1: Long Battery Life (5) | 9 | 1 | 0 |
| CR2: Fast Performance (4) | 1 | 9 | 1 |
| CR3: Lightweight (3) | 3 | 0 | 9 |

**Step 1 — Compute Absolute Weight for each How.**

The formula is: $W_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \times S_{ij}$

Where $R_i$ is the customer importance and $S_{ij}$ is the relationship score.

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{Battery}} &= (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 3) \\
W_{\text{Battery}} &= 45 + 4 + 9 = 58
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{Processor}} &= (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 9) + (3 \times 0) \\
W_{\text{Processor}} &= 5 + 36 + 0 = 41
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{Weight}} &= (5 \times 0) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 9) \\
W_{\text{Weight}} &= 0 + 4 + 27 = 31
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Absolute Weight (Denominator).**

$$\sum W = 58 + 41 + 31 = 130$$

**Step 3 — Compute Relative Weights as Percentages.**

$$\begin{aligned}
RW_{\text{Battery}} &= \dfrac{58}{130} \times 100 \approx 44.62\% \\
RW_{\text{Processor}} &= \dfrac{41}{130} \times 100 \approx 31.54\% \\
RW_{\text{Weight}} &= \dfrac{31}{130} \times 100 \approx 23.85\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Priority.**

| Rank | Technical Characteristic | Relative Weight |
|------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **1** | Battery Capacity | 44.62% |
| **2** | Processor Speed | 31.54% |
| **3** | Weight (Reduction) | 23.85% |

**Step 5 — Strategic Implication.**

> Engineering should allocate **~45% of R&D budget to battery technology**, ~32% to processing power, and ~24% to weight reduction. This ensures resource alignment with customer voice.

---

### 3.3 Customer Satisfaction — Disconfirmation Worked Example

**Problem Statement:**
A restaurant chain surveys 100 customers on three attributes (rated 1–5).

| Attribute | Avg. Expectation (E) | Avg. Perception (P) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Food Quality | 4.2 | 4.6 |
| Service Speed | 4.0 | 3.2 |
| Ambience | 3.8 | 4.0 |

**Step 1 — Compute Disconfirmation for each.**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{Food}} &= P - E = 4.6 - 4.2 = +0.4 \quad (\text{Delighted}) \\
D_{\text{Service}} &= P - E = 3.2 - 4.0 = -0.8 \quad (\text{Dissatisfied}) \\
D_{\text{Ambience}} &= P - E = 4.0 - 3.8 = +0.2 \quad (\text{Delighted})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Overall Customer Satisfaction Index.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CSI} &= \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i - E_i) \\
\text{CSI} &= \dfrac{(+0.4) + (-0.8) + (+0.2)}{3} = \dfrac{-0.2}{3} \approx -0.067
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Strategic Interpretation.**

> Overall CSI is **negative (−0.067)**, driven entirely by service speed. Management must urgently invest in service training or process redesign (e.g., kitchen-display systems).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: COQ Profile & Strategy

| COQ Profile | PC : AC : IFC : EFC Ratio | Strategic Stance |
|-------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| **World-Class (Ideal)** | 70 : 20 : 5 : 5 | Prevention-dominated; failure costs near zero |
| **Mature TQM** | 50 : 30 : 15 : 5 | Balanced investment; very low external failure |
| **Average Firm** | 30 : 30 : 25 : 15 | Traditional inspection-heavy approach |
| **Reactive (Poor)** | 10 : 20 : 30 : 40 | Failure-dominated; "fire-fighting" mode |

### 4.1 House of Quality (HoQ) — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block represents the **House of Quality** structure used in QFD, mapped as a functional block diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CRBlock["CUSTOMER REQUIREMENTS - WHATS - Voice of Customer - Ranked by Importance"]

    TRBlock["TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS - HOWS - Engineering Metrics - Measurable Specifications"]

    RelMatrix["RELATIONSHIP MATRIX - Strong 9 - Moderate 3 - Weak 1 - Links Whats to Hows"]

    RoofCorr["ROOF CORRELATION MATRIX - Strong Positive - Positive - Negative - Strong Negative - Hows to Hows interactions"]

    CustComp["CUSTOMER COMPETITIVE BENCHMARKING - Our Product - Competitor A - Competitor B - Per Whats"]

    TechComp["TECHNICAL COMPETITIVE BENCHMARKING - Our Product - Competitor A - Competitor B - Per How"]

    AbsWeights["ABSOLUTE AND RELATIVE WEIGHTS - Engineering Priority Ranking - Output for R and D"]

    TechTargets["TECHNICAL TARGETS - Target Values - Units - Tolerances - Specifications"]

    CRBlock --> RelMatrix
    TRBlock --> RelMatrix
    RelMatrix --> AbsWeights
    TRBlock --> RoofCorr
    CRBlock --> CustComp
    TRBlock --> TechComp
    AbsWeights --> TechTargets
    RoofCorr --> TechTargets
    CustComp --> TechTargets
    TechComp --> TechTargets
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

### 4.4 Customer Satisfaction — Expectancy-Disconfirmation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Perception["CUSTOMER PERCEPTION - Actual Product or Service Experience"]

    Compare{"COMPARE P vs E"}

    PosD["POSITIVE DISCONFIRMATION - P greater than E - Customer Delighted - Loyalty and Advocacy"]

    SimCon["SIMPLE CONFIRMATION - P equals E - Customer Satisfied - Neutral Loyalty"]
```

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Strategic Quality Management. List any four obstacles to its successful implementation.**

**Model Answer:**
Strategic Quality Management (SQM) is the integration of quality principles into the organization's long-term strategic planning, ensuring quality becomes a core business objective rather than a peripheral activity. It aligns quality goals with business goals to drive competitive advantage.

Four obstacles to successful SQM:
1. Lack of top management commitment and visible leadership
2. Inadequate quality culture and employee engagement
3. Short-term financial focus that ignores long-term quality investment
4. Resistance to change and fear of new methodologies

> **Word count: ~80 words | Marks: 3**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Cost of Quality? Differentiate between Cost of Good Quality and Cost of Poor Quality.**

**Model Answer:**
Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total financial cost associated with ensuring conformance to quality standards plus the cost of non-conformance.

| Aspect | Cost of Good Quality (COGQ) | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) |
|--------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Costs to *prevent* and *detect* defects | Costs resulting from *failures* |
| **Components** | Prevention + Appraisal | Internal Failure + External Failure |
| **Nature** | Investment / Proactive | Loss / Reactive |
| **Strategic Goal** | Should *increase* initially | Should *decrease* continuously |

> **Word count: ~75 words | Marks: 3**

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice Option 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** framework in detail. Discuss all four categories with at least two examples each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company's COQ data is given below. Compute Total COQ, COGQ, COPQ, and the Quality Cost Ratio. Interpret the result strategically. **(7 Marks)**

| Cost Item | Amount (₹) |
|-----------|------------|
| Operator training | 1,00,000 |
| Incoming material inspection | 80,000 |
| Rework on defective units | 1,40,000 |
| Customer returns | 2,00,000 |
| Quality planning | 60,000 |
| Equipment calibration | 50,000 |
| Warranty repairs | 1,60,000 |
| Scrap disposal | 90,000 |

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Cost of Quality (COQ)** framework, pioneered by Joseph Juran and refined by the ASQ, classifies all quality-related expenditures into four categories:

1. **Prevention Costs (PC)** — Proactive investments to *stop defects from occurring*.
   - *Examples:* Quality planning, employee training, process design, FMEA, supplier capability assessment.

2. **Appraisal Costs (AC)** — Costs of *detecting defects* before delivery.
   - *Examples:* Incoming inspection, in-process testing, final QC, measurement equipment calibration.

3. **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** — Costs when defects are caught *within* the organization.
   - *Examples:* Rework, scrap, re-inspection, production line downtimes.

4. **External Failure Costs (EFC)** — Costs when defects reach the *customer*.
   - *Examples:* Warranty claims, product recalls, returns, lawsuits, lost reputation.

**Strategic Insight:** The most expensive category is **External Failure**, because it carries hidden costs (brand damage, future sales loss). Smart organizations *shift left* by investing in **Prevention**, which has the highest ROI.

> **Allocation: 2 marks for definition + 4 marks for categories/examples + 1 mark for strategic insight = 7 marks**

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Classify costs.**

| Cost Item | Amount (₹) | Category |
|-----------|------------|----------|
| Operator training | 1,00,000 | Prevention |
| Incoming material inspection | 80,000 | Appraisal |
| Rework on defective units | 1,40,000 | Internal Failure |
| Customer returns | 2,00,000 | External Failure |
| Quality planning | 60,000 | Prevention |
| Equipment calibration | 50,000 | Appraisal |
| Warranty repairs | 1,60,000 | External Failure |
| Scrap disposal | 90,000 | Internal Failure |

**Step 2 — Sum categories.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PC} &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 1{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{AC} &= 80{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 1{,}30{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{IFC} &= 1{,}40{,}000 + 90{,}000 = 2{,}30{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{EFC} &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000 = 3{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute COQ, COGQ, COPQ.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COGQ} &= \text{PC} + \text{AC} = 1{,}60{,}000 + 1{,}30{,}000 = 2{,}90{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{COPQ} &= \text{IFC} + \text{EFC} = 2{,}30{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000 = 5{,}90{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{COQ} &= \text{COGQ} + \text{COPQ} = 2{,}90{,}000 + 5{,}90{,}000 = 8{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Quality Cost Ratio.**

$$\text{QCR} = \dfrac{\text{COGQ}}{\text{COPQ}} = \dfrac{2{,}90{,}000}{5{,}90{,}000} \approx 0.49$$

**Step 5 — Strategic Interpretation.**

> Since **QCR = 0.49 < 1**, the company is in a **Reactive COQ profile**. Failure costs (especially external) dominate. The company should aggressively increase **Prevention investments** (training, robust design, supplier development) to drive down EFC and IFC, following Juran's 1-10-100 rule.

> **Allocation: 2 marks classification + 2 marks calculation + 1 mark ratio + 2 marks interpretation = 7 marks**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice Option 2)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** What is **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**? Explain the **House of Quality** with the help of a neat labelled diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A QFD team for a laptop manufacturer identifies **4 customer requirements (Whats)** and **3 technical requirements (Hows)**. The relationship matrix (using 9/3/1/0 scoring) and customer importance data are given below. Compute the **absolute and relative weights** of each technical requirement and rank them. **(7 Marks)**

| Customer Requirement (Importance) | Battery Life (How1) | Processor Speed (How2) | Screen Resolution (How3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Long Battery Life (5) | 9 | 1 | 0 |
| Fast Performance (4) | 3 | 9 | 1 |
| Sharp Display (3) | 0 | 1 | 9 |
| Light Weight (2) | 3 | 1 | 0 |

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured, team-based product development methodology that translates **Customer Requirements (Whats)** into **Technical Requirements (Hows)** using a visual matrix called the **House of Quality (HoQ)**. It ensures the "Voice of Customer" drives engineering design decisions.

**House of Quality — Components (5 marks):**

The HoQ is built from these interconnected matrices:

1. **Left Wall — Customer Requirements (Whats):** Ranked by importance (1–5 scale) based on VOC.
2. **Ceiling — Technical Requirements (Hows):** Measurable engineering specifications.
3. **Central Room — Relationship Matrix:** Cells filled with 9 (strong), 3 (moderate), 1 (weak), or 0 (no relationship).
4. **Roof — Technical Correlations:** Shows how Hows interact — strong positive (+), positive (+), negative (−), strong negative (⊝).
5. **Right Wall — Customer Competitive Assessment:** How our product vs. competitors on each CR.
6. **Floor — Technical Competitive Assessment:** How our product vs. competitors on each TR.
7. **Basement — Technical Targets:** Final target values, units, and tolerances.
8. **Output — Absolute & Relative Weights:** Ranked engineering priorities.

> The "house" metaphor is apt: the *roof* shows technical synergies, the *walls* contain customer voices, and the *foundation* locks in targets.

> **Allocation: 2 marks definition + 4 marks components + 1 mark summary = 7 marks**

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Set up the formula.**

$$W_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \times S_{ij}$$

Where $R_i$ = customer importance and $S_{ij}$ = relationship score.

**Step 2 — Compute Absolute Weight for each How.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{Battery}} &= (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 3) + (3 \times 0) + (2 \times 3) \\
&= 45 + 12 + 0 + 6 = 63
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{Processor}} &= (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 9) + (3 \times 1) + (2 \times 1) \\
&= 5 + 36 + 3 + 2 = 46
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{Screen}} &= (5 \times 0) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 9) + (2 \times 0) \\
&= 0 + 4 + 27 + 0 = 31
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total weight.**

$$\sum W = 63 + 46 + 31 = 140$$

**Step 4 — Relative Weights.**

$$\begin{aligned}
RW_{\text{Battery}} &= \dfrac{63}{140} \times 100 = 45.00\% \\
RW_{\text{Processor}} &= \dfrac{46}{140} \times 100 = 32.86\% \\
RW_{\text{Screen}} &= \dfrac{31}{140} \times 100 = 22.14\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final Ranking.**

| Rank | Technical Requirement | Absolute Weight | Relative Weight |
|------|----------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **1** | Battery Life | 63 | 45.00% |
| **2** | Processor Speed | 46 | 32.86% |
| **3** | Screen Resolution | 31 | 22.14% |

**Step 6 — Engineering Decision.**

> Engineering R&D should allocate ~45% of resources to battery technology, ~33% to processing power, and ~22% to screen resolution. This ensures alignment with customer priorities.

> **Allocation: 2 marks formula + 3 marks calculations + 1 mark ranking + 1 mark decision = 7 marks**

---

### > [!WARNING] — KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **COQ Misclassification (3 marks lost):** Students often misclassify *warranty* as "Internal Failure." It is **External Failure** because the defect reached the customer.
> 2. **QFD Scoring Errors (2 marks lost):** Using 5/3/1 instead of the standard **9/3/1** scoring. Always use 9-3-1 in KTU exams.
> 3. **Forgetting the Roof of HoQ (2 marks lost):** A 7-mark "Explain HoQ" answer that omits the *roof (technical correlations)* is incomplete.
> 4. **Customer Satisfaction Formula Confusion (2 marks lost):** Confusing $P - E$ with $E - P$. The correct order is **Perception minus Expectation**.
> 5. **No Interpretation (3 marks lost):** Pure numerical COQ calculations without a *strategic recommendation* are considered half-answered.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (QEM)**

**Strategic Quality Management:**
- [ ] SQM = Quality integrated into corporate strategy, not just operations
- [ ] Six-step cascade: Vision → Mission → Environmental Scan → SQO → Hoshin Deployment → Review
- [ ] Top management commitment is the **#1 success factor**
- [ ] Hoshin Kanri is the standard deployment methodology

**Obstacles to SQM:**
- [ ] 10 main obstacles: leadership gap, culture, short-termism, communication, training, change resistance, wrong metrics, supplier disconnect, regulatory inertia, silos

**Cost of Quality (COQ):**
- [ ] COQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC
- [ ] COGQ = PC + AC; COPQ = IFC + EFC
- [ ] QCR = COGQ / COPQ; target **QCR ≥ 1** for world-class firms
- [ ] Juran's 1-10-100 rule: \$1 prevention saves \$10 appraisal, \$100 failure

**Customer Satisfaction:**
- [ ] Expectancy-Disconfirmation Theory: $P - E$
- [ ] Positive disconfirmation = delight; Negative = dissatisfaction
- [ ] SERVQUAL 5 dimensions; NPS = %Promoters − %Detractors
- [ ] Kano Model: Basic, Performance, Excitement needs

**Quality Function Deployment (QFD):**
- [ ] QFD = Voice of Customer → Engineering specifications
- [ ] House of Quality is the central tool
- [ ] Standard scoring: 9 (strong), 3 (moderate), 1 (weak), 0 (none)
- [ ] Absolute Weight: $W_j = \sum R_i \times S_{ij}$
- [ ] Relative Weight: $RW_j = (W_j / \sum W) \times 100$
- [ ] HoQ has 6 inputs and 7 outputs; do not forget the **roof correlations**

**Cross-Connections to Remember:**
- [ ] COQ savings can fund **Prevention** → drives **Customer Satisfaction** → enables **Strategic Quality** objectives
- [ ] QFD uses **Customer Satisfaction data** as primary input
- [ ] All three (COQ, CS, QFD) are *tools* of Strategic Quality Management
- [ ] Taguchi's Quality Loss Function connects directly to COQ External Failure

**Memory Hooks:**
- [ ] **COQ Order:** "PArents INtend EXternal failures" → **P**revention, **A**ppraisal, **IN**ternal Failure, **EX**ternal Failure
- [ ] **HoQ Structure:** "The roof is technical, the walls are customer, the floor is targets"
- [ ] **NPS Range:** −100 (all detractors) to +100 (all promoters)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, proactive, and structured group engineering technique used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and document potential failure modes of a product, process, or service, and to determine the effect of each failure on system performance, safety, and customer satisfaction.

In a more rigorous engineering lexicon:
- **Failure Mode (FM)**: The *manner* in which a component, sub-system, or process could potentially fail to perform its intended function.
- **Effect Analysis (EA)**: The study of the *consequences* of those failures on product output, the next higher assembly, the end customer, and/or regulatory compliance.

The expanded modern variant, **Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA)**, additionally incorporates a *Criticality* assessment (qualitative or quantitative) to rank the relative impact of each failure mode.

---

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Pre-Surgery Checklist" Analogy**
> Imagine a cardiac surgeon preparing for open-heart surgery. Before making the first incision, the surgical team runs through a structured mental checklist: *What if the patient's blood type doesn't match? What if the anesthesia machine fails? What if the defibrillator pads don't adhere?* Each potential failure is rated for **how bad it would be** (severity), **how often it might happen** (occurrence), and **how likely we are to catch it before it becomes fatal** (detection). This is *exactly* what FMEA does for engineering designs — it is the **pre-emptive surgical audit** of a product before it reaches the customer.

A second accessible analogy is a **building fire-safety audit**. Before the building opens, fire marshals walk through every floor, asking: *"If this exit is blocked, what happens to the people on the 8th floor? How often does such a blockage occur? Will our smoke detector catch it?"* The FMEA worksheet is the documentation of that audit.

---

### 1.2 Types of FMEA (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| **Type** | **Full Form** | **Application Stage** | **Primary User** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DFMEA** | Design FMEA | During product/system design | Design Engineer |
| **PFMEA** | Process FMEA | During manufacturing process planning | Manufacturing/Process Engineer |
| **SFMEA** | System FMEA | At overall system architecture level | Systems Engineer |
| **UFMEA** | Usage FMEA | User/misuse scenarios | Service/Human Factors Engineer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip**: When asked *"Differentiate DFMEA and PFMEA,"* always anchor your answer to **WHEN** the analysis is performed and **WHAT function** is being audited (design intent vs. process capability).

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Constants

- **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** — Traditional ranking index: range **1 to 1000**.
- **S (Severity)** — Range: **1 (negligible) to 10 (catastrophic)**.
- **O (Occurrence)** — Range: **1 (very unlikely) to 10 (very frequent)**.
- **D (Detection)** — Range: **1 (very high detection capability) to 10 (cannot detect)**.
- **AP (Action Priority)** — Newer AIAG-VDA 2019 ranking: **H (High), M (Medium), L (Low)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FMEA Methodology — Structured Logic Flow

The FMEA procedure follows a **disciplined 10-step workflow** standardized under **AIAG-VDA (2019)** and legacy **SAE J1739 / QS-9000** protocols. The steps are deliberately sequenced to move from broad system decomposition to narrow, actionable risk reduction.

### Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition:

1. **Define the Scope & Boundary**
   *Establish the system, sub-system, or process to be analyzed; identify the analysis type (Design, Process, System).*
   - *Why?* Without boundaries, the analysis becomes unbounded and unmanageable.
   - *How?* Use a **Block Diagram** or **Process Flow Chart (PFC)** as the analysis framework.

2. **Assemble the Cross-Functional Team (CFT)**
   - *Why?* FMEA relies on collective expertise — a single engineer cannot see all failure modes.
   - *How?* Include Design, Manufacturing, Quality, Reliability, Service, and Customer voices.

3. **Identify Functions of the Item / Process Step**
   - *Why?* You cannot fail at something you have not first defined as a function.
   - *How?* Use the **"verb + noun + specification"** pattern (e.g., *"Conduct 5 amps of current continuously at 12V"*).

4. **Determine Potential Failure Modes**
   - *Why?* A failure mode is the *loss of function*; without it, no analysis exists.
   - *How?* Brainstorm with **"What could go wrong?"** for each function.

5. **Determine Potential Effects (Consequences)**
   - *Why?* Effects justify the business case for action.
   - *How?* Trace the failure through the **next higher assembly, the system, and the end customer**.

6. **Determine Potential Causes (Mechanisms)**
   - *Why?* Causes are the *root mechanisms* you must eliminate or control.
   - *How?* Use tools like **5-Why Analysis**, **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**, or **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagrams**.

7. **Assign Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) Ratings**
   - *Why?* Quantification enables prioritization.
   - *How?* Use the **standardized 1–10 scales** documented in the FMEA Reference Manual.

8. **Compute RPN or Determine Action Priority (AP)**
   - *Why?* Ranking determines the order in which the team acts.
   - *How?* Traditional: $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$. Modern: Apply **AP Table Lookup**.

9. **Recommend Actions & Re-rate**
   - *Why?* The goal of FMEA is **risk reduction**, not just risk identification.
   - *How?* For high-RPN / High-AP items, propose elimination, substitution, detection enhancement, or acceptance criteria.

10. **Document, Validate, and Refresh**
    - *Why?* FMEA is a **living document**.
    - *How?* Update after every design change, supplier change, or field failure.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations for the Board Exam**

| **Formula / Metric** | **Mathematical Form** | **Variables** | **Range / Units** | **Real-World Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Priority Number (Traditional) | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | $S, O, D \in \{1,2,\ldots,10\}$ | 1 to 1000 (dimensionless) | Universal risk ranking metric |
| Criticality (Quantitative FMECA) | $C_m = S \times \beta \times \lambda_p$ | $S$ = Severity; $\beta$ = Failure mode ratio; $\lambda_p$ = Part failure rate | Failure modes / $10^6$ hrs | Aerospace/defence reliability allocation |
| Severity-only Threshold (Catastrophic) | $S \geq 9$ | Independent | 9 or 10 | Triggers mandatory action regardless of RPN |
| Action Priority Reduction Test | $\Delta\text{RPN} \geq 30\%$ | Before vs. after action | Percentage | Validates action effectiveness |
| Detection Capability Rate | $D_c = 1 - \frac{N_{\text{escaped}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | $N_{\text{escaped}}$ = escapes; $N_{\text{total}}$ = opportunities | 0 to 1 | SPC/MSA validation metric |
| AP Threshold Logic | $\text{AP} \in \{H, M, L\}$ | Look-up table driven | Categorical | Modern AIAG-VDA 2019 prioritization |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Many students write $\text{RPN} = S + O + D$. This is **WRONG**. RPN is strictly a **multiplication**. Adding the three is meaningless.

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where FMEA is Indispensable

- **Automotive (IATF 16949)**: Mandatory pre-launch requirement for every new vehicle programme.
- **Aerospace (AS9100D, FAR 25.1309)**: Required for safety-critical flight systems.
- **Medical Devices (ISO 14971)**: Integral to the risk management file for FDA / CE submission.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication**: Drives **Process FMEA** for every new lithography node.
- **Service Industries**: Adapted as **Service FMEA (SFMEA)** to map failure modes in banking, healthcare, and hospitality workflows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: RPN Ranking Logic

The traditional **Risk Priority Number** is constructed from the premise that risk is jointly proportional to **how severe** a failure is, **how often** it occurs, and **how poorly** it is detected before reaching the customer. Each factor is independently rated on a **1-to-10 ordinal scale**, and the three are multiplied to yield a single scalar that enables cross-comparison across many failure modes in a worksheet.

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

To formalize the bounding behaviour of this metric, observe that since each rating is constrained:

$$
1 \leq S \leq 10, \quad 1 \leq O \leq 10, \quad 1 \leq D \leq 10
$$

The RPN is bounded above and below as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\min} &= 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1 \\
\text{RPN}_{\max} &= 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

The total count of unique RPN values is finite:

$$
N_{\text{RPN}} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000
$$

But the distribution is **non-uniform** — many combinations map to the same RPN value, which is precisely the **central limitation** that motivated the move toward Action Priority (AP) tables in **AIAG-VDA 2019**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Braking System Design FMEA (Board Exam Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context**: An automotive OEM is performing a **Design FMEA** on an electronic brake-by-wire actuator. The cross-functional team has identified **three critical failure modes** with the ratings tabulated below. Compute the **RPN** for each and recommend the priority for corrective action.

| **Failure Mode ID** | **Description** | $S$ | $O$ | $D$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FM-01 | Signal wire chafes, causes intermittent braking | 8 | 5 | 6 |
| FM-02 | Hydraulic seal leaks under thermal cycling | 9 | 3 | 7 |
| FM-03 | ECU software miscalculates deceleration | 10 | 2 | 9 |

### Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1: Compute RPN for FM-01**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM-01}} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 8 \times 5 \times 6 \\
&= 40 \times 6 \\
&= 240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute RPN for FM-02**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM-02}} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 9 \times 3 \times 7 \\
&= 27 \times 7 \\
&= 189
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute RPN for FM-03**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{FM-03}} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 10 \times 2 \times 9 \\
&= 20 \times 9 \\
&= 180
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Rank by RPN**

$$
\text{FM-01 (240)} > \text{FM-02 (189)} > \text{FM-03 (180)}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Severity Override Rule**

Even though FM-01 has the highest RPN, FM-03 has **S = 10 (Catastrophic — loss of vehicle control)**. Per the FMEA Reference Manual, **any failure mode with $S \geq 9$ requires mandatory action** regardless of RPN ranking. Hence, the **revised priority order** is:

$$
\text{FM-03 (S=10)} \rightarrow \text{FM-01 (RPN=240)} \rightarrow \text{FM-02 (RPN=189)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Action Priority (AP) Determination (Modern AIAG-VDA 2019)

The **Action Priority** method replaces single-number RPN with a **lookup table** that considers $S$, $O$, and $D$ jointly. The simplified AP logic for the matrix is:

$$
\text{AP} = f(S, O, D) \in \{H, M, L\}
$$

where the function $f$ is defined by the **AP Table** excerpted below for the most common zones.

### Simplified AP Lookup Matrix:

| **Severity Range** | **Occurrence** | **Detection** | **Action Priority** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S \geq 9$ | Any | Any | **H (High)** |
| $S = 7 \text{ or } 8$ | $O \geq 4$ | $D \geq 5$ | **H (High)** |
| $S = 7 \text{ or } 8$ | $O \leq 3$ | $D \leq 4$ | **M (Medium)** |
| $S = 5 \text{ or } 6$ | $O \geq 6$ | $D \geq 6$ | **M (Medium)** |
| $S = 5 \text{ or } 6$ | $O \leq 5$ | $D \leq 5$ | **L (Low)** |
| $S \leq 4$ | Any | Any | **L (Low)** |

### Applying to FM-01: $S=8, O=5, D=6$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1:} \quad S \geq 9 &\rightarrow \text{False (S=8)} \\
\text{Check 2:} \quad S = 7 \text{ or } 8, \; O \geq 4, \; D \geq 5 &\rightarrow \text{True} \\
\therefore \text{AP}_{\text{FM-01}} &= \mathbf{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Applying to FM-02: $S=9, O=3, D=7$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1:} \quad S \geq 9 &\rightarrow \text{True (S=9)} \\
\therefore \text{AP}_{\text{FM-02}} &= \mathbf{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Applying to FM-03: $S=10, O=2, D=9$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1:} \quad S \geq 9 &\rightarrow \text{True (S=10)} \\
\therefore \text{AP}_{\text{FM-03}} &= \mathbf{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

In the AP framework, all three failure modes demand high-priority action — but FM-03 still takes precedence due to the **$S=10$ catastrophic ceiling**.

---

## 3.4 Risk Matrix Visualization (Severity × Occurrence)

The risk matrix is a **2D visualization** with **Severity on the Y-axis** and **Occurrence on the X-axis**, where each cell is colour-coded to indicate required action level. Detection is overlaid as a separate axis in 3D representations.

$$
\text{Risk Level} = \begin{cases} \text{Critical (Red)} & S \geq 9, \; O \geq 4 \\ \text{High (Orange)} & S \in \{7,8\}, \; O \in \{5,6,7\} \\ \text{Medium (Yellow)} & S \in \{4,5,6\}, \; O \leq 6 \\ \text{Low (Green)} & S \leq 4 \end{cases}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Question Type** | **RPN Method** | **AP Method (AIAG-VDA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Single scalar | Categorical |
| Tie-breaking | None (many RPNs can equal) | Built-in via table |
| Severity Override | Manual application | Built-in |
| Industry Adoption | Legacy / older manuals | Modern (post-2019) |
| Recommended for Board Answer | Yes (classical) | Yes (if asked "modern") |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMEA Process Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Initiate FMEA Project]) --> stepB[Define Scope and Boundary]
    stepB --> stepC[Assemble Cross Functional Team]
    stepC --> stepD[Identify Item Functions]
    stepD --> stepE[List Potential Failure Modes]
    stepE --> stepF[Determine Effects on System]
    stepF --> stepG[Determine Root Causes]
    stepG --> stepH[Assign Severity Score]
    stepH --> stepI[Assign Occurrence Score]
    stepI --> stepJ[Assign Detection Score]
    stepJ --> stepK{RPN or AP Threshold Exceeded}
    stepK -->|Yes| stepL[Recommend Corrective Action]
    stepK -->|No| stepN[Accept Risk and Monitor]
    stepL --> stepO[Implement Action]
    stepO --> stepP[Re-rate S O D]
    stepP --> stepQ[Verify RPN Reduction]
    stepQ --> stepR[Update FMEA Document]
    stepN --> stepR
    stepR --> stepS([Continuous Improvement Loop])
```

## 4.2 Risk Matrix Heatmap (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph axisX[Occurrence Axis Left to Right]
        x1[O1 Very Low] --> x2[O2 Low]
        x2 --> x3[O3 Moderate]
        x3 --> x4[O4 High]
        x4 --> x5[O5 Very High]
    end
    subgraph axisY[Severity Axis Bottom to Top]
        y1[S1 Minor] --> y2[S4 Moderate]
        y2 --> y3[S6 Significant]
        y3 --> y4[S8 Major]
        y4 --> y5[S10 Catastrophic]
    end
    zoneA[Green Zone Low Risk]:::green
    zoneB[Yellow Zone Medium Risk]:::yellow
    zoneC[Orange Zone High Risk]:::orange
    zoneD[Red Zone Critical Risk Mandatory Action]:::red
    zoneA --- zoneB
    zoneB --- zoneC
    zoneC --- zoneD
```

## 4.3 FMEA Worksheet Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    header[Header Project Name Date Team] --> col1[Column Item Function]
    col1 --> col2[Column Failure Mode]
    col2 --> col3[Column Effect on Customer]
    col3 --> col4[Column Severity S]
    col4 --> col5[Column Potential Cause]
    col5 --> col6[Column Occurrence O]
    col6 --> col7[Column Current Controls Detection]
    col7 --> col8[Column Detection D]
    col8 --> col9[Computation RPN equals S times O times D]
    col9 --> col10[Column Recommended Action]
    col10 --> col11[Column Responsibility and Target Date]
    col11 --> col12[Column Action Taken and Revised RPN]
    col12 --> footer[Footer Sign off Quality Manager]
```

## 4.4 Cause-Effect Chain Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cause1[Material Defect] --> fm1[Failure Mode Seal Hardening]
    cause2[Temperature Excursion] --> fm1
    cause3[Vibration] --> fm1
    fm1 --> eff1[Effect Hydraulic Leak]
    eff1 --> sys1[System Impact Brake Failure]
    sys1 --> cust1[Customer Impact Vehicle Accident]
    ctrl1[Control Incoming Material Inspection] -.->|Reduces| cause1
    ctrl2[Control SPC on Process Parameters] -.->|Reduces| cause2
    ctrl3[Control End of Line Pressure Test] -.->|Detects| eff1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA). List any three benefits of conducting FMEA during the product design phase.**

#### Model Answer:

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA) is a systematic, proactive engineering technique used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and document potential failure modes of a product or process and to determine the effect of each failure on system performance and customer satisfaction.

**Three Benefits of FMEA in Design Phase:**

1. **Early Risk Identification**: Potential failures are caught at the design stage, where corrective action is **10 to 100 times cheaper** than fixing them post-launch.

2. **Improved Product Reliability**: By systematically addressing high-RPN failure modes, the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of the product is enhanced.

3. **Cross-Functional Knowledge Capture**: The FMEA worksheet becomes a **living institutional memory** of design rationale, accessible to manufacturing, service, and quality teams.

4. **Regulatory Compliance**: Many industry standards (IATF 16949, AS9100, ISO 14971) mandate FMEA as evidence of risk-based thinking.

*(Examiner will accept any three valid benefits.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Design FMEA (DFMEA) and Process FMEA (PFMEA) in terms of purpose, timing, and focus.**

#### Model Answer:

> [!NOTE]
> **Tabular Comparison**:

| **Parameter** | **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Identify failures in the product design itself | Identify failures in the manufacturing process |
| **Timing** | Conducted during product development, before tooling | Conducted during process planning, before production launch |
| **Focus** | Design intent, tolerances, materials, interfaces | Process variables, machine capability, human factors |
| **Failure Source** | Engineering drawing, specification gaps | Set-up errors, operator mistakes, machine wear |
| **Detection Method** | Design reviews, FEA, prototype testing | SPC, poka-yoke, automated inspection |
| **Responsibility** | Design Engineer leads | Manufacturing / Process Engineer leads |

---

---

## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and may contain **internal choice**. The structure below provides **two complete alternative questions** that satisfy the optional-choice format.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`**

**(a) Explain the Risk Priority Number (RPN) method of risk evaluation in FMEA with its limitations. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is the traditional metric used to quantify and rank the relative risk of each identified failure mode in an FMEA worksheet. It is computed as the product of three independent ratings:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

where:

- **S (Severity)** ranges from **1 to 10** and quantifies the seriousness of the failure's effect on the customer (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic, safety-impacting).
- **O (Occurrence)** ranges from **1 to 10** and quantifies the likelihood of the failure mode occurring (1 = very rare / 100 in $10^6$, 10 = very frequent / $\geq$ 100 in $10^2$).
- **D (Detection)** ranges from **1 to 10** and quantifies the likelihood that the current control will detect the failure before reaching the customer (1 = almost certain detection, 10 = cannot detect).

The RPN value ranges from **1 to 1000**. Higher RPN values indicate higher relative risk and require higher priority corrective action.

**Limitations of RPN:**

1. **Non-Unique Mapping**: Many different $(S, O, D)$ combinations can yield the same RPN value, masking fundamentally different risk profiles.

2. **Severity Override Ignored**: A failure with $S=10, O=1, D=2$ (RPN = 20) appears less risky than one with $S=4, O=5, D=10$ (RPN = 200), even though the former is catastrophic and the latter is minor — mathematically incorrect prioritization.

3. **Arbitrary Thresholds**: There is no universally agreed RPN cutoff for "must act" vs. "monitor," leading to inconsistent decisions across teams.

4. **Ordinal Multiplication Misuse**: Multiplying ordinal rankings assumes a linear relationship that does not actually exist; a Severity of 10 is not "10 times worse" than Severity of 1.

5. **Ignores Action Cost-Effectiveness**: RPN reduction does not account for the engineering effort or cost required.

*(Examiner expects at least 4 limitations for full 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) A hydraulic cylinder in a construction equipment has been analyzed using PFMEA. The team identified three failure modes with the following ratings. Compute the RPN for each, rank them, and recommend action priority using the Severity Override Rule. (7 Marks)**

| **Failure Mode** | **Description** | $S$ | $O$ | $D$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Piston seal leakage | 8 | 6 | 5 |
| F2 | Hydraulic hose burst | 10 | 3 | 8 |
| F3 | Cylinder rod scoring | 5 | 7 | 6 |

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1: Compute RPN for each failure mode**

For F1 (Piston seal leakage):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{F1} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 8 \times 6 \times 5 \\
&= 48 \times 5 \\
&= 240
\end{aligned}
$$

For F2 (Hydraulic hose burst):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{F2} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 10 \times 3 \times 8 \\
&= 30 \times 8 \\
&= 240
\end{aligned}
$$

For F3 (Cylinder rod scoring):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{F3} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 5 \times 7 \times 6 \\
&= 35 \times 6 \\
&= 210
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Initial Ranking by RPN (Numerically)**

$$
\text{F1 (240)} = \text{F2 (240)} > \text{F3 (210)}
$$

Note that F1 and F2 are tied numerically, which itself illustrates a **limitation of RPN**.

**Step 3: Apply the Severity Override Rule**

Failure mode **F2 has Severity $S = 10$**, which represents a *catastrophic* failure (potential worker injury from hose burst). Per the FMEA Reference Manual, **any failure with $S \geq 9$ must be addressed with mandatory action regardless of RPN ranking**.

**Step 4: Revised Action Priority Order**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Priority 1:} \quad & \text{F2 — Hydraulic hose burst (S=10, mandatory)} \\
\text{Priority 2:} \quad & \text{F1 — Piston seal leakage (RPN=240)} \\
\text{Priority 3:} \quad & \text{F3 — Cylinder rod scoring (RPN=210)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommended Actions:**
- **F2**: Replace hose material with higher burst-pressure rating; add 100% pressure-test at end-of-line.
- **F1**: Switch to double-lip seal; add SPC on seal groove dimensions.
- **F3**: Improve rod surface finish to Ra ≤ 0.4 μm; add automated visual inspection.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Process FMEA (PFMEA). (7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

The **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** is a structured, team-based analytical method used to identify and mitigate potential failures in the manufacturing process. The step-by-step procedure is as follows:

**Step 1: Define the Process Scope**
Use a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** or **PFMEA worksheet header** to identify the process step, machine, operator, and product being analyzed.

**Step 2: Assemble the Cross-Functional Team**
Include Process Engineer, Quality Engineer, Operator, Maintenance, and Supplier representative.

**Step 3: Identify the Process Function**
For each process step, write a function statement using the **"verb + noun + specification"** pattern. Example: *"Drill a 6 mm diameter hole to depth of 12 ± 0.1 mm."*

**Step 4: Identify Potential Failure Modes**
Failure mode is the *manner* in which the process step could fail to meet its function (e.g., *"Hole diameter too large"*).

**Step 5: Determine Potential Effects**
Effect is the consequence of the failure mode on the product or downstream process (e.g., *"Mis-fit with mating part"*).

**Step 6: Determine Potential Causes**
Cause is the *mechanism* triggering the failure (e.g., *"Tool wear," "Operator set-up error"*).

**Step 7: Assign Severity (S) Score**
Rate how severely the effect impacts the customer (1–10).

**Step 8: Assign Occurrence (O) Score**
Rate how often the cause occurs, based on process capability ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) and historical data (1–10).

**Step 9: Identify Current Controls and Assign Detection (D) Score**
Rate how well the current control (SPC, poka-yoke, gauge) detects the failure (1–10).

**Step 10: Calculate RPN and Prioritize**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 11: Recommend Actions**
For high-RPN items, propose corrective actions: process redesign, error-proofing, capability improvement.

**Step 12: Re-rate and Document**
After action implementation, re-compute RPN and verify reduction.

**Step 13: Establish a Review Cycle**
Update the PFMEA whenever there is a process change, machine upgrade, or recurring field complaint.

---

**(b) The following are the failure modes identified in a DFMEA for an electric vehicle battery pack. Apply the modern AIAG-VDA Action Priority (AP) method to determine the AP level for each. (7 Marks)**

| **Failure Mode** | **Description** | $S$ | $O$ | $D$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | Cell voltage imbalance | 7 | 5 | 7 |
| M2 | Thermal runaway in cell | 10 | 2 | 8 |
| M3 | Loose busbar connection | 6 | 4 | 4 |

#### Model Answer:

**Step 1: AP Method Review**

The **Action Priority (AP)** approach (AIAG-VDA 2019) replaces RPN with a categorical lookup that respects the asymmetric importance of Severity.

**Step 2: Apply AP Logic to Each Failure Mode**

For **M1** (Cell voltage imbalance) — $S=7, O=5, D=7$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1:} \quad S \geq 9 &\rightarrow \text{False} \\
\text{Check 2:} \quad S \in \{7,8\}, \; O \geq 4, \; D \geq 5 &\rightarrow \text{True (S=7, O=5, D=7)} \\
\therefore \text{AP}_{M1} &= \mathbf{H \; (High)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **M2** (Thermal runaway in cell) — $S=10, O=2, D=8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1:} \quad S \geq 9 &\rightarrow \text{True (S=10)} \\
\therefore \text{AP}_{M2} &= \mathbf{H \; (High)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **M3** (Loose busbar connection) — $S=6, O=4, D=4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1:} \quad S \geq 9 &\rightarrow \text{False} \\
\text{Check 2:} \quad S \in \{7,8\} &\rightarrow \text{False (S=6)} \\
\text{Check 3:} \quad S \in \{5,6\}, \; O \geq 6 &\rightarrow \text{False (O=4)} \\
\text{Check 4:} \quad S \in \{5,6\}, \; O \leq 5, \; D \leq 5 &\rightarrow \text{True (S=6, O=4, D=4)} \\
\therefore \text{AP}_{M3} &= \mathbf{L \; (Low)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Final Priority Ranking**

$$
\text{M2 (S=10)} > \text{M1 (H)} > \text{M3 (L)}
$$

**Step 4: Recommended Actions**

- **M2** (AP=H, S=10): Mandatory action — install cell-level thermal fuse and BMS temperature cut-off.
- **M1** (AP=H): Add active cell-balancing circuit and end-of-line voltage monitoring.
- **M3** (AP=L): Monitor only — torque-marking already provides adequate detection.

**Note**: Observe how M2, despite having the lowest Occurrence (O=2), still receives the highest priority due to Severity ceiling. This is the central advantage of the AP method over RPN.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply**: Writing $\text{RPN} = S + O + D$ instead of $S \times O \times D$ leads to **complete loss of the calculation marks** (typically 2–3 marks).
> 2. **Skipping the Severity Override Rule**: When two RPNs tie or when Severity = 10, students ignore the override and lose **1–2 marks** on the priority-recommendation part.
> 3. **Confusing RPN with AP**: AP is **categorical (H/M/L)**, not numeric. Writing "AP = 350" will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Not stating the rating range**: Always explicitly mention "1 to 10 scale" when discussing S, O, D — examiners reward this precision.
> 5. **Missing the "Why" behind FMEA**: Pure procedural answers without context (why FMEA matters, when it is done) lose application-level marks under KTU's outcome-based evaluation.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **FMEA** = Systematic, proactive, structured group technique to identify, evaluate, and mitigate potential failure modes.
- **Three pillars of FMEA**: Severity ($S$), Occurrence ($O$), Detection ($D$), each rated **1 to 10**.
- **Traditional ranking**: $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, bounded between **1 and 1000**.
- **Modern ranking (AIAG-VDA 2019)**: Action Priority $\text{AP} \in \{H, M, L\}$ via lookup table.
- **Severity Override Rule**: Always escalate when $S \geq 9$, irrespective of RPN value.
- **Three FMEA variants**: **DFMEA** (design), **PFMEA** (process), **SFMEA** (system).
- **FMEA is a living document**: must be updated on every design, process, or supplier change.
- **Origin**: Developed by **NASA** in the 1960s for the Apollo programme; formalized by the **U.S. Department of Defense** MIL-STD-1629; later standardized by **SAE J1739** and **AIAG-VDA**.
- **10-Step workflow**: Scope → Team → Functions → Failure Modes → Effects → Causes → Rate S/O/D → Compute RPN/AP → Recommend Actions → Document and Refresh.
- **Cost-of-change principle**: A failure caught at design stage costs **~10× less** than at process stage, **~100× less** than in the field.
- **FMEA vs. FTA**: FMEA is *inductive* (failure → effect); FTA is *deductive* (effect → root cause). They are **complementary**, not substitutes.
- **Board-exam keywords** to memorize: "Risk Priority Number," "Severity Override Rule," "Action Priority," "10-1 rating scale," "Cross-Functional Team."
- **Mandatory industries**: Automotive (IATF 16949), Aerospace (AS9100D), Medical Devices (ISO 14971), Defence (MIL-STD-1629).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)** is a powerful statistical inference technique developed by **Sir Ronald Aylmer Fisher (1920s)** that is used to test the hypothesis that the means of **two or more populations are equal** by partitioning the total observed variability in a dataset into components attributable to different sources of variation.

In Quality Engineering, ANOVA is the mathematical backbone for:
- Comparing the performance of multiple machines, suppliers, processes, or operators.
- Validating whether a process change (Design of Experiments) has a statistically significant effect on the output quality characteristic.
- Testing the main effects and interaction effects of multiple factors on a response variable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Taste Test" Intuition

Imagine a food company wants to know if **3 new cake recipes** are equally rated by customers.

- **Old method (Pairwise t-test):** You would compare Recipe 1 vs 2, then 1 vs 3, then 2 vs 3. With more recipes, the number of comparisons explodes, and the probability of a **Type I error** (falsely declaring a difference) inflates drastically.
- **ANOVA method:** You bake **all 3 recipes**, ask customers to taste them, and then ask: *"Is the total variation in scores I see mostly because of the recipe (Between-Group), or because of random customer preference differences (Within-Group)?"* If the **Between-Group variation** is significantly larger than the **Within-Group variation**, at least one recipe is genuinely different.

**ANOVA does NOT directly tell you which means differ** — it tells you whether *at least one* differs. To pinpoint which one, you must follow up with post-hoc tests (Tukey's HSD, Bonferroni, etc.).

### 1.3 Why Not Just Use Multiple t-tests? (Core Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> With **k** groups, the number of pairwise t-tests is $\frac{k(k-1)}{2}$.
> For **k = 5 groups**, that is **10 tests**. At a significance level $\alpha = 0.05$, the family-wise error rate balloons to $1 - (0.95)^{10} \approx 0.40$. ANOVA controls this at exactly 0.05 in a single test.

### 1.4 Key Statistical Assumptions of ANOVA

1. **Normality:** The response variable in each group is normally distributed.
2. **Homogeneity of Variance (Homoscedasticity):** All populations have the **same variance** $\sigma^2$.
3. **Independence:** All observations are independent of each other.

> [!WARNING]
> Violating these assumptions (especially homogeneity of variance) invalidates the F-test. In such cases, use **Welch's ANOVA** or transform the data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Hypothesis Pair

For **One-Way ANOVA** with **k** groups:

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \ldots = \mu_k \quad \text{(All population means are equal)}$$

$$H_1: \mu_i \neq \mu_j \quad \text{for at least one pair } (i, j) \quad \text{(At least one mean differs)}$$

### 2.2 The Logical Decomposition — "Where Did the Variation Come From?"

The foundational logic of ANOVA rests on the **Partitioning of the Total Sum of Squares**:

$$\underbrace{SS_{Total}}_{\text{Total Variation}} = \underbrace{SS_{Between}}_{\text{Between-Group (Treatment)}} + \underbrace{SS_{Within}}_{\text{Within-Group (Error)}}$$

Where:
- $SS_{Between}$ captures variation due to the **factor/treatment effect** (explained variation).
- $SS_{Within}$ captures variation due to **random/unexplained errors** (unexplained variation).

### 2.3 Mathematical Formula Sheet (KTU High-Yield)

| **Statistic** | **Formula** | **Degrees of Freedom (df)** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Sum of Squares | $SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(x_{ij} - \bar{x}_{..})^2$ | $N - 1$ | Total variability in the dataset |
| Between-Group SS (Treatment) | $SS_B = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i(\bar{x}_{i.} - \bar{x}_{..})^2$ | $k - 1$ | Variability due to the factor |
| Within-Group SS (Error) | $SS_W = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(x_{ij} - \bar{x}_{i.})^2$ | $N - k$ | Random/unexplained variability |
| Mean Square Between | $MS_B = \frac{SS_B}{k - 1}$ | — | Variance estimate if $H_0$ is true |
| Mean Square Within | $MS_W = \frac{SS_W}{N - k}$ | — | Pooled variance estimator |
| **F-Statistic** | $F = \frac{MS_B}{MS_W}$ | $(k-1, \; N-k)$ | Test statistic compared to $F_{\alpha}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation:** $x_{ij}$ is the j-th observation in the i-th group. $\bar{x}_{i.}$ is the mean of group i. $\bar{x}_{..}$ is the **Grand Mean** (mean of all observations). $n_i$ is the sample size of group i. $N = \sum n_i$.

### 2.4 The F-Distribution — The Backbone of the Test

The **F-distribution** (named after Fisher) is a continuous, right-skewed probability distribution defined by two parameters — the numerator and denominator degrees of freedom: $df_1 = k-1$ and $df_2 = N-k$.

- **Always positive:** $F \geq 0$.
- **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calculated} > F_{\alpha, \, k-1, \, N-k}$ (right-tailed test).
- If $F_{calc} \leq 1$, $H_0$ is virtually never rejected (means are almost surely equal).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **ANOVA Application** |
|---|---|
| Manufacturing | Compare the average tensile strength of steel from 4 different suppliers |
| Six Sigma | Test if cycle time differs across 3 shifts in a plant |
| Design of Experiments (DOE) | Validate the main effect of 5 factors on product yield |
| Service Quality | Compare customer satisfaction across 4 branch offices |
| Pharmaceuticals | Test if drug efficacy differs across 3 dosage levels |

### 2.6 Extension — Two-Way ANOVA (Brief Overview)

When **two categorical factors** influence the response, Two-Way ANOVA decomposes the variation into **three** components:

$$SS_T = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E$$

Where $SS_A$ (Factor A), $SS_B$ (Factor B), $SS_{AB}$ (Interaction effect), and $SS_E$ (Error). Each gets its own F-test.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — The 3-Machine Production Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Standard Format):**
> A quality engineer suspects that **3 different machines** (M1, M2, M3) produce parts with different average hardness (in HRC units). To verify, **4 parts** are sampled from each machine. The hardness readings are tabulated below. At $\alpha = 0.05$, test whether the mean hardness differs across machines.

| Observation # | Machine 1 (M1) | Machine 2 (M2) | Machine 3 (M3) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 62 | 60 | 63 |
| 2 | 60 | 59 | 64 |
| 3 | 63 | 61 | 62 |
| 4 | 61 | 58 | 65 |

**Given:** $k = 3$ groups, $n_1 = n_2 = n_3 = 4$, $N = 12$.

---

### Step 1: State the Hypotheses

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 \quad \text{(All machines produce equal mean hardness)}$$

$$H_1: \text{At least one } \mu_i \text{ is different}$$

---

### Step 2: Compute the Group Means and the Grand Mean

Group sums and means:

$$T_1 = 62 + 60 + 63 + 61 = 246 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{x}_{1.} = \frac{246}{4} = 61.5$$

$$T_2 = 60 + 59 + 61 + 58 = 238 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{x}_{2.} = \frac{238}{4} = 59.5$$

$$T_3 = 63 + 64 + 62 + 65 = 254 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{x}_{3.} = \frac{254}{4} = 63.5$$

Grand total and grand mean:

$$T_{..} = 246 + 238 + 254 = 738$$

$$\bar{x}_{..} = \frac{738}{12} = 61.5$$

---

### Step 3: Compute the Correction Factor (CF)

$$CF = \frac{T_{..}^2}{N} = \frac{(738)^2}{12} = \frac{544644}{12} = 45387$$

---

### Step 4: Compute the Total Sum of Squares ($SS_T$)

$$SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} x_{ij}^2 - CF$$

First, compute $\sum x_{ij}^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_{ij}^2 &= (62^2 + 60^2 + 63^2 + 61^2) + (60^2 + 59^2 + 61^2 + 58^2) \\
&\quad + (63^2 + 64^2 + 62^2 + 65^2) \\
&= (3844 + 3600 + 3969 + 3721) + (3600 + 3481 + 3721 + 3364) \\
&\quad + (3969 + 4096 + 3844 + 4225) \\
&= 15134 + 14166 + 16134 \\
&= 45434
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$SS_T = 45434 - 45387 = 47$$

---

### Step 5: Compute the Between-Group Sum of Squares ($SS_B$)

$$SS_B = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{T_i^2}{n_i} - CF$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_B &= \frac{(246)^2}{4} + \frac{(238)^2}{4} + \frac{(254)^2}{4} - 45387 \\
&= \frac{60516}{4} + \frac{56644}{4} + \frac{64516}{4} - 45387 \\
&= 15129 + 14161 + 16129 - 45387 \\
&= 45419 - 45387 \\
&= 32
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6: Compute the Within-Group Sum of Squares ($SS_W$)

By the identity $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$:

$$SS_W = SS_T - SS_B = 47 - 32 = 15$$

---

### Step 7: Construct the ANOVA Table

| **Source of Variation** | **SS** | **df** | **MS** | **F-ratio** | **F-critical ($\alpha=0.05$)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Between Groups (Machines) | 32 | $k - 1 = 2$ | $\frac{32}{2} = 16.0$ | $\frac{16.0}{1.667} = 9.60$ | $F_{0.05, 2, 9} = 4.26$ |
| Within Groups (Error) | 15 | $N - k = 9$ | $\frac{15}{9} = 1.667$ | — | — |
| **Total** | **47** | **11** | — | — | — |

**Verification:** $MS_W$ calculated as $\frac{15}{9} = 1.6667$ (rounded to 1.667). $F_{calc} = \frac{16.0}{1.667} \approx 9.60$.

---

### Step 8: Decision Rule and Conclusion

Compare the calculated F-statistic with the critical value:

$$F_{calc} = 9.60 \quad > \quad F_{crit} = 4.26$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Since $F_{calc} > F_{crit}$, we **reject the null hypothesis $H_0$** at the 5% significance level. There is **sufficient statistical evidence** to conclude that the **mean hardness produced by at least one of the three machines is significantly different** from the others. The Quality Engineer should now investigate which specific machine(s) is/are responsible — typically via **Tukey's HSD post-hoc test**.

**Practical Engineering Implication:** Machine 2 (mean = 59.5) appears to be the underperformer, producing softer parts than M1 (61.5) and M3 (63.5). A corrective maintenance audit on M2 is warranted.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of ANOVA Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Research Question with 3+ Groups"] --> B{"Does data meet\nNormality and Equal\nVariance assumptions?"}
    B -- "No" --> C["Use Welch ANOVA\nor Kruskal-Wallis"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D["Compute Group Means\nand Grand Mean"]
    D --> E["Calculate CF, SSB, SSW, SST"]
    E --> F["Compute MSB and MSW"]
    F --> G["Calculate F-statistic\nF = MSB divided by MSW"]
    G --> H["Determine df1 = k-1\nand df2 = N-k"]
    H --> I["Look up F-critical\nfrom F-distribution table"]
    I --> J{"Is F calculated\ngreater than F critical?"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Reject H0\nAt least one mean differs\nRun Post-hoc Test"]
    J -- "No" --> L["Fail to Reject H0\nAll means are statistically\nequal at alpha level"]
    K --> M["Identify specific\ndiffering groups\nvia Tukey HSD"]
    L --> N["Document Conclusion\nand Proceed"]
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 The Partitioning Tree of Total Variation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["SS Total - Total Observed Variability - df = N minus 1"] --> node2["SS Between - Explained Variation - df = k minus 1"]
    node1 --> node3["SS Within - Unexplained Error - df = N minus k"]
    node2 --> node2a["Measures how much\ngroup means deviate\nfrom grand mean"]
    node3 --> node3a["Measures random\nfluctuation within\neach group"]
    node2a --> msb["MSB = SSB divided by k-1"]
    node3a --> msw["MSW = SSW divided by N-k"]
    msb --> fratio["F = MSB divided by MSW"]
    msw --> fratio
    fratio --> dec["If F is large,\ngroup differences are\nreal and significant"]
```

### 4.3 F-Distribution Decision Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regionA["Region of Acceptance\nF is between 0 and F critical"]
    regionB["Region of Rejection\nF is greater than F critical"]
    nodeFcrit["Critical Value F alpha at df1, df2"]
    nodeFcalc["Calculated F from data"]
    nodeFcrit --> regionA
    nodeFcrit --> regionB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The F-distribution is **right-skewed**. The critical value $F_{\alpha}$ cuts the right tail, leaving a small area $\alpha$ (e.g., 0.05) under the curve. If your calculated F-statistic falls inside this shaded tail, the between-group variation is too large to be explained by random chance alone — so you reject $H_0$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define Analysis of Variance (ANOVA). State the null and alternative hypotheses for one-way ANOVA with k populations.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** ANOVA is a statistical technique developed by **R.A. Fisher** used to test whether the means of **three or more populations** are significantly different, by partitioning the total variation in the data into variation *between* groups and variation *within* groups, and comparing them via the **F-distribution**.
>
> **Hypotheses (1 Mark):**
> $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 = \ldots = \mu_k$ (all population means are equal)
> $H_1:$ At least one $\mu_i \neq \mu_j$ (at least one mean differs).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Explain the meaning of the F-statistic in ANOVA. What does a large value of F indicate?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The F-statistic is defined as the ratio of two variance estimates: $F = \frac{MS_B}{MS_W}$, where $MS_B$ is the **Mean Square Between groups** and $MS_W$ is the **Mean Square Within groups**. Both are estimates of the common population variance $\sigma^2$ under $H_0$.
A **large F-value** (greater than the critical value $F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$) indicates that the **between-group variation is significantly larger than the within-group variation**, meaning the differences observed between group means are too large to be attributed to random chance. This leads to **rejection of $H_0$** at the chosen significance level.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (Module Choice: Answer ANY ONE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Rule: For Module 4 questions, students must answer **EITHER Question A OR Question B** in full (14 marks each). Both must include sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the fundamental assumptions underlying one-way ANOVA. Discuss the consequences of violating the homogeneity of variance assumption. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company tests 4 formulations of a drug on patient recovery time (in days). Five patients are tested per formulation. Using the data below, perform a one-way ANOVA at $\alpha = 0.05$ and state your conclusion. *(7 Marks)*

| Patient # | Formulation A | Formulation B | Formulation C | Formulation D |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 8 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| 2 | 9 | 7 | 10 | 6 |
| 3 | 7 | 5 | 8 | 8 |
| 4 | 8 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| 5 | 8 | 6 | 9 | 7 |

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

> **Assumptions of One-Way ANOVA (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Normality:** The response variable in each group is approximately normally distributed.
> 2. **Homogeneity of Variance:** All $k$ populations have the same variance $\sigma^2$.
> 3. **Independence:** Observations are independent across groups and within groups.
>
> **Consequences of Violating Homogeneity (2 Marks):**
> - The F-test becomes **unreliable** — the Type I error rate is no longer maintained at $\alpha$.
> - The pooled $MS_W$ estimate of variance is distorted.
>
> **Remedies (2 Marks):**
> - Use **Welch's ANOVA** (robust to unequal variances).
> - **Transform** the data (log, square root).
> - Use non-parametric alternative: **Kruskal-Wallis test**.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Group Sums:**
$T_A = 8+9+7+8+8 = 40$, $T_B = 6+7+5+6+6 = 30$, $T_C = 9+10+8+9+9 = 45$, $T_D = 7+6+8+7+7 = 35$

Grand total $T_{..} = 40+30+45+35 = 150$; $N = 20$.

**Step 2 — Correction Factor:**
$CF = \frac{(150)^2}{20} = \frac{22500}{20} = 1125$

**Step 3 — Total SS:**
$\sum x^2 = (64+81+49+64+64) + (36+49+25+36+36) + (81+100+64+81+81) + (49+36+64+49+49)$
$= 322 + 182 + 407 + 247 = 1158$
$SS_T = 1158 - 1125 = 33$

**Step 4 — Between SS:**
$SS_B = \frac{40^2}{5} + \frac{30^2}{5} + \frac{45^2}{5} + \frac{35^2}{5} - 1125 = 320 + 180 + 405 + 245 - 1125 = 1150 - 1125 = 25$

**Step 5 — Within SS:**
$SS_W = SS_T - SS_B = 33 - 25 = 8$

**Step 6 — ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | F-crit ($\alpha=0.05$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Between | 25 | 3 | 8.333 | **17.71** | $F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$ |
| Within | 8 | 16 | 0.500 | — | — |
| Total | 33 | 19 | — | — | — |

**Step 7 — Conclusion (2 Marks):**
Since $F_{calc} = 17.71 \gg F_{crit} = 3.24$, we **reject $H_0$**. At $\alpha = 0.05$, there is strong evidence that the **mean recovery time differs significantly across the 4 drug formulations**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Distinguish between **One-Way ANOVA** and **Two-Way ANOVA**. When would you prefer the latter in a manufacturing context? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** In a textile factory, a Quality Engineer tests the effect of **2 types of cotton blends** (Blend X, Blend Y) and **3 dyeing temperatures** (60°C, 80°C, 100°C) on fabric tensile strength. Two replicates are taken per cell. The following interaction effects are observed. Set up the **Two-Way ANOVA framework** with the relevant null hypotheses for each factor and interaction. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

| **Aspect** | **One-Way ANOVA** | **Two-Way ANOVA** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Number of Factors | One categorical independent variable | Two categorical independent variables |
| Source Decomposition | $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$ | $SS_T = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E$ |
| Hypotheses Tested | 1 main effect | 2 main effects + 1 interaction effect |
| Use Case | Comparing 3+ machine outputs | Studying blend × temperature effect on strength |

**Manufacturing Preference (3 Marks):** Two-Way ANOVA is preferred when the engineer suspects that **two process parameters** (e.g., pressure and temperature) jointly influence the output, and wants to test both their **individual main effects** and their **synergistic interaction effect** simultaneously — saving experimental cost and time.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Model (2 Marks):**
The model is: $x_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$
where $\alpha_i$ = effect of blend, $\beta_j$ = effect of temperature, $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ = interaction effect.

**Step 2 — State Three Null Hypotheses (3 Marks):**

$$H_{01}: \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = 0 \quad \text{(Blend has no effect on strength)}$$

$$H_{02}: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta_3 = 0 \quad \text{(Temperature has no effect on strength)}$$

$$H_{03}: (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0 \quad \text{for all } i,j \quad \text{(No interaction between blend and temperature)}$$

**Step 3 — Set Up the ANOVA Table Structure (2 Marks):**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Blend (A) | $SS_A$ | $a-1 = 1$ | $MS_A = SS_A/1$ | $F_A = MS_A/MS_E$ |
| Temperature (B) | $SS_B$ | $b-1 = 2$ | $MS_B = SS_B/2$ | $F_B = MS_B/MS_E$ |
| Interaction (AB) | $SS_{AB}$ | $(a-1)(b-1) = 2$ | $MS_{AB} = SS_{AB}/2$ | $F_{AB} = MS_{AB}/MS_E$ |
| Error | $SS_E$ | $ab(n-1) = 6$ | $MS_E = SS_E/6$ | — |
| Total | $SS_T$ | $N-1 = 11$ | — | — |

Each F-statistic is compared to its respective $F_{\alpha}$ with appropriate degrees of freedom.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the Hypothesis Statement** — Always write $H_0$ and $H_1$ explicitly at the start. (2 marks lost if omitted.)
> 2. **Arithmetic in CF, SSB, SSW** — The Correction Factor is $\frac{T_{..}^2}{N}$. Many students incorrectly compute $\frac{T_{..}}{N}$. Double-check division.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$** — KTU examiners award 1 mark as a "consistency check." Always verify.
> 4. **Wrong degrees of freedom** — Common mistake: writing $df_W = N-1$ instead of $N-k$. Memorize the table structure.
> 5. **Comparing with the wrong F-critical value** — Always use $F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$, NOT $F_{\alpha, N-k, k-1}$. Order matters!
> 6. **Concluding "all means differ"** — ANOVA only tells you at least one differs. Never claim ALL means are different. That requires post-hoc tests.
> 7. **No engineering interpretation** — Always end with a **practical conclusion** in the manufacturing/quality context. KTU rewards applied thinking.

---

### 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — ANOVA**

- ⭐ **ANOVA** tests equality of **3 or more** population means simultaneously using a **single F-test**.
- ⭐ The Total Sum of Squares partitions into **Between-Group** and **Within-Group** components: $SS_T = SS_B + SS_W$.
- ⭐ The **F-statistic** is the ratio $F = \frac{MS_B}{MS_W}$, with degrees of freedom $(k-1, \, N-k)$.
- ⭐ **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$ (right-tailed test on F-distribution).
