# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define Kaizen. State any four key principles that distinguish Kaizen from Innovation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Kaizen (改善) is a Japanese management philosophy, popularised by **Masaaki Imai (1986)**, meaning *"continuous improvement through small, incremental, employee-driven changes"*. It originated in the **Toyota Production System**.

**Four distinguishing principles:**

1. **Incremental vs Radical** — Kaizen uses small, frequent changes; Innovation uses large, disruptive breakthroughs.
2. **Employee-driven vs Top-down** — Kaizen originates from shop-floor workers; Innovation is driven by R&D and senior management.
3. **Low-cost vs High-cost** — Kaizen suggestions typically cost very little; Innovation demands heavy capital investment.
4. **Continuous vs Episodic** — Kaizen is an everyday, ongoing mindset; Innovation occurs in discrete project cycles.

> **Marking Key**: Award 1 mark for the definition and 0.5 mark each for the four principles (max 3 marks).

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the 5S methodology with its Japanese and English equivalents. Why is 5S considered the foundation of Kaizen?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Japanese | English | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1S | 整理 (*Seiri*) | **Sort** | Remove unnecessary items |
| 2S | 整頓 (*Seiton*) | **Set in Order** | Arrange essentials for easy retrieval |
| 3S | 清掃 (*Seiso*) | **Shine** | Clean the workplace daily |
| 4S | 清潔 (*Seiketsu*) | **Standardise** | Make 1S–3S repeatable and visual |
| 5S | 躾 (*Shitsuke*) | **Sustain** | Train and embed self-discipline |

**Why 5S is the foundation of Kaizen:**
- A clean, organised workplace **exposes problems** (leaks, defects, waste) that would otherwise remain hidden.
- 5S provides the **visual baseline** without which no improvement can be reliably measured.
- It cultivates the **discipline and ownership** mindset necessary for every Kaizen suggestion to thrive.

> **Marking Key**: Award 1 mark for the 5S table, 1 mark for Japanese names, 1 mark for the "why foundation" reasoning.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**: Students most-commonly lose marks on Kaizen questions by:
> 1. ❌ Writing generic definitions without the **Japanese terminology (Kai/Zen, 5S terms, TIMWOODS)**.
> 2. ❌ Confusing **Kaizen with Innovation** in contradictory phrasing.
> 3. ❌ Skipping the **PDCA cycle** — examiners treat this as essential in any 14-marker.
> 4. ❌ Failing to provide a **worked example** or **analytical walk-through** with numbers.
> 5. ❌ Ignoring **Gemba** and the **Three Ms (Muda, Mura, Muri)**.
>
> To secure **full 14 marks**, your answer MUST contain: definition + 5S/PDCA + seven wastes + worked analytical example + comparison table.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **Kaizen philosophy** in detail. Discuss the **PDCA (Deming) cycle** and describe how it operationalises continuous improvement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Identify and explain the **seven wastes (TIMWOODS)** of the Toyota Production System. For any **three wastes**, provide a real-world engineering example and a corresponding Kaizen countermeasure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**

Kaizen, coined by **Masaaki Imai (1986)**, is a Japanese management philosophy centred on **continuous, small, incremental improvements** involving **every employee from CEO to shop-floor**. The literal meaning is *"change for the better"* (改 = change, 善 = good).

**Core Philosophy (2 marks):**

- **Process-orientation**: Improvement targets processes, not people.
- **Customer-focus**: Every improvement must add **value** to the customer.
- **Respect for people**: Workers are problem-solvers, not cogs.
- **Long-term horizon**: Improvement is a marathon, not a sprint.
- **Low-cost, low-risk**: Many ₹500 suggestions beat one ₹5 crore project.

**PDCA Cycle (4 marks):**

The **Deming Wheel** operationalises Kaizen as a four-step iterative loop:

$$
\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{Plan} \rightarrow \cdots
$$

| Step | Activity | Kaizen Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Define problem, analyse root cause (5-Whys, Fishbone), draft hypothesis | Countermeasure plan |
| **Do** | Pilot on small scale, document observations | Real-world data |
| **Check** | Compare actual vs. expected results statistically | Validated learning |
| **Act** | Standardise via SOP if successful; restart cycle if not | New baseline |

The cycle creates a **continuous upward spiral** — each rotation lifts the baseline, ensuring perpetual improvement.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b)**

**The Seven Wastes (3 marks):**

| # | Waste | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| T | Transportation | Moving materials unnecessarily |
| I | Inventory | Excess stock of any kind |
| M | Motion | Unnecessary human movement |
| W | Waiting | Idle time of men/machines |
| O | Overproduction | Producing more than required |
| O | Over-processing | Adding features beyond customer needs |
| D | Defects | Rework, scrap, warranty claims |

**Worked Examples with Countermeasures (4 marks):**

| Waste | Engineering Example | Kaizen Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| **Defects** | 12% rejection in welding line | Implement **Poka-Yoke** fixture + SOP; reduce defects to 1.5% |
| **Waiting** | CNC machine idle for 45 min between jobs | Deploy **Heijunka** level scheduling + SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) |
| **Motion** | Operator walking 80 m/shift to fetch tools | Apply **5S Set-in-Order** — shadow board within arm's reach; motion ↓ 90% |
| **Inventory** | ₹2 crore of WIP piled between stations | Implement **Kanban** pull system; WIP ↓ 60% |

> **Concluding remark (embedded)**: Eliminating these wastes directly improves **OEE**, **FPY**, and **COPQ** — the three Kaizen-aligned KPIs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Describe the **5S methodology**. For each S, give one practical engineering example and explain how 5S supports the **Kaizen philosophy**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable **worked example**, demonstrate how the **5-Whys technique** is applied to solve a quality problem in a manufacturing process. Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a)**

**5S Methodology — Detailed Walk-Through (7 marks):**

| # | Japanese (Romaji) | English | Practical Engineering Example | Kaizen Link |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1S** | 整理 (*Seiri*) | **Sort** | Remove broken tools from CNC cell; tag "Red Label" for disposal | Removes **Muda** of clutter |
| **2S** | 整頓 (*Seiton*) | **Set in Order** | Paint floor markings; install shadow-board for spanners | Enables visual management |
| **3S** | 清掃 (*Seiso*) | **Shine** | Daily 10-min cleaning of injection-moulding machine | Detects **oil leaks early** |
| **4S** | 清潔 (*Seiketsu*) | **Standardise** | Document SOP with photographs; colour-coded bins | Makes 1S–3S repeatable |
| **5S** | 躾 (*Shitsuke*) | **Sustain** | Monthly 5S audit with scorecard; recognise best cell | Builds **discipline culture** |

**5S as Foundation of Kaizen:**
- 5S **exposes hidden waste** — once the floor is clean, problems are visible.
- 5S establishes the **baseline discipline** without which Kaizen suggestions would never be implemented.
- 5S delivers **quick wins** in 1–2 weeks, building organisational buy-in for deeper Kaizen.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b)**

**5-Whys on a Manufacturing Quality Problem (7 marks):**

**Scenario**: An aluminium extrusion plant is producing **surface scratches** on 18% of output. KTU target is < 2%.

**Step 1 — Identify the symptom**

$$
\text{Defect Rate} = \frac{180}{1000} \times 100\,\% = 18\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 5-Whys**

| Why # | Question | Answer |
|---|---|---|
| **Why 1** | Why are surfaces scratched? | Billet rubbing against die-entry guide |
| **Why 2** | Why is billet rubbing? | Misalignment of entry guide (tolerance > 2 mm) |
| **Why 3** | Why is alignment off? | Guide bolts loosen due to vibration |
| **Why 4** | Why are bolts loosening? | No preventive maintenance schedule for guide alignment |
| **Why 5** | Why no PM schedule? | Maintenance checklist does not include guide alignment |

**Root Cause**: *Absence of preventive-maintenance SOP* — a systemic, addressable cause.

**Step 3 — Countermeasure**
- Create **PM-SOP-014**: weekly alignment check using dial indicator.
- Replace bolts with **nord-lock washers** (vibration-proof).
- Conduct **Kaizen audit** every fortnight.

**Step 4 — Verification**

$$
\text{Defect Rate}_{\text{after}} = \frac{15}{1000} \times 100\,\% = 1.5\,\%
$$

$$
\text{Improvement} = \frac{18 - 1.5}{18} \times 100\,\% \approx 91.7\,\%
$$

**Limitations of 5-Whys (2 marks embedded):**
1. **Single-cause bias** — assumes one linear chain, but real problems often have **multiple parallel root causes**.
2. **Expertise dependency** — requires deep domain knowledge; naive "whys" may stop at human error.
3. **Not statistically rigorous** — qualitative reasoning, not quantitative root-cause analysis.
4. **Blaming culture risk** — if not carefully facilitated, may devolve into finger-pointing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist for rapid recall before the exam.**

- ✅ **Kaizen (改善)** = *Change for the Better*; coined by **Masaaki Imai (1986)**.
- ✅ Originated in **Toyota Production System (TPS)**.
- ✅ **Incremental + employee-driven + low-cost + continuous**.
- ✅ **PDCA Cycle** = Plan → Do → Check → Act → (loop). Also called **Deming Wheel**.
- ✅ **5S** = Seiri (Sort), Seiton (Set in Order), Seiso (Shine), Seiketsu (Standardise), Shitsuke (Sustain).
- ✅ **Three Ms** = **Muda** (waste), **Mura** (unevenness), **Muri** (overburden).
- ✅ **Seven Wastes (TIMWOODS)** = Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects (Skills = 8th modern waste).
- ✅ **Gemba** = the actual place where work happens.
- ✅ **Kaizen Event (Blitz)** = 3-to-5-day focused improvement workshop.
- ✅ **Tools frequently tested**: 5-Whys, Fishbone (Ishikawa), Pareto, 5S, Poka-Yoke, Kanban, Heijunka, Jidoka, SMED, VSM.
- ✅ **Quantitative KPIs**: OEE = $A \times P \times Q$; FPY; RTY; DPMO; COPQ; ROI of Kaizen.
- ✅ **Kaizen ≠ Innovation** — Kaizen is *incremental grass-roots*; Innovation is *radical top-down R&D*.
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke** = mistake-proofing (zero-defect enabler).
- ✅ **Suggestion system** = bottom-up employee idea engine; Toyota saves ₹10,000+ crore/year from it.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam-Strategy Reminder**: In every KTU 2024 answer, use the **Japanese terms in italics with Romaji pronunciation**, draw at least **one comparative table**, walk through **one numerical example with explicit formulas**, and conclude with a **process/strategic statement**. This four-part structure is what examiners in the 2024 scheme consistently reward with full marks.

topics.title: Six Sigma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Six Sigma

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — SIX SIGMA
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Sigma** is a **disciplined, data-driven, statistical methodology** aimed at eliminating defects (non-conformities), reducing process variation, and improving quality to a benchmark of **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**, which corresponds to a process operating at **±6 standard deviations (±6σ) from the mean**, allowing for a **1.5σ long-term process shift**.

Six Sigma was pioneered by **Engineer Bill Smith** at **Motorola Inc.** in **1986**, popularized globally after **General Electric (GE)** under **Jack Welch** adopted it in 1995 and reportedly saved over **$12 billion** in the first five years.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin & Institutional History (High-Yield KTU Point):**
> - **1986** – Bill Smith (Motorola) introduces Six Sigma.
> - **1987** – Motorola launches the "Six Sigma Quality" program.
> - **1995** – Jack Welch adopts it at GE; term becomes a global standard.
> - **Today** – Mandatory methodology in manufacturing, BPO, IT, healthcare, and service sectors.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of **Six Sigma like a championship archer** 🏹 hitting the bullseye:

| Archery Concept | Six Sigma Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Bullseye (target centre) | Customer requirement / specification |
| The arrow's path | Process output |
| Spread of arrows on the target | Process variation |
| Arrows missing the target | Defects / non-conformities |
| Practice & steady posture | Statistical Process Control (SPC) |
| Wind / movement disturbances | Common & Special cause variation |

A casual archer may hit the target 80% of the time (≈ **3σ to 4σ quality**). An elite Olympic archer hits it **99.99966%** of the time — that is **Six Sigma quality**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Students:** *"Six Sigma = Surgical precision in every transaction."*

---

### 1.3 Why Sigma?

The Greek letter **σ (sigma)** represents **standard deviation**, a statistical measure of variation. The higher the sigma level, the lower the variability, and the closer the process operates to **near-zero defects**.

### 1.4 Strategic Goals of Six Sigma

1. Reduce process variation (defect prevention rather than detection).
2. Improve yield and reduce cost of poor quality (COPQ).
3. Achieve customer satisfaction through defect-free products/services.
4. Build a culture of continuous improvement and data-based decision making.
5. Generate sustainable, measurable financial returns to the bottom line.

---

### 1.5 The 1.5σ Shift (Why the Benchmark is 3.4 DPMO, not Zero)

The statistical quality level of **±6σ** mathematically implies **0.002 DPMO** (truly zero defects). However, real-world processes experience a **mean shift of up to ±1.5σ** over time due to:

- Gradual tool wear
- Environmental drift (temperature, humidity)
- Operator fatigue and shift changes
- Maintenance variation

After accounting for this **±1.5σ long-term shift**, the achievable defect rate becomes **3.4 DPMO**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 1.1 — Sigma Level vs. DPMO (Long-Term, with 1.5σ shift)**

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.1% |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32% |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.379% |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.9767% |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966%** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Six Sigma Methodologies

Six Sigma projects are executed using one of two structured frameworks:

### A) DMAIC — For Existing Processes (Improve)

| Phase | Full Form | Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **D** | **Define** | Identify the problem, customer (CTQ — Critical-to-Quality), project scope, and goals. |
| **M** | **Measure** | Quantify the current performance; establish baseline; map the process. |
| **A** | **Analyze** | Identify the **root causes** of variation and defects using statistical tools. |
| **I** | **Improve** | Develop, pilot, and implement solutions to remove root causes. |
| **C** | **Control** | Standardize improvements using SPC, FMEA, control plans, and documentation. |

### B) DMADV — For New Processes / Products (Design for Six Sigma / DFSS)

| Phase | Full Form | Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **D** | **Define** | Define design goals aligned to customer needs and strategy. |
| **M** | **Measure** | Measure CTQs, identify capabilities, and benchmark. |
| **A** | **Analyze** | Analyze design alternatives; develop high-level design. |
| **D** | **Design** | Develop detailed design; optimize using DOE. |
| **V** | **Verify** | Pilot, validate, and roll out the design. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** *"DMAIC = Fix existing processes. DMADV = Design new ones from scratch."*

---

## 2.2 Six Sigma Roles & Belts System (Master–Sub Master Architecture)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Six Sigma uses a **martial-arts inspired belt hierarchy** to define expertise:

| Belt / Role | Responsibility | Typical Profile |
|---|---|---|
| **Executive Leadership** | Sets vision, allocates budget | CEO / VP |
| **Champions** | Sponsor projects, break silos | Senior Manager |
| **Master Black Belt (MBB)** | Trains Black Belts, expert statistician, mentor | Full-time, technical authority |
| **Black Belt (BB)** | Leads 4–6 projects full-time, applies DMAIC | Full-time project leader |
| **Green Belt (GB)** | Leads small projects part-time, supports BBs | Part-time practitioner |
| **Yellow Belt** | Project team member, basic Six Sigma awareness | Operational staff |
| **White Belt** | Fundamental awareness only | All employees |

---

## 2.3 Statistical Foundation & Z-Score Theory

Six Sigma relies on the **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution**:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = process mean
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation
- **Z-score** = distance from mean in units of σ:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

A defect occurs when an output falls **outside** the specification limits (USL / LSL).

$$\text{Yield} = P(LSL \leq X \leq USL)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU ESE — Module 4**

| # | Formula | Purpose / Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | $D$ = defects, $U$ = units, $O$ = opportunities per unit |
| 2 | $\text{Defect Rate} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O}$ | Probability of a single defect |
| 3 | $\text{Yield} = 1 - \text{DPMO} \times 10^{-6}$ | First-pass yield at the unit level |
| 4 | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Rolled Throughput Yield (multi-step processes) |
| 5 | $\text{Normal Yield} = \Phi(USL) - \Phi(LSL)$ | Area under normal curve within spec limits |
| 6 | $\text{Process}\sigma = Z + 1.5$ | Long-term sigma with mean shift correction |
| 7 | $Z = \Phi^{-1}(\text{Yield})$ | Inverse normal (NORMSINV in Excel) |
| 8 | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Cost of Poor Quality (Taguchi/Feigenbaum) |
| 9 | $\text{Process Capability } C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability (centred process) |
| 10 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Actual capability (shifted process) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Symbol Restriction:** Vertical bars $\vert$ have been replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ in formulas like $\Phi^{-1}\!\left(\text{Yield}\right)$ to comply with Markdown-safe notation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility & Engineering Applications

| Industry | Six Sigma Application |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Defect reduction in PCB assembly, automotive painting |
| **Healthcare** | Reducing surgical errors, medication mistakes |
| **IT / Software** | Reducing bug density, improving code release cycle |
| **BPO / Services** | First Call Resolution, Average Handling Time (AHT) |
| **Banking** | Loan processing turnaround, transaction error rate |
| **Aerospace** | Zero-defect tolerance in critical components |

---

## 2.6 Six Sigma vs TQM vs ISO 9000

| Parameter | TQM | Six Sigma | ISO 9000 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Customer satisfaction | Process capability & defects | Documentation & compliance |
| **Approach** | Cultural / people-centric | Statistical / data-driven | Standards-based |
| **Origin** | Deming, Juran, Feigenbaum | Motorola (1986) | ISO (1987) |
| **Metric** | Customer satisfaction index | DPMO / Sigma level | Conformance to standard |
| **Toolkit** | Soft tools (Pareto, fishbone) | Statistical (DOE, regression) | Audits, procedures |
| **Certification** | None | Belt system | Yes (ISO 9001) |

> [!NOTE]
> Six Sigma **complements** TQM and ISO 9000; it does not replace them. Modern firms adopt all three under an Integrated Management System (IMS).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — DPMO & Sigma Level Calculation

> **Problem:** A printed circuit board (PCB) manufacturing line produced **180 units** in one shift. Each unit has **100 soldering opportunities** for defects. The quality inspector recorded **45 defects** in total. Calculate:
> 1. DPMO
> 2. Yield
> 3. Process Sigma Level (with 1.5σ shift)

---

### Step 1 — Calculate Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$$

Substitute $D = 45$, $U = 180$, $O = 100$:

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{45 \times 10^{6}}{180 \times 100}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{45 \times 10^{6}}{18{,}000}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = 2{,}500 \; \text{DPMO}$$

---

### Step 2 — Calculate First-Pass Yield

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}$$

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{2{,}500}{1{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 99.75\%$$

---

### Step 3 — Convert Yield to Z-Score (Short-Term Capability)

Using the **inverse standard normal** (Excel: `=NORMSINV(0.9975)`):

$$Z_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}(0.9975) \approx 2.81$$

---

### Step 4 — Apply the 1.5σ Long-Term Shift Correction

$$\text{Process}\sigma = Z_{ST} + 1.5$$

$$\text{Process}\sigma = 2.81 + 1.5 = 4.31\sigma$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The PCB line is operating at approximately **4.31σ quality** — acceptable but short of the Six Sigma benchmark of **3.4 DPMO / 6σ**. The project should target reducing variability to climb from 4.31σ → 5σ → 6σ.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

A 4-stage assembly process has the following per-stage yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= 0.97 \\
Y_2 &= 0.95 \\
Y_3 &= 0.99 \\
Y_4 &= 0.92
\end{aligned}$$

Calculate **Rolled Throughput Yield** and the **total defects allowed at 6σ**.

---

### Step 1 — Apply the RTY Formula

$$\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 \times 0.92$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.8388 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 83.88\%$$

---

### Step 2 — Calculate the Scrap Cost

$$\text{Scrap} = 1 - \text{RTY} = 1 - 0.8388 = 0.1612 \;\; (16.12\%)$$

> [!TIP]
> RTY exposes the **"hidden factory"** — defects lost across multiple stages. A process can have a great per-stage yield (e.g., 97%) but a terrible end-to-end yield (83.88%).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — From Yield to Sigma Level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Conversion Table (Excel `=NORMSINV` based)**

| Yield (%) | DPMO | Short-Term Z | Process σ (Z + 1.5) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 99.0 | 10,000 | 2.326 | 3.83 |
| 99.5 | 5,000 | 2.576 | 4.08 |
| 99.73 | 2,700 | 3.000 | 4.50 |
| 99.9 | 1,000 | 3.090 | 4.59 |
| 99.99 | 100 | 3.719 | 5.22 |
| 99.999 | 10 | 4.265 | 5.77 |
| **99.99966** | **3.4** | **4.500** | **6.00** |

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Why Six Sigma Means 3.4 DPMO

The **area under the right tail** of the standard normal beyond $Z = 4.5\sigma$ gives the short-term defect probability:

$$P(Z > 4.5) = 1 - \Phi(4.5)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi(4.5) &\approx 0.9999966 \\
1 - \Phi(4.5) &\approx 3.4 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 3.4 \; \text{DPMO}
\end{aligned}$$

With the **±1.5σ shift** model, the long-term defect rate equals the right-tail probability above $Z = 6 - 1.5 = 4.5$, confirming **3.4 DPMO**.

> [!WARNING]
> Some textbooks (Harry & Schroeder) defend the 1.5σ shift as empirical; pure mathematicians argue it is an industry convention. KTU accepts the **1.5σ shift convention** for all calculations.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Process Capability Indices

A shaft diameter specification: **LSL = 24.90 mm, USL = 25.10 mm**. The process mean $\mu = 25.02$ mm, $\sigma = 0.02$ mm.

### Step 1 — Calculate $C_p$ (Potential Capability)

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.02}$$

$$C_p = \frac{0.20}{0.12} = 1.667$$

### Step 2 — Calculate $C_{pk}$ (Actual Capability)

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\frac{25.10 - 25.02}{0.06}, \frac{25.02 - 24.90}{0.06}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(1.333, 2.000\right) \\
&= 1.333
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** $C_p = 1.667$ indicates the *spread* is acceptable. $C_{pk} = 1.333$ is lower because the mean is **off-centre** (closer to USL). A Six Sigma target requires $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ (i.e., 6σ-capable).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Six Sigma DMAIC Master Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DEFINE\nIdentify CTQ, Voice of Customer\nProject Charter, SIPOC"]
    nodeB["MEASURE\nBaseline Data, MSA, FMEA\nProcess Capability Cpk"]
    nodeC["ANALYZE\nHypothesis Testing, Regression\nPareto, Ishikawa 5-Why"]
    nodeD["IMPROVE\nDOE, Pilot Runs, Kaizen\nSolution Selection Matrix"]
    nodeE["CONTROL\nControl Plans, SPC Charts\nMistake Proofing Poka Yoke"]
    nodeF["PROJECT CLOSURE\nDocument Savings, Lessons Learned"]
    nodeG["CUSTOMER VALUE"]
    nodeH["DEFECT REDUCTION"]

    nodeA ==> nodeB
    nodeB ==> nodeC
    nodeC ==> nodeD
    nodeD ==> nodeE
    nodeE ==> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each phase builds on the previous one. Skipping a phase is the **#1 reason Six Sigma projects fail** in industry.

---

## 4.2 Six Sigma Organisation / Belt Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    role1["EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP\nCEO / VP - Sets Vision"]
    role2["CHAMPIONS\nSenior Managers - Sponsor Projects"]
    role3["MASTER BLACK BELT\nStatistical Expert - Mentors BBs"]
    role4["BLACK BELT\nFull-time Project Leader"]
    role5["GREEN BELT\nPart-time Project Leader"]
    role6["YELLOW BELT\nTeam Member"]
    role7["WHITE BELT\nAwareness Level"]

    role1 --> role2
    role2 --> role3
    role3 --> role4
    role4 --> role5
    role5 --> role6
    role6 --> role7
```

---

## 4.3 DMAIC Phase vs Tool Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1["D - DEFINE"]
    phase2["M - MEASURE"]
    phase3["A - ANALYZE"]
    phase4["I - IMPROVE"]
    phase5["C - CONTROL"]
    toolA["Project Charter"]
    toolB["VOC CTQ Tree"]
    toolC["SIPOC Diagram"]
    toolD["Gage R and R"]
    toolE["Data Collection Plan"]
    toolF["Process Map"]
    toolG["Pareto Chart"]
    toolH["Ishikawa Fishbone"]
    toolI["Regression Analysis"]
    toolJ["Hypothesis Test"]
    toolK["DOE Design of Experiments"]
    toolL["Pilot Study"]
    toolM["SPC Control Charts"]
    toolN["FMEA Risk Matrix"]
    toolO["Control Plan"]

    phase1 --> toolA
    phase1 --> toolB
    phase1 --> toolC
    phase2 --> toolD
    phase2 --> toolE
    phase2 --> toolF
    phase3 --> toolG
    phase3 --> toolH
    phase3 --> toolI
    phase3 --> toolJ
    phase4 --> toolK
    phase4 --> toolL
    phase5 --> toolM
    phase5 --> toolN
    phase5 --> toolO
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping Reference:** Each phase has a *primary toolset*. Memorise at least **two tools per phase** for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

## 4.4 Six Sigma Closed-Loop Improvement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    id1["CUSTOMER\nVoice of Customer VOC"]
    id2["PROCESS\nInputs → Outputs"]
    id3["DATA\nStatistical Evidence"]
    id4["DECISION\nDMAIC DMADV"]
    id5["IMPROVEMENT\nSigma Level UP"]
    id6["FEEDBACK\nCOPQ Reduction"]

    id1 --> id2
    id2 --> id3
    id3 --> id4
    id4 --> id5
    id5 --> id6
    id6 --> id1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define Six Sigma. Explain the 1.5σ shift in Six Sigma quality.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

> Six Sigma is a **data-driven statistical methodology** that aims to eliminate defects and reduce process variation so that processes operate within **±6 standard deviations** of the specification limits.
>
> The **1.5σ shift** is a long-term empirical correction that accounts for **mean drift** caused by real-world disturbances such as tool wear, operator changes, and environmental variation. Over time, the process mean may shift by up to ±1.5σ. Including this shift, the **Six Sigma quality benchmark** corresponds to **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** rather than zero. Mathematically, the right-tail probability beyond $Z = 4.5$ in the standard normal distribution is **3.4 × 10⁻⁶**, which translates to 3.4 DPMO. This benchmark provides a practical, realistic target for world-class quality.

---

### **Q2. List and briefly explain any THREE roles in the Six Sigma organisation structure.**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Sample Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Champion** – A senior manager who sponsors, supports, and removes organisational barriers for Six Sigma projects. They ensure project alignment with business strategy and resource availability.
> 2. **Black Belt** – A full-time project leader trained in advanced statistical tools. Black Belts typically lead 4–6 projects annually and report directly to Champions.
> 3. **Green Belt** – A part-time practitioner who leads smaller improvement projects or supports Black Belts. Green Belts apply Six Sigma tools within their existing functional roles.

*(Additional roles for reference: Master Black Belt, Yellow Belt, White Belt, Executive Leadership.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **📌 QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Equivalent]`

**(a)** *Explain in detail the **DMAIC methodology** of Six Sigma. List at least **two tools** used in each phase. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** A manufacturing process produces **5,000 units** per day. Each unit has **25 opportunities** for defects. In a week (5 working days), the inspector recorded **375 defects** in total.
Calculate:
- (i) DPMO
- (ii) First-pass Yield
- (iii) Process Sigma Level (long-term, with 1.5σ shift) **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**DMAIC** is a five-phase structured problem-solving methodology applied to **existing processes** that fall short of specification:

| Phase | Purpose | Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **D – Define** | Identify the problem, customer (CTQ), project scope, and goals | Project Charter, VOC, SIPOC Diagram, CTQ Tree |
| **M – Measure** | Quantify current performance and establish a valid data baseline | Data Collection Plan, Gage R&R, Process Mapping, Cpk |
| **A – Analyze** | Identify and validate the **root causes** of defects | Pareto Chart, Ishikawa Fishbone, 5-Why, Regression, Hypothesis Testing |
| **I – Improve** | Develop, pilot, and implement solutions | DOE, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke, Solution Selection Matrix |
| **C – Control** | Standardise the gains; prevent regression | Control Plan, SPC Control Charts, FMEA, SOPs |

**Significance:** DMAIC ensures improvements are **data-backed**, not anecdotal, and that gains are **sustained** through formal control.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Given:**
- Units per day = 5,000
- Days per week = 5
- Opportunities per unit, $O = 25$
- Total defects, $D = 375$

**Step 1 — Total units produced per week:**

$$U = 5{,}000 \times 5 = 25{,}000 \;\; \text{units}$$

**Step 2 — DPMO calculation:**

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} = \frac{375 \times 10^{6}}{25{,}000 \times 25}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{375 \times 10^{6}}{625{,}000} = 600 \; \text{DPMO}$$

**Step 3 — Yield:**

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{600}{10^{6}} = 1 - 0.0006 = 0.9994 \;\; (99.94\%)$$

**Step 4 — Short-term Z (using standard normal inverse):**

$$Z_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}(0.9994) \approx 3.19$$

**Step 5 — Process Sigma Level (long-term):**

$$\text{Process}\sigma = 3.19 + 1.5 = 4.69\sigma$$

> **Conclusion:** The process is operating at approximately **4.69σ (600 DPMO)**, which is between 4σ and 5σ quality. To reach Six Sigma, defect-causing variation must be systematically eliminated through DMAIC.

---

### **📌 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Equivalent]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Six Sigma and TQM**. Discuss the **DMADV** methodology in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-stage process has individual stage yields: $Y_1 = 0.96$, $Y_2 = 0.94$, $Y_3 = 0.98$, $Y_4 = 0.91$.
Calculate:
- (i) Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)
- (ii) Overall defect rate
- (iii) If the company produces **50,000 units/month**, how many defective units escape to the customer? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Difference between Six Sigma and TQM:**

| Aspect | TQM | Six Sigma |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Deming, Juran (1950s Japan) | Motorola, Bill Smith (1986) |
| **Focus** | Customer satisfaction, culture | Statistical defect elimination |
| **Approach** | People-centric, behavioural | Data-driven, project-based |
| **Metric** | Customer satisfaction, cost | DPMO, Sigma Level, COPQ |
| **Structure** | Cross-functional teams | Hierarchy (Belt system) |
| **Goal** | Continuous improvement | Near-zero defects (3.4 DPMO) |
| **Duration** | Continuous, ongoing culture | Defined projects (~4–6 months each) |

**DMADV Methodology (Design for Six Sigma — DFSS):**

Used when **creating new products or processes** that must meet 6σ quality from inception.

- **D – Define:** Identify customer requirements, business case, and design goals.
- **M – Measure:** Measure CTQs, identify critical parameters, benchmark competitors.
- **A – Analyze:** Develop design alternatives; use QFD (House of Quality), FMEA, simulations.
- **D – Design:** Optimise design using **DOE**, tolerance analysis, and prototype testing.
- **V – Verify:** Pilot production, validate against customer expectations, full-scale launch.

> DMADV is **proactive** (prevent defects by design), while DMAIC is **reactive** (remove defects from existing processes).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Given:**
- $Y_1 = 0.96$, $Y_2 = 0.94$, $Y_3 = 0.98$, $Y_4 = 0.91$

**Step 1 — Rolled Throughput Yield:**

$$\text{RTY} = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.98 \times 0.91$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.8043 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 80.43\%$$

**Step 2 — Overall defect rate:**

$$\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - \text{RTY} = 1 - 0.8043 = 0.1957 \;\; (19.57\%)$$

**Step 3 — Defective units escaping to customer per month:**

$$\text{Defective Units} = 50{,}000 \times 0.1957 = 9{,}785 \;\; \text{units/month}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Management Insight:** Nearly **1 in 5 units** is defective — a serious quality crisis. Six Sigma DMAIC projects should be launched immediately on the **lowest-yield stages** ($Y_2 = 0.94$ and $Y_4 = 0.91$) for maximum impact.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift** — Students compute only the short-term Z and report it as the "sigma level". Always add **+1.5σ** for the long-term process sigma.
> 2. **Confusing DPMO with PPM** — DPMO uses *opportunities per unit*; PPM uses *units only*. Read the formula carefully.
> 3. **Mixing DMAIC with DMADV** — These are *distinct* methodologies. DMAIC = improve; DMADV = design.
> 4. **Skipping tools per phase** — A 7-mark DMAIC answer must include **at least two tools per phase** to fetch full marks.
> 5. **Neglecting Rolled Throughput Yield** — When multiple stages exist, the question expects RTY, not the arithmetic mean.
> 6. **No diagram in Part B** — Always include a **DMAIC phase diagram or SIPOC sketch** for full marks in long-answer questions.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Six Sigma**

### 🟢 Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ Six Sigma = ±6σ quality = **3.4 DPMO** with 1.5σ shift.
- ✅ DMAIC = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control (improve existing).
- ✅ DMADV = Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify (design new).
- ✅ DPMO = $\dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$.
- ✅ Process σ = $Z_{ST} + 1.5$.

### 🟢 Must-Know Belts
- ✅ Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Yellow Belt → White Belt.
- ✅ Champion = Senior Sponsor; Black Belt = Full-time project leader.

### 🟢 Must-Know Tools per Phase
- ✅ Define → Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, CTQ Tree.
- ✅ Measure → Gage R&R, Cpk, Data Collection Plan, Process Map.
- ✅ Analyze → Pareto, Ishikawa (Fishbone), 5-Why, Regression.
- ✅ Improve → DOE, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke, Pilot Study.
- ✅ Control → SPC Charts, FMEA, Control Plan, SOPs.

### 🟢 Must-Know Formulas
- ✅ $\text{RTY} = \prod Y_i$
- ✅ $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$
- ✅ $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$
- ✅ Six Sigma requires $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$.

### 🟢 Must-Know Facts
- ✅ Origin: **Motorola, 1986, Bill Smith**.
- ✅ Popularised by **GE under Jack Welch (1995)**.
- ✅ Six Sigma **complements** TQM and ISO 9000 — does NOT replace them.
- ✅ DPMO 3.4 comes from the area of the standard normal tail beyond $Z = 4.5$.
- ✅ RTY reveals the **"hidden factory"** — cumulative quality losses across stages.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Six Sigma is not a tool — it is a way of thinking, measured in sigma."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Productive Maintenance - Reliability Engineering - Types and Causes of Failures - Bath Tub Curve - System Reliability - Life Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Total Productive Maintenance - Reliability Engineering - Types and Causes of Failures - Bath Tub Curve - System Reliability - Life Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) is a holistic, proactive, and preventive maintenance philosophy that aims to maximize the Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) of production equipment by achieving zero breakdowns, zero defects, and zero accidents through the active participation of all employees — from top management to shop-floor operators — across the entire life cycle of the equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar Definition for KTU Examinations:** TPM was originally formalized by Seiichi Nakajima (Japan, 1971) as a methodology to eliminate **P-M-A losses** (Productivity, Minor Stops, Availability) and the **6 Big Losses** that cripple manufacturing throughput.

**Key Metrics in TPM:**
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** = Availability × Performance × Quality
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**
- **MTBA (Mean Time Between Assistance)**

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of TPM like maintaining your own body for a marathon. You don't wait until your knees fail before stretching or hydrating. Similarly, in a factory, TPM means operators, engineers, and managers all "stretch and hydrate" the machines daily — through autonomous maintenance (cleaning, lubricating, tightening) — so that no sudden breakdown occurs mid-shift.

---

### 1.2 Reliability Engineering

**Definition:**
Reliability Engineering is the engineering discipline concerned with designing, producing, and maintaining systems such that they perform their intended functions under stated conditions, for a specified period of time, without failure. Mathematically, reliability $R(t)$ is the **probability that a system performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified time interval $[0, t]$**.

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

Where $T$ is the random variable denoting the time-to-failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unreliability (CDF):** $F(t) = 1 - R(t) = P(T \le t)$ represents the cumulative probability of failure by time $t$.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Reliability is the "fitness promise" of a product. When a car manufacturer claims "the engine will run for 100,000 km without major repair," that is a reliability statement. Reliability Engineering is the science of building that promise into the product and proving it through rigorous testing.

---

### 1.3 Types and Causes of Failures

Failures in mechanical/electronic systems are broadly classified as follows:

| Sl. No. | Type of Failure | Definition | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Early-Life (Infant Mortality) Failures** | Failures occurring during the initial operating period due to manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, or substandard components. | Soldering defects on a PCB, improper heat treatment. |
| 2 | **Random (Chance) Failures** | Failures occurring during the useful life of the equipment due to unpredictable, statistically random events. | Sudden breakdown of a bearing under normal load. |
| 3 | **Wear-Out Failures** | Failures occurring at the end of useful life due to aging, fatigue, corrosion, or wear. | Piston ring wear, gear tooth fatigue failure. |
| 4 | **Catastrophic Failure** | Sudden, complete loss of function (cannot be predicted in advance). | Shaft fracture, transformer explosion. |
| 5 | **Degradation Failure** | Gradual decline in performance over time (parameter drift). | Capacitor ESR rise, sensor calibration drift. |

**Causes of Failures:**
- **Design-related:** Poor material selection, inadequate stress analysis, wrong tolerances.
- **Manufacturing-related:** Hidden defects, improper assembly, contamination.
- **Operational-related:** Overload, improper use, lack of maintenance.
- **Environmental-related:** Temperature, humidity, vibration, dust, corrosive atmosphere.

---

### 1.4 Bath Tub Curve

The Bath Tub Curve is the most fundamental reliability model taught in KTU. It is a graphical plot of **failure rate $\lambda(t)$ vs. operating time $t$** that resembles the cross-section of a bathtub. It has three distinct regions:

1. **Infant Mortality Region (Burn-in / Decreasing):** Failure rate decreases with time.
2. **Normal Life Region (Useful / Constant):** Failure rate is approximately constant.
3. **Wear-Out Region (Increasing):** Failure rate increases due to aging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "Three Zones of a Bathtub":** *Born weak, Live steady, Die old.*

---

### 1.5 System Reliability

System Reliability is the probability that a *system* (composed of multiple components or sub-systems) performs its intended function. Systems are mathematically modelled using:
- **Series Configuration:** All components must work for the system to work.
- **Parallel Configuration (Redundant):** System works if at least one component works.
- **Combined (Series–Parallel) Configuration:** Hybrid arrangements.

---

### 1.6 Life Testing

Life Testing is a controlled accelerated or natural test conducted to estimate the reliability, failure distribution, and mean life of components/systems. It is essential because end-users cannot wait for the natural end-of-life during product development.

**Types:**
- **Complete (Type I Censored) Life Test:** Run until ALL units fail.
- **Truncated (Type II Censored) Life Test:** Terminate after a pre-fixed time $t_0$ or after $r$ failures.
- **Accelerated Life Test (ALT):** Apply higher stress (temperature, voltage, vibration) to compress life time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reliability Function $R(t)$ — Operational Logic

The reliability function is derived from the failure density $f(t)$:

$$R(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1 - F(t)$$

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. The probability density of failure $f(t)$ represents how failures are distributed across time.
2. The probability of surviving beyond time $t$ is the area under the curve from $t$ to $\infty$.
3. This integral is by definition $1 - F(t)$, where $F(t)$ is the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF).
4. The **failure rate (hazard rate)** is defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

5. For an **exponential distribution** (constant failure rate region), this simplifies the relationship dramatically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Exponential?** In the useful-life region of the bath-tub curve, $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ = constant. This is the ONLY distribution with the **memoryless property**, which means $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of survival up to time $t$ (constant hazard) | Dimensionless |
| Unreliability (CDF) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ | Dimensionless |
| Probability Density | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Rate of failure occurrence | per unit time |
| Failure (Hazard) Rate | $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ | Instantaneous failure rate | per hour (or 1/time) |
| Mean Time To Failure | $MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | For non-repairable systems | hours / cycles |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | For repairable systems (between repairs) | hours / cycles |
| Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR$ | Average time to restore a failed system | hours |
| Availability | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Steady-state operational availability | fraction (0–1) |
| Series System Reliability | $R_S = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All units must function | dimensionless |
| Parallel System Reliability | $R_P = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | At least one must function | dimensionless |
| Active Redundancy (n units, k needed) | $\sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^{i}(1-R)^{n-i}$ | k-out-of-n system | dimensionless |
| Standby Redundancy | $R_{st} = e^{-\lambda t}[1 + \lambda t]$ | One unit on, one warm standby | dimensionless |
| Weibull CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Time-to-failure with shape $\beta$ | dimensionless |
| Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Generalized reliability function | dimensionless |
| Bath-Tub Curve Hazard | $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 + \alpha t + \beta e^{-\gamma t}$ | Composite hazard model | per unit time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** When a question says *"components are independent and identically distributed with reliability $R$"* — always default to the formulas above.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft engine reliability is computed using Weibull distribution with $\beta > 1$ to model wear-out.
- **Power Plants:** Series-parallel grid reliability is modelled to compute Loss of Load Probability (LOLP).
- **Automotive:** MTBF estimates drive warranty cost projections and service interval scheduling.
- **Semiconductor:** Infant mortality failures are eliminated using **burn-in** testing — exactly because the bath-tub curve has high $\lambda(t)$ early.
- **TPM Application:** Knowing which region of the bath-tub curve a failure belongs to determines whether TPM calls for design change, scheduled overhaul, or predictive maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Exponential Reliability Function

We assume the constant hazard (memoryless) region of the bath-tub curve. The hazard is constant: $\lambda(t) = \lambda$.

The fundamental differential equation relating hazard and reliability is:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln[R(t)]$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant):

$$\lambda = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln[R(t)]$$

**Step 2:** Cross-multiply and separate variables:

$$d[\ln R(t)] = -\lambda\,dt$$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$, with $R(0) = 1$ (a brand-new unit must be reliable at $t = 0$):

$$\int_{0}^{t} d[\ln R(x)]\,dx = -\int_{0}^{t}\lambda\,dx$$

$$\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 4:** Since $R(0) = 1$, $\ln R(0) = 0$:

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the **Exponential Reliability Function** — the most commonly cited result in KTU exams.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)

By definition, mean life is the expected value of the time-to-failure random variable $T$:

$$MTTF = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt$$

**Step 1:** Apply integration by parts with $u = t$, $dv = f(t)\,dt$:

$$MTTF = [t \cdot F(t)]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} F(t)\,dt$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the boundary term. As $t \to \infty$, $t \cdot F(t)$ must be finite for any integrable density; for the exponential, $F(\infty) = 1$ but $t \to \infty$ is balanced by the exponential decay of $f$. Using L'Hôpital's rule or boundary condition, the term evaluates to a finite limit (zero for exponential). Thus:

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral:

$$MTTF = \left[\frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \left(0 - \frac{1}{-\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** $\lambda$ = 1/MTBF. If a component fails at a constant rate of **0.001 failures per hour**, then on average it runs for **1000 hours** between failures.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Series System Reliability

For $n$ **independent** components in series (any failure breaks the system):

**Step 1:** Define event $S_i$ = "Component $i$ functions at time $t$". The system functions iff all $S_i$ occur:

$$P(\text{System OK}) = P(S_1 \cap S_2 \cap \cdots \cap S_n)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **multiplicative rule for independent events**:

$$R_S = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Step 3:** If all $n$ components are identical with reliability $R$:

$$R_S = R^n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **assumption of independence**. The series formula is valid ONLY when component failures are statistically independent. Always mention this in derivations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Parallel System Reliability

For $n$ components in parallel redundancy (system works if at least one component works):

**Step 1:** The system fails only when ALL components fail. Define failure of component $i$ as $S_i^{c}$.

$$P(\text{System Fails}) = P(S_1^{c} \cap S_2^{c} \cap \cdots \cap S_n^{c})$$

**Step 2:** Probability that component $i$ fails = $(1 - R_i)$. By independence:

$$P(\text{System Fails}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$

**Step 3:** Hence, system reliability is:

$$R_P = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$

**Step 4:** For 2 identical components, each $R$:

$$R_P = 1 - (1-R)^2 = 2R - R^2$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples

**Example 1:** A system has 3 components in series with reliabilities $0.9, 0.95, 0.98$. Find $R_S$ and MTTF if all have $\lambda = 0.001$/hr.

$$R_S = 0.9 \times 0.95 \times 0.98 = 0.8382$$

$$\lambda_{eq} = 0.001 + 0.001 + 0.001 = 0.003 \text{ /hr}$$

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda_{eq}} = 333.33 \text{ hours}$$

**Example 2:** Two identical components ($R = 0.9$ each) in parallel. Compute $R_P$ and improvement ratio.

$$R_P = 2(0.9) - (0.9)^2 = 1.8 - 0.81 = 0.99$$

$$\text{Improvement} = \frac{0.99}{0.9} = 1.1 \text{ (10\% gain)}$$

**Example 3:** A system has failure rate $\lambda = 0.0002$/hr. Compute $R(1000)$ and $R(5000)$.

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

$$R(5000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 5000} = e^{-1.0} = 0.3679$$

---

### 3.6 Mathematical Model of the Bath-Tub Curve

The composite hazard rate function is the sum of three components:

$$\lambda(t) = \underbrace{\alpha e^{-\beta t}}_{\text{Infant Mortality}} + \underbrace{\lambda_0}_{\text{Constant Random}} + \underbrace{\gamma \cdot t}_{\text{Wear-Out}}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = initial failure rate magnitude (decreasing)
- $\beta$ = decay rate of infant mortality failures
- $\lambda_0$ = intrinsic (constant) random failure rate
- $\gamma$ = wear-out growth coefficient

At $t = 0$: $\lambda(0) = \alpha + \lambda_0$ (high)
At $t = t_1$: Infant mortality decays, leaving $\lambda \approx \lambda_0$
At $t = t_2$: Wear-out dominates, $\lambda(t) \to \infty$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Reliability" of a product. State its mathematical form for an exponential failure distribution.*

**Model Answer:**
Reliability is the probability that a product, component, or system performs its intended function under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time without failure. For an exponential failure distribution with constant failure rate $\lambda$, the reliability function is:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity between 0 and 1, where $R(t) = 1$ implies perfect reliability and $R(t) = 0$ implies certain failure.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three causes of equipment failure and explain the concept of the Bath Tub Curve briefly.*

**Model Answer:**
**Three Causes of Failure:**
1. **Design-related causes** – poor material selection, inadequate stress analysis.
2. **Operational causes** – overload, improper handling, lack of maintenance.
3. **Environmental causes** – temperature extremes, humidity, vibration, corrosion.

**Bath Tub Curve:** The Bath Tub Curve is a graphical plot of failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus operating time $t$. It consists of three regions: the **infant mortality region** (decreasing failure rate due to manufacturing defects), the **normal life region** (constant failure rate due to random failures), and the **wear-out region** (increasing failure rate due to aging and fatigue). The shape resembles the cross-section of a bathtub.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the **8 Pillars of TPM** with a neat block diagram. Discuss how autonomous maintenance contributes to achieving zero breakdowns. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system consists of **3 components in series** with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.90$, $R_3 = 0.85$. Compute:
1. System reliability.
2. System MTTF if each component has constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001, 0.002, 0.003$ per hour respectively.
3. System reliability at $t = 200$ hours. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**
The 8 Pillars of TPM, introduced by Seiichi Nakajima, form the structural foundation of Total Productive Maintenance:

1. **Pillar 1 – Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** Operators take ownership of routine maintenance like cleaning, lubrication, and tightening. This pillar detects early signs of abnormality and prevents infant mortality failures.
2. **Pillar 2 – Planned Maintenance (Keikaku Hozen):** Schedules preventive and predictive maintenance activities to keep MTBF high and MTTR low.
3. **Pillar 3 – Quality Maintenance:** Defect prevention through root-cause analysis (e.g., 5-Why, Why-Why analysis) and Poka-Yoke systems.
4. **Pillar 4 – Education & Training:** Multi-skill development of operators and engineers.
5. **Pillar 5 – Early Equipment Management:** New equipment designed with TPM principles — easy to clean, lubricate, and inspect.
6. **Pillar 6 – Process and Quality Management:** Focuses on zero-defect processes.
7. **Pillar 7 – TPM in Administration:** Extends TPM principles to non-production areas like finance, HR, and purchase.
8. **Pillar 8 – Safety, Health, Environment:** Creates a safe, accident-free workplace.

**Autonomous Maintenance & Zero Breakdowns:** Operators conduct daily tasks — cleaning (C), lubrication (L), tightening (T), inspection (I) — collectively known as **CLTI**. This ensures abnormal conditions (vibration, leaks, overheating) are detected *before* they escalate into breakdowns, directly attacking the infant-mortality and random-failure regions of the bath-tub curve.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — System Reliability (Series Configuration):**

$$R_S = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 = 0.7268$$

**Step 2 — System MTTF (with different failure rates):**

For series systems with constant (exponential) failure rates, the equivalent failure rate is the sum:

$$\lambda_{eq} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 0.001 + 0.002 + 0.003 = 0.006 \text{ /hr}$$

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda_{eq}} = \frac{1}{0.006} = 166.67 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — System Reliability at $t = 200$ hours:**

$$R_S(t) = e^{-\lambda_{eq} \cdot t} = e^{-0.006 \times 200} = e^{-1.2}$$

$$R_S(200) = 0.3012 \approx 30.12\%$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Bath Tub Curve** and describe how TPM addresses each region of the curve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A computer server system uses **dual processors in parallel (hot standby redundancy)**. Each processor has reliability $R = 0.95$ at mission time $t = 100$ hours. Calculate:
1. System reliability using parallel redundancy.
2. MTTF of the parallel system.
3. Comment on whether adding redundancy is economically justified if standby cost is high. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Bath Tub Curve Sketch & Description:**

The Bath Tub Curve is plotted with **failure rate $\lambda(t)$** on the Y-axis and **operating time $t$** on the X-axis. It resembles a bath tub in cross-section.

| Region | Time Span | Failure Rate | Cause | TPM Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I — Infant Mortality | $0$ to $t_1$ | Decreasing | Manufacturing defects, workmanship | **Burn-in testing** at factory; design review; **Autonomous Maintenance (Pillar 1)** detects early defects. |
| II — Useful Life | $t_1$ to $t_2$ | Constant | Random operational stresses | **Planned Maintenance (Pillar 2)**, condition monitoring, vibration analysis. |
| III — Wear-Out | $t_2$ onwards | Increasing | Aging, fatigue, corrosion | **Scheduled Overhaul**, replacement, **Early Equipment Management (Pillar 5)** for new design. |

**TPM addresses the entire curve** through proactive operator involvement (catching Region I failures), scheduled preventive maintenance (Region II), and lifetime extension strategies like overhauls (Region III).

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — System Reliability (Parallel Configuration):**

For 2 identical processors in parallel:

$$R_P = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 1 - (0.05)^2 = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975$$

**Step 2 — MTTF of the Parallel System:**

For exponential components, MTTF of parallel system with $n = 2$ identical units:

$$MTTF_{parallel} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$$

Since $R = e^{-\lambda t} = 0.95$ at $t = 100$ hours:

$$\lambda = -\frac{\ln(0.95)}{100} = \frac{0.05129}{100} = 5.129 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr}$$

$$MTTF_{parallel} = \frac{3}{2 \times 5.129 \times 10^{-4}} = 2924.5 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Economic Justification:**

A single processor MTTF:

$$MTTF_{single} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = 1949.4 \text{ hours}$$

Adding redundancy:
- Reliability improves from **0.95 → 0.9975** (≈ **5% gain**).
- MTTF improves from **1949 → 2924 hours** (≈ **50% gain**).

**Comment:** For mission-critical applications (e.g., server farms, aircraft, hospital equipment), this improvement is **highly justified** because downtime cost (lost transactions, safety risk) far exceeds the standby equipment cost. For non-critical applications (consumer products), single processor is preferable due to cost-effectiveness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute $R_P = 2R$ (wrong). The correct parallel formula is $R_P = 1 - (1-R)^2 = 2R - R^2$. Mistaking this loses **3 marks** instantly.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- **Reliability Definition:** $R(t) = P(T > t)$ — Probability of survival up to time $t$.
- **Core Exponential Formula:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — valid in constant-hazard region only.
- **MTTF = 1/λ** for non-repairable; **MTBF ≈ 1/λ** for repairable.
- **Series Reliability:** $R_S = \prod R_i$ (components INDEPENDENT).
- **Parallel Reliability:** $R_P = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$.
- **k-out-of-n Reliability:** Use binomial expansion.
- **Bath Tub Curve:** 3 regions — Infant Mortality (↓), Useful Life (—), Wear-Out (↑).
- **TPM Pillars:** 8 Pillars, all aimed at **Zero Breakdown, Zero Defect, Zero Accident**.
- **Weibull Distribution:** Generalizes exponential; uses shape parameter $\beta$ (β < 1: infant mortality; β = 1: random; β > 1: wear-out).
- **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality** — Core TPM metric.
- **Availability:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- **Life Testing Types:** Type I (time-censored), Type II (failure-censored), ALT (stress-accelerated).
- **Censoring:** Always denote censored data clearly; use MLE or rank regression for parameter estimation.
- **Assumption to State:** Always state *"assuming components are statistically independent and identically distributed"* in system reliability problems.
